Original_Search_Title: Saraki Advises W/Bank On Funds Release
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Advises W/Bank On Funds Release
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State has advised the World Bank to review the procedures for releasing funds to states for development programmes.
A statement by the Chief Press Secretary to the governor, Mr.Bisi Abidoye in Ilorin quoted the governor as saying this at the just concluded Nigeria Country Assistance Strategy Consultative Forum at Ijebu-Ode in Ogun State.
The governor urged the bank to focus its assistance on key projects capable of enhancing the rapid development of the country, pleaded that only sectors and projects on which careful assessment had been done based on the actual needs of states should be given priority.
Arguing that many years of assistance by the World Bank had not yielded commensurate results, the governor decried the poor method of data collection in the country and called for appropriate yardsticks for measuring the performance of states by the Bank.
Saraki said assistance of the bank should be intensified in provision of water in rural areas; road network; improved power generation and qualitative education.
He called for small-scale processing unit at the council level to minimise wastages of cassava, which he said, could be processed into cassava chips and pellets.
He urged the bank to engender competitiveness and partnership to ensure the success of its programmes and praised its vision at focusing on states.

Original_Search_Title: Spare Time for Your Children This Xmas
Headline: Uganda: Spare Time for Your Children This Xmas
Body_Text:
Kampala — Memories of my childhood fill me with nostalgia. I remember December as a time of strengthening our family bonds. It was a time when our family could sit around a fireplace and enjoy each other's company. Our father could share tales of the olden days as we roasted sweet potatoes and cassava. While other more affluent families gathered around their television sets, we gathered around a fireplace, not watching movies but enjoying wisdom-filled tales from an old man. Those days are long gone and we cannot enjoy the fireplace union anymore but the memories will never be erased.
I still long for the fireplace moments, not because of the roasted potatoes we shared, but the solidarity those moments created in our family.
As parents dig deep into their pockets for stylish Christmas gifts, there is a gift often forgotten, yet so precious. Time. Children recall clearly and fondly the time spent together more than a birthday or a Christmas present. Any family wishing to build solidarity must invest quality time together.
We are living in a fast-paced world. Families are caught in life corridors as they struggle to make ends meet. Relationship between family members is no longer a priority. Survival takes the centre stage. We are becoming part-time parents and strangers to our own children. God forbid!
Do you belong to a category of parents who care for their children through remote control? Many parents desire to have time with their children, but the strains of livelihood make this impossible. The good news is, time is here and your child too is at your disposal. Will you spare some precious time for your child this festive season? Help your child to build a bank of precious memories to keep for life.
The following tips would help you strengthen your family bonds and establish precious memories:
l Develop a culture of having a family time together. Whatever faith you belong to, prayer is an indispensable family activity. Indeed, holding hands during family prayer evokes a sense of belonging and cultivates closeness.
l Spare some time to play with your child. This might sound crazy to many parents, yet children value it so much. Play helps you to relate with your child in a free, relaxed atmosphere. Fly kites, play indoor games and jump around together. This is the joy of parenthood. Enjoy it!
l Hold hands in an evening or nature walk with your child. Answer their questions as they wonder why the sky is blue and leaves are green.
l These family activities set your family apart and accord it a unique identity. Did you know that children transfer family culture and values from parents to their own families later in life? It looks simple yet you are building a legacy, which will remain through generations. Remember, your home is a model. Ensure that your child receives the right orientation from your family.
Your child doesn't need things as much as he needs you. Although childhood is a precious stage, it is passing away. Utilise it and leave a rubberstamp in your child's life. How great will you feel when your child describes you as 'a faithful, caring and responsible parent' 15 years from now? Do not forget that time spent with your child is more precious than an expensive Christmas gift. Wishing you a merry Christmas in your child's company.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria And the World
Headline: Nigeria And the World
Body_Text:
Lagos — Philip Asiodu at Seventy
"An indiscreet use of oil revenues could create a false standard-of-living and a pattern of consumption which, if not backed up by the development of internal industrial capacity, would lead to serious economic and social troubles when the revenues from oil, relative to rising expectations of the people and the growing population, begin to decline.
Philip Asiodu as Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Mines and Power, March 1972. I return yet again to the place of the intellectual enterprise in nation-building. I was privileged to have a close encounter with Mr. Allison Ayida, Nigeria's former Head of Service, in Oxford in the late spring of 2003. The occasion was a conference of scholars to chart a direction for Nigeria's foreign relations in the new century.
Mr. Ayida presided over one of the panels at the conference. He also gave the keynote address during the conference dinner. He spoke little during the deliberations, only intervening when there was a real danger that discussions were veering off course. He was ever courteous, articulate. In the refectory of St Antony's College where we had repaired to for lunch, he carried his food tray himself, stood in line with the rest of us younger scholars, and patiently waited his turn. Allison Ayida was the very picture of humility.
Mr. Ayida's countenance, radiating a gentle serenity, and his unique insights all through the conference, forced me to undertake a reassessment of the place of that first generation of incredibly young civil servants and policy makers who were saddled with the task of piloting the nation to prosperity and social order after independence. Shortly after defending my thesis in Oxford in the late summer of 2002, I had returned to the Bodleian Library and re-read Ayida and Asiodu's writings on Nigeria and how she might best serve her diverse peoples. I had been re-educated, an epiphany the conference encounter further confirmed.
On returning to Nigeria a couple of months later I went to visit with Mr. Ayida in his Lagos office but was told he was away on holiday. I sought out a friend who is related to Philip Asiodu and asked him to kindly arrange a meeting. I had had occasion to say harsh things about Asiodu's role in government in this column, and I felt a need, in the light of the new understanding my reading in the Bodleian afforded me, to make ammends where I considered this necessary, but also to defend my stance where I judged it right.
Sadly, the meeting did not take place as I had to leave Lagos overnight to travel to the Niger delta where Nigerian troops had surrounded yet another village and were threatening to flatten it with machinegun fire - all because the villagers had had 'the audacity' of asking Shell executives to install electricity in their hamlet in exchange for the millions of dollars in oil they take away from their land every month.
When news reached me in January 2004 that Asiodu was about to clock seventy and that preparations were underway to mark the event in various social gatherings, I chose to stay away and instead asked for latest material on his life and times published in Nigeria that were not available in Oxford, to enable me have a rounded picture of the celebrant.
I hardly attend birthday parties and sundry social events. The photographs of overdressed Nigerians that smile at you from the Sunday newspaper social pages never fail to irritate me. What is there to smile at when four decades after independence Nigerian homes are without piped water or reliable power supply or efficient and affordable phone lines? What is there to smile at when some fifty million young Nigerian women and men are without work and without skills and without hope, and are now drifting into armed robbery and prostitution and sundry vices, a prelude to the fire that will storm into the multi-million Naira homes of these smiling 'Owambe' personages one day soon?
Thankfully, the events marking Asiodu's seventieth birthday were low-keyed, restrained, and animated by the intellectualism that is now a rarity in Nigerian social life, but which was taken for granted in the late 1950s into sixties when he and his fellow permanent secretaries set out to do battle with poverty, illiteracy and disease - the legacies that four hundred years of slavery and colonialism had saddled the peoples of Africa with. I have been reading two books, one a collection of essays written by the celebrant himself, and the other a biography entitled Philip Asiodu: A Pillar of Nation Building, written by Oladapo Odebiyi. This is my way of participating in the ceremony of eulogies.
The subject of a biography is best served when the author maintains a distance the easier to see him or her in the round, foibles, virtues and all. Indeed, our virtues shine forth ever more brightly when they are made to do battle with the less noble parts of our nature and emerge triumphant in the fullness of time. Oladapo Odebiyi stood too close to his subject for comfort, forcing the reader of this otherwise fine biography to seek out alternative narratives to balance his story. But one fact stands incontestable: Philip Chukwuedo Asiodu, like his friend Allison Ayida, is a remarkable man, a path-breaking achiever in diverse fields of life.
I should like to dwell on three achievements, among so many, for which future chroniclers of Nigeria's political and economic history will have cause to remember Philip Asiodu. The young Asiodu, then only thirty two, was a member of the high-powered Federal Economic and Finance Advisory Committee, a policy think tank set up by the government of the late Prime Minister Tafawa Balewa when General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi seized power in January 1966 and began preparations to dissolve the regions and impose a unitary government on the country.
It is to their credit that Asiodu, Ayida and several other permanent secretaries on the Committee warned the military government on the dangers of Decree No. 34 - a position that was to be fully vindicated a year and half later when the country was plunged into civil war. As Permanent Secretary in the Federal Ministry of Industries, Asiodu put in place in 1966 a five year development plan which by 1971 had installed a backward integration programme for the textile industry, ending the importation of baft. Asiodu was one of the chief pioneering spirits behind economic integration in the West African region - through facilitating the emergence of the regional ECOBANK group.
Long before Obasanjo's merry crowd latched on cassava export as the 'new' golden elixir, Philip Asiodu had written a series of policy papers in the early 1970s highlighting the pivotal place of this product in a strategy for Nigerian industrialization. It is for these reasons that I dedicate this column today to Philip Asiodu.
And I urge you, dear reader, to get hold of a copy of his biography in the bookshop. It is not only the story of the man; it is also the story of our nation on the cusp of seismic changes in her turbulent history and the manner in which a remarkable generation responded to these challenges.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria/China in Cassava Export Deal
Headline: Nigeria/China in Cassava Export Deal
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria and China have reached an agreement for the export of over 5,000 metric tonnes of cassava to China.
The Minister of Commerce, Mr Idris Waziri announced this on 15 December in Abuja when he met with members of the Cassava Exporters Association of Nigeria.
He also announced that the Nigerian Export Import Bank has set aside N400 million to assist cassava chips exporters.
Waziri challenged exporters to take advantage of the opportunity provided by business with China, as this could diversify Nigeria's economy.
In a recent visit to China, Waziri was informed of the Chinese preference for Nigerian cassava chips over Taiwanese cassava.
Exportation is expected to begin before the end of the first quarter of 2005.

Original_Search_Title: Killer Baby Food Floods Markets - NAFDAC Raises Alarm
Headline: Nigeria: Killer Baby Food Floods Markets - NAFDAC Raises Alarm
Body_Text:
Lagos — NATIONAL Agency for Food and Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC) yesterday alerted the public to a fake, killer SMA infant baby formula in the market, saying the product is stuffed with cassava flour.
Already, a suspect, Mr. Sixtus Agbaegbon, has been dragged before a Federal High Court, Abuja charged with being in possession of dangerous products that could kill children and for illegal manufacture and faking of SMA baby milk.
Speaking at a training programme for NAFDAC Monitoring Officers on Marketing of Breast Milk Substitutes (BMS) in Abuja, the Director General of the agency, Dr (Mrs) Dora Akunyili, said the infant formula did not comply with international safety standards.
Akunyili said due to non-adherence to the international marketing standards, Nigerian babies were exposed to avoidable health hazards.
Stressing that NAFDAC was worried that child killer ailments like low intelligent quotient (IQ), infant mortality, physical disabilities, low vision as well as other sickness have been traced to non-compliance with international safety codes in the production and marketing of baby food items.
At the court in Abuja yesterday the prosecutor Mr Kingsley Ejiofor, of the office of the Attorney-General of the Federation, said the offence contravenued section 1(9) of the Counterfeit and Fake Drugs and unwholesome Processed Foods (Miscellaneous) Act Cap C 34 CFN, 2002 and punishable under section 3 (1) (9) of same Act.
Also, Mr. Agbaegbu was alleged to have contavened section 8 of the Marketing (Breast Milk Substitutes) Act Cap M5 LFN, 2002, punishable under section 11 of the same Act.
Mr Ejiofor told Justice Awuli Chikere that the accused could not physically appear in court to answer to the charges because he was sick.
The counsel had allegedly confessed to the crime which has the consequences leading to vomiting, diarrhoea, stomach pain, fever and death if consumed by children.
The arraignment followed an apology demanded from the Anambra State Pure Water Manufacturers Association. According to Mrs Akunyili, while briefing newsmen in Abuja, the association had lied to the public that the agency had increased the registration fee for pure water manufacturing license fees.
Pointing out that at no point did NAFDAC do such a thing, she said the fees paid for product registration were fixed since 2002 adding that "we do not have any intention to increase the fees."
She stated that rather than "singly increase the price of pure water, they (seller) should not hide under an alibi that NAFDAC increased its registration fees whereas we did not do so."
Mrs Akunyili noted that the agency would be forced to mobilise against them, stressing that "what they did was very wrong."
She said even though pure water manufactures receive much returns on their investment, "they should be satisfied with what they make rather than compound the masses hardship, especially by telling lies with NAFDAC name."

Original_Search_Title: 'Why Foreigners Snub Investment in Nigeria'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Why Foreigners Snub Investment in Nigeria'
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Public Affairs Officer, United States of America (USA) embassy in
Nigeria, Ms Claudio Anyaso has advanced a reason why foreign investment is
low in Nigeria.
Fielding questions from newsmen in Port Harcourt yesterday, the officer
said foreigners shun investment in Nigeria because Nigerians themselves are
not investing in their own country.
"If Nigerians do not invest in their own country, why should other
people invest in Nigeria", she said; stressing that Nigerians would always
want others to do something for them which they could do by themselves.
The embassy official who was in Port Harcourt to commission the Public
Information center on America called America Corner, said investment was the
only way to build the economy adding that the key to economic development
was self-help and any nation that wants to progress must put in to its own
development its resources.
She maintained that the American government's foreign policy in
democracy, free trade and any other area will continue without any
significant difference from what is presently implemented. Anyaso commended
the Nigerian government for its current reforms going on in the country and
some projects like the cassava project and programmes on HIV/AIDS.
She emphasised that accountability, transparency, the rule of the law
among other things are crucial for the success of the reforms.

Original_Search_Title: Everlight College: a School Chance for Bunga's Deprived
Headline: Uganda: Everlight College: a School Chance for Bunga's Deprived
Body_Text:
Kampala — Many affluent families employ house helps the majority of whom are either orphans or those who dropped out of school for various reasons. Some are very intelligent, but could not continue with education.
It was against this background that in 2001, Mrs. Specioza Suruma started Everlight College, Bunga, to cater for these wretched of the earth. From a tree shed school with only one building then, the wife to Dr. Ezra Suruma, the director of economic affairs at the Movement Secretariat, hopes to give these children a bright future.
"It was the situation at my home that drove me to open the school," says Mrs. Suruma, the director and proprietor of Everlight College.
"I discovered that my houseboy was very intelligent, but his intelligence was misguided. He leaned more toward destructive work. I decided to start the school as guidance to children who would otherwise have no chance to go to secondary school.
"I look at these children. Some of them are very brilliant but unless somebody guides them, they cannot be useful to themselves, their families or the nation," she says with concern.
Mrs. Suruma holds a Bachelor of Education degree from Makerere University and has taught in several schools, including Kibibi Secondary School, Mengo SS, Lincoln International (now International School of Uganda) as well as Rainbow Academy.
Using her meagre resources, she partitioned one of the rooms of the only building then into senior one and two. She then put an advert for teachers and employed nine of them.
She had three pioneer students - her houseboy, a sixteen-year old girl from the neighbourhood and her friend's niece. Each of the teachers stood before one student and taught with enthusiasm.
"It was very difficult in the beginning because I had to find means of paying the teachers," she recalls.
But with the help of her husband, the school limped on and soon the enrolment grew to 15, but most studied without paying any fees. It became hard to pay teachers' salaries when her husband also run out of resources. "At one point, one of the teachers had to go to Bunga Hill to borrow chalk," Mrs. Suruma recounts.
By the end of the year, Mrs Suruma was so stressed that she decided to close the school and start looking for a job.
"I decided to find a job so that I could pay the teachers," Mrs. Suruma says.
The following year, 15 students showed up with old teachers and new ones looking for vacancies. The need was big and so the school had to reopen. By the end of the term, they were 40.
Today, Everlight College has grown to three blocks, 12 teachers and 93 students, the pioneers now being in senior five. The school also has a boarding section. The girls' dormitory is a four-bedroom house, which is part of the Suruma family property. Mrs. Suruma's Boys' quarters serve as the boys' dormitory.
The school has school gardens growing maize, cassava, potatoes, onions, matooke, beans and vegetables. These supplement their diet.
The students are also involved in co-curricular activities like music, dance and drama and sports, occasionally holding competitions with neighbouring schools.
As Mrs. Suruma walks around the school, it is evident that she has a personal relationship with the students as she calls them by name. "My vision is to give the students my heart and to have their hearts, to be able to love each other, to grow together," she says.
The students are calm, neat and disciplined. "It is the Christian base here that has helped us transform these children," she says. She says that about 80% of the students are orphaned and some live on their own, hence, have no one to discipline them.
"The motto here is character building. By the end of their term, their character has been transformed and the community can testify to that," she says.
The school instills in the children discipline, the fear of God, the spirit of voluntarism and the motivation to work hard. Mrs. Suruma's desire is to produce educatable citizens who can use their creativity to make a living.
Even though the school is Christian-founded, it is open to people from all religious denominations.
There is still a big need for expansion of the school. NGOs and government tend to be bent toward younger children and neglect secondary education.
Mrs. Suruma believes that someone has to pick up those children who have only ended at primary level and give them secondary education, so that they have a better chance in the future.
Vision for Africa currently sponsors about 20 students in the school, while Mrs. Suruma herself sponsors 10. They have enough land to expand but no money.
"My plan is to find funding and people who are like-minded to join me in this noble cause. I can't do it on my own," she says.
Mrs. Suruma insists that Everlight College is not a school for house boys and house girls or for the disadvantaged as many people think.
She is just giving chance to people who would have otherwise not had a secondary education a chance to have one.

Original_Search_Title: 15 Freed From Angolan Captivity
Headline: Zambia: 15 Freed From Angolan Captivity
Body_Text:
ALL the 15 Zambians arrested by Angolan security forces on October 20, 2004 have been unconditionally released.
Home Affairs Minister Ronnie Shikapwasha told Parliament in a ministerial statement yesterday that the 15, who were all men, were released on November 19, 2004 and brought to Mwinilunga district by the Zambian immigration officers on November 20.
Lieutenant General Shikapwasha said the men, who came through Kamapanda border post, were in good health and reported that they had not been harassed but rather subjected to manual work like slashing during the captive period.
The minister said the men, who were arrested for using bush paths, had no weapons and the bicycles they used to enter Angola had since been handed back.
However, one bag of cassava, bundles of fish and money that that the men were found with had not been recovered.
Gen Shikapwasha warned Zambians that crossing into a foreign country without proper documentation was a serious and punishable offence.
He paid tribute to the Angolan authorities for the unconditional release of the Zambian men.
"This only goes to show the strong bondage that exists between the two sister countries," Gen Shikapwasha said.
The minister also paid tribute to the security and defence forces of the two countries for working together in an operation that did not result into any loss of life.
And the minister said Government was still negotiating for easy entry into Angola for Zambians living along the border lines as was the situation along other border points where people living within the radius of 30 kilometres were only given passes to cross into the other country.
Gen Shikapwasha said the situation was different with Angola which has just emerged out of a war situation and systems had to be put in place, among them, the pass system for those along the border lines through the Joint Permanent Commission (JPC) for the two countries.
The minister said Government was doing what it could to mark the border and the process was already underway in Eastern Province through the ministry of Lands.
And Parliament heard yesterday that 21,867 cases of cholera were recorded between 1991 and 2004 out of which, 1,230 deaths were recorded.
Health Deputy Minister Kapembwa Simbao told the House that no records were available at the ministry for the same incidences of cholera for the period between 1970 and 1981.
The deputy minister was responding to a question by Chiengi MP Katele Kalumba (MMD) who wanted to know the yearly incidences of cholera morbidity recorded for the periods 1970 and 2004.
The House also heard that the ministry of Health would deploy medical doctors currently at the Central Board of Health to hospitals as soon as the National Health Act was repealed.
Health Minister Brian Chituwo told the House that the ministry had embarked on a retention scheme to attract medical personnel to rural areas through an incentive mechanisms with the support of the Netherlands government.

Original_Search_Title: Would You Like Some Goat, Please?
Headline: Kenya: Would You Like Some Goat, Please?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When Matheka Mwololo and Joyce Wanza married in 1983, they made a decision that stunned their guests: instead of a wedding cake, they had roast goat. Expecting tiers of cake with icing, ribbons and flowers, they were faced with roast goat on wooden spikes.
But the guests soon caught the spirit of the unique offering and dug in. They left licking their lips, having thoroughly approved of the alternative cake. Says Mwololo, a doctor: "The wedding cake represents the first meal the newly-weds offer their special guests. Among the Akamba, the goat is slaughtered only for very special guests."
In honour of the occasion, the goat was roasted in vertical rather than the horizontal position in ordinary goat eating parties. "It was propped on three sticks for ease of cutting of the meat, the same way a cake is laid to make cutting easy," says Mwololo.
Why go to so much trouble? Mwololo laughs, then says: "I just wanted to be different in a positive way. I took into consideration the fact that my forefathers had no cakes, but lots of goats to feed their guests on occasions they considered important."
He pauses, then adds: "I considered my wedding an African cultural event. In keeping with Kamba tradition, my mother smeared my wife with raw ghee inside the church. Ordinarily, that happens at the entrance to the mother-in-law's kitchen as a sign that she has accepted and respects her son's choice of bride."
Mwololo went out of his way to look for a traditional equivalent of the ring, with no luck. In place of suits and ties, Mwololo and his party wore kitenge outfits. "My wife was prevailed upon weeks to the ceremony to sport the conventional wedding dress complete with tiara and a net. It was quite a scene to behold as we went down the aisle in colourful diversity!"
Mwololo's "return to our roots" approach is typical of a movement by many Africans to reclaim their traditions. Take the case of baptism. Why should we adopt foreign names? I constantly ask myself why my parents gave me the Jewish name Joseph at baptism and not that of my paternal grandfather Odiembo or my maternal one, Odegi, who was famous for his bravery and foresight.
Jaramogi Oginga Odinga dropped his baptismal name, Adonijah, because it probably did not mean much to him. And President Mwai Kibaki is rarely referred to as Emilio. And, of course, there was the case of Zairean dictator Joseph Mobutu, who exchanged Joseph for Sese Seko Kuku Ngbendu wa Nzambanga (the cock that rules hens) and prevailed upon his people to discard their foreign names.
For most of us, it is a mish-mash of the traditional and a modern attitude. While clergy in mainstream churches still dress in the full regalia of the ancient Roman empire, those in the Seventh Day Adventist Church have gone ordinary and dress is not an issue. Among the Legio Maria, it is expected that followers remove six of their lower teeth in keeping with Luo tradition that is hardly practiced outside of the sect.
So, think about it: What's the worst that would happen if you had cassava, mukimo or fish instead of the ceremonial cake? That famous cake that has become synonymous with weddings and other ceremonies in modern times, according to theology lecturer Joseph Ettori, has nothing to do with Christianity. "The cake, as we know it today, is British in origin but has been widely adopted as the ceremonial special meal due to the indelible influence of the British," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Mumuni: We'll Uproot NPP
Headline: Ghana: Mumuni: We'll Uproot NPP
Body_Text:
Takoradi —  ...Like Cassava
Alhaji Mohammed Mumuni, the crutches bearing running mate of Prof. Atta
Mills has told the zongo communities in the Sekondi-Takoradi metropolis that
with their help, the ruling National Patriotic Party (NPP) government would
be uprooted like cassava after the December 7th election to enable the
National Democratic Congress (NDC) come back to implement its human-centered
policies to save Ghanaians from their current hardship.
He said even though the NPP had asked Ghanaians in their 2000
electioneering campaign to study their life style before casting their
votes, they had now worsened the plight of the very people they asked to
study their life style through increases in utilities and consumable
goods. The worst of it all was the unreasonable increase in school fees,
which parents were struggling to pay.
Alhaji Mumuni who was on a campaign tour of the moslem communities in
the western region said the NPP was pursuing the policy of property-owning
democracy, which usually favored the rich in society without thinking about
the downtrodden as the NDC government, had been doing.
He said when they were in government, they had been approached several
times to embrace the HIPC initiative but they rejected it because of the
conditions attached to it, but the NPP had accepted it with both arms and
the result was the current hardships, though the government would still not
accept that HIPC was a failure.
He noted that the astronomical increase in the prices of petroleum
products, high electricity and water tariffs and the general high cost of
living in the country could be attributed to the HIPC conditions but because
the NPP government did not care about the poor in society, they were not
worried about these increases.
Mumuni said what the NPP was rather trumpeting upon was their so-called
success in the management of the economy at the macro level by bringing down
inflation and also reducing interest rate. He said some time past, the NDC
reduced inflation to single digit but they did not make the kind of noise
that the NPP was making even though they were yet to achieve this feat-
single digit inflation.
The NDC running mate further said if the achievements the NPP was
trumpeting were true, there would not have existed the kind of abject
poverty, unemployment and collapse of companies that Ghanaians were
experiencing at the moment.
He assured the moslem community and Ghanaians in general that when given
the chance to rule the country once again, the NDC, as a human- centered
party, would fight poverty with sharp focus and also reach out to the
vulnerable in the society to save them from their present suffering.
The promises the NPP made about positive change had come to nothing he
said, adding that politics was about the welfare of the people, not
suffering. "You compare the cost of living in 2000 and what is pertaining
today- everything is out of gear because we have a government that pursues
property-owning democracy that only addresses the top bracket in the
society," he said.
Mumuni who a pulled large crowds in the moslem communities that he
visited, especially Effia Kuma zongo in Takoradi, appealed to the people not
to make the mistake and vote for NPP again since that would spell their
doom. He thanked the Moslems also for their prayers especially in the recent
accident that he had and thanked God that he was still alive, contrary to
what others had been expecting.
A former western regional minister, Mrs. Esther Lily Nkansah who was
also the NDC parliamentary candidate for Takoradi told the people that the
NPP government was using money to buy the conscience of the Ghanaian
woman. She noted that if indeed the government was desirous of helping women
in the society, they would not have waited till a few days to the election
to start distributing money under the guise of boosting their trading
activities. "We as women should note that this government is deceiving us,
so let us vote them out from power," she urged.

Original_Search_Title: A Woman At Work
Headline: Uganda: A Woman At Work
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kora Award nominee Halima Namakula is not just a musician, she is behind the Women At Work International NGO, which is helping to educate young women about the dangers of HIV/Aids
She came into the limelight as a star songstress after her 1999 release of the single Ekimbeewo, which was her nursery school rhyme.
Artiste Halima Namakula, also proprietor of No End Entertainment, one of the biggest local music companies, was born and grew up in Mulago (ku bbiri) with her parents, Hajji Ali Mukiibi and Ms Hafua Namuddu.
She is her mother's only daughter among seven children.
"It is not that my father has only seven children; you know Moslems marry many wives. You may not believe it but Hajji has a 10-year-old daughter, Mariam Namakula. Maybe he might even add more," Halima says smiling.
Halima went to Kawoya Primary School in Kaleerwe and Mandal Primary School, now Bat Valley. She recalls walking from Mulago ku bbiri to Bat Valley with her friends; Nalukenge and Nalule who were more of sisters to her. She nicknamed them the Nalus.
Namakula recalls how they would buy cassava at Wandegeya from a man she only remembers as Simon.
Being the only daughter, Halima was obviously her mother's jewel.
Halima with her late husband in 1977. Below is Halima at four months (Courtesy photos).
"Any minor wrong that my elder brothers did, I would report them to mummy and they would be punished. But they used to 'bribe' me by adding more meat onto my plate if they wanted me to drop the accusations," she says.
Halima was however a stubborn child and was spanked by her mother on daily basis. "Mummy chased me more than once everyday. I remember one day when she chased me from Kaleerwe to Wandegeya where the basketball pitch is situated now; everyone on the way was just yelling at us: kati baabo nga bulijjo bayitawo (there they go as usual)."
Halima went to City High School in Kololo for her secondary education, where she also acted part time with Kampala Nigel Angels Dramactors.
It was in Nigel Angels that she met her late husband, Sam Semaala Kiwanuka. Halima got her eldest son, Hemdee Kiwanuka and second child, Rashid while in secondary school.
"When Semaala got a scholarship to the US, I joined him in Oklahoma and my life completely changed," Halima says. Semaala took her back to school and she pursued diplomas in Beauty and Modeling from Casablanca Modeling School.
Halima and Semaala got two other children; Rachael and Christopher. "They were given Christian names because their father said Muslim names are not coveted in the US. He however gave them an option of practicing any religion of their choice. Now, they all practice Islam," Halima says.
Semaala returned to Uganda in 1994 but unfortunately died, that same year. Halima by then had moved from Oklahoma to
California and was working as a school bus driver.
After the release of Ekimbeewo in 1999, it became a hit and it was then that Halima decided to take music more seriously. She has so far released four albums: Ekimbeewo, Tonkutula, Kyama kyange and Sambagala.
Halima also starred in The Ebonies' TV soap, That's life Mwattu as Michelle. While in the US, she worked as a production assistant at Studio 28, a video shooting company, where she met several popular artistes like Snoop Dogg, Nelly and Lil' Zane among others.
With her just founded NGO, the Women At Work (WAWI) International, Halima is now helping to educate young women about the dangers of HIV/Aids.
In four years, Halima says WAWI will accomplish four projects. The NGO also consists of songstresses like Annet Nandujja of The Planets, Rachael Senkebejje, Sophie Gombya and Remy Male among others.
Popular Security International (PSI) the suppliers of Protector condoms and Pill-plan are supporting the projects. The four projects include, Commercial sex work (CSW), Help HIV orphans, Help immature mothers and Counseling of HIV positive widows.
Halima says all the four projects will be done countrywide but they have started with the CWS.
They started with Makerere Kivulu and Katanga slams recently. "We are first counselling young prostitutes operating in Kampala slums before heading for Masaka and Mbiiko-Jinja," says Halima.
She adds they have a goal of reducing the number of sex workers and educating them about the dangers of unprotected sex.
"We want these girls to ignore the big money and consider their lives," Halima said. After the CSW project, WAWI plans to move around the country, helping HIV positive orphans by providing them with kits and food.
They will also go to the war ravaged Northern and Eastern regions to help young mothers. Halima says many young mothers in these regions have at one time been victims of rape and therefore need strong counselling .
They will be supplied with free Pill-Plan tablets and protector condoms. Halima's group will wrap up the project with HIV positive widows by encouraging them to use condoms to reduce the spread of the pandemic.

Original_Search_Title: Nakonde Girl, 14 Forced Into Marriage
Headline: Zambia: Nakonde Girl, 14 Forced Into Marriage
Body_Text:
Lusaka — A 14-year-old school girl of Nakonde spent three days hiding in the bush
after running away from a forced marriage that was due to be blessed at a
local church in Ntatumbila area.
However, the girl was caught and has since been forced into
marriage.
The grade five girl of Ntatumbila Basic School in Ilindi village ran
away from the church just after being called to stand up so that the
congregation could know her and her husband-to-be.
The father of the girl arranged for the marriage after receiving K20,000
cash, two hoes and some beads.
He also asked his prospective son-in-law to deliver five heads of cattle
at a later time after the planned wedding.
The girl was beaten by her father for allegedly embarrassing him in the
presence of the congregation when she disappeared into a nearby bush.
She was found in a cassava field after a serious search and immediately
married off unceremoniously.
And National Single Parents Association of Zambia (NSPAZ) director John
Mulalika has condemned the move to marry off a girl who is supposed to be in
school.
Mulalika said his association which was charged with the responsibility
of protecting children's rights would ensure that both the father of the
girl and her husband faced the wrath of the law.
He called on the police's victim support unit to urgently move in and
arrest all the culprits.
Mulalika said there was need to promote the education of girl-children
adding that those found frustrating the efforts should be punished heavily
once found wanting.
And when this reporter made efforts to visit the girl, he was nearly
stoned.

Original_Search_Title: KCC Hikes Market Dues
Headline: Uganda: KCC Hikes Market Dues
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kampala City Council (KCC) has increased dues in all the 27 markets in the district's five divisions, a statement from the city authority has said.
Among the hiked charges are those of second-hand clothes (mivumba) and produce which have increased from sh9,000 to sh13,500 per unit every month in St. Balikudembe and Nakivubo markets.
In Nakasero Market, shop dues have risen from sh75,000 to sh100,000, eating houses will pay between sh40,000 and sh60,000, while butcheries are paying sh100,000 from sh50,000 per month.
Bugolobi Market vendors pay sh9,100 from sh7,000 for vegetables, eating stalls, fish, bananas, cassava, potatoes, cereals and old clothes, while shops are paying between sh13,000 and sh15,000 from sh10,000.
Most markets in Nakawa Division are paying rates ranging between sh6,000 and sh13,500, while Kawempe vendors are charged between sh6,000 and sh40,000.
"We revised them because our charges are lower compared to surrounding districts and municipalities. Inflation also led to the revision," Kampala town clerk, James Sseggane, said recently.
The rates have also been hiked because the cost of delivering services has risen due to increasing prices of inputs like fuel and electricity.

Original_Search_Title: The Artist Who Fights With Paintbrush On Canvas
Headline: Angola: The Artist Who Fights With Paintbrush On Canvas
Body_Text:
Windhoek — "ETONA" in Portuguese Kikongo dialect means a birthmark, symbol or a mark of evidence. As if to live up to his nickname, a 43-year-old Angolan artist has definitely left his mark not only on the African continent, but the world at large.
Talented as a universal artist and sculptor, Antonio Tomas Ana, better known as 'Etona' arrived in Namibia last Sunday to host his second exhibition of artworks at the Angolan Embassy in Windhoek. Angolan Ambassador to Namibia Alexandre Duarte Rodrigues will officially open the Exhibition, which starts tonight at 19:00. The exhibition will be hosted up until the 12th of this month.
Etona is no stranger to Namibia, since he has been here in 1996 for his first exhibition. Now he's back to display more colourful oil paintings on canvas, depicting realistic and abstract lifestyles of the African culture and lifestyle.
Speaking Portuguese with a modest tone in his voice, Etona says art brings him peace and joy to promote African art to the rest of the world.
"While some people fight with guns, I fight with the paintbrush on canvas to show the real picture of Africa," says Etona with a smile.
Southern Africa is known for its rich cultural and traditional history and prominent artists like Etona have wasted no time in making this heritage known to the world. Etona therefore wants to see SADC artists develop a culture for art and he is therefore here to familiarise himself through sharing ideas with local Namibian artists.
"Ever since my painting career started in 1975, I have been a strong defender of black African art, because people need to realise the importance of art in the development of Africa. It helps communities to interpret their world and act in a meaningful fashion," he explains.
His flourishing paintwork has also earned him a reputation worldwide, having sold and exhibited his paintings in countries like Portugal, Cuba, Germany, Spain, Brazil, Japan, France, Uganda, Zimbabwe and South Africa, to mention but a few.
With the use of mostly earthly colours, Etona has moved away from the mundane way of painting symmetrically.
"Each colour sets the mood and feelings found within a painting," he says pointing to the oil painting of an African woman pounding cassava or mahangu.
"The reds, browns, yellows and purple colours symbolise African states, while the white line is a connecting thread between the different countries," he explains, putting the artwork back on the table. With so much philosophical symbolism in the paintings the exhibition is therefore titled "From the Discolour form to the Colour Informal." Other images that will be shown at tonight's exhibition portray a flower that ceases to be beautiful, a mango that stops from becoming a fruit, a woman expiring with her maturity and a man who cannot assume his male spirit.
With a strong African mentality, Etona sees former South African President Nelson Mandela and current Angolan President Jose Eduardo Dos Santos as his role models.
As a father of eight children, Etona was born on June 22, 1961 in Soyo, north of Angola. Even though a strong family man, he has spent most of his time travelling the world to showcase his artwork and to teach at the same time.
The Angolan artist and sculptor is also one of the candidates selected for the Mundi Arts Award in Wales in 2006. He has also done a proposal to set up an African Art Centre in Angola's capital Luanda. The Angolan artist is also a representative of the Africa Museum and Las Americas House in Havanna, Cuba.
As a global African artist, Etona's next exhibition after Namibia will be held in Brazil and the Soviet Union. "I will always remain a citizen of the world and a universal artist, but will never lose my African roots," he concludes on a positive note.

Original_Search_Title: Malaria Treatment : Chloroquine Ruled Out
Headline: Africa: Malaria Treatment : Chloroquine Ruled Out
Body_Text:
Reports show that the poor spend about half of their income on malaria treatment.
Statistics from the Africa Malaria Report office show that chloroquine is among the leading drugs still widely prescribed for malaria patients by some pharmacists and drug vendors in the country. The drug, which can no longer destroy the malaria parasite in some areas of the tropics, was ordered out of the market in 2002 by the Minister of Public Health, Urbain Olanguena Awono.
According the Africa Malaria Report, children under five years and pregnant women are most exposed to the malaria parasite. Consequently, malaria morbidity and mortality rates are relatively high within this group. The prevalence rate is said to be quite high among children from poor families, with chronic treatment failures. The children are either infected during pregnancy, or develop acute febrile illness and chronic repeated infections after birth. Studies rate Fontem Sub Division (in Lebialem Division) and Limbe Sub Division (in Fako Division) as "highly affected areas."
Malaria is said to have imposed a heavy financial burden on many families in the country. Statistics show that the poor allocate over 34 per cent of their income on malaria treatment alone, while the well-to-do spend less than two per cent. Reports also show that about 44 per cent of low income earners take malaria treatment at home. Studies carried out by Bosco and Ringwald in 1998 show that chloroquine registered 51 per cent clinical failures among children of less than five years inYaounde.
Drug resistance is a persistent problem facing malaria patients. Drugs are widely hawked on the streets and market places by individuals who lack any basic medical training. The situation is worse in the countryside. Vendors who prescribe drugs hardly take into consideration the gravity of infection or age of the patient and their past medical records.
That apart, the quality of most drugs sold is appalling. Many patients complain that pirates now locally fabricate fake drugs like paracetamol, chloroquine, etc from cassava. This has often resulted to serious side effects.
Researchers say the prevalence rate of malaria in Africa South of the Sahara, has increased steadily because of three main reasons: drug resistance, frequent exposure of non-immuned population, emergence of HIV/AIDS and dormant malaria control programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Power in Kampala Resides On Hills
Headline: Uganda: Power in Kampala Resides On Hills
Body_Text:
Kampala — The fight for Kampala City by Buganda and the Central Government has reminded me of historical accounts of bitter exchanges between the then Kabaka of Buganda, Mutesa and the then Prime Minister of Uganda, Dr Milton Obote, way back in 1960s. I was not yet born but all biased academic accounts abound to feed oneself on.
Whether the argument is for Federo or Kampala or both, the whole matter remains interesting. Clearly on top of Kampala's hills, men are slowly but surely repeating history, but this time with some sort of modification because Mengo knows that it has to tow its lines carefully. After all it owes the little it has to the man who will not let go of Buganda's other demands until such a time that it is of political consequence.
The other interesting thing is that Nakasero feels it has so much to shout about, after all, Mengo is what it is today because of the latter. The Kingdom spent lots of time musing with empty promises and bubbles of a gift to take home. Worse still, the entire Cabinet is half full of Mengo's men who are not willing to relent to their King's wish.
By nature, that is the politics of Kabaka of Buganda whether it is Muwenda Mutebi and his friend, President Yoweri Kaguta Museveni, or some other pair. My prayer is that Mutebi should not dare advise Museveni to leave Buganda with his cabinet. That may turn out to be much sour than what transpired in Mengo in the 60s, considering who the man they would be playing with is.
I lived most of my youthful life in Buganda as an internally displaced person. Kampala was my home for well over 15 years; Kayunga was my other home because I took four years of boarding school life at St Kalemba Senior Secondary school, Nazigo. So to me, Kampala and Bugerere are much my homes as Pader is.
In my well over 15 years in Kampala, I saw the city grow, from the stockpile of garbage at Lugogo highway where on average, three human bodies were found every morning by early nineties, to the ultra modern highway it is today. I watched the hills of Ntinda, Mbuya, Namuwongo, Muyenga and so forth grow up with beautiful glistening self-contained houses and bungalows. I watched Bugolobi hill develop and thefFlats deteriorate. I also watched Nakawa and Naguru quarters sink into a tribulation of low-income earners where people from all lifestyles found compensatory sanctuary.
The one thing I found interesting about Kampala is that while the city grew, or developed, the developmental pattern took off in a rather bizarre approach. The city started sudden and rapid expansion at around the era of pathetic corruption, with the wealthy getting homes built around the hills and the poorer city dwellers finding themselves being pushed and shoved more down at the foots of the hills.
The Western Ntinda hills for instance which had been our play fields as kids growing up around that bushy area, was first opened up by a grand radio Sanyu complex in mid nineties, which then required an access road; then myriads of buildings came pouring all over the hills from down going up. The only noticeable human settlement then was the white flats for the Public Service Ministry way back in late eighties and early nineties. Today, that is a different story.
As one comes from Ntinda Road towards the North eastern part of the hill, just behind the Bukoto brown flats, one finds a very mysterious self-sustaining village. Complete with all the remoteness as though a section of Butambala was cut and pasted in this place. It is amazing. There was this gossip around town that some politicians visited witchdoctors there for more political powers, that is why at one time a Minister was involved in a bar brawl in that area.
Naguru Hill has its own story. From a rag tag slum, there came a rare sprout of some western styled architecture with grand appeal. However, the growth here was stunted because, by nature, Naguru seems to be the rightful place for those who were expelled by developments from Ntinda and Kololo. As such, its terrain made it a natural slum stretch. The composition of Naguru valley and its way of life could be a great sampling point for the World Bank and IMF researchers who over praise Uganda's economic strides.
Namuwongo too, in the late eighties was a disaster. There were characteristic pit latrines which were mounted into a high rise complex, landlords were fed up with ever filling pit latrines due to population overload so they quickly started building flats for a toilet with a reservoir that could spill at any moment on anyone. The houses were pathetic and a true human disaster.
With the new development plans, Namuwongo took a serious path to a grand settlement area with beautiful private houses and properties. However, this development was quick to dislodge its poor into the Nakawa housing estates and Kataza zone and places around the southern end of Kibuli hill.
The Eastern part of Mbuya was as bushy as any other tropical rain forest by 1990s. The children here fed on jackfruit (ffene), wild guavas, paw paws, mangos and jjambula, cassava and beans for their meals. One would easily find traditional Gganda enclaves where one could experience the real Kiganda culture of a homestead. But now, everything is gone to the wild investors. The only thing is that, while the property developers have established themselves everywhere, the top of Mbuya hill was long taken over by the Military. It was the main Military Hospital in Kampala city.
Mbuya too, offloaded its poor ones down the foothills; just by Coca-Cola/ Pepsi-Cola processing factories, and some trekked to Kitintale for cheaper accommodation. A good number of the inhabitants rolled down Nakawa and Kataza zones and Bbanda too.
Bbanda hill has a tale too. The top of the hill is an official residence to the King of Buganda, so the poorer subjects are littered around about Banda trading centre. This slum that is highly populated results from similar developments from Kyambogo hills and the Eastern Ntinda slopes. It also came because of intended trade with the Kyambogo Institutes, now University, just as Wandegeya is a university town/slum.
Mulago has lots to do with Mulago slums and Kamwokya, which it shares with Kiwatule hills as a send off point for the poorer city dwellers who have no access to huge funds for buying off plots and putting up some grand shelter for themselves.
Every November I stroll around the tops of the hills in Eastern Kampala taking pictures of the hills and how they are growing up just as a loving father would monitor the growth of his beloved child. I find the art of photography and my own appreciation of nature such an overwhelming artesian phenomenon. My only disappointment is that, while I live away from the dictatorship in Uganda, all my collections have been laid to waste, probably never will they ever make it to a gallery.
In my analogy, all these slums, apart from being a send off point for those who have been swept away by economic gravity from the hill sides, many of them are actually sustained by low income activities such as supplying foods and drinks to schools, hospitals, military, police or prisons establishments, selling local brew and in some cases, flesh peddling.
Every hill developed in Kampala City has been responsible for nourishing or creating a slum area because by nature, those who own properties are not miscible with those who only have a miserable life to look after.
That is why, in my final analysis, two big bulls, all dwelling on top of two of Kampala's most political hills: Mengo and Nakasero, have been making noise about the rightful ownership of this city where their echo have reverberated down the hills and are being felt by those who are at the base(slums) of these two hills.
In Kampala, key decisions are made from hill tops where the most powerful are, so everyone struggles to get higher. Kampala is a place for strugglers.

Original_Search_Title: NEEDS Will Create Over 7m Jobs, Says Perm. Sec
Headline: Nigeria: NEEDS Will Create Over 7m Jobs, Says Perm. Sec
Body_Text:
Abuja — The National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy, (NEEDS) will
generate over seven million jobs when its full implementation starts from
next year to 2007, the Permanent Secretary of the National Planning
Commission (NPC), Dr. Babangida Mu,azu Aliyu, has said
Speaking in Abuja, he said that government has made useful and positive
contacts in several countries of the world that would yield to the the
numerous industrial products that will be derived from the expanded cassava
scheme. He said that Nigeria, can actually generate billion of dollars from
it with millions hands engaged, annually.
According to him, under NEEDS, the Information and Communication
Technology sector would be a major area that would prosper hundreds of
thousands of skilled hands before 2007, when the NEEDS package is
unfolded.
He explained that though the implementation of NEEDS has started through
the various reforms and restructuring going on in the public service, it is
yet to be felt among the populace, because government was busy putting in
place the desired infrastructure that NEEDS can build on from next
year.
He noted that the present reforms in the public service, which forms
part of NEEDS, may not be pleasant to workers, but insisted it was necessary
if Nigeria would stop using the bulk of its annual budget to finance the
running of bureaucracy, to the detriment of developmental programmes.
He affirmed his belief in the NEEDS document, positing that it has all
that past economic plans lacked, making open for broad participation, with a
formidable implementation machinery put in place to ensure compliance among
all the tiers of government.
He lamented that though, the National Planning Commission was enacted by
law to make all the economic blue prints, and human resources planning for
the country, the NPC has been so muddled in the past with wrong and
incompetent hands and that, as one of the engine room of NEEDS, the NPC has
been a given a nod by the Presidency to start the right sizing of staff and
plan for the merger of some of its key parastatals next year.
According to him, the present ten directorates of the NPC would be
merged into six, with qualified directors to man them.
He said that only professionals would be engaged in the new NPC, "After the Head of Service has substituted the irrelevant workforce we
have now from his pool,", said Dr. Babangida, "the NPC would use its power
to advertise, examine and engage more qualified hands to complement what we
have", he said. He noted that most of the times policy makers are not in
touch with the masses, making it hard for them to get a first hand
information on the realistic demands of the people, thus causing a
communication gap that brings about opposition to certain policies.
"I would encourage us to always have meetings and engagements with civil
society groups, students unions, trade unions and mix with ordinary people
to get their feelings before we make policies for them", he advised his
peers in government.

Original_Search_Title: FG Appoints Cobalt As New Pre-Shipment Inspection Agent
Headline: Nigeria: FG Appoints Cobalt As New Pre-Shipment Inspection Agent
Body_Text:
The federal government has appointed Cobalt International Services Ltd
as the nation's Pre-Shipment Agent (PSA) of Non-Oil exports from
Nigeria.
The Minister of State for Finance, Mrs Nenadi Usman, who disclosed this
at a press briefing in Abuja, recently said that a two-year renewable
contract has been entered into between the company and the federal
government. The contract takes effect from October 1, 2004.
Mrs Usman also announced a new list of Export Guidelines which would
serve as a work tool for the PSA said to be Nigerian/British owned in order
to ensure repatriation of export proceeds into the country.
Mrs Usman said that the decision to appoint a PSA for the nation"s
non-oil exports was in line with the economic reform process aimed at
diversifying Nigeria's export base and increasing foreign exchange
inflow.
"The progamme also has the capacity to improve the volume, quality and
value of goods exported from Nigeria to other parts of the world and to
ensure that they conform to the recommendations of the \international
standards Organisation (ISO). Furthermore, non-oil exports, if encouraged,
will no doubt promote job creation.
"Government envisages that, well over $1 billion could be earned through
non-oil exports (excluding the cassava project) within the first year of the
programme with expected increases in revenue generation in subsequent
years," Mrs Nenadi said.
According to the minister, the PSA would ensure high quality and
quantity of exports from Nigeria, by issuing a Clean Certificate of
Inspection (CCI), while making sure that exporters paid the necessary fees
to the Central Bank of Nigeria.
"Upon opening of the file, the Inspection Agents shall commence the
quality, quantity and price evaluation.
"The Inspection agents shall ensure the quality, quantity and the true
value of the goods to be exported to the consignee and shall, if satisfied,
issue a Clean Certificate of Inspection (CCI) in respect of such goods. The
CCI shall comprise 8 copies.
"No loading into any export vessel or shipping container shall be
authorized without the issuance of a CCI by Inspection agents. As soon as
loading is completed, the exporter shall hand over their Inspection Agents a
copy of Bill of Lading/Airway Bill and copy of commercial (final) invoice",
the guidelines stipulate.
On the repatriation of exports proceeds, the federal government directed
that each exporter must open a domiciliary account with a Nigerian bank
which would issue the exporter with Nigerian Export Proceeds (NXP)
Form. Export proceeds are to be credited to that account.
Mrs Nenadi said that it remained the responsibility of the Inspection
Agents to ensure the repatriation of the proceeds from all exports. However,
exporters have the privilege of expending not more than 10 per cent of the
proceeds.
The agents are to work in collaboration with the Nigeria Customs
Service, Officials of the Ministry of Finance, Ministry of Commerce, Central
bank of Nigeria, Federal office of Statistics, and the Office of the
Secretary to the Government of the Federation, to which monthly statistical
returns must be submitted.
Sensitization workshops have been planned for Nigeria"s major commercial
cities of Kano, Aba and Lagos on September 30, October 5, and 7,
respectively, enlighten exporters on the new guilines.

Original_Search_Title: Elders Fight Over Body
Headline: Uganda: Elders Fight Over Body
Body_Text:
Mukono — Residents of Kirinnyabigo village, Kimenyedde sub- county were treated to free drama when friends of a dead man stopped Bagisu cultural leaders from circumcising the body.
Patrick Luwumu, of Lweza, Mukono Town Council died in a road accident.
However, burial was interrupted by Bagisu elders who insisted on circumcising the body in accordance to their culture.
Colleagues to the deceased denied the elders access to the body and threatened to beat them up if they insisted.
The chaos stopped when the elders, led by the deceased's father, Fred Wamboga withdrew and burial went on.
Mourners were again puzzled when a Moslem Sheik, gave the final word at the burial of the christian.
The deceased, who has been a cassava trader in Kame Market, and said to be a crude waragi addict, was buried with cassava and waragi.
The residents accused the deceased's relatives of ignoring him until his death. Every adult male is supposed to be circumcised in Gishu culture.

Original_Search_Title: Report Faults Government On Children
Headline: Uganda: Report Faults Government On Children
Body_Text:
Kampala — "The armed conflict in northern Uganda has been overlooked and misunderstood for the past 18 years. It is a tragic struggle for power involving children, who are used as pawns for military and political purposes. They are abused; they are manipulated; and by most they are pitied and ignored."
This is how a new 66-page book authored by World Vision Uganda introduces the plight of children in conflict-torn north. The book, Pawns of Politics: Children and Conflict in Northern Uganda will be launched on Monday, September 27 at International Conference Centre.
"The nature and duration of the conflict has created tremendous humanitarian, social and economic costs for all Uganda, particularly for children.
The protection of children has not been a priority for governments, despite national and international laws guaranteeing their basic rights," reads the book in part.
It is argued in the book that as a result, a situation of mass abduction has occurred. Children are forcefully recruited into rebel ranks.
An excerpt from a former abductee tells it all: "When I was abducted, I was given fresh cassava and beans to carry.
I got very tired. As a punishment for moving to slowly my leg was pierced with a bayonet by another abducted child who had been taught to be a soldier."
Another former abductee, now a child mother says "I was 13 when I was abducted 10 years ago. I tried to escape several times in vain. The man I was given severely beat me when he suspected I helped others to escape."
The book states that despite several UN resolutions to protect, little has been done to translate the provisions into concrete actions in north.
The report urges specific actions by the UN including appointing a special peace Envoy for the war-torn region.
At the Ugandan level, the authors argue that the children in the north have a right to protection under the Children's Statute 1997, the Constitution, the African Charter for the Rights and Welfare of Children.
UPDF and World Vision recommend that security of civilians in north should be primary in quest for stability.
"There have been serious questions detentions and recruitment of children into the Ugandan army. The inclusion of civilians, whose only mandate is to pursue the interests of children in the army would increase public confidence of UPDF."
The authors argue that spiritual weapons are used to psychologically enslave both abducted children and the targeted population with fear.
The book, whose aim is to act as an advocacy tool for the restoration of peace in northern Uganda puts the economic costs of the war at a conservative figure of $1.3b and costing more than $100m per year. This is more than the country's national budget for health.
The book notes that the war will not end with the end of Joseph Kony. The conflict will end only when Kony surrenders, is captured or agrees to some form of settlement; when hidden LRA weapons caches are found; when LRA commanders are resettled and when people in IDPS are able to safely leave camps.
Several people have called for a national reconciliation. "This will improve relations between the Acholi people in the north, people groups in other parts of the country and the national governing structures. Not only is some form of national reconciliation essential, it will be important to ensure youth involvement in the process," the book argues.
"One of the observations of youth who have escaped is the difficulty of being considered in their innocence after their life of combat. Based on experiences in other places such as Cambodia and Sierra Leone, the active participation of youth is essential to successful reconciliation."

Original_Search_Title: Countdown to the December 2004 Presidential Election
Headline: Ghana: Countdown to the December 2004 Presidential Election
Body_Text:
Will it be asomdweehene or abotarehene?
Whether we like it or not, the political environment in the run-up to
the forthcoming elections will suffer from severe atmospheric thunderstorms
and pollution caused by accidental trajectory diversions. The liberal
Nigerian press calls the verbal missiles "accidental discharges" from
trigger-happy trouble shooters.
There will be decent, good, and also foul, vulgar language. Reassuring,
and sometimes provocative bombast.
Some acolytes and political neophytes will recoil into their shells like
the proverbial folder oyster when the squelching weather attains the highest
point of heretical blasphemies.
That is when the last resort to political double-talk or the overweening
syndrome of 'polytricks' will unmask the genuine from the fake, and the
charlatans from the sheep in wolf skin. The chaff will be separated from the
wheat.
For, when the day of reckoning comes in December 2004, many acclaimed
saints will suddenly turn into satanic desperados trembling with acrimony
like wild leaves possessed by the devastating Florida hurricanes.
Verily, verily, I say into you, that the hapless Ghanaian hard working
journalists will become the whipping boys of the "aggrieved politicians"
licking their wounds but always in search of the proverbial fall-guy
scapegoats for their intellectual pomposity and political shenanigans.
The crescendo of carefully crafted press rejoinders will inundate the
sacred homes of the printed word or electronic media from where words are
given momentous wind to fire out dangerous ballistic missiles at quixotic
adversaries and avowed antagonists.
Good jobs, there will be many, and galore for the outstanding wordsmiths
who retired long ago from the topsy-turvy but noble inky journalistic
profession.
They will turn their otherwise stale stock in trade into adroit
spin-doctors firing salvoes to mend the broken fences for their rejected pay
masters like mercenaries and Ninja fighters recruited from the Great Lakes
into the sun-drenched dry land of the west tropical forest.
Son of man, beware, it is not going to be easy when vaulting ambition
comes round to the sudden realisation that the sooth-sayer was merely
looking at the wrong side of the star-gazer's interpretation of the
political crystal ball which says ahh, the only hunter capable of shooting
down the rampaging elephant must out of necessity, come from the sacred
forest of Antoa Nyama instead of Gbagbladza in the sacred shrines of
Ekpelekpedzi.
Overgrown beards will be set on inferno in uncontrollable fury of
disappointment.
The political computations will produce grit, and gristle. And that will
lead to the bashing of heads and the gnashing of premolars and elephantine
incisors cola stained by the last few hard years of chewing the political
cud.
I pull no punches for Professor Evans Atta Mills or Lawyer John Agyekum
Kufuor.
Quiet in his political chemistry but methodical in approach, Professor
Atta Mills has soon earned the enviable seemingly unbeatable media accolade,
ASOMDWEEHEN, the peacemaker.
Blessed are the peace makers, for they shall inherit the Kingdom of
God.
That is a media spin quite hard to crack like the stony nut from Elubo
in Osagyefo's kube constituency.
President Lawyer Kufuor, soft in outlook, but firm and resolute in
character may need more that the incantations of a special Presidential
cassava magic to invoke the powers of the rainmakers to inflict a torrential
deluge inspired disaster on the leaking umbrella canopy.
Maybe, the spin-doctors could come up with another innovative accolade
befitting the calm composure of President Kufuor whose ability to tolerate
vitriolic bombshells and irritating provocative insults has become more than
legendary.
Will it be AHENEPA NKASA, or is it, as a colleague wordsmith put it
nonchalantly ABOTAREHENE? Patience, they say, overcomes all man-made
obstacles. It has tolerance, forgiveness as its divine breastplate.
Many floating voters, in their fleeting moments of indecision have
become romantic and enamoured with the magical attraction associated with
the political symbol ASOMDWEEHEN.
But if Ghana needs a political abracadabra for enduring peace during the
up coming democratic elections, which promise to be fiercely contested, then
a large section of the followers of the virtues of tolerance and forgiveness
will be roosting more for the newly baptised media accolade ABOTAREHENE,
for, patience they say, conquers all.
My dear brother, juxtapose the magic wand of ABOTAREHENE with the
breastplate of ASOMDWEEHEN, and politically speaking, the algebra
automatically equals peaceful, tolerant, democratic elections come December
2004.
And so the specific warning notice at the main entrance of the theatre
of action when the D-day comes should be unambiguous and clear: KEEP OUT ALL
TROUBLE SHOOTERS AND WAR-MONGERS.
WELCOME ABOTAREHENE, WELCOME ASOMDWEEHEN!!! Please forgive me if I have
left out the moustachioed Doctor alias Baby Saver, the Pedigree of
Domestication Daniel "Never Say Die" Lartey or the Security burglary proof
door mender, "Iron Man" Aggudey.
Every dog, they say, has got its beautiful day. So dear readers, let us
wait and see.
The baptism of fire and strange tongues is just around the corner. A
little to the right. And then just a little to the left.

Original_Search_Title: Sakeni Calls for Enforcement of Annual Fish Ban
Headline: Zambia: Sakeni Calls for Enforcement of Annual Fish Ban
Body_Text:
LUAPULA Province Minister, Kennedy Sakeni has called for the
introduction of good fishing methods and strict enforcement of the annual
ban to preserve fish stocks in Luapula.
In an interview from Mansa yesterday, Mr Sakeni said the catch in fish
stocks had dropped below average in most rivers due to over-fishing.
Mr Sakeni said the drop had affected income levels for fishermen in the
province who entirely depended on fishing for their livelihood, the most
affected areas are Luapula river and Lake Mweru.
He said fish stocks had reduced because some people were using nets
which caught even the smallest fish in the rivers.
Mr Sakeni said restocking alone would not help matters and that
authorities should ensure that the fish ban was enforced.
He said there was need for fishermen to observe the ban between December
and February to allow the fish to breed.
"The best thing that needs to be done is to introduce proper fishing
methods and also ensure that the fish ban is implemented and observed to
allow fish to breed," he said.
He said if left unchecked, the methods currently being used would wipe
out all the fish.
The minister said fishermen in the area were now facing financial
problems because even their cassava produce was not being bought by the Food
Reserve Agency (FRA).

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Falls By 0.6% - Nsemukila
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Falls By 0.6% - Nsemukila
Body_Text:
Lusaka — CENTRAL Statistical Office (CSO) director Dr. Buleti Nsemukila yesterday
announced a 0.6 per cent fall in inflation for this month despite pressure
from escalating fuel prices.
This means that inflation has fallen from 19.5 per cent lasted month to
18.9 per cent. As compared with last year, this is 1.4 percentage points
better than the 20.3 per cent recorded in August 2003.
And CSO acting deputy director for information and research Solomon
Tembo fears that unless international fuel prices revert to reasonable
levels, the impact on Zambia would worsen conditions.
Dr. Nsemukila attributed the drop to falls in inflation levels for food,
beverages and tobacco, which has declined to 9.8 per cent from 10.4 percent
in last month.
"Of the total 18.9 per cent annual inflation for August 2004, increases
in food prices accounted for 9.8 percentage points while non-food items
accounted for 9.1 percentage points," he said.
"Annual food inflation was recorded at 18.7 per cent, declining by 1.2
percentage points on the July rate of 19.9 per cent. From July to August
2004, the index for food increased by 0.9 per cent."
Dr. Nsemukila said the fall in annual food inflation was due to lower
prices for maize meal, fresh meat and vegetables, dried beans, fresh fish,
dressed chicken, sweet and Irish potatoes, millet and cassava meal.
The fall in these commodities' prices overrode price increases for
roller meal, maize-grain, dried Kapenta, other cereals and cereal products,
milk and milk products, fresh fruits, raw cassava tubers, eggs, cooking oil
and other processed food items.
"A comparison of prices between July and August 2004 shows that the
average price of a 25kg bag of breakfast meal dropped by 0.7 per cent from
K32,342 in July to K32,114 in August," Dr. Nsemukila said.
"The average price of a 25kg bag of roller meal almost remained
unchanged at K24,281 in August compared with K24,249 recorded in July. The
average price for 1kg of tomatoes decreased by 14.4 per cent, while the
price of 1kg of dressed chicken went down by 2.8 per cent. However, the
price of maize grain measured in a 20-litre tin increased by 8.1 per cent."
He added that non-food inflation increased by 0.2 per cent over July's
rate to post 19.2 percent in August, resulting in 1.1 percent increase in
the non-food index.
"Non-food inflation was mainly due to increases in the cost of fuel,
household energy (paraffin), house rent, furniture and household appliances,
leisure goods (video recorders), clothing and footwear," Dr. Nsemukila
said.
And in an interview, Tembo said while it was difficult to predict the
full impact of the current volatility in international fuel prices, it would
portend bad news for Zambia.
"We are an oil importing country and fuel is, as it were, the engine of
economic activity. We should just hope and pray that the situation is
reversed and supply is stable," he said.
"Otherwise, these speculative prices will hit us hard. In fact, the
government should do something about cushioning the effect of this
speculation on the local economy if we are to meet the set targets."
International fuel prices have continued to escalate in the recent past,
rising 33 per cent since June. This has raised concerns that it might force
a slowdown in the growth of the world economy.
To cushion its local market, the government of India last week reduced
duty on fuel imports.
For Zambia, President Mwanawasa last Sunday ruled out reduction of duty
on petroleum products but said the government was exploring ways of
cushioning the Zambian market.

Original_Search_Title: N25Billion Re-capitalisation May Collapse the Economy, Warns PENGASSAN
Headline: Nigeria: N25Billion Re-capitalisation May Collapse the Economy, Warns PENGASSAN
Body_Text:
wants National Assembly to pass law against abuse of expatriate
quota
PETROLEUM and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association (PENGASSAN) has
called on the Central Bank Governor, Professor Charles Soludo, to reduce the
minimum re-capitalisation of banks in the country to between N10
andN15billion within the first year warning that the consequence will be too
grave for the economy.
The association called on the National Assembly to pass a law against
the abuse of expatriate quota in the nation's oil and gas industry as its
abuses have been weakening capacity development and growth of the
sector.
According to him: "The reform currently going on in the banking sector
is a welcome one. I am not an economist and I am not pretending to be one
but I have taken time to follow contributions on this issue. Again, we
cannot agree less with those positing that the N25b recapitalization fund is
too huge a leap from the former N2b.
While one sincerely appreciates the concerns of Prof. Soludo, an
achiever of no mean status, there is nothing to state that a lower margin of
between N10b-N15b cannot suffice within the first year and achieve his other
target in another phase. Our grouse has always been the hasty manner with
which we jump into these reforms. The Prof. cannot say that he is not
bothered about the unemployment and inflation situations in the
country.
We sincerely hope that this is not a reform that will churn out workers
into the already saturated unemployment market. Our other consideration is
