that as a developing economy and proponent of poverty alleviation on one
hand, and empowering the people as a proposition of NEEDS on the other, one
is at a loss in reconciling how the small-scale cassava grater in the
village will get a loan to prosecute her business with this mega banking
system".
"We leave our economic gurus and bankers to continuously advise on this
issue, so that the same Nigerians the reform is set to protect will not be
worse of for it. However, the one that worries us so much is the withdrawal
of government funds from banks. In time past, a similar action was taken,
but was later discontinued.
This calls to mind the veracity of undoing previous policy actions taken
by others. During this period, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation
(NNPC) where we have a large number of our members suffered untold hardships
in places like Warri that has no Central Bank. There were delays in salary
payments and operations were hampered due to the bureaucratic
bottlenecks".
On abuse of expatriate quota, he noted: "If a bill on the above has not
been sent to the National Assembly the government must with the same speed
of sending bills do so now. The oil and gas industry is infested with
unqualified expatriates. Apart from not adding value to our operations, it
is an indirect way of reducing unemployment abroad and compounding our own
unemployment situation.
It does not only insult our collective intelligence, it portrays our
agencies of gross inability to protect our citizens from exploitation.
There are instances where expatriate young school leavers who are
equivalent of NYSC are made supervisors. The implications of this are
numerous. Apart from increasing joint venture overhead costs, it is an abuse
of expatriate quota process and it negates the technology transfer
objective. Cases abound where we have artisans on our rigs. The jobs they do
are not in tune with the definition of experts. Experts are specialized
people whose skills we cannot readily provide. When they come, the policy
advocates that two Nigerians must work with each expert as an
understudy. This is not the case in the oil companies".
"There is a typical case in an upstream company, where the first top
five officers in a department are expatriates. If these brazen acts of due
process continue unabated, then government should forget its local content
policy drive. The other implication is that of security. It is only in
Nigeria we see expatriates working as security and community liaison
officers in remote villages. These people stay in offices without making
contacts with the locals.
They use Nigerians to perform their functions while we pay for their
indolence. There is another case of some personnel, who came in as turbine
specialists, but working as Nurses on the rig of an oil company. Assuming
they are nurses, for them to practise in Nigeria, they must register with
the Nursing and Midwifery Council of Nigeria.
This is the rule all over the world. True experts are welcome because we
have a lot to learn from them, but let us not globalize to the extent of
allowing our shores to become a dumping ground for people, who ordinarily
cannot be employed in their home country to do what they currently do in
Nigeria".

Original_Search_Title: Army Kills 21 LRA Rebels
Headline: Uganda: Army Kills 21 LRA Rebels
Body_Text:
Kitgum — The army killed 21 rebels of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) during the
last two weeks in various battles in Kitgum and southern Sudan. Briefing
journalists in Kitgum town on Tuesday, the UPDF 5th Division spokesman
Lt. Chris Magezi said the operations involved both air raids and infantry
units. He said LRA commander identified only as Angola was attacked along
Alebi river, west of Parajok1 inside southern Sudan close to the Uganda
border.
Other rebel forces attacked in the same period were under the commands
of Brig. Lakati from Beyo-goya village, Anaka Parish in Palabek Gem
sub-county. Magezi said the army recovered 11 SMG rifles, three sacks of
used clothes, one camera belonging to Lakati, 30 bags of dry and fresh
cassava and nine sacks of sorghum.
Magezi said the army rescued 15 abducted children. In a related
development, Magezi said 17 LRA rebels surrendered with 13 SMG rifles on
Sunday in Kitgum district. He said security west of Kitgum has been beefed
up by a special Brigade at Palabek Kal, where there is an IDP camp. He
assured journalists that the LRA have been permanently dislodged from Nyono
Hills by a stationary unit there.
Meanwhile, Hudson Apunyo reports from Lira that a man who gave President
Yoweri Museveni a lift on his motorbike during presidential campaign in Lira
in 2001 has been given money to buy a new motorcycle. The Assistant Private
Secretary to the President in Charge of Political Affairs, Ms Judith Obina
Okumu handed over the money to Akaca John Bosco on Tuesday.
The RDC Ken Peter Lucap was present. Akaca gave the president a lift
from Uganda Technical College Lira to Akii Bua Stadium.
Okumu said the money, Shs1.8 million was given by the President in
appreciation. She described him as a very vigilant person. Okumu said the
money is to help him buy a new motorcycle to help generate income for his
household in line withthe President's plan to empower the youth.

Original_Search_Title: GM Foods Bill Ready, Says Officer
Headline: Kenya: GM Foods Bill Ready, Says Officer
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A bill to vet the introduction of Genetically Modified Organisms that
could harm the environment and human health has been developed.
The National Council for Science and Technology says among other things,
the Bill calls for the formation of a strong and competent authority to
promote awareness and on biotechnology and biosafety policies.
Speaking at a workshop in Nakuru, a legal officer at the council, Mrs
Rachel Shibarila said the draft bill has been forwarded to the Cabinet
awaiting approval before it is tabled in Parliament.
Shabarila made the remarks during a workshop organised by the council in
Nakuru for its members and regulatory agencies.
While opening the workshop, Education Assistant minister, Kilemi Mwiria
said the government would support the Bill. The minister said regulations
for GMOs trials have been applied in the trial of sweet potato, production
of rinderpest vaccine, cotton, maize and transgenic cassava.
Prof George King'oriah, the council executive officer said attempts have
been made to harmonise the biosafety regulations at regional levels.

Original_Search_Title: VP Mumba Wants to Go for HIV Test
Headline: Zambia: VP Mumba Wants to Go for HIV Test
Body_Text:
Chiengi — VICE-President Nevers Mumba has indicated that he wants to go for an
HIV/AIDS test.
And senior chief Puta of Chiengi district fumed at the police commanding
officer for ignoring him when he went to surrender guns.
During a stakeholder's meeting at Puta Boma on Sunday, Vice- President
Mumba said if people were not careful, the HIV/AIDS pandemic could continue
claiming their lives.
He said he had instructed his senior private secretary Clement Siame to
arrange for a day for his HIV test.
He said he wanted to do an HIV test because he wanted to encourage
others on the importance of taking the test.
Vice-President Mumba said he was speaking about HIV/AIDS with wounds
after losing about three close relatives, two uncles and a sister.
Vice-President Mumba advised people to keep themselves safe from the
disease.
"We need to get HIV tests so that we know our status so that if you are
found positive, you are put on antiretroviral (ARV) treatment to prolong
your life," Vice-President Mumba said. "If you are not positive then you are
free."
Vice-President Mumba said government policy was to introduce ARVs at
district level.
And Vice-President Mumba said lack of hardship allowance had made many
workers to leave rural areas for towns.
He promised the workers that government would provide incentives to
those willing to work in the rural areas.
And addressing a public rally at Chiengi Boma, Vice-President Mumba
advised newspapers against being used by selfish politicians as "boxing
rings".
He observed that selfish politicians who wanted to get at others were
using the papers into "boxing rings" to get at other.
He advised politicians against engaging in politics of hatred and
insults but rather to concentrate on politics of development.
Vice-President Mumba expressed concern at the poor state of
Nchelenge-Lunchinda road and directed that works on the road should
begin.
He told the crowd that feasibility studies on the Chembe Bridge and
Pedicle Road had been completed and awaiting Mitsubishi Corporation to start
the work.
And speaking when he paid a courtesy on him at his palace, the visibly
annoyed senior chief Puta said the police commanding officer who was later
discovered that he had retired ignored him when he went to Mansa to
surrender guns on behalf of his subjects.
"I went to police command and he never attended to me. I travelled a
long distance to Mansa using my own fuel not government but he told me that
he was going to Mansa where there was a riot. Is that respect?" senior chief
Puta asked. "I took the firearms which I got from my people."
Chief Puta insisted that the commanding officer should stand up so that
he answered the charges but he was told that the officer had since been
retired.
He complained that this discouraged people from working with the
government.
Chief Puta said he had not been paid for the 16 guns he surrendered on
behalf of his subjects.
He complained of lack of market for maize and cassava in his
chiefdom.
Chief Puta appealed to the government to upgrade the rural health centre
in the district to a hospital.

Original_Search_Title: Funding University Education Should Be a Cooperative Effort-Says High Commissioner
Headline: Ghana: Funding University Education Should Be a Cooperative Effort-Says High Commissioner
Body_Text:
Mr Isaac Osei, Ghana's High Commissioner to the United Kingdom, has said in London that funding of university education should be a cooperative effort of all stakeholders.
He dismissed claims that the government's subvention for the running of Ghanaian universities was dwindling and pointed out that universities all over the world were grappling with the rising cost.
A release emailed to the GNA from the Ghana High Commission, London said Mr Osei pointed out that resources channelled by the government into the tertiary sector had increased substantially to keep pace with the increasing cost of university education.
He said since there were not enough resources to cope with the increasing demands all stakeholders including the private sector, civil society, the Alumni, parents and students should assume greater responsibility in the running of the universities.
Mr Osei made the call at an International Congress of the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST) Alumni Association in London on the theme, "The role and place of the Alumni in enhancing higher education in Ghana".
He said the NPP government had embarked on the improvement and expansion of existing infrastructure of both polytechnics and universities in fulfilment of its constitutional mandate of making education accessible to all sections of the society.
"Under the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy, the government is supporting private sector and civil society involvement in tertiary education through fund-raising, curriculum development and access to loan schemes".
In keeping with its core goal as the premier Technological University in Ghana, Mr Osei said, "products of KNUST have offered Ghana a myriad of practical solutions, which have spawned new businesses in many sectors of the economy'.
Professor Kwesi Andam, Vice Chancellor of (KNUST) led an eleven-member delegation to the Congress. He announced that KNUST had been admitted into the Alliance for Global Sustainability, a think-tank, comprising five top universities of the world, which provided technical advice to the United Nations on issues pertaining to sustainable development.
He indicated that plans for the establishment of a Faculty of Aeronautic Engineering, the first in the Sub-Sahara Africa, in collaboration with leading universities in the USA had reached an advanced stage.
Prof Andam explained that KNUST had begun to show leadership through the publication of its research findings in the Technocrat magazine to complement efforts to lead Ghana to attain middle-income status.
Touching on some of the recent research projects by KNUST, Professor K. Adarkwa, Pro Vice Chancellor, announced that the University had come out with special hybrids of cassava and sweet potato popularly known as 'tech bankye' and 'tech santom', respectively.
The Pro Vice Chancellor gave an elaborate account of recent developments on KNUST campus and announced that a Human Resource Department and a quality assurance unit had been introduced to help improve service delivery of the University staff.
While in the UK, the Vice-Chancellor and his entourage also paid a working visit to the Ghana High Commission, London and the Cambridge University.

Original_Search_Title: A Tale of UPDF's Congolese Women
Headline: Uganda: A Tale of UPDF's Congolese Women
Body_Text:
Kampala — In 2001, as hundreds of Uganda Peoples Defence Forces trekked back home, after a controversial stay in the DR Congo, they were followed by hundreds of Congolese women, some with babies clinging on their backs, some pregnant.
Susan Beni, then 18 was among those women; she was with her husband, Peter Okwi (not real names).
Like many a Congolese young woman, Beni had hoped that she had found love and run away from the chaos in her country; that she would now settle down with her man in a more peaceful country, have children and pay occasional visits to her parents back home, in Congo.
The story, however, turned out to be different. Today, she lies ill and alone in a dump, wretched and dusty hut in Kasubi, a squalid suburb of Gulu town, just a few metres away from the 4th Division headquarters. Her husband, she said, dumped her and that he is now in Soroti "married" to two more women.
Beni has no money, no decent clothes and nobody to take care of her.
"My biggest problem is that I don't have any money, even my friends and relatives don't want to visit me, they say I am going to die, " she said desperately trying to hold back her tears. She couldn't; she broke down into sobbing.
Like many Congolese women, Beni now wants to go back home, but she sees no way and has no way. When they came to Uganda, most of the Congolese women headed north where most of their husbands had been taken.
Today, they are scattered in small patches all over northern Uganda, following their men, as they got transferred. Some are even now married to civilians, having been deserted by their soldier husbands.
They are now living deep inside the heartland of Acholi sub region, in places like Amuru, Pajimu, Alero and Pajule. They too, like the locals, survive on the food doled out by World Food Programme.
But here they are shunned; local artists are having a field day, composing hilarious songs about them and their "immorality."
" They keep telling us that UPDF went to work and not to marry women in Congo, that Acholi girls are not getting men because of us, when we are moving in the villages, children run after us shouting "Congole, Congole," says Anna Shaku, 29, originally from Gwade, which she says is deep inside Congo
"We are the real Zaireans, not these ones from the border like Kisangani," she said.
Anna Shaku has twins, Acen and Apio. Her husband Opio died two and a half years after they set foot in Uganda, from Congo "When we were coming with my husband I was pregnant with those twins that you see," she said, while pointing at two big bellied little girls.
"Soon they will need to start school, but there is no money, there is no where I can get it, my husband died in battle in Sudan two and a half years ago," she said with a voice full of emotion.
"I want to go back home and see my parents. Since I came, I have never heard from them, I don't know whether they are a live or dead," she adds. " I have no garden here, no work I am just suffering. In Gwade, I used to sell fish, just like the women in Gulu, that's were my UPDF husband got me, what am I doing here? Nothing," she answered herself emphatically.
When her husband died she went to Lira and buried him, she then stayed there for a year but left because she says her husband's family were "treating her well."
Now with many other Congolese women, they live in a tiny stuffy room in Koro, next to Koro military barracks. There are six adults, two heavy with pregnancies and four little kids including Shaku's twins.
"We shall die any time we fall sick because we don't have money to get proper medical treatment," says Ekuse Zhozeti Misherini, a plump, very light skinned woman aged 20 and a mother of two with the third in her belly.
"Bemba [rebel leader] ate our Kasurube (bride price), he signed our papers when we were coming, let him bring the big plane that brought us here to take us back home," said Marie Zhoze, 20.
At the time of their coming to Uganda, a large swath of eastern Congo was under the control of Jean Pierre Bemba's Congolese Liberation Front (MLC)
Like many other Congolese women, Marie Zhoze is not happy about the treatment the army gives them when they lose their husbands.
"They told us that when our husbands die, we would get their salaries and use it to go back home, we aren't getting that money, the big men are eating it all," says Zhoze.
About five kilometres away, on the other side of Gulu town, Mamisha who says she has only one name, lies wriggling in great pain at the 4th Division military hospital in Gulu barracks. She is very ill, has nobody to take care of her except her seven year old daughter Paalwa Lwize.
On the day I visited her, she was squatting under her hospital bed, trying painfully to wash her beddings. Her little daughter Paalwa struggled on with a can to fetch the water.
Dr John Lusiiba, 4th Divisions acting director of Medical Services, says that Congolese women normally get free medical treatment in the hospital as long as they prove that they are or were once wives of UPDF soldiers.
"In the division hospital, we give free medical treatment to soldiers, their families and any emergency cases that may come up. For example if you came to visit a soldier in the barracks and collapsed, we would treat you," says the youthful military doctor.
"The problem is that some of these women just came following the others, they were not even wives to UPDF soldiers. In that instance, it might be difficult," adds the doctor.
That aside, some Congolese earn their daily bread through selling themselves to men as a result of the hardships they are under going, and this, local leaders say, is a danger as it might increase HIV/Aids prevalence.
"These people are really suffering, this has made them prostitutes, in my area there is a risk that HIV/Aids will increase as a result of the presence of these Congolese women," says Akena Richard, LC1 chairperson of Techo-Subward, an area with a large Congolese presence.
He, however, says people don't hate the Congolese, "because of the problems they are in, people are sympathetic to them, they allow them to pluck cassava and potato leaves from their gardens which they cook as vegetables."
"The other day one of them died and they had no where to bury her, one of my resident here allowed her to be buried in his compound, next to the graves of his children," Akena adds.
The issue of Congolese women, and their fate in a foreign country, has caught the attention of some social workers, among them Sandra Oder, the founder of Children's Environmental Health Network (CEHN), a local child focused NGO.
"The issue of Congolese women is a big issue that should be addressed and acted upon. As an NGO we have tried our level best, but we are constrained by inadequate resources," said Ms Oder.
"There are those who now want to go back home, but they have no money, what do you do with those ones? What about their children?" Oder asks.
Since her mother got admitted to hospital, Paalwa Lwize has been getting help from CEHN "we visit her and the mother, counsel them give them sugar, bread and eggs but as you know, that is not enough," she says.
Seated in front of her small hut, Sophia, 23, another Congolese who says she comes from Bujimali in Gbadolite, in north-eastern Congo, points dejectedly towards Gulu airfield and says;
"There is a bush some where there, it's were we get buried when we die," Sophia said, then she added "I would rather be buried in a bush in my own country."
Dr Lusiiba however says "when they die, where they get buried is an arrangement between their husbands and their families."
Several attempts made by this writer to get a comment from the army's northern region spokesperson Lt. Paddy Ankunda were futile as he kept saying; "I know you want to write about how these people are suffering and all that, there I am not talking to you" on more than three occasions when contacted over the issue of Congolese women.
But its not all doom and gloom for the Congolese women. Sheila, who declined to reveal her age and surname, says that unlike other Congolese women who followed their UPDF husbands, she came with a "mission" to do business.
She is running a bar in Gulu town that her husband whom she declines to name but says is a " UPDF officer" helped her set up. She is from Aruu in Eastern Congo; she says she is happy in Uganda because she has a "lovely husband who cares for her". The only thing, she adds, that disturbs her is that it's very difficult to communicate with people back home in Congo.
They have a child with her UPDF husband now in P.4 at an elite Catholic mission run school, Negri Primary School, located about 4kms from Gulu town.
Their home is in Pece, a suburb of Gulu town. They live in a two-roomed apartment, and it's a neat room with a fan blowing cool air and Congolese music booming out of a Sony radio cassette.
Mid way in our interview, a well built man walks in, they both smile to each other. I suspect the man is Sheila's husband so I explain my mission to him, then they both laugh and the man says; "I don't know anything, talk to her," before going into the inner room.
As I was working on the last bits of this story, two things happened; first I suddenly bump into Anna Shaku and another colleague in the streets of Gulu. They were walking at a terrific speed and sweating profusely, they then tell me that Ekuse Zhozeti Misherini, their other pregnant colleague whom I had earlier on met had been admitted in hospital.
"She is going to give birth, but it seems there is a problem, she is taking too long, her husband is not there, we want to give him a call but we don't have the money, help us."
I then give them my mobile, they dial Ekuse's husband, then in Kiswahili tell him to immediately rush to Gulu Referral Hospital. They thank me, make a U-turn and dash towards the direction of the hospital. My little help was a small solution to a big sea of troubles for a group of people facing hard times in a foreign land.
The second thing that happened is that I got news that Mamisha had died, may be like many others, she is now lying in that bush near the airfield, where the Congolese normally get buried.
My prayer is that little Paalwa who was taking care of her, albeit without gloves never contracted HIV/Aids that her mum eventually succumbed to.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Plant Underway
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Plant Underway
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA will become a cassava starch exporter, when a venture to construct a cassava-processing factory kicks off this year.
The $3m project will be located at the Uganda- Kenya border and is a brainchild of Todd Teoh, the executive director of the Malaysian-based Tagling Group.
"We are planning to construct the biggest cassava processing firm in this region. By mid next year, production would have started," Teoh said.
This was during a recent visit of the Malaysian delegation to Apparels Tri-Star factory in Bugolobi.
Teoh said they were eyeing a 2000-acre patch of land in Malaba to lower transportation and operational costs because it was near the Indian Ocean.
He said the factory would produce 50 tonnes of cassava starch per day, which would be used as raw materials in food, pulp and textile production.
Teoh said the factory would initially directly and indirectly employ about 300 people.

Original_Search_Title: New Envoys Receive Letters of Credence
Headline: Nigeria: New Envoys Receive Letters of Credence
Body_Text:
Abuja — Minister of Foreign Affairs, Ambassador Olu Adeniji, on 17 August
presented letters of credence to three new ambassadors.
The new ambassadors and their country postings are as follows:
Â· Mr Godfrey Preware - France
Â· Mr Clarkson Umela - Belgium
Â· Mr Olu Olusanmoku - Cote d'Ivoire
The Minister said that Nigeria attaches great importance to the three
nations and tasked the envoys to be "very alert."
He specifically charged the Ambassador to France to work towards gaining
support for Nigeria's current efforts at cassava development.
In all, Ambassador Adeniji urged the new diplomats to be spokesmen for
Nigeria's development prospects, and encouraged them to do everything
possible to attract investors into the emerging markets.
He also advised them to be considerate in their expenditure and to
ensure effective methods of assessment before approving the disbursement of
funds.
Adeniji's advice on finances was engendered by what he referred to as
"perennial under-funding" suffered by many Nigerian missions abroad. He
promised to take on the responsibility of improving the situation.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Wants Labour Bill Passed
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Wants Labour Bill Passed
Body_Text:
Minna, Osogbo, and Bauchi — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday restated the resolve of his
administration to decentralise Labour as he urged members of the House of
Representatives to pass all pending executive bills, including the Trade
Union Reform Bill, into law.
Declaring open the three-day House retreat in Minna, Obasanjo, who
arrived the venue of the programme six and half hours behind schedule, said
that all the executive bills currently before the House are critical to the
reform initiatives of his administration.
In his words: "Honourable members, in addition to the important bills
and executive proposals which you have graciously and responsibly passed,
let me use this opportunity to remind and to urge you to pass other bills
which are pending before you and which are equally as critical to government
reform initiatives.
"These are the Electoral Power Sector Reform Bill; the Local
Government/Area Councils New Revenue Sharing formula from the Federation
Account, Trade Unions Reform Bills; the Niger Delta Development Commission
(amendment) Bill; Armed Forces Service Commission (establishment) Bill;
National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (amendment) Bill; Mandatory
Non-Discrimination Against Persons Living with HIV/AIDS Bill; the Compulsory
and Universal Basic Education Bill; Privatisation and Commercialisation
Bill; Consessioneering Bill; Fiscal Responsibility Bill; Tax Reform Bill;
Electoral Act Amendment Bill; Incorporation of Cassava Flour into Wheat
Flour Bill," he said.
He also lauded the cordial relationship existing between the executive
and the legislature, adding that it has enabled his administration to
achieve more within the last one year.
Obasanjo commended the present legislature for choosing dialogue in
place of confrontation in its relationship with other arms of government,
saying it is a sign of maturity which the executive has been
reciprocating. He maintained that no arm of government is entirely
independent of the others, arguing that this makes collaboration among the
three arms of governemnt a necessary ingredient for development.
He consequently urged Nigerians not to see the cordiality between the
executive and legislature as capable of impacting on their interests
negatively.
"As a matter of fact, I believe that we have a House that is becoming a
willing partner and a working companion of a decidedly reformist executive -
an executive that is persistent without impudence and generous without undue
indulgence. Let us continue to work together in the area of transparency,
accountability, eradication of corruption, individual and collective
integrity and probity.
"This is the new kind of Executive-Legislative relations that we must
have with the National Assembly. Mischief makers choose to call it
"compromise", but I call it concord and that is how it should be: mutual
interdependence as against the wrong notion of exclusive independence. We
must move away from culture of measuring performance and strength only in
terms of conflicts, abuses, and subversion or negation of polity," he
stated.
Obasanjo also spoke on the decision of the Federal Government to
withhold funds due to councils in states where new local governments were
created, adding that once there is compliance with government's directive,
the affected councils' funds would be released. In an apparent reference to
Lagos, he said that out of the five states that created new councils, four
have reverted to the status quo and have begun to receive their withheld
funds.
At another venue of the retreat held at Miccom Gold Hotels and Resorts,
Ada, Osun State, Vice President Atiku Abubakar urged House members to look
into the possibility of reviewing laws affecting electoral system in the
country, adding that this would go a long way at putting an end to post
election crises.
Atiku, who was represented by the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) Deputy
National Chairman (South), Alhaji Shuaib-Oyedokun, stated that the reviewing
of laws guiding the electoral system by the members of the House of
Representatives would bring peace and harmony to the nation's polity.
He also solicited the support and co-operation of the lawmakers for the
on-going economic reforms of the Federal Government.
This, he said, would bring a new lease of life to all Nigerians and
thereby enhance the growth and development of the country.
In his goodwill message at the opening ceremony at the Bauchi venue of
the retreat, Senate President Adolphus Wabara urged members of the House of
Representatives to use their legislative, representative and oversight
powers to ensure that the nation's democracy translates to meaningful and
concrete improvements in the lives of the people.
Wabara reminded members that they have the onerous task of ensuring that
the current democratic experiment endures.
According to him, "we need to underscore the fact that the parliament is
the soul of democracy and it is only the parliament that provides
representation for the disparate constituencies in the country and ensures
that their concerns are known and that their interests are taken care
of".
While stating that the parliament was the core of representative
democracy, the Senate President pointed out that the fact that they "are the
direct representatives of the people and that the institution symbolises the
heart of democracy" should help us to improve our commitments to both our
constituents and to democratic stability".
He added that the way to do both of these was "to continuously remind
ourselves that we are in the National Assembly first and foremost to serve
our people, not ourselves; and to remind ourselves that we are here because
of democracy and that we have vested interest and responsibility to ensure
that democracy endures".
Wabara urged legislators to use the retreat not only to review how far
they have succeeded in fulfilling their individual and collective mandates,
"but it should help us to deepen our capacity to function better", adding
that "it is very important that we continue to improve on our capacities so
that we will be better prepared to confront the many challenges before our
country today".
In his speech, Speaker of the House of Representatives, Alhaji Aminu
Bello Masari, said the retreat was organised to enable members have an
undistracted retrospective look at their past activities with a view to
forging new ways of legislating more effectively for the benefit of the
nation.
He expressed the hope that at the end of the retreat, members capacity
to make better legislative inputs would have been enhanced in law making and
legislative functions.
Former Senate President, Senator Evan Enwerem hilariously recalled the
turbulent nature of retreats, especially in the Senate where in Calabar in
1992 and Port Harcourt this year, almost led to an unpleasant end.
In his welcome speech, the Deputy Governor of Bauchi State, Alhaji
Abdulmalik Mohammed said the state government has proved to the people that
"it is only democracy that is better than autocracy and that the worst
democratic government democratic is better than the best military rule in
terms of the provision of people orientated policies, programmes and project
to the people of the state".
According to him, in the past five years, the state administration has
embarked on and completed various developmental projects which have direct
bearing on the lives of the people, pointing out that what the
administration has achieved in terms of socio-economic and political
developments were much more than what all the past distractions had done for
the people of the state.
He then enjoined the lawmakers to use the retreat to reflect on the
journey so far, and hoped that they would leave Bauchi with a greater
commitment to the daunting task of lifting the nation out of the woods.
Also speaking, Deputy Governor of Osun State, Erelu Olusola Obada sought
the intervention of the members of the House at reviving the two ailing
federal companiesin the state, Osogbo Steel Rolling Mill (OSRC) and Machine
Tools Limited, Osogbo (NMT).
She said that the development would go along way in enhancing the
socio-economic growth of the state and the country in general.

Original_Search_Title: Finally, Your Fraudulent Name
Headline: Nigeria: Finally, Your Fraudulent Name
Body_Text:
Lagos — SOMEBODY somewhere in Nigeria goes by the name, "Ade Bendel". I have heard that, that is not anybody's real name. Some other person calls herself Mariam Murmundi, but I have also heard that that is not a real name either. Some blokes at the Financial and Economic Crimes Commission are claiming that these "names" are criminal aliases of persons dubiously duping other persons, but without their victims knowing who their milkers are.
It brings us back to the matter we have been discussing in the past two weeks: that there is a prevalence of willful change of names among Nigerians. We have thought that these changes are almost always for the wrong, no, not wrong, but criminal reasons. One has heard such balderdash that some names conjure up evil covenants made by ancestors, which would be visited on generations unless they changed the names.
It is safe now, to state that such claim of repercussions is only the figment of the imagination of the claimants. Or, how does one think of the pot-pouri of belief-cum-ritualism that has come to be known as the "Okija orgy" in Anambra State? This is happening in a community that is very rich in Christian (religious) "credentials" with both the (Roman) Catholic Church, and the Anglican Communion hosting most of the very prominent members as knights, cathecists, cannons, monsignors and bishops!
Come to think of it, the phenomenon of change of name, particularly among males (and regarding their root-names) is most "popular" among the Igbo, who seem to have no compunction with obliterating their origin. It did not begin today: such nouns as "Awka", "Onitsha", "Awgu", even "Onuorah" are certainly not Igbo words - they are adulterations of original Igbo names of places and words which were allowed to be corrupted just to please visitors or invaders.
Such may not easily happen to the Yoruba who retain such so-called inelegant names and nouns as "Ogunbufunmi", "Fadare" ("the truth told by IFA"), "Ogunyemi" (literally, "I worship the God of Iron, no regret!"). One world renown personality of Yoruba origin once proudly announced himself as "Anikulapo-Kuti". The italicised end of the name translates, "I have juju or death in my pocket!" Could you beat that?
As for the northerner, whether he is Hausa-Fulani, Nupe, Yargam, Kataf or Ba-Berberi, you might do your damnest best but fail to get him to root out his origin. Otherwise, why would one of them who might have been born and bred in Lagos insist that his name remains "Mahmuda Mai-Dabino na-Alhaji Kashim Gwarzo", Gwarzo being the birthplace and origin of the Mahmuda's ancestors? Towns in most parts of the north with such seemingly unenviable names as "Ungwar-Rogo" (the commune of cassava growers), or "Yanshanu" (which could easily be translated as "calves" or the "community of cow breeders") still proudly keep their antecendents.
And, among peoples of other communes, root-names, whatever the etymology, are revered. No, there are hardly any records that people with such surnames (root-names) as "Carpenter", "Witchmonger", "Cattlebleat" or even "Forest" had sometime desired to change their names (root-names) because they thought the same to be un-edifying. People across the globe are generally always almost proud to flaunt their history, origin and culture - most of these amplified by their names. Why not our people?
You wonder why our laws are so lax, any goat could change its name to a lamb? I don't. I wonder only why it takes so little to swear to an affidavit for the change of a name: no interview, no questions asked, nothing! Nothing. "Are we really helpless in these matters?" (and this rhetoric is borrowed from a very senior colleague). Especially so, when it appears obvious that name change among most Nigerians is often the prelude to criminality.
But, we said, at the beginning of this journey that something is fishy. We had asked:
"But, why do people change their names, their identity: especially anything to do with their roots?
"The first attempt at criminality is for one to try to obliterate his/her identity. And, the logic is simple: most people would not contemplate involvement in criminal or dishonourable activity if they were certain that they would be caught. Which also goes for the belief that, where the victim of a criminal activity happens, unfortunately, to recognise any one of the criminals, that victim is near certainly dead.
"Yet, you wonder, is it only the pandering to criminality that leads people to wish to obliterate their original natural identity? Is it? Are there egocentric nuances that may lead people to change their identities? You ask these questions because, some warped ideologues have posited that "people who wish to be 'born again' are only those who were not born well in the first place".
"That brings you back to your starting block: would anybody wish to change his/her root-name (original natural identity) from, say Nnamdi-Azikiwe to "Robot", or from Aminu-Dantata, Sijuwade, Ibrahim-Babangida, Maccido, Etiebet, Akenzua, Igbinedion, Pius-Okigbo, Bayero (etc) to, say, Scatterhead, Bastardnovich or Littleton? There must be something wrong somewhere, with the Nigerian collective psyche, which is responsible for people always "needing" to change their names, especially root-names - particularly because often when these root-names are up-rooted, they are replaced with near meaningless nomenclatures which simply go to insult the culture of our people."
Where a man chooses to abandon the honour of his roots and changes his name to "Gospel Fynecountry", "Gift Godgives", or "Wonder Voodoomania", something is certainly wrong. The law should do something.

Original_Search_Title: Dateline Monrovia: Winning the Hearts And Minds of Liberians
Headline: Liberia: Dateline Monrovia: Winning the Hearts And Minds of Liberians
Body_Text:
Freetown — Wow, another hectic day again! Foday Fofanah had just called from CJFE
barking orders that if our flats were not ready within the next one-hour,
the paper would not hit the newsstands the next day.  Because he had
collected several adverts, my Adverts Manager, Ephraim got infuriated and
came knocking at my door seeking to know why the delay.
He was followed by Ehis who repeated the same question. Before all this,
James and Poppay had permanently taken residence in my office and like a
hungry Lion; they monitored each move I made on the computer and the
telephone as if these were going to provide the instant answer to their
worries. Adding salt to Syrian wounds was the fact that Regina Pratt had not
yet returned from Parliament and Court where she had vowed to get the lead
story and Ajami was not yet back from NEC to get the latest happenings in
the conduct of the local government elections. I looked at my watch, it was
6:45 P.M and my only hope now was on Rachel Horner who had gone to cover the
Police Briefing. Then, hopes were dashed.
Regina came back and reported there was not much happening. Followed was
Ajami who said everything has been put on hold until the next day and amid
this steam of mental confusion, Rachel called to say she had diverted her
course to attend a workshop instead of the Briefing.  Alas! I was mad and
hopes of coming out the next day were dashed. It was during this state of
despondency that a mosquito started singing into my ears. I folded my arm to
give it a deadly blow and when I unleashed my arsenal, I struck my face,
which instantly woke me up.  Behold! I was dreaming. ! Can this be true?
Another day has started but I am no longer at Concord Times. I suspiciously
inspected my surroundings and now became conscious that I am already late
for work somewhere in my new assignment outside Sierra Leone in
fact. However, for some of us who had made it a requirement in life to read
Literature and particularly Chinua Achebe's made in Africa brand of
Literature, we must have benefited from the literary reservoir of this great
son of Africa who once reminded us years back that, "every generation has
the responsibility to tell their story." Surely, that was exactly what I was
doing way back in town until fate or maybe destiny caught up with me and
made it a responsibility to be in this war torn country trying to bring back
hope and succor to a Liberian nation just waking up from the harsh realities
of a crisis. Here, I don't have to wake up and see if having a Le.10, 000
bank note is the most sensible thing to do in the 'eve' of regional economic
integration nor do I have to bother myself about the levity that is being
exhibited within the APC who have now made it a policy to dump their linens
in muddy water and wash them in public or rather, even bother myself with
Solo B's statements that we must shout hallelujah because we are still
ranked last in the UN Human Development Index as it would bring us more
donor money. For me, here the talk has been; rehabilitating and
reintegrating ex-combatants in society and building bridges between them and
their communities. However, ever since I came here, I have been inundated
with e-mails seeking that I must send a Dateline-Monrovia. I have dragged my
feet all this while not because my hands could have been rusty but had
simply wanted to know how much I may have been missed at home and at the
same time, got the blessing from my boss, Kingsley. But where do I start?
Don't wait to hear how I departed Freetown amid tears from loved ones who
were only concerned that the Liberian babes were not going to devour me nor
would I even mention that we competed with bags and baggage to secure space
in the flight that came with me from Freetown. Weasua (I heard it is a
Liberian flight) however brought back memories of our Trans Atlantic Slave
Trade primary school history lessons, which told tales of how slaves were
packed in the slave ships by the slavers like sardines in tins. Thankfully,
we were able to meander our way through into Roberts International Airport
which is no different to what we have at Hastings and perhaps something our
guys at the Sierra Leone Airport Authority (SLAA) including its
P.RO. Mohamed Swaray (Rado Mara) would want to use at glorifying our own
Lungi airport as a bastion of hope.
When I was coming, Swaray bought me food at our airport's canteen after
enjoying my eyes and nose with the sweet fragrance that comesform our Duty
Free Shop.
At Roberts Airport, this is hundred if not one million years beyond
them. Shamefully, since I arrived here almost two weeks ago, I have not had
time to worship allah not out of levity but for the simple reason that
Monrovia is literally saturated with hundreds if not thousands of churches
and finding a mosque in central Monrovia is like passing a camel though a
needle's eye. Coming to this city which gained her independence some 157
years ago when Sierra Leone was still in diapers or rather, in its embryonic
stage for the first time would want to give you a spectacle of a people
closer to God but then, you may begin to wonder, with all this sense of
advertised Christian religiosity, why were they killing one another? Why was
brother pitched against a brother or a sister against another? Just like
Freetown, Monrovia is also battling for awards in terms of the
constant.
Availability of electricity and water and! in fact, while Freetown at
times, could boast of electricity for some twenty - four hours or less,
here, it is virtually not possible. Apart from the visible scars of war
still evident everywhere and anywhere, the leftovers of buildings here are
relics. A major component that has dominated my menu here is oil. If you
want to eat your cassava or potato leaves, then you must be prepared to
accept that it is not done like the way we do our there. What a small world
indeed. In my lodge, we have all sorts of people ranging from Europe,
America, Australia and of course Africa and just the other night, we had a
party and you would have loved to see an Indian guy dancing, ' da tumba
dae.' Because of our jobs, we are now allowed to stay out late at
night. Don't call it curfew but I have been moving out and about to see
where the happenings are but sadly, one thing you note after visiting their
restaurants or pubs is that these people have literally mortgaged their
Liberian Liberty (currency) to the almighty $ U.S and you can even buy
pepper or onion in the market with $U.S. But what do most Liberians still
make of Jarule (I mean Charles Taylor)? Some are silent on this but some
still believe he is the man of the hour and I heard that during his
birthday, it took centre stage on their newspapers here with Centre spread
birthday congratulations to the man David Crane is hunting for.
Another name for him here is, Ja rule. Though the country is wiping the
dust of war from its eyes, this has not however disturbed them from wads of
thousands of $ U.S bills from their wallets to buy the latest cars in the
market. For UNMIL here, everybody is working the extra hour to ensure that
Liberians snore and sleep with both eyes shut at night particularlyin areas
they have deployed and if UNAMSIL could boast of D.J Base or Valcarcel 247
on their radio, then come and see what Kojo and others are doing
here. Everybody tunes to UNMIL radio as through it; UNMIL has been able to
win the hearts and minds of even those hardhearted combatants. Disarmament
is gaining momentum and of course, they were overwhelmed with the fifty nine
thousand combatants that have now disarmed even though the speculated figure
was thirty eight thousand. Say whatever you may but UNMIL is the bride of
the hour in downtown Monrovia and the honeymoon is just beginning. In them,
Liberians see hope and particularly for the politicians, once disarmed was
laid it rest, elections beckons but till then, the party is still on.
Editor's note: Osman Benk Sankoh was until recently editor of this
medium. He now works for UNMIL's Public information Section in Liberia

Original_Search_Title: NOA Takes Fight Against Poverty to Grassroots
Headline: Nigeria: NOA Takes Fight Against Poverty to Grassroots
Body_Text:
Ilorin — The National Orientation Agency (NOA) has commenced a training programme
for women on cassava utilisation at the grassroots in its bid to fight
poverty.
The training programme, in conjunction with Ilorin East Local
Government, according to the state director of the agency, Mr. Femi
Mapaderun, is targeted at the empowerment of women in the state The
programme, he noted, is the first in the series of programmes on youth and
women empowerment and would be taken to other local councils in the state He
said the two-day training workshop, whose emphasis is on utilisation of so
that cassava, was packaged by the agency cassava can be put into various
other uses other than the customary items of garri.
NOA boss maintained that the workshop is aimed at improving the living
condition of people at the grass roots, pointing out that during the
training, women would be taught how cassava can be used to produce such
confectioneries as biscuits, cake, doughnuts, meat pies and salad cream,
among others.

Original_Search_Title: Sudan Gives Kony Deadline
Headline: Sudan Gives Kony Deadline
Body_Text:
Birinyang — THE Sudan People's Armed Forces (SPAF) has set August 6 to end Joseph
Kony's bloody 18-year long era of terror.
Last Friday, it was all hugs, handshakes as SPAF Brigadier Mohammed
Habib assured Brigadier Kale Kaihura of Sudan's commitment to end the
war.
"We are very committed to end this Kony problem. I assure Uganda that we
shall either capture him and hand him over in seven days from today or we
shall kill him if he resists," said Habib as the generals and their teams
sipped hot black tea served in tiny glasses
Kaihura, now based in Juba to negotiate with the Sudan authorities,
said, "We have done our part. We trust that we are going to work together to
end this menace once for all and for our common good."
Habib affirmed, "My brothers, I assure you again that it is not the SPLA
who are our enemy but Kony. We are tired. Our people here in Sudan have
suffered from his terrorism just as much as our brothers and sisters in
northern Uganda. The time is now. We are going to capture him in seven days
or kill him."
The deal was sealed at Malu, 15km south of Juba in the vast south Sudan
wilderness.
The SPAF delegation included Col. Tajsir Abdallah, Col. Abdul Muhammed,
Maj. Khalid Hamadrallah, Lt. Thomas Baziha, 2nd Lt. Badrudddin Abdulgadir
Mohammed, Lt. Hafiz Alhaj Mukhtar, Sgt. Tiberio Owen Bernado, Cpl. Peter
Valeriano Bohol, Sgt. Nimeiry Giza Lege and Pte James Ladu.
Uganda's team included the UPDF intelligence coordinator for northern
Uganda, Col. Charles Otema, the operations commander in southern Sudan,
Lt. Col. Sam Kiwanuka and Capt. Younes Bagada.
As the generals sealed their pact, 4km away in Birinyang Game Reserve,
UPDF troops under the scorching sun were burying decomposing bodies of more
than 100 LRA killed last week in the bushes by a UPDF attack on Kony's camp
hidden behind SPAF troops across the red line.
The stench of rotting human flesh was heavy in the air as The New Vision
was guided through Kony's razed camp that doubled as an observation post and
a training ground that is concealed by rocky ridges.
There were scores of acres of gardens of opium, simsim, cassava, millet,
sorghum and cattle.
Only the walls of Kony's round mud and wattle grass-thatched hut were
left standing.
More dead rebels and weapons were discovered as the UPDF continued
combing the bushes.
Many rebels surrendered due to hunger and injuries.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo, Labour Rift Serves NLC Right - Wada Nas
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo, Labour Rift Serves NLC Right - Wada Nas
Body_Text:
Kaduna — FORMER Minister of Special Duties, Alhaji Wada Nas, says that the
Nigeria Labour Congress [NLC] does not deserve pity in its current face-off
with President Olusegun Obasanjo. Nas said that although Obasanjo's effort
to break up the NLC was condemnable, it was wrong for Labour to have
supported the president in 2003 when it was obvious that the presidential
elections were massively rigged.
He spoke at a news conference in Kaduna yesterday. The former Minister
also chided Professor Wole Soyinka for urging former military president,
Ibrahim Babangida to apologise to Nigerians for annulling the June 12, 1993
presidential elections won by late Chief M.K.O. Abiola.
"How can Wole Soyinka ask Babangida to apologise to Nigerians for what
happened in 1993. What did IBB do? What he did is insignificant compared to
what President Obasanjo has been doing to Nigerians since 1999.
"Obasanjo should first apologise to Nigerians for doing more damage to
democracy. Free and fair election is the backbone of any democratic
institution.
Obasanjo rigged elections and Babangida cancelled elections. Which one
is greater?.
"There is no security of lives and property. There is no law and order
in Obasanjo's government, so, why did Soyinka not ask Obasanjo to apologise
first? It is because Obasanjo is his tribesman? Soyinka is a coward.
"On the plan by Obasanjo to destroy labour, I have no sympathy for
labour. They supported Obasanjo when he [Obasanjo] rigged himself into
office in 2003; they should have realized that he was preparing to become a
full blown dictator", he said.
Nas also said that the recent Federal Government's directive that
exporters of made in Nigeria textile materials would attract zero duties in
the United States was nonsense because the same government introduced
policies that led to the closure of most textile industries in the
country.
"There is no textile materials to be exported as 70 percent of the
industries have been closed down and thousands of their workers
sacked. There is nothing in that policy. Where is the black oil which is a
major raw material to be used?
"Ordinarily, this good news should gladden the hearts of our textile
manufacturers and could be counted as one of the greatest achievements of
the Obasanjo administration. Nigerian textiles are in great demand
especially among the about 30 million African Americans, painfully as good
as the news is, the opportunities have already been lost precisely because
there are no longer textile materials to be exported having killed by the
various economic policies of the administration"
Nas further said that the new textile policy would end up like the one
for cassava which only helped to make the commodity which was one of the
cheapest food items to become scarce and costly.

Original_Search_Title: Escaping the Jaws of Kony
Headline: Uganda: Escaping the Jaws of Kony
Body_Text:
Kampala — Joe Nam recently flew to Kicwa Reception Home in Kitgum town, courtesy of Uganda Red Cross Society. The home shelters some of the children who fled from Kony. They narrate harrowing tales. Excerpts:
Christine, 15, abducted on June 2003 in Padibe- Kitgum.
I was in senior one in Padibe SS when they took me. We walked for days in the bush. Then we were taken to Soroti, Lira and Gulu.
I was baby sitting the child of one of the women rebel soldiers. She beat me many times and said she would kill me if I tried to escape.
Then one day, the army attacked us. There was a lot of fighting. I ran with the baby on my back as others ran in all directions.
I walked for five days and nights, all this time, giving the baby only water. Later, I met some people who took me and the baby to the army on June 2004.
Peter, 12, abducted on June 14, 2003 in Katakwi.
We were taken to Lango, then to River Agago in Gulu, then to Kitgum.
Many times, we were hungry and would eat only leaves. We were beaten many times for no cause.
One day fighting started when we were near Kitgum. The army had attacked. I was shot in the leg and could not run.
The army found me there and took me to Kitgum town in May 2004.
Nassan, 11, abducted June 2003 at Palabek Kitgum.
I was digging in the morning when they took me at gunpoint. We crossed Aswa River with the the rebel group.
Then a certain man in the group tried to escape. Seven of us were ordered to beat him to death. The commander told us to lick all his blood or his spirit would haunt us. Many children died in the bushes.
One day, I tried to escape, but I was caught and beaten with a hoe until I fainted. Some people in the group wanted me killed, but the commander stopped them. One day, a helicopter attacked and scattered us in the bushes.
I jumped into River Pager and swam across it, then I ran into the bush. The rebels shot many times at me, but missed. For many days, I roamed the bushes until I met a woman who took me to the soldiers.
Janet, 19, abducted 1998 Pader.
I was going home in the evening when I met rebels.
They abducted me and we walked for two weeks to Jebilen in the Sudan. Many children died of cold in the mountains, there was nothing to eat and there were heavy rains all the way. But we were given food when we arrived.
I found many people, about four brigades. We used to speak in Acholi and Arabic.
We would pray like in a church and Kony would also attend, and also sometimes lead the prayers himself.
We left Jebilen and transferred to Rubanga Tek in 2002. Then when the soldiers followed us, we went up to the Imatong Hills.
The last year when we were sent to collect firewood, I escaped with my baby and returned to Uganda.
Beatrice, 16, abducted on July, 23 2003 at Alokolum Gulu.
I had just sat for primary Living Examinations and was waiting for my results when I was abducted.
We walked for many days. We mainly carried the rebels' luggage.
There was a lot of hunger and only cassava was available for eating. We were beaten all the time for no cause.
One day, as we crossed River Pager, a helicopter attacked and we scattered all over the place.
After running for a short distance, I felt tired, raised my arms and the army rescued me and took me to the barracks.
Pamela, abducted in February 2003 in Kitgum district.
I was assigned to cook for Opoka. For five months, I could not go anywhere without being escorted.
Then they started to leave me to collect firewood with other girls.
One day after walking for three days and nights, the commander felt tired. While he slept, I was sent to collect firewood.
I crawled for some distance, but when I got up to run, a rebel on a tree, who watches over us saw me and shouted. Three boys were dispatch to get me, but I continued to run.
After running across two valleys, the boys gave up on me. A few days later, I came to a road where I met some people, who took me to the army on July 3.

Original_Search_Title: The Love for Cinema Made Awori Steal
Headline: Uganda: The Love for Cinema Made Awori Steal
Body_Text:
Kampala — Fifty-two years ago, the now fiery legislator Aggrey Awori successfully evaded the 'public accounts committee' in his parents' home. Crime? He would hide the one shillings coin, given to him as return fare from school - and instead trek home, some 20 miles away. Reason? to spend the money on watching movies in Mbale town.
The lawmaker, whose parents were adherent Christians, has kept this secret for the past five decades. He says he would endure the 20 kms walk, punctuated with hunger, because of his love for movies; an addiction he developed while at Kakungulu Primary School, in Mbale.
Awori says sometimes he would sell mangoes to Indian traders so as to get some money for watching movies (file photo)
Awori would wake up at 3a.m. in the morning and start the journey to town with a couple of friends, because he had to get to town by midday. In the evening, the old folks would think he had used a bus to return home. He shared the secret with his nephew, Horace Awori. Nobody else ever got to know his surreptitious ordeal.
During one of his habitual journeys, he left without the knowledge of his nephew - not knowing that the young boy had followed him. After giving Horace his transport fare, Awori as usual descended on the footpath, along with his acquaintances, in the scorching sun towards home.
But as Awori returned home, he met Horace along the way. "Horace then told me that he had also walked the 20 kms, after learning that I used to walk home," Awori says he warned him not to tell his parents or else they would be thumped; a secret Horace kept.
Awori grew up in Mbale town. He would also sneak from school to go looking for mangoes in the nearby areas, to fend off hunger. But he relished watching cinema most. "It is one of my fondest memories," he says.
He still reminisces the great times at Ashok Cinema hall in Mbale town, where he would watch Indian movies. "I used to do this on the weekends, without permission from my parents. Sometimes we would sit on the floor to watch the movies - free of charge," He would also window-shop round Mbale town and sometimes sell mangoes to Indians to earn a few shillings to pay for the movies.
He however says, it was evident in his early days that he would turn into a great athlete. Such skills enabled him represent Uganda at the 1960 and 1964 Olympic games. "I was too fast for my age group. I used to run 100m, 200m, 300m and 400m," he says.
Years earlier, while aged ten, Awori was engaged in some other mischief; 'stealing' his father's car. He would 'steal' the car after his dad, an archdeacon, had gone to church on Sundays. "It was a Ford-Anglia. I would drive it after tying bags of sand behind the tyres to disguise the tyre tracks. By the time my parents would return from church they would find the car washed," he recounts.
For his secondary education, Awori went to Budaka Secondary School, where he underwent a cultural shock. "Life was extremely hard. We had meat once in a week, with dilute soup. The diet was mainly cassava and groundnuts sauce," he said.
Awori says because he was a sportsman and excelled in academics, he would usually catch the eye of any girl. "I also liked debate and politics."
He later studied at Nabumali High School. Awori was liked by one of his teachers, the late James Aryada, who became the first Ugandan to get an Honours degree in Maths at Oxford University.
"He coached me in English and Maths; subjects which I passed with high grades," he says. Together with his two close friends, at Nabumali, Awori joined King's College Budo. Among the two was the current MP for West Budama, Akisoferi Ogoola. By coincidence, the latter became a career diplomat, like Awori.
Being among the first Ugandans to sit A-levels at Kings College Budo, Awori says he opted out of joining Makerere University because he wanted to join Harvard University in the United States of America. He is one of the few Ugandans that have ever studied at one of the US prestigious universities - Havard.
Awori, who contested and lost the 2001 presidential polls, however says his ambitions have not been extinguished. He plans to contest for the highest office in the land, once more, during the 2006 polls - before retiring as one of Uganda's greatest political actors.

Original_Search_Title: As Liberian Language Studies Continue At Lott Carey: Elementary Students Exhibit Academic Skills
Headline: Liberia: As Liberian Language Studies Continue At Lott Carey: Elementary Students Exhibit Academic Skills in Vai
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Elementary students at the Lott Carey Baptist Mission School in
Brewerville, outside Monrovia, last Friday thrilled audience when
they exhibited their academic skills in one of Liberia's languages.
The students proved that the Vai language has magnificent, distinct
and unique set of characters that have long since been in use in
Grand Cape Mount County as a means of communication.
Impressed by the outstanding performance of the young elementary
students, the audience cheered joyously as the students wrote some
common nouns, verbs, short sentences as well as the days of the week
in the Vai scripts and phonetics.
On a blackboard displayed before the audience, the students scribed
the Vai phonetics "ji" for water; "Taaye"for walk; "Taa" for go; "Na"
for come, "gbasa" for cassava and many more.
Lott Carey is one of two high schools that teaches Liberian language.
The other is the St. Peter's Lutheran School that teaches the Kpelle
language.
Students Dearest Cooper, Oscar Johnson, Bearlyn Wesseh, Catherine
Jones, and Momolu Varmah were outstanding in their presentation of
the Vai language.
Others were Amos Blango, Dennis Zinnah, John Ahmed, Celestine Darblo,
Geeta Banks, Caroline Nimely, Onesimus Borkuah, Amelia Kengo, Teagbae
Poindoe and Juliana Chenoweth.
The Vai language instructor, John K. Benson, traced the introduction
of Vai language studies at Lott Carey to 1993, and thanked the
school's Superintendent, Rev. Dr. Jeremiah W. Walker for the
inclusion of Vai language studies in the school's curriculum.
Superintendent Rev. Dr. Walker lauded the students for their interest
in the Vai language describing them as great. He stressed the need
for Liberians to value their culture, noting that the teaching of the
Vai language encompasses the blessings of God.
Superintendent Walker said the rationale behind the inclusion of the
vernacular is to generate a sense of commonality, to uphold and
promote a cultural heritage and unite us as Liberians.
The Vai language is one of the sixteen major Liberian languages. It
is widely spoken in the Western part of the Country, specifically in
Grand Cape Mount County.
In addition to this, Principal Pandora Banks, challenged the students
to be studious in their studies of the Vai language. He said the Vai
language is a required course at the University for non-science
students.

Original_Search_Title: Frankadua Goes Gay ... As NPP, NDC Supporters Dance Over Electricity
Headline: Ghana: Frankadua Goes Gay ... As NPP, NDC Supporters Dance Over Electricity
Body_Text:
Frankadua — Residents of Frankadua in the Asuogyaman District Assembly (ADA) demonstrated their political maturity during the commissioning of the electricity project in that town quite recently.
Overjoyed with the commissioning of the electricity to enable them also get hooked to the national grid, the residents, irrespective of their political affiliations, joined hands to make the occasion a successful one.
What made the occasion more significant was how supporters of the NPP and the NDC donned their party T-shirts and danced to "boboobo" beat provided by a cultural troupe that beat drums that were painted in NDC colours.
No one isolated himself because the project was executed by yhe NPP government or did not share his joy by dancing because the beat was not coming from drums painted with NPP colours.
Frankadua is one of the 13 communities in the district that to facilitate the growth and development of small and medium scale industries, the (ADA) has completed the self-help electrification programme (SHEP) in the area.
Conspicuously absent was the MP for the area, Mr. Dwamena Bekoe, who was represented by Mr. Jonathan Ayawdoo, praised the government for not abandoning development projects started by the NDC government.
He claimed the project was started by the NDC but could not be completed due to change in governments in the year 2000.
However, the Deputy Eastern Regional Minister, Mr. Gustav Narh-Dormetey, decleared that the project was started by the NDC but the NPP government changed it for the better because the transformers were too small with old cables for the voltage supply to the towns. This exchange created a long laughter among the politicians at the occasion.
During his turn, the District Chief Executive ( DCE) of Asuogyaman, Dr. Godfred Kofi Siaw, challenged that since the inception of the NPP government, a lot of projects and programmes had taken place in the district in areas of education, health, water and sanitation, construction and rehabilitation of roads to mention but a few.
The DCE disclosed that 16 schools had been constructed and rehabilitated all over the area, 1,955 desks provided to 52 schools, 16 institutional KVIPs and three (3) unit teachers bungalows constructed.
Under health, water and sanitation, 35 boreholes to communities, 375 ventilated improve pit latrines, six inviro-loo toilet facilities, five public stand pipes, six clinics; one of which had been completed with nurses quarters.
The Chronicle learnt that under the President's Special Initiative for cassava starch production, 140 acres of land was currently under cultivation at Frankadua, whiles Ghana Telecom was in the process of installing public pay phones along the Akrade Combine Community.
Dr. G.K. Siaw, however, expressed his worry about the legal exclusion of the Akosombo Township from his jurisdiction.
According to him, this arrangement had seriously narrowed the revenue base of the assembly making it the only district in the country with a "Vatican city".
"A review of the Akosombo Township Instrument 1989 EI 42 to reflect current decentralization arrangements will be most welcomed," he appealed.

Original_Search_Title: Zaire Governor Gets Traditional Authorities Blessing
Headline: Angola: Zaire Governor Gets Traditional Authorities Blessing
Body_Text:
Mbanza — The newly appointed governor of Angola's northern Zaire province,
gen. Pedro SebastiÃ£o, was officially presented Saturday here to the local
traditional authorities, during a ceremony held in the Congo Kingdom Museum,
sponsored by Mbanza-Congo chieftains.
At the end of the ceremony, the governor pledged to do his best during
his mandate, to meet the expectations of the local populations.
Pedro SebastiÃ£o said he is aware of the difficulties he might come
across in his daily work, but stated that with the cooperation of the local
populations and teachings of the traditional authorities he will seek
successfully undertake Zaire province's reconstruction tasks.
To him, the ceremony the chiefs held to welcome him, is very important
to his personal life, as it represents the culture and historic values of
the Bacongo people.
"From what we have witnessed and received from this ceremony, we have
the responsibility to preserve these values and teachings and transmit them
to our children," the governor stated.
During the ceremony, Pedro SebastiÃ£o received the blessing of the local
population that also offered him goods (some goats, poultry and cassava
flour) in recognition of the work he has done for the people of the
region.
The governor, accompanied by two deputies with the ruling MPLA party's
provincial constituency, was ushered to the tomb in which, according to
history, a living madam Apolonia was buried by her own son, the King Nzemba
Ya Nginga.
He also visited the local Cathedral (Kulumbimbi), built in 1491 by
catholic missionaries.
Pedro SebastiÃ£o was appointed Zaire provincial governor early this
year.

Original_Search_Title: Child Trafficking "Zone" At Akateng
Headline: Ghana: Child Trafficking "Zone" At Akateng
Body_Text:
GNA
The Ministry of Women and Children's Affairs (MWCA) has identified
Akateng, a fishing community in the Manya Krobo District in the Eastern
Region, as a child trafficking zone.
Mr. Andrews Kwesi Teye, the District Chief Executive, made the
disclosure in a speech at a ceremony to outdoor a new market valued at
584.4-million-cedi for the community. He said many children of school
going-age were being exploited as child labourers.
He called for measures to save the children from the influence of
anti-social activities such as armed robbery.
The market, constructed under the Village Infrastructure Programme
(VIP), comprises four open sheds, two stalls and a warehouse.
Mr. Teye said the District Assembly in collaboration with the MWCA would
collect data on the children, and guarantee their total integration into
society. He expressed the hope that the Akateng market would increase the
revenue of the District Assembly.
Mr. Gustav Narh Dometey, Deputy Eastern Regional Minister, who outdoored
the market, said it was another step towards poverty reduction and asked the
traders to justify the huge capital investment through regular maintenance
of the structures.
Mr. Dometey, called on the political parties to ensure peace and
stability during the electioneering campaigns, "since the peace and
stability of the country is more important than the political ambition of
any individual or group."
He asked the people to embrace the President's Special Initiative on
Cassava and the National Health Insurance Scheme to help eradicate poverty
in the area.
