Original_Search_Title: First War Crimes Trial Starts At UN-Backed Court
Headline: Sierra Leone: First War Crimes Trial Starts At UN-Backed Court
Body_Text:
Freetown — A Special Court that includes five judges
appointed by the United Nations has begun the trial of those deemed
primarily responsible for war crimes and human rights abuse committed
during Sierra Leone's 10-year civil war.
The first three individuals to stand trial for commiting atrocities during
the 1991-2001 conflict were led into the dock of a specially built court
house in the capital Freetown on Thursday.
Controversially, they were not leaders of the Revolutionary United Front
(RUF) rebel movement which sought to overthrow the elected government of
President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah.
They were members of the Civil Defence Force (CDF), a civilian militia
group, based on traditional societies of hunters known as Kamajors, which
fought alongside the Tejan Kabbah's army against the rebels.
Special Court Prosecutor David Crane, a former lawyer with US Department
of Defence, said in his opening statement that the CDF had brutally killed
and raped and terrorised thousands of people, recruited child soldiers and
commited acts of cannibalism during the decade-long conflict.
"The just cause of a civil defence force in Sierra Leone, set up to defend
a nation, became distorted and twisted beyond measure," Crane told a
packed court room.
"The ghosts of thousands of the murdered and dead stand among us. They cry
out for a fair and transparent trial to let the world know what took place
here in Sierra Leone," he added.
Before him in the dock stood the three top leaders of the CDF: Sam Hinga
Norman, the National Coordinator of the militia movement, who went on to
become Interior Minister, Moinina Fofana, the National Director of War of
the militia force, and Alieu Kondewa, the High Priest of the CDF, who
supervised the traditional initiation rites that its members were obliged
to go through.
Crane said he would produce evidence of several atrocities for which these
men were directly responsible.
He cited one incident in which CDF gunmen arrested 65 people who had been
forced to work in a diamond mine by the RUF and shot them dead in groups
of three or four. When the executioners realised they were running low on
ammunition, they resorted to beheading the last 10 one at a time, he
added.
Deputy prosecutor Joseph Kamara meanwhile explained that witnesses would
testify how Kondewa, the High Priest of the CDF, repeatedly raped a woman
over the course of a week and how some CDF militia men cleaned out the
intestines of some of their victims and roasted and ate them with cassava.
While these allegations of brutal murders and cannibalism were being
recounted to the spellbound court, Hinga Norman smiled and began writing a
note to the judge, dismissing his defence team.
Kondewa looked shocked as rape allegations against him were recounted.
Fofana, the third accused, listened to a translation of the proceedings
through headphones with a smile crossing his face now and then.
Presiding judge Benjamin Itoe of Cameroon adjourned the proceedings after
the prosecution's 100-minute opening statement upon receiving the
hand-written note from Hinga Norman stating that he would conduct his own
defence before the court.
"It is a fundamental issue which the chamber would like to address and
deliver a reasoned decision on," he said.
The trial was due to resume on Tuesday 8 June.
A second separate trial of three RUF leaders, Issa Hassan Sesay, Morris
Kallon and Augustine Gbao, is due to begin on 5 July.
The Special Court is an international war crimes tribunal set up under the
terms of an agreement between the Sierra Leone government and the United
Nations signed in January 2002.
It has so far indicted 13 people of whom two have since died. The
remaining nine accused are currently in custody, but court officials have
hinted that other indictments may follow.
This is the second international war crimes tribunal to be set up in
Africa after the international court established in Arusha, Tanzania, to
try those responsible for the genocide in Rwanda.
Itoe, the presiding judge, said in his opening remarks at the CDF trial
that the proceedings would be fair and the accused would be presumed
innocent unless proven guilty "beyond all reasonable doubts" by the
prosecution.
"We, as a court....are not bound by the findings or conclusions of these
investigations or the contents of the indictments, which so far are mere
allegations," Itoe said. "Our decisions will be entirely based on the best
oral, documentary and other evidence that is advanced by the parties
before us."
But Itoe also stressed the role of the court in combatting impunity and
contributing to the process of national reconciliation in Sierra Leone
following the brutal decade-long conflict.
In recent months, the relevance of the tribunal, where five UN-appointed
foreign judges sit alongside three Sierra Leonean judges, has been
questioned by many Sierra Leoneans.
Critics have pointed out that the four men widely seen as those most
responsible for the atrocities of the civil war are beyond the Special
Court's reach.
The two top leaders of the RUF, Foday Sankoh and Sam Bockarie are now
dead, while two other key indictees; former Charles Taylor of Liberia, who
armed and backed the RUF rebels, and Johnny Paul Koroma, the leader of a
military junta which tried to join forces with the rebels in 1997 and
1998, have escaped its clutches.
Koroma went into hiding in January 2003 following an abortive attack on an
army barracks in Freetown, in which his followers were implicated.
Taylor, who was forced out of power in August last year, has been granted
political asylum in Nigeria.
Some supporters of Tejan Kabbah, who was re-elected for a second term as
president in 2002, have also questioned why the leaders of the CDF, which
was supporting a constitutionally elected government, should be put on
trial at all.
All the same, the court, financed by foreign donors, is being keenly
watched as a potential model for war crimes tribunals in other conflicts.
Reuters news agency quoted Robin Vincent, the Special Court's UN-appointed
Registrar, as saying he had been invited by the US State Department to
take part in a planning mission to Iraq "because they felt quite strongly
there were similarities."

Original_Search_Title: Is Ghana Becoming a Lawless Country?
Headline: Ghana: Is Ghana Becoming a Lawless Country?
Body_Text:
The turn of events in the country deserves serious attention if our
beloved Ghana should remain a well organized society. It is with good
intentions that all societies enact rules and regulations. These rules
translate into the orderly way activities are carried out. Wherever there is
lack of order. lives and properties are destroyed.
Ghana has had defined rules and regulations since our independence, some
of which have undergone amendments and modifications. Even dating back to
the very ancient empires like Songhai and Mali. there existed well cherished
traditional rules and regulations. One distinguished beauty about these
rules however is that, they were well respected for fear of the gods and
traditional sanctions.
In modern times however, statutory rules and regulations are constantly
being overlooked even by the supposed law enforcer. Emphasis is most
glaringly being placed on the apprehension of serious criminal breaches of
the law like armed robbery while the 'petty" disregards for the law are
deemed normal.
The problem is whether these "petty" offences do not contribute to the
breakdown of law in the society. If it is not important to punish such
offences, then why formulate laws about them?
Drivers feel absolutely comfortable to be in control of vehicles that
are without side driving mirrors, yet some of our police only stand by our
road sides on camouflage duty. But for the recent abatement of armed robbery
in the country and the escort of presidential convoys, I would confidently
say that our police are totally ineffective. Ghana seems not to have any
punishment for traffic jumpers.
More disturbing is the manner in which our traders are carrying out
their noble means of existence. It is worrying indeed. Pavements meant as
pedestrian walkways are now being turned into market stalls. Cassava and
tomatoes are exhibited for sale even on the roads. What an unpleasant
development.
Having doubt? Then clear it by visiting the Makola, Tudu and Circle
business areas.
Ironically, when one walks along Who is responsible for clearing this
mess? AMA or the Police? Are we to destroy already scarce foodstuffs by
driving over them on the roads? Who bears the blame in this case too?
Maybe politics accounts for all these problems. The fear of losing votes
should they stand up to the law and put these offenders in their right shoes
deter the authorities of doing the right thing. Others still argue that the
offenders need to make their daily bread. But why not make your daily bread
through the right mechanism? Should all traders accept the responsibility of
locating in designated markets, would customers who are in need of goods not
chase them there?
I have always maintained that we are contributors to our own
underdevelopment. We seem not to follow any order. Without order, life is
full of unnecessary hassle and, precious time which could have been used for
productive activities is wasted.
Ouch! there is even open disregard towards authority by owners of
unauthorized buildings at undesignated places, which had been served "remove
by order" notices. These buildings are comfortably completed still with some
of the notices appearing in the "dangerous red", and yet our authorities see
nothing wrong. Taxpayers' hard earned money is in turn used to compensate
these owners in the future simply because they were affected by a road
construction.
Moreover, it seems some rules are enforced in only Accra and other big
cities.
For instance, trotros and taxis still pick passengers in excess at Ho
and Keta while it is highly feared in Accra. Does it mean there are no
police in these towns? Soon, the policeman rather may be the overloaded
passenger. You see, the problem about Ghana is not the nonexistence of the
law, but its ineffective enforcement. If noise is made about these, then the
proper thing is done for a few days.
I assigned a weighted disproportionate blame to the law
enforcers. Nonetheless, we are all to blame for the lawlessness Ghana is
degenerating into.
Despite repeated campaigns against indiscipline, the right thing is
never done.
We still throw water sachets around indiscriminately, encourage
overloading, and trade on pavements.
I challenge our leaders to live up to expectation by enforcing rules and
regulations. Above all, we must all help ensure that rules and regulations
are obeyed so as to create a more orderly society. Ghana is our homeland and
only her children can make it a better home. May our help come from God as
we strive to make Ghana a better home.

Original_Search_Title: Entries Close for 20th Maggi National Cooking Contest On June 4
Headline: Nigeria: Entries Close for 20th Maggi National Cooking Contest On June 4
Body_Text:
Participants interested in taking home the star prize of a brand new Honda Civic Exi Inspire 2004 model car plus weekend for two at Nicon Hilton Hotel, Abujo and other exciting mouth-watering prizes on offer for winners in the 20th edition of the Maggi National Cooking Competition have been advised to send in their entries before the closing date of June 4, 2004.
According to NestlÃ©'s Marketing Manager, Mrs. Iquo Ukoh, interested participants should ensure their entries are well written creative recipes outlining clearly how to prepare a 2 course Nigerian menu incorporating cassava or guinea-corn for main course and paw-paw for dessert. She explained that participants must fill out their application forms clearly, attach their well-written recipes and send to the Nestle office closest to them, while those who have difficulty in writing their recipes can contact NestlÃ©'s direct Consumer Services line on 01-7940425 for assistance.
Mrs. Ukoh also explained that the competition is open to all interested members of the public or those who strongly believe that they possess outstanding and creative culinary skills.
As we have done over the years we intend to give all interested contestants of all sexes from different walks of life a fair chance to participate. We have therefore continued to encourage online applications. Already, we have received numerous applications online and interested participants can still obtain entry forms by sending e-mail requests to maggi.contacts@ng.nestle.com. Entry forms obtained from Nestle regional offices, major supermarkets and cut-outs from newspaper ads and magazines will also be accepted", Mrs. Ukoh stated.
Mrs. Ukoh also assured the public that participation in the competition is at no cost to the selected contestants, as all welfare costs including transportation, accommodation, feeding and the cost of ingredients for preparing the various menus will be borne by the company.
She explained that the 20th Maggi National Cooking Competition is an improvement on past competitions, as the first 3,000 entries received will also get a free copy of Maggi's recipe book on 50 Nigerian soups a highly researched compilation on Nigerian dishes.
Participants at the zonal level contests that are scheduled to hold in Kaduna on July 10, and Ilorin on July 17, stand a chance of winning prizes which include a bedroom fridge plus set of pots, 14-inch colour television sets plus 20 piece dinner sets, deep fat fryers plus electric kettles, and consolation prizes of radio sets for the remaining participants.
The star prize for the finals slated for August 7 in Lagos include a brand new Honda Civic Exi Inspire 2004 model car plus weekend for two at Nicon Hilton Hotel, Abuja for the Cook of the Year, a six-burner gas cooker, fridge freezer and microwave oven for the first runner-up, a deep freezer, set of pots and set of chinaware for the second runner-up, and consolation prizes of three loader VCD's plus coke mixer for other contestants.

Original_Search_Title: Why Teso Girls Will Not Stay in School
Headline: Uganda: Why Teso Girls Will Not Stay in School
Body_Text:
Kampala — Jacqueline Atim, 15, a candidate at Oriamo Primary School in Kaberamaido, has a workload of a married woman. She wakes up at 6.00am, digs, fetches water, then cooks food before going to school at 9.00am. She leaves the food on fire and returns to serve at lunchtime, before afternoon classes. As she narrated her ordeal to local leaders and parents who gathered at the school on April 29, Atim broke down and cried and so did woman MP, Kaberamaido, Mary Amajo and several guests.
"I work hard in the morning, I report to school late and I'm punished for it, then I return from school to work till late in the night," she says. "When I fail to accomplish a single task at home in a day, I also get punished."
Janet Amuge, 14, says whenever she tries to revise after dinner, her parents accuse her for finishing the only family paraffin. "Sometimes they grab the candle from me and threaten to beat me," she says
The girls were speaking during a series of meetings organised by the Association for the Re-orientation and Rehabilitation of Teso Women for Development (TERREWODE) in collaboration with the Forum for the Education NGOs in Uganda to mark the Gobal Education Campaign Week in Soroti and Kaberamaido.
Like Atim and Amuge, many rural girls drop out of school or fail to perform well, due to the adult tasks heaped on them by their parents. Others are forced to get married due to poverty, wars and negative cultural attitudes that do not favour girls' education.
In a memorandum, presented to the civil, political, religious and cultural leaders and others including the Arrow boys of Kaberamaido District at Lwala Girls SS, the girls decried early marriages and called for massive sensitisation of parents. Most parents give their daughters many domestic tasks to prepare them for marriage.
"We appreciate the existing laws on marriage and defilement but we want the government to be firmer on the culprits," reads the memorandum.
The girls appealed to the Government to provide free secondary education for all girls. They want the school fenced and a permanent barracks near the school.
The memorandum was compiled by 13 girls that have returned to the school from which the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels abducted 150 girls in June, 2003.
Florence Akullo, LCV counsellor for Alua sub-county, Kaberamaido asserts that most parents, particularly the mothers have surrendered family responsibilities to the girls.
"When the girls assume all the domestic work, they begin to reason and prefer marriage." She advises parents to grow more food and support the education of their daughters.
Amajo pledges to fundraise for girl's education from the USA. "I am also discussing with the Kaberamaido district officials the possibility of hosting highly educated women as role models to inspire girls to return to school.
In separate meetings held in Soroti and Kaberamaido districts during the Global Education Campaign (GEC) week, Teso leaders decried the alarming rate at which girls are falling out of school. They pledged to work closely with the Civil Society Organisations to develop strategies for re-enforcement of the country's existing marriage laws by introducing by-laws. The GEC is an annual event marked in the last week of April.
The leaders resolved to develop a comprehensive education strategy to embrace all categories of child marriage victims. They challenged Teso teachers to renew their commitments to pupils home visitation exercises to encourage parents to return their daughters to school. They called on other development workers to support them to mobilise and sensitise communities on the Children's statute and to ensure psychosocial counselling for the girls who are affected by the war and child marriages.
Bishop Charles Obaikol of Church of Uganda demanded for an explanation for not allowing child mothers from the war ravaged regions of Uganda to return to school. He argued that although girls' education has not been priority in Teso, current low education levels characterised by high absenteeism and eventually dropouts among girls was worsened by the so many wars Teso suffered.
Obaikol who was the chief guest in one of the meetings called for concerted effort by all stakeholders to improve girls' education in the region. "We shall continue to advocate for more girl school dropouts to regain access to education."
It was agreed that registration of couples intending to wed is one way of re-enforcing the existing marriage law on the age of consent for the girls.
The situation is so bad that during insurgencies, some parents give away their daughters to men in exchange for pigs, a garden of cassava or a bicycle.
The 2001 report on Global Campaigns to prevent child marriages by the UN Children's Fund states that child marriages is a global practices which is more prominent in Africa and South Asia. The report isolates poverty as one major factors underpinning child marriages.
"When poverty is acute, a young girl may be regarded as an economic burden and her marriage to an older man is a family survival strategy and may even be seen as in her interest.
"Until now there has virtually been no attempt to examine child marriages as a human violation. Forcing children, especially girls into marriages can be physically and emotionally harmful. It violates their right to personal freedom and growth."
The Soroti District Local Government Plan indicates that women and children form 75% of the district population, with girls being a majority. Girls are less likely to go to school. They drop out sooner as they reach P5. The proportion of boys to girls in secondary schools is only one-half that found at primary level. Literate rates have remained low among female with 49.75% as compared to male 60.59%.
The 2003 UNICEF report says without accelerated action to get more girls into school over the next two years, global goals to reduce poverty and improve the human condition would simply not be reached.

Original_Search_Title: Social Justice: Living with leprosy and its stigma
Headline: Ghana: Social Justice: Living with leprosy and its stigma
Body_Text:
Gladys Adobea lives in a world of outcasts. It is a place where, regardless of how marred their bodies and faces are, they belong.
In the leprosy colony, in the McCarthy hill area of Weija, the people have lost limbs, fingers and toes, but what they suffer from most is the stigma attached to the disease.
"We have deformities," said Adobea, 72. "If you have leprosy, people don't like you to stay in the house. They think they will get it, whether you are cured or not. They are afraid."
It's because of that fear that even though Adobea is cured of the disease, she has lived most of her life, cut off from society, in leprosy colonies.
Leprosy is a disease caused by bacteria that attacks the nerves in the body. Although treatable, leprosy still attacks a few people in Ghana every year. As the bacteria enters the system, people lose sensation in thier arms, legs, even eyes, leading to loss of limbs and blindness. However, if caught early, leprosy is completely curable.
Adobea contracted the disease when she was six years old. Her grandmother dropped her off at a colony in the Volta Region and left.
In 1945 she was moved to a clinic in Labadi where other lepers had formed a settlement.
But in 1950, she was uprooted again.
"The people of Labadi didn't want us there. That place was not built for lepers," she said.
It wasn't until she moved to the settlement near Weija that Adobea finally felt at home.
There are 40 people living in Adobea's colony. All of them have been cured of leprosy. They are different ages and they come from different backgrounds but they are bound by a common link, they have all been shunned by society.
Many of the people in the colony married each other and had children. They prepare meals together and care for each other when they are sick.
Louis Oseih has designated himself the unofficial nurse for the settlement.
Oseih isn't sure how old he is but he thinks he's around 85. He has a kind smile and is missing his legs from the knees down. The ulcers on his knees leak inside stained bandages. He ignores the flies settling on his wounds.
"I don't fear the disease because I have it," he said.
Oseih will care for the others in the colony when even doctors turn their backs on them.
Oseih said a few years ago he took a woman to the hospital with an infected ulcer on her knee. He came back to visit her one day and he said the bandage hadn't been changed.
"The nurse and doctor told her she had to change it herself but she didn't have fingers," he said.
So every day, Oseih would hobble to the hospital and change the bandage himself.
That's the way the settlement operates. They receive little help from the government so the people take care of each other.
According to Father Andrew Campbell, an Irish Catholic priest who has lived in Ghana for 33 years, the government gives each person in the settlement only Â¢1,600 per day to live on.
Father Campbell said that at Christmas time the government announced they would increase the funding to Â¢6,000 per person.
But so far, they have received no additional money.
The people at the settlement make soap and rugs out of scrap pieces of cloth to sell for money.
But mostly, they are dependent on the charity of others. Surviving off charity is difficult when most people are afraid of them.
Father Campbell said that he begs his parish every Sunday for money to give to the lepers. The money is used to purchase bandages, cotton cloth and medication.
Although everyone at the colony is cured, they are still affected by ailments associated with leprosy.
Because their skin has been so damaged, their bodies break out in painful ulcers, said Mary Tandh Ashong, a nurse who takes care of the people in the colony. Many of them also suffer from serious eye problems.
Because of leprosy, Nancy Ayee, a woman who is completely blind - has lost all sensation in her hands.
One day while cooking, she set her hands on fire and didn't realize it. Now she is left with two burnt stumps for hands.
"She peels cassava and cooks and pounds fufu," said Father Campbell. "She doesn't take this as something to sit down and cry over.They don't want to be pitied. They want to be treated as human beings."
Father Campbell is hopeful that one day the stigmas will be broken down and people with leprosy can integrate themselves back into society.

Original_Search_Title: Nestle's Maggi Promotes Cassava, Guinea Corn Production
Headline: Nigeria: Nestle's Maggi Promotes Cassava, Guinea Corn Production
Body_Text:
Nestle Nigeria Plc says it would use the opportunity of this year's
Maggi Cooking Competition to promote the production, consumption of cassava
in the country.
In an address to mark the kick off of the 20th edition of the contest,
the company's Marketing Manager, Mrs. Iquo Ukoh, told journalists in Lagos,
last Thursday that this year's edition will focus on Cassava or Guinea Corn
for the main dish and Pawpaw for the dessert.
According to her, Nestle choice of Cassava is deliberate. "Nigeria is
the world's largest producer of Cassava. We believe that Nigerians need to
know a lot more interesting and delicious ways of preparing Cassava as a
main dish. Cassava is largely grown in Southern region of Nigeria whilst
Guinea Corn is grown in the Northern region," she said.
Explaining how to participate in the competition, Ukoh noted that the
first stage is the written recipe where contestants are expected to send in
their written recipes comprising the name of the dishes, ingredients, the
quantities of ingredients and methodology for preparing the dishes.
Short-listed participants, she said, will then move on to the cooking
contests at the zonal and final levels.
"Entries will be short-listed for a completely 2-course Nigerian menu
i.e. main course and dessert. The zonal cooking competition will take place
in 2 zones i.e. North and South and these will be in Kaduna and Ilorin on
10th and I7th of July, respectively.
The first 3 winners from each of the 2 zones will participate at the
Grand Finale scheduled for Le Meridien Eko Hotel, Lagos, on 7th August,
2004.
On prizes to be won, she disclosed that The 2004 Cook of the Year will
drive home a Honda Civic EX1 Inspire -- 2004 Model car and have the
privilege of spending an exciting weekend at Nicon Hilton Hotel, Abuja, with
his/her spouse, all expenses paid by Maggi. Other contestants, she said will
also go home with mouth-watering prizes both at the zonal and final
competitions. Last year, a Pharmacist, Mr. Oluboro Idowu, who won the
contest, went home with a car. Earlier, his wife, was the 1994 cook of the
year.
It will be recalled that the first Maggi National Cooking Competition
was organized in1982 with the following objectives in mind:
To contribute to the establishment of a Nigerian Food Culture through
dissemination of information about recipes and dishes from different ethnic
groups.
To raise the standard of preparation and service of Nigerian foods to
the international standards
To facilitate the availability of complete Nigerian menus in Hotels and
restaurants throughout Nigeria
To encourage skills and creativity in the culinary arts
To document and distribute information on Nigerian food culture through
the publication of recipe booklets and cookbooks
It will also be recalled that Maggi seasoning was invented by Julius
Maggi, a professional miller, who in 1882 was requested to find a solution
to the industrial problems caused by the industrial revolution which
attracted increasing number of women to factories as a result of which they
no longer had the time to prepare meals. By 1884, exactly 200 years ago, his
first powdered soup was ready for the market heralding the era of "instant"
soup. But Julius Maggi did not stop there.
He continued to invent and his efforts led to the introduction of the
first commercial seasoning called Bouillon Cube pioneering the age of brand
seasonings. Maggi Cubes marketed in Nigeria by Nestle Plc remains not only
the largest selling seasoning in the country but the entire world as well
there is hardly any home where one can not find a maggi cube making it the
second largest brand in Nigeria behind Coa-Cola. Nestle Maggi range of
seasonings, include Maggi Cube, Maggi Chicken, Maggi Crayfish, and Maggi
Maxi Cube.

Original_Search_Title: Nestle Dangles Fantastic Gifts Before Cooks
Headline: Nigeria: Nestle Dangles Fantastic Gifts Before Cooks
Body_Text:
Lagos — FOOD and beverages giant, Nestle Nigeria Plc, is set for the 20th

scheduled for Le Meridien Eko Hotel, Lagos on August 7, 2004.
Marketing manager of Nestle Nigeria Plc, Mrs Iquo Ukoh, who unveiled

Business Champion notes that the zonal cooking competition will take

However, the first three winners from each of the two zones will

Those products include Maggi cube, Maggi chicken, maggi crayfish and

In all, the first 3,000 entries received from prospective participants

Nigerian soups, a highly researched compilation of Nigerian dishes.

Original_Search_Title: Rep Wants FG to Address Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Rep Wants FG to Address Poverty
Body_Text:
Ibadan — Deputy Chairman of the House Committee on Works, Hon. Mufutau Gbadebo
Ayinde has appealed to the Federal Government to give priority to the
implementation of poverty alleviation programmes as contained in this year's
budget.
Ayinde, who gave the advice yesterday while addressing members of his
constituency in Ede, said the only thing that Nigerians will appreciate now
is the implementation of the programmes that can improve the living
conditions of the people, saying the present situation called for urgent
solution from the nation's policy makers.
Ayinde representing Ede, Egbedore and Ejigbo Federal Constituency in
Osun State stated that to alleviate poverty among the people of his
constituency, he has launched a programme tagged "Honourable Mufutau Ayinde
Peoples Empowerment Prog-ramme (HMAP)" which will gulp over N35
million.
According to him, he has encouraged the people in his constituency to
form themselves into cooperative bodies and they will be supplied with
materials such as Keke NAPEP, motorcycles, palm oil mill and palm kernel
mill machines.
Other materials to be supplied are barbing, hairdressing equipment and
cassava processing machines.
"I am convinced that with this equipment, people who are unemployed will
be able to engage themselves in money generating ventures which will
eventually improved their living condition.
Ayinde also disclosed that the poverty alleviation programme will take
off in his constituency this year and will be extended to other parts of the
state in future.
He reaffirmed his belief that if people are gainfully employed, the
crime rate will be drastically reduced in the society, saying "people will
live in peace and achieve progress."
Ayinde also assured the people that he will ensure that his constituency
gets its own share of both the federal and state appointments.

Original_Search_Title: NRM Has No Wage Policy
Headline: Uganda: NRM Has No Wage Policy
Body_Text:
Kampala — In my last piece, a short story, The Man who Defeated Death (April, 11),
the small chamber in which Yeshu was lying after his "resurrection" was
described as "... no more than 10 cm across".
While a spooky "resurrected" Christ could conceivably be squeezed into a
mouse trap-sized room, Magda, who was sitting beside him, couldn't. In my
original description, the room was "... no more than 10 cubits across".
A cubit, an ancient measure, is about 30 centimetres (12 inches)
long. The rest of the story was printed exactly as it was E-mailed, and I
take full responsibility for any other errors therein.
Perhaps we should start with stories from Museveni's bush war days. I
vaguely remember someone giving his account of those days to the media. I
think it was one of the children of the fallen war hero (or victim),
Luttamaguzi, or some other such fellow who had been a youngster at the
time.
According to that account, the then guerrilla leader, Yoweri Museveni,
ate his chicken without the skin.
Of course, because of its high cholesterol content, most diet and health
experts condemn the skin of chicken. Shuttling between his apartment in
Sweden and Luweero's bush war shacks, an enlightened Museveni would know
about this.
But the bush story is interesting because it implies that, even in the
Spartan bush conditions, Museveni enjoyed the luxury to practice his dietary
discrimination.
I don't know whether the birds eaten in the bush were looted, paid for
in cash, or part of the war debt that has burdened Ugandan tax-payers for so
long, but bush accounts suggest that chicken was usually reserved for senior
officers.
So you had the lower fighters eating posho or cassava with beans or
water, the officers having their chicken, and Museveni discarding or tossing
his chicken skins to the kids.
You may question the morality of that system of effort and reward in a
situation where the lives of many small people were directly in the line of
fire, but the system was clearer than any pretence, that the NRM might have
presented as a wage policy since 1986.
I am sometimes amazed that President Museveni and all those professors
around him do not seem to recognize that wages are supposed to be part of a
total concept of state management.
Most times, NRM approaches have been indifferent to the reality in
people's households. Otherwise they have been inconsistent appeasement
reactions to agitation and strike action in various sectors.
Consider the following examples of the NRM vision:
There was a time when science teachers in schools were paid more than
arts teachers. Then, seeing the resentment and damage that ensued, school
administrators used their PTA funds (in reality additional school fees) to
address the imbalance. But some schools, especially in rural areas, had no
PTA funds to speak of. Eventually, the amorphous scheme was abandoned.
But the NRM has not been cured of "top-up" theory. You would get dizzy
if you tried to catalogue the top-up allowances our finance ministers have
laid out over the last seventeen years; sometimes in their budget speeches,
and sometimes stealthily.
Whenever the NRM government is in a fix and can't pay everyone a
reasonable wage, it concocts an allowance for the most agitated group,
sometimes a sub-group.
The Makerere University private students' scheme has been mainly driven
by top-up theory. Now it seems that even this scheme cannot finance the
greed and legitimate demands made by lecturers and other workers at the
university. The dons have been on strike.
Cost-sharing in government hospitals, an on and off policy subject to
political expediency, is also partly related to top-up theory. But this has
not stopped the underpaid medical workers from threatening to down their
tools again.
There is a variant of the top-up idea that comes in the form of
exemptions. That is, someone not having to pay fees, taxes etc. Its most
conspicuous expression has been the sometimes-contested rule relieving
judges from income tax obligations.
But then why not increase their salaries and retain the principle that
everybody - especially judges as an example - should pay their taxes? Well,
presumably because increasing their already handsome untaxed 3 million
shillings to, say, a taxable 5 million would look too "unfair" in a sector
where many others are below the half-million mark.
Better to hide the top up as a tax exemption.
It is possible that president Museveni gets a huge feeling of
gratification when petitioned to address wage-related agitation; the feeling
that all these angry people acknowledge that they are hostage to his
power.
Unfortunately, however, the hotchpotch scenario, the crisis-driven
"solutions" and unfulfilled promises from the president are testimony of a
severely defective wage policy.
At bottom, of course, is the truth that the government does not have
enough money, which can be explained by an average 16 year-old school
boy. In short, state mismanagement.
This week, the president told Makerere dons to "stop this business of
striking because your country is fragile." Hopefully, he returned to his
political officials and told them that they must stop this business of war,
corruption and reckless wastefulness, because their country was
fragile.
If he didn't, the best the country's workers can expect from the present
NRM administration is a more honest application of the chicken morality of
the bush war days.

Original_Search_Title: Great Lakes: Displaced in Bujumbura
Headline: Central Africa: Great Lakes: Displaced in Bujumbura
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — The family hut resembles the hundreds of others in Nyarumanga, a
settlement in the Buterere suburb of the capital, Bujumbura. The site hosts
314 families, including 187 households of Batwa, otherwise known as
"pygmies", who had fled fighting in Bujumbura Rural, Bubanza and Cibitoke
provinces and were given some land in Nyamuranga on which to build
themselves huts.
Nyarumanga is surrounded by lush rice fields, but residents do not own
land. Moreover, because they have little formal education, they cannot
access jobs easily in Bujumbura.
Unprepared for city life, pygmies in Nyarumanga have had difficulty
adjusting to their new environment. Monique Sinzobakwira, who is too old to
remember her age, said she used to make clay pots, a major occupation among
the Batwa, before moving to the city, where she has nothing to do.
"I can't get clay here, and even if I could, customers are getting
rare," she said. So, she scavenges in Buterere's refuse dump to eke out a
living. "Sometimes I find charcoal and sell it at 20 or 50 [Burundi]
francs," she said. "If I'm lucky, I make 500 francs [less than US $1] per
day, then I can buy two kilos of cassava flour and ndagala [small fish found
in Lake Tanganyika]."
The more able-bodied Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) pick up petty
jobs or work in nearby rice fields for 200 francs per day. Others just beg
in the city centre.
Aid from NGOs
Despite Sinzobakwira's condition, which mirrors those of hundreds of
other IDPs, Vital Bambanze, the secretary-general of the Union for the
Promotion of the Batwa, said Nyarumanga's residents fared better than other
Batwa in the country, because several organisations were providing them with
food and non-food items.
CARE Burundi has built homes for 80 of the Batwa families. A social
worker with CARE Burundi, Jacqueline Ntahompagaze, said the NGO had also
initiated health education services for pregnant women, and mosquito
bed-nets for under-five children. But, she noted, many recipients often sold
the nets a few days after receiving them.
Jesuit Refugee Services also provides Nyarumanga residents medical care
and school materials, and the government has backed that action by exempting
Batwa children from school fees as it does for other destitute
children. Although grateful for such help, some Batwa say such long-term aid
could create dependency among members of their community and discourage
individuals from trying to improve their lot. "One of these days, the
assistance will stop and Batwa will resort to begging," Bambanze said.
Landlessness a problem
The only way out of the ostracism and lowly status to which they are
subjected in society, he said, would be for the Batwa to be given lan. This
would open the way to education, which Bambanze described as the key to
development. He said some 90 percent of Batwa did not own land, thus
exposing them to all kinds of exploitation, including something akin to
serfdom. "In Mukike and Mugongo-Manga, [two communes of Bujumbura Rural],
building a hut on somebody's property entitles the owner to demand three or
four days of unpaid labour per week. This is slavery," Bambanze said.
Like many residents of the site, Sinzobakwira used to live on someone's
land at Mubone in Bujumbura Rural, where her entire family were serfs. But
now, even though she lives in the city and owns no land, she prefers her
present condition, because she lives free and without the constant threat of
eviction.
Serfdom, which affected all of Burundi's landless, irrespective of
ethnicity, was outlawed in 1976 by the then president, Jean-Baptiste
Bagaza. Despite this measure, however, the Batwa say they have not seen any
benefits. Their lack of land ownership still makes them feel like slaves in
their relationships with landowners.
But the vice-president of the National Committee for the Rehabilitation
of Displaced Persons and Refugees, Joseph Nzeyimana, said if Nyarumanga
residents were found to be genuine war victims they would be given land like
other war-affected persons. This, however, excludes some other Batwa who
have not been so displaced but are nevertheless landless and, like other
Burundians, aspire to owning a piece of turf.
Batwa in Burundi are estimated to number between 50,000 and 60,000.

Original_Search_Title: Plea for Aid for War-Displaced in Kabezi
Headline: Burundi: Plea for Aid for War-Displaced in Kabezi
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Up to 25,000 civilians who were displaced following days of fighting
between rebels and the Burundian army in the southeastern commune of Kabezi,
Bujumbura Rural Province, need urgent relief aid, a local official told IRIN
on Tuesday.
"We are appealing humanitarian agencies to come to their rescue,"
Felicien Ntahombaye, the administrator of Kabezi Commune, said.
He said the displaced had sought refuge at Kabezi Primary School, at the
Kabezi Health Centre and at the Kabezi Market.
"They fled violent fighting that took place on Wednesday and Friday on
the hills of Masama, Gitenga and Kivomo," he said. "The children have begun
to show signs of malnutrition, we also fear an outbreak of diarrhoea due to
the poor hygiene conditions."
The fighting was between the army and Forces nationales de liberation
(FNL) rebels loyal to Agathon Rwasa. The faction remains the only rebel
group in the country that has refused to sign a ceasefire agreement with the
transitional government.
Ntahombaye said that there were only small quantities of cassava and
tomatoes at the market. "Food here is insufficient, some people have now
resorted to fishing for Ndagala [small fish]," he said
The commune borders Lake Tanganyika.
Ntahombaye said he had contacted army authorities to see if the
displaced could helped to return to their homes. "On some hills the
situation has returned to normal, the important thing to do now is to tell
people to go back home to cultivate their fields as they wait for
humanitarian assistance," he added.

Original_Search_Title: UN Official Visits Burundi As Thousands of Refugees Return From Tanzania
Headline: Burundi: UN Official Visits Burundi As Thousands of Refugees Return From Tanzania
Body_Text:
A senior United Nations official is touring Burundi to speak to
government officials and examine the situation of the tens of thousands
refugees returning home from Tanzania, the Office of the High Commissioner
for Refugees (UNHCR) said today.
Assistant High Commissioner Kamel Morjane, who arrived yesterday, was
scheduled to meet President Domitien Ndayizeye today.
He was going tomorrow to Ruyigi province, an area where many thousands
of returnees per week are pouring through border crossing-points at Gisuru,
Kobero and Gahumo. More than 27,400 Burundian refugees have returned home
from Tanzania this year through the three crossing-points, 2,400 of them per
week through Gisuru, which was opened in late January, according to
UNHCR.
The agency is using a fleet of 43 heavy vehicles to take the returning
refugees to their final destinations.
Meanwhile, UNHCR has also been working with 9,000 refugees from the
eastern region of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) living in
Cishemeye temporary refugee camp, in the hills of north-western Burundi, to
find creative ways of comforting rape victims and training men and women to
eliminate rape.
One innovative approach involved the appointment of 72 older refugee
women as "mÃ¨res volontaires," or volunteer mothers, who help identify and
provide special care to rape victims.
The commission has also trained a group of young male and female
community assistants to teach others how to eradicate sexual and
gender-based violence and the spread of HIV/AIDS and sexually transmitted
diseases.
The mÃ¨res volontaires and the community assistants have already reduced
the number of rapes by convincing young women not to make unnecessary trips
outside the camp to pluck cassava leaves for traditional cooking, gather
firewood or grind grain.
UNHCR responded to local recommendations by arranging deliveries of
smaller pieces of wood and installing grain mills in the camps, said Leonie
Nyakageni, a community service worker.

Original_Search_Title: Interview With Aids Activist Milly Katana
Headline: Africa: Interview With Aids Activist Milly Katana
Body_Text:
Maputo —  Milly Katana is a members of the Commission on HIV/AIDS and Governance
in Africa, which was established last year by UN Secretary-General Kofi
Annan. A long-standing AIDS activist and founder of the Pan African
Treatment Access Movement, she spoke to PlusNews on the need for accelerated
treatment programmes.
QUESTION: A whole raft of treatment initiatives is underway, but they
seem to be quite small scale. Are there significant challenges in the
scaling-up that concern you?
ANSWER: I think, as far as HIV is concerned, we have very little
evidence that small pilot programmes scale up to massive activities. That's
why we're advocating for government to get involved, because in Africa it's
only governments that have the capacity to do things [nationally]. The NGOs
have good intentions, but their capacity just cannot match the needs; day by
day the scale of HIV is bursting the banks. A country like Botswana is
hitting [an infection rate of] 40 percent, South Africa 25 percent. The
magnitude is so huge, we should no longer be thinking of pilot, small-scale
programmes; we must be starting large-scale initiatives that are intended to
reach all the population.
Remember, the way HIV spreads, if you arrest it in one place and don't
arrest it in the other, in a matter of a few days we'll have
cross-infections from one community to another. So, in a nutshell, we
[should] no longer [be] trying things at this point in time; we know what
works; we know what doesn't work. When you talk about treatment, we know
that generic drugs work - cheap and simple regimens - just give them to the
people."
Q: And yet, it's not happening - why?
A: It's not happening. I think we have many reasons: one of them is the
different priorities between the different stakeholders; between the donors
and the governments on the one hand, and also within governments. Our
African governments have completely failed the people; they have failed to
set the right priorities. Somebody said that in Africa we spend billions
each year on weapons, but we cannot buy ARVs, which are now costing 50 US
cents.
Q: The argument of a lack of capacity - is that a bit of red herring?
A: No, that's very genuine, but we cannot just moan about it; we have a
problem with capacity - try and fix it and move on.
Q: What do we need to do to push the treatment process forward?
A: I think, first of all, we need to take a stand as African people, as
leaders of African people. We need to decide, and get our choices right. Do
we want to save the lives of our people, or play games at the expense of our
people? If our stand is to save our people from HIV and AIDS, then we must
say, 'we shall do whatever it takes'. If it means sweet-talking the donors
into putting money into sustainable programmes ... then we can do that.
Q: Are you sometimes upset or disillusioned that much of the pressure
for treatment seems to come from outside? As African people we don't seem to
be demanding treatment as a right and using it as a political weapon, except
for, perhaps, in South Africa?
A: I think we just have different ways of working. In my country
[Uganda], if I went to the streets with my friends living with HIV, we can
be shot at, and I don't want to take that responsibility. In South Africa
it's different. People can go and toyi-toyi: a few of them maybe are put in
police cells, they sign papers and they're released. We do things
differently. Not that the voices are not there; the voices are very much
there, [but] in some of our countries we don't have access to media, but the
voices are there. People are desperate - they know treatment is there;
treatment can save lives, and they're waiting to be treated.
Q: Obviously, gender is a vital, crosscutting issue. Are there special
steps that should be taken that helps empower women?
A: For sure! We all know HIV in Africa wears a woman's face, and if we
are to overcome HIV we must address the challenges that face women. If we're
talking about prevention, we must empower women. If we're talking about
treatment and care, we must empower women and children. We are seeing, now,
substantial numbers of children being born to mothers living with HIV, and
therefore have HIV.
To me, the magic bullet here is providing free access to care,
prevention and treatment. Then the ... social issues - we can go slowly and
mop them up. Of course, there are some men who won't allow their women to go
to clinics because they've not authorised them; some women don't have
transport money to get on a bus to go to a health centre. But if they know
that 'where I am going to go the treatment is free', then they can do
whatever they can: sell their cassava; sell their millet, to get the money
to get to the clinic.

Original_Search_Title: Akwa Ibom Floats N6bn Bond
Headline: Nigeria: Akwa Ibom Floats N6bn Bond
Body_Text:
Lagos — AKWA IBOM State Government is bidding for N6 billion fresh funds through
the issue of a Floating Rate Redeemable Revenue Bond.
The amount represents the first tranche of a proposed N12 billion
multiple tranche bond issue that is being planned by the state.
Government Victor Attah, announced this yesterday, to capital market
operators at the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE).
He said the proceeds of the Bond will be applied to financing of
infrastructural facilities critical to the accelerated economic
development.
Specific projects tied to the Bond proceeds include the rehabilitation
and dualisation of Abak and Eron roads in Uyo capital city, development of
Housing estate in Uyo, and an industrial estate.
Obong Attah said the bond which has a three-year tenor to mature in
2007, had been given an 'A' rating by Global Credit Rating Company, an
international credit rating firm.
Features of the bond include a secured direct irrevocable obligation of
Awka Ibom State Government charged upon and payable out of the consolidated
revenue fund of the state statutory allocation with the Federal
Government.
The bond bears a common rate of 4.5 per cent above the CBN minimum
Rediscount Rate (MRR) per annum with a cap of 23.5 per cent and a floor of
19.5 per cent.
The bond is further secured by a sinking fund supported by an
Irrevocable Standing Payment Order (ISPO) from the states statutory
allocation.
Interest earnings on the bond, also, enjoy tax exemption.
Obong Attah said state as a key oil producer and a major producer of oil
palm and cassava is in a very strong position to justify the confidence of
investors.
This confidence is further boosted by the recent abrogation of the
onshore/offshore dichotomy and the very low debt profile of the state, he
said.
With this development, Akwa Ibom becomes the seventh state to access the
capital market for funds within the last five years.
Others were Delta State with N4 billion bond, Edo State N5 billion,
Ekiti State N4.0 billion, Yobe State, Lagos State N15.0 billion, while Abia
State and Cross River states with N3.0 billion and N4.6 billion respectively
have reached advanced stages in their arrangements.

Original_Search_Title: Rebels Kill Four in Katakwi
Headline: Uganda: Rebels Kill Four in Katakwi
Body_Text:
Kampala — REBELS of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) on Monday sneaked into
Katakwi district and killed four people at Odilil in Orungo sub-county.
The Arrow Group militia coordinator, Musa Ecweru, said LRA remnants
tried to infiltrate the Teso region through Omoro sub-county in the southern
part of Lira district.
"A small group attempted to sneak back into Katakwi. They are an
insignificant group that we shall decisively deal with," Ecweru said.
He said the group was trying to instill fear in the displaced people who
were returning to their homes.
Local leaders in Obalanga said this was the second attack by the rebels
in two weeks.
Obalanga LC3 chairperson, John Patrick Erau, said thugs suspected to be
LRA rebels killed two people at Oringoi village, Alito parish on March
3.
He said the dead were Gerepasio Oluga and Elobu son of Opio.
"They got these people in their gardens uprooting cassava. Some of them
escaped from the rebels but the two were killed," Erau said.
The UPDF 3rd Division commander, Lt. Col. Silver Kayemba, said, "We are
yet to establish the killers."
The 3rd division chief of intelligence, Lt. Col. Stephen Oluka, said
facts about such incidents were often distorted by civilians.
"Intelligence on rebels has been distorted by rumours. It is becoming
difficult to separate right from wrong because the public does not want to
volunteer information."

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Clear Retirees' Benefits
Headline: Nigeria: Govt to Clear Retirees' Benefits
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt, Rivers — Rivers State government has directed that all the outstanding benefits
owed retired civil servants in the state should be paid within six months
from now.
The state government took the decision to clear allowances and benefits
of the state retirees at the end of the state executive meeting held within
the week.
A spokesman for the state government Hon. Magnus Abe while briefing
newsmen on the state exco resolution at the end of the meeting said
"government was worried about the plight of the retired civil servants who
had served the state diligently."
Besides the clearance of the retirees allowances, state government also
resolve that henceforth, civl servants in the state "who retired at the end
of their service period would have their pensions and gratitudes paid to
them within two months of retirements.
"We want to use this opportunity to appeal to all those involved in the
process to see that the council's directive is carried out as efficiently as
possible, so that we no longer have a situation where our retirees after
serving government faithfully for many years go into the streets as
destitute on retirements."
Abe who in the state commissioner for information, disclosed that the
state has set aside about $11 million for the development of cassava in the
state.

Original_Search_Title: Inside the UPDF Mamba
Headline: Uganda: Inside the UPDF Mamba
Body_Text:
Kampala — It started like any other Friday but turned out like no other day
before. For me, it was a day of firsts that will last long in my memory. On
March 5, at 5.49pm, I saw for the first time ever the body of an LRA rebel
killed by the UPDF. Burnt black and bullet-riddled, the body was of a small
boy - about 12 years old.
As I lay on the undersize bed in my smelly lodge room at 1.49 am, that
boy's piteous body came back to haunt me.
I struggled between resting my tired, beaten body and thinking about
what I had seen. The thrills of travel are said to be in the journey rather
than in the destination. But this gruelling journey to the frontline said a
lot about those who kill and, or get killed in northern Uganda.
The four of us, journalists from Kampala and Bombo, flew from Entebbe
Air base aboard a UPDF chopper or Surambaya as Maj. Shaban Bantariza prefers
to call it. On board were several boxes, whose colour I cannot reveal for
security reasons.
The Mamba in action
An MP once accused the chopper of ferrying obushera from Rwakitura to
President Museveni's war camps in the north. But these wooden boxes just
didn't look like they contained obushera. By 2 p.m. we were in Lira
barracks, having picked up Army Commander Maj. Gen. Aronda Nyakairima from
Soroti.
Minutes later, I was boarding the famous "Mamba" - that armoured car
that Kony rebels reportedly fear more than Kony himself. At first, I was
baffled by the simplicity of the Mamba. It looked like a Land Rover, save
for the three soldiers manning the gun erected atop.
The youthful driver, in a blue vest, was sweating even before we started
and continued to sweat with worrying ease throughout the journey.
Despite the slow speed, the mood was good, with the soldiers once in a
while chatting in their Swahili. Being the only civilian in nearly a dozen
people, I was a bit lonely. Both my feet rested on AK47 rifles and my mind
went to those reported cases when a gun "accidentally" went off.
At one point we met a convoy of UC (President's office) vehicles led by
a minesweeper - which looks like a grader. Sitting right behind the
co-driver, I felt a little protected - at least from land mines.
But I still feared that an LRA sniper could easily shoot at the glass
window and by the time the guys above rained bullets in the direction of the
sniper, some one could be dead, and that person could as well be
myself.
I particularly felt that vulnerability as we passed Abia, where the LRA
killed 51 people only last month. After about two hours, we reached Orum
trading centre in Otuke County, where soldiers behind me burst out
laughing. One of them called out my name and said "Omara Atubo's
house".
I later learnt that the soldiers expected the MP's country home to be
bigger and perhaps more fancy. At Orum, we changed into another Mamba where
I sat with two other media colleagues.
The journey into the real jungle now started. For 45 minutes, with the
mamba moving at the speed of a snail on a village road, we did not meet a
single person or see a home.
Since we were at the head of the armoured convoy, often our driver had
to stop and communicate by radio to get directions. Then we branched off the
security road and into the bush.
The driver closed the roof, just as I was looking at the branch of a
thick tree and wondering why an LRA rebel could not haul a grenade into our
Mamba. Perhaps the driver too was aware of that possibility.
But the Mamba ploughed its way through the bush, dodging anthills and
flattening one short tree after another. Now and again we stopped, as if
lost in the jungle. There was nothing but trees and thickets.
"Mamba one " the driver would say, holding the radio. The other party
would say something like "mbele" meaning forward. Just to be sure, the
driver, sweating without a shirt would say, "Say again".
Soon, a UPDF jet on reconnaissance appeared in the skies. Perhaps the
rebels are nearby, I thought. Why else would the jet be in this particular
area? It was routine check.
After seeing some of the bodies of rebels killed at Gotojwang, at the
border between Lira and Pader, and after talking to the rescued children and
chest-thumping officers, it was time to leave. But not before seeing those
horrific decomposing bodies of boys who were abducted the other day, given
guns and turned into fighters. There was also time to hear UPDF soldiers
complain of hunger, time to see a frontline soldier drawing black water from
a stagnant stream.
Under such conditions, you had to understand why the soldiers were
ecstatic for having killed so many rebels.
The SPG-9, one of the guns captured from the rebels is the smallest
weapon that can destroy or significantly damage the mighty Mamba, the driver
told us. The AK47 can only cause a scratch on the glass window, while the
RPG may occasion a minor crack.
The journey back was longer and harder. The Mamba is very
uncomfortable. Sometimes you want to lean against the body of the vehicle
and doze off, but each pothole you fell into something pinched you. So you
try to sit without leaning against anything.
Soon the bottoms were hurting and you wished you could stand instead. At
one point, passing a under a mango tree, the driver stopped and the soldiers
ate some raw mangoes, throwing some inside for us to eat.
A part from some roasted cassava bought from Adwari Church on the way to
the scene of the fighting, these mangoes were all we ate.
The soldiers, we were told, are used to one meal a day. It would be
11.30 p.m. before we got to Lira town, beaten and hungry, but safe inside
the Mamba. May be we were always safe anyway, as long as the rebels didn't
have the SPG-9.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Export Will Diversify Revenue Base - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Export Will Diversify Revenue Base - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday said that Nigeria's revenue base
would be diversified through the export of cassava and its by-products.
Speaking with stakeholders on the promotion of cassava export at the
State House, he stated that because of this, the Federal Government, would
rigorously encourage the export of cassava in various forms.
He said that Nigeria being the largest producer of cassava in the world,
must now concentrate on exporting the product to derive maximum benefits,
adding that a number of African countries had shown interest in processed
cassava from Nigeria.
"Whatever it takes to do this, must be done," the President noted,
stressing that quality, standards, good packaging and presentation were
crucial to the success of the exports.

Original_Search_Title: Undue Delay in the Payment of Export Refund
Headline: Ghana: Undue Delay in the Payment of Export Refund
Body_Text:
KINGSLEY AYIMADU — Emphasis has been placed on the need to solemnly promote and foster the
exportation of non-traditional products on account of the government's
realization that the earnings from our major traditional commodities, for
example Cocoa, Gold, Timber etc alone could not solve the over all economic
problems of the country.
Incentive packages have therefore been introduced by the government to
encourage the promotion and enhancement of the non-traditional export
sector.
It is against this background that the government has launched the PSI
on oil palm production, garment, salt, and cassava.
Pursuant to VAT Act, article (546 section 25), a refund should be paid
to a claimant within 30 days after the submission of all the relevant export
documentations.
But from the look of things, what is happening at the VAT Service
regarding the payment of export refund is not in accordance with the
dictates of the VAT law.
It seems the commissioner is using his prerogative powers to deviate
from the law, to the detriment of the non-traditional exporters and the
economy at large.
The point is that, despite thorough examination and scrutiny of all the
relevant documentations by the personnel of the service, the process could
take as long as three months, (complete 90 days) before one gets his or her
refund.
The most embarrassing and worrisome situation is that, after everything
had been done and the relevant cheques issued and forwarded to the
commissioner's office for his signatory, he subjects it to dilatory (or in
other words, sits on it) for almost one month before he appends his
signature to it.
We must say that the whole process is characterized by undue delay,
therefore, something must be done about it quickly otherwise at the end of
the day, the whole effort of government to expand the capacity of our
non-traditional export base would not achieve the expected result.
To this end, we wish to say that the situation, however, does not only
adversely affect the exporter's purchasing power but provides a disincentive
to the non-traditional export business as well, while undermining the golden
age of business programme and further more inhibiting the growth and
promotion of the non-traditional export sector.
We hope the authorities will take a very serious view of this
situation.

Original_Search_Title: OPS Faults FG on Investment Environment
Headline: Nigeria: OPS Faults FG on Investment Environment
Body_Text:
Organised Private Sector (OPS) says the Federal Government offers only
lip service in its claims of providing a conducive investment and trade
environment for the private sector noting that government disregards
international treaties which retards growth of investment and trade in
Nigeria.
The OPS at a workshop on Multilateral Trading System (MTS) organised
jointly by the International Trade Centre (ITC) and the Lagos Chamber of
Commerce and Industry (LCCI) in Lagos accused the Federal Government of not
honouring international agreements.
A representative from the manufacturing sector said that the free trade
among West African countries which Nigeria is a signatory to, is abused by
the government. According to him, his company followed due processes when it
wanted to export its products to Cote d'Ivoire which it did successfully but
when it wanted to import from the country, the goods were held at the
Nigerian port by men of the customs. On inquiry, customs told his company
that it has no right to import. An action described as contrary to the free
trade agreement.
The free trade relationship is a two-way thing, there is no way a
country continues to import from you and you do not import from the country,
they said. Nigeria's attitude to the agreement is unfair, and it stifles
growth of trade .
They cited an example where Nigerian businessmen smuggle garri (cassava
flour) to Ghana, repackage it well for export to United States and Europe
because the tax or levies placed on such commodities before export is
enormous. Therefore, these businessmen find it cheaper to take it to Ghana
and follow the export procedures which they said is more profitable..
OPS called for a review of operations of both immigration and customs
department if government sincerely wants to grow trade and investment in
Nigeria. They also observed Nigeria's poor international communication as
another impediment to growth of investment in Nigeria. A member of the OPS
pointed out that Nigerian embassy in London does not have an e-mail box
.
They said that over-interference of government in the nation's export
and import businesses is excessive and has made the country to be thinking
globally and not doing anything locally.
Meanwhile, some of the major challenges confronting international trade
in Nigeria were identified as follows: Weakness in National business
coordination,
*How to enhance competitiveness through industry sector networking, and
*How to create institutional structures to support and coordinate
efforts to improve national industry competitiveness.
However, the OPS noted that it has a united approach of talking to
government to develop profitably the international trade particularly for
the private sector while it asked government to review the bureaucracy,
taxes and levies on export and import activities. This they say account for
why most Nigerians choose to go to neighbouring countries to carry out their
import and export businesses, as a result government is losing money.

Original_Search_Title: Okpe Group Gives Fresh Conditions for Peace in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Okpe Group Gives Fresh Conditions for Peace in Delta
Body_Text:
Sapele — Okpe Progressive Movement (OPM) a Pan-Okpe socio-political and cultural
movement and the Okpe communities of Ugbukrusu and Ugborhen which were
recently attacked by armed Itsekiri militants have given Okpe Chiefs and the
Delta state government fresh conditions for peace in the state.
In a joint communique following a damage evaluation tour of the affected
communities, the OPM and the two Okpe Communities reviewed the 21-day
ultimatum given to the Delta State government and the Itsekiri by OPM to
produce and prosecute the Itsekiri militants involved in the attack on the
Okpe communities and recent efforts by some Itsekiri and Okpe Chiefs to
broker peace and noted that certain conditions must be given for any peace
to endure.
They tasked the joint peace committee led by Chief Gbinije for Okpe and
Chief Mabiaku for the Itsekiri to ensure that the masterminds of the Okpe
invasion identified by the concerned Itsekiri in Warri in their letter which
was sent to Governor James Ibori, the Speaker Delta State House of Assembly,
the SSS and the Commissioner of Police were immediately brought to
book.
Fresh conditions
"We demand that the indicted senior Delta State functionary,
Commissioner, junior federal minister and one-time transition committee
chairman in one of the Warri local government councils identified by the
concerned Itsekiri in Warri to have sponsored the invasion of Okpe be
immediately relieved of their official positions and be made to face the
wrath of the law," they said.
For bloodbath to be averted between the entire Okpe and Itsekiri in Okpe
Kingdom, they asked the state government to fulfill the following conditions
in the next few days as stipulated in the various letters by the communities
sent to the state government through their chiefs:
*Rebuild or pay appropriate monetary compensation to owners of the
eleven (11) houses razed down at Ugborhen town, and replace the NEPA
transformer in the community destroyed by the invading Itsekiri.
*Liaise with elders of the community to appease the respected Okpe
deities of Ovho Shrine, Orha meeting, Egbe and Ogiedi shrines.
*Pay appropriate monetary compensation to owners of the fourteen (14)
houses burnt down at Ikeresan town.
*Rebuild all burnt houses, or pay appropriate monetary compensation to
owners of the 69 houses razed by the Itsekiri at Ugbukurusu.
*Replace all government and community properties destroyed during the
invasion. These include the former town hall, cassava processing factory,
transformer, Abazare primary school and the 10,000 gallons of water tank and
tower.

Original_Search_Title: UPDF Kills Rebels
Headline: Uganda: UPDF Kills Rebels
Body_Text:
Kampala — The army on Friday killed three Lord's Resistance Army rebels and
captured another in a battle at the Pader-Lira border.
"The group was part of those that committed the carnage in Abia camp," 2
nd Lt. Chris Magezi, the UPDF's fifth Division publicist told Sunday Monitor
by phone yesterday.
Magezi said the 81st battalion, under the 401 Brigade, killed the rebels
after a battle at the confluence of Agago and Oroma rivers, about 40
kilometres from Lira town.
Magezi said that after the rebels committed the gruesome attack in which
they killed 47 people. They were however pursued by the army.
He said the army recovered two SMG guns, two magazines and foodstuffs -
including six sacks of cassava.
"The rest fled in disarray, but we are pursuing them," he said. Magezi
said that UPDF's India battalion, under the 503 Brigade in Kitgum, also
rescued 15 abductees. He added that many areas in the north were now
calm.

Original_Search_Title: Five Lagos School Boys to Die for Murder
Headline: Nigeria: Five Lagos School Boys to Die for Murder
Body_Text:
LAGOS — JUSTICE James Oduneye of an Ikeja High Court has sentenced to death five
Lagos State secondary school students for killing a businessman, Mr Daniel
Obi on February 1, 1995 in Ajegunle area of the state. The convicts are
Williams Owodo, Ikechukwu Nwaogu, Aniefiok Ukpa, Tony Ataloye and Amaike
Doripolo. All of them are to die by hanging. As at the time they committed
the offence eight years ago, four of them were SS1 students in different
secondary schools in Lagos State while the fifth convict was a final year
student awaiting his SSCE results.
Williams was attending Creek High School, Ajegunle at the time while
Aniefiok was of Oluwa Memorial Secondary School, Lagos. Tony, another
convict was a student of the Gaskiya College, Ijora Badiya while Amaike was
a registered student of Ojora Memorial Secondary School, Tolu, Lagos at the
time when the long hands of law caught up with them. Lagos State government,
seven years ago, charged all the five before an Ikeja High Court on just one
count charge of murder under section 319 of the criminal code.
Lagos State justice ministry which prosecuted the case called four
witnesses during trial and tendered five exhibits while the five accused
persons took turn to defend themselves in the case. The case of the
prosecution was that all the accused persons gathered together gambling on a
street in Ajegunle on the day the deceased was killed by the convicts. While
the said gambling exercise was on-going, the accused persons were said to
have placed dried cassava on the road as bait to pounce on anybody that
stepped on it. The deceased, Daniel, was passing by at the time the trap was
set. He was said to be carrying a black polythene bag which the accused
persons suspected to contain money.
In an attempt to dispossess him of the money, the accused persons
swooped on him but he resisted them. His boldness enraged one of them, Tony,
who reached for a broken bottle and stabbed him while another hit him with a
lamp. He died of the serious injury he sustained from the bottle
cuts. Defending themselves, each of the accused persons confirmed their
signatures on their confessional statements made to the police and tendered
in court but alleged that they did not author the statements. Their argument
was that the police made it for them and forced them with gun to append
their signatures on them.
Besides, they all claimed that they knew nothing about the murder,
saying they were raided by plain-clothed detectives who, days later,
confronted them with murder charge. Delivering judgment in the case, Justice
Oduneye held that for a charge of murder to be established, the prosecution
had to prove the following ingredients, including that the deceased died;
that the death of the deceased had resulted from the acts of the accused and
that the act of the accused was intentional with knowledge that death or
grievously bodily harm was its probable consequence.
The judge held that it was not in doubt that the deceased died. He also
held that the two other ingredients were established by the prosecution
against the five in that all of them gathered with a common intention to
commit the unlawful act.
Resolving two fundamental issues raised by the convicts bordering on a
case of alibi and obtaining indicting information from them under duress,
the judge held that the convicts raised the issue of alibi for the first
time when they got to court, indicating that it was an after-thought. He was
of the belief that the case of alibi ought to be raised earlier, especially
at the police station rather than court.
On the issue of obtaining indicting information from them forcefully,
the judge held "the practice in trial courts is for an accused person who
denies the voluntariness of his extra-judicial statement made to the police
to object to the statement when the prosecution seeks to tender it in
evidence.
"When this is done at that stage, the court proceeds to test its
voluntariness by conducting a trial within trial on the admissibility of the
statement and the onus is on the prosecution to prove that it was free and
voluntary and it is the prosecution who should begin.
"In the instant case, their statements were properly admitted in
evidence as its voluntariness was not challenged at the appropriate
time. There was no irregularity in its admission as confessional statement
by the trial court," he added.
Pronouncing on the matter, Justice Oduneye held: "I am therefore of the
view that Tony is guilty of the charge of murder of Daniel Obi. The other
four accused are also guilty of the charge of murder of Daniel Obi as they
are caught by section 8 of the criminal code. They had a common purpose to
prosecute an unlawful act.
"I therefore sentence all the five accused persons to death by
hanging. May the good Lord have mercy on their souls," he held.

Original_Search_Title: Akunyili: Prime Suspect Escapes to London
Headline: Nigeria: Akunyili: Prime Suspect Escapes to London
Body_Text:
Lagos — The prime suspect in the attempted assassination of the Director -
General of the National Agency for Food, Drug Administra-tion and Control,
(NAFDAC), Dr. (Mrs) Dora Akunyili, has escaped to London.
The suspect, a wealthy Onitsha-based businessman dealing in the sales of
drugs (name withheld), was said to have been fingered by Akunyili based on
several verbal threats he allegedly issued to her to steer off his business
or risk imminent death.
THISDAY checks reveal that Akunyili had reported the suspect to the
police prior to the attack but nothing apparently was done. But following
the attempt on her life, security men swooped on his residence only to meet
his wife who said he had gone out.
The next day, when police came back to the residence, the whole family
had been evacuated while the man reportedly sighted in town that day is now
said to be in London.
THISDAY checks further revealed that at the instance of President
Olusegun Obasanjo, the Inspector General of Police, Mr. Tafa Balogun is said
to be making efforts to get Interpol's help in the arrest of the suspect and
subsequent extratition to Nigeria for tiral.
Speaking to THISDAY in a telephone chat on the extent of investigation
on the matter by the police, force spokesman, Mr. Chris Olakpe said: "We
don't want distractions. Investigation is going on right now. On the
whereabouts of the prime suspect, he said: "I am not aware he has
eloped. And if he has, I am not in a position to confirm that."
Meanwhile, a retired Assistant Inspector General of Police (AIG),
Mrs. Ferida Mzamber Waziri, has condemned in strong terms the recent gun
attack on the Director General (DG) of the National Agency for Food and
Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC), Dr. Dora Akuyili
The Ex Police Chief who spoke to news men at the weekend on the state of
affairs of the nation also attributed the action to avarice
Mrs. Waziri, a former boss at the Special Fraud Unity (SFU) of the
Nigeria Police, regretted that instead of Nigerians to applaud the good work
which the NAFDAC DG was doing to rid the country of the menace of fake,
substandard and adulterated drugs, some evil minded persons were bent on
eliminating her.
"It would be a big shame for Nigeria and Anambra State in particular, if
Dr. Akuyili was killed in the cause of bringing sanity and respectability to
the drug trade in the country' she added.
Though, she noted that the security of mankind was in the hands of
God,. the ex - police boss who is the founder of an Abuja based
Non-Governmental Orga-nisation (NGO), for women, Youth, children and crime
(WCCO), maintained that the recent attack has showed that it is high time
the Federal Government provides a bullet proof vehicle for the DG for her
official use.
According to her, God fearing Nigerians would no longer seat down and
watch criminals snuff out life from Akuyili because of the good work she is
doing to sanitize the food and pharmaceutical industry.
"The issue of providing the NAFDAC DG a bullet proof vehicle should be
given a top priority now" she said.
The retired police boss further stated that the attack under scores the
danger those out to stamp out corruption in the country are made to
face.
She stated that the DG has served the country meritoriously, and winning
both National and International accolades for Nigeria and as such must not
be allowed to continuously be threatened by criminals and lowly rated
persons. She added that this would only make Nigeria and Nigerians a
laughing stock before the intentional community.
