"It gladdens my heart that some of the suspected assailants have been
arrested and I urged the police to carry out a thorough investigations so as
to bring the culprit to book urgently as justice delayed is justice denied"
she stated.
Mrs. Waziri, there fore, called for the reassessment of the country's
value system. She added that it is callous and devilish for the sick and
dying to be administered with fake and adulterated drugs made up of chalk,
semovita, cassava and other forms of substances this millennium.
The Director General of the National Agency for Food and Drugs
Administration and Control (NAFDAC), Dr. Dora Akuyili, was attack on Boxing
Day, Friday, December 26, 2003, at Agulu, Anambra State, when she went to
visit her family. She however, escaped unhurt by the whiskers.

Original_Search_Title: Parmalat Breaks New Export Ground
Headline: Malawi: Parmalat Breaks New Export Ground
Body_Text:
PARMALAT Zambia has started exporting its Longlife milk with an initial
consignment of 24 tonnes to Malawi.
Sales and marketing manager Sandress Nyirenda said in Lusaka yesterday
that the consignment was exported two weeks ago.
Mr Nyirenda said that the company was, apart from satisfying the local
market, still looking for other export markets in the region for it
products.
"A few weeks ago I was in Tanzania to explore the market for our
milk. Zimbabwe and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) are also potential
markets," he said.
And Mr Nyirenda has said that the launch of the Yam Chum and Santal in
November has been received with an overwhelming response from the public
especially from within Lusaka.
Meanwhile, the company yesterday gave out prizes worth about K7.5
million to children who participated in the Yam Chum draw.
The first prize of T-shirt, a cap, book label and a hamper went to
10-year-old Muliya Mudenda, the second to Towela Kalikeka, third to Kaliko
Nalulywa while seven others went away with consolation prizes.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian Elections And the Indian Magic
Headline: Nigerian Elections And the Indian Magic
Body_Text:
Lagos — Hidden away in the inside pages of some newspapers last week is the news that a federal government delegation is currently in India to study the electronic voting system in use there. Insider information available however claims that a decision has already been taken to implement this system in Nigeria especially for the 2007 elections. Ordinarily, this should be welcome news. However, in this case, we could be on a path to squander a lot of money on a system that will only make it easier for politicians to rig the elections.
Electronic voting has been the dream of many election authorities world wide. They are widely used in the US, Brazil, India, Holland and other countries. Most countries in the EU have either experimented with electronic voting or adopted electronic voting in some areas. An electronic voting machine is designed to capture the ballot cast by a voter electronically. In most parts of the world, this is achieved using touch sensitive computer screens. The votes so captured are then collated and counted electronically. In the case of the Indian system, the touch sensitive screen is replaced with a set of switch buttons. The voter presses a button to cast his vote. In electronic voting, there is no need for ballot papers and ballot boxes and all the associated security and logistic problems. The major attraction of electronic voting machines is this reduction in logistic requirements and of course the ease with which counting can be done, literarily, at the touch of a button. Brazil for instance announces results within hours of the close of polls. Ditto for India, which just recently conducted the largest electronic only voting in history.
Electronic voting has however sparked intense debate because of significant shortcomings in the areas of security and verifiability. These shortcomings currently afflict all existing systems (at least those that have been used in elections!) including the system in use in India. The Indian system has the advantage only of lower cost, ruggedness and being more suited to environments with poor infrastructure. Given our history of electoral manipulation, we cannot afford to ignore the more fundamental shortcomings of these systems.
The first issue is that of voter authentication. The Indian system like all the other systems assumes that there is an independent way to identify and authenticate the voter and so this capability is not built in. Any one permitted to vote by the poll workers can vote. In Nigeria, these could be thugs, unregistered voters or just about anybody. Once the system is enabled to capture a vote, it does not check if the voter is a registered voter or not or if he is supposed to vote at that voting station or not. Picture this scenario: Our able candidate hijacks the voting system and poll workers, takes it to his house and over pounded yam and isiewu happily votes as many times as they like. One of the security features in the Indian system disallows voting except within set time intervals which will not matter if the voting is being done inside the house of the candidate and at his leisure.
A second problem is that of verifiability - was the vote cast by Abdul counted? Was the vote cast by Abdul for PDP actually counted for PDP or ANPP? Is the total count received actually a true reflection of the vote actually cast? Sadly, for existing electronic voting machines and the Indian system, these questions can not be answered with any certainty. At least for manual voting, it is possible for the ballots to be recounted. Difficult may be, but possible. Can we recount the votes in the Indian system certain that the votes have been accurately recorded? The answer is no. So what we have is a black box that gives us a tally of votes which we do not have any way of verifying. As we do not have any way of verifying the tally or the votes, we also cannot check whether the tally or the votes have been tampered with. Clearly Nigerian politicians will be delighted with this system: they can stuff the ballot box without the system stopping them or they can produce results which cannot be verified.
The issue is not whether electronic voting systems can assist in making the Nigerian electoral system more transparent. The obvious answer is yes it can. The more significant question is whether any of the existing systems especially the Indian system can be used in the Nigerian environment given the usual tricks of our politicians. From the brief analysis earlier on, the truth is that none can be used not less the Indian system. The Indian system has no inherent method of authenticating voters (so thugs can cast all the votes), it has no way of verifying that the ballots cast has been captured and totaled correctly (so we can manipulate the total votes or even credit parties with votes cast for another party) and it has no way of checking whether the ballots were being cast at the voting centre or in the house of the local political chieftain. These scenarios are the major problems facing the electoral system in Nigeria and their solution should be the major considerations for selecting any system.
But can such a system be built? One that addresses the Nigerian environment? Absolutely yes and it can be built here in Nigeria by Nigerians. It is instructive that the Indians built their system to address the issues in their environment and the Brazilian system is tailored to the Brazilian environment. Why is Nigeria going to India? Especially now that the government is willing to spend N5 billion on an electronic village. Or are we looking for an electronic rigging system?
It is imperative therefore that we prevail on the federal government to be open with us on their reason for choosing the Indian system. We also need to know and debate the merits of this system given our peculiar circumstances and review the safeguards that are in place to address the known problems of electronic voting machines especially in those areas that can be exploited by Nigerian politicians.
As a last point, ExpertEdge Software and Systems Limited is currently building an electronic voting system with finger print recognition capabilities which can be used to authenticate voters. INEC already has a database of registered voters. The system also includes a global positioning system in order to be able to validate the location where voting is being carried out. Additional technology is being built in to allow for result verification. This system is being built in Nigeria today by Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: UN Refugee Agency Launches First Seaborne Repatriation
Headline: Liberia: UN Refugee Agency Launches First Seaborne Repatriation to Liberia
Body_Text:
In the first sea movement of the United Nations refugee agency's Liberian repatriation programme, nearly 400 people are on their way home from Ghana as part of an operation that is expected to see the eventual return of 340,000 exiles from all over West Africa now that peace is taking hold in their war-shattered country.
The inaugural sea voyage brings to nearly 1,400 the number of Liberians who have returned home from Ghana since the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) started facilitating repatriation in October. The majority returned by air. In all, some 2,000 of the 42,000 Liberians in Ghana have so far expressed a desire to go home.
The latest group of 385 Liberians left Ghana's main port of Tema on board the MV Cerano on Wednesday for the three and a half day journey, carrying with them livestock, personal effects, commercial bread ovens and food such as yams and peanuts.
Over the next three years, UNHCR plans to repatriate some of the estimated 340,000 Liberians who fled 14 years of vicious civil war to neighbouring countries, with 150,000 expected to return in 2005 alone.
Once home, UNHCR pursues a range of efforts to facilitate the refugees' reintegration of Liberian refugees, including education programmes, infrastructural development and vocational skills training.
On arrival, the returnees are provided with an assistance package comprising food, household items and basic tools to support their reintegration. Some receive onward transportation or a transport allowance to cover the trip to their home areas.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Crisis: Egwu Panel Unnecessary - Ngige's Aide
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Crisis: Egwu Panel Unnecessary - Ngige's Aide
Body_Text:
Lagos — The former Director-General of National Orientation Agency (NOA), Dr. Ifeanyi Chukwuka, who is currently Senior Assistant to Governor Chris Ngige on Mobilization and Enlightenment in this interview with our reporter, FELIX UKA, gives clues to ways of enthroning sustainable peace in the state after the November mayhem. Excerpts:
Sir, there have been accusations and counter accusations of who is behind the November mayhem in Anambra State. Some people in the Chief Chris Uba-led group of PDP allege that it was the Governor Chris Ngige's faction that prosecuted the mayhem for them to curry public sympathy and stop conduct of the council polls in December 18, 2004. What is your own opinion?
Well, I want to start by saying that if somebody tells lies, I will go straight to say so, I do not want to say that the person is guilty of terminological inexactitude. This is not a case of terminological inexactitude, that is not using the right terminology. I think the Uba group has told blatant lies. There is no way the state governor who had just come down from America and gone to Abuja to answer the President when this mayhem started can be said to be responsible for the mayhem.
I know Dr. Chris Ngige is not a saddist to inflict this kind of unquantifiable injuries upon himself. Now, they said he doesn't want to conduct the council elections and that is why he probably destroyed these things. Do they conduct local government elections in governor's office? Do they conduct local government elections in Anambra Broadcasting Service (ABS) radio and television stations? Do they conduct local government elections in judiciary offices? They conduct local government elections in the wards and ANSIEC (the electoral body) offices. And you heard the governor saying that he has released money to ANSIEC to continue to prepare and put structures necessary for this election before he, Ngige travelled overseas.
Now, if the governor had gone to the extent of releasing money to ANSIEC and put a date for that election how can any right thinking man wake up to say that the governor woke up and decided to inflict injuries on himself. These people said their lists had been changed. The ANSIEC has not even published a list of those going for that election. So, it is obvious that they reacted on hearsay, on rumours, they did not wait for the list to be published so that they could properly hold on a particular proven issue that the list of people going for the election had been changed as from what the national executive of PDP said. The National Executive was said to have signed a list and sent same to ANSIEC and I tell you the list has not been changed. So, why did they act on rumours. You are in Anambra State, you saw in the Vanguard newspaper of November 17, 2004. It was a full page advertorial. Those persons call themselves local government chairmanship aspirants, 21 of them, they signed that publication. They said they were the people who did these things, that they are ready to do more. They said their grievance was that the list was changed. So, who showed them the list? Who told them the authentic list to be used? So, how can anybody turn around because of their wickedness, because of their atrocities, because of their nefarious insensibility to the problems of people of Anambra State and say it was the governor. They saw that the people have subjected them to the court of public opinion and decided that they were all going to be disassociated from the people of Anambra State and decided to blame the governor. Shame is catching them. The Bible said the wicked shall bow at the gate of the righteous. They are now coming to bow at the gate of the righteous, but instead of showing remorse, it is unfortunate, unbecoming, it is humiliating, it is even astonishing, overwhelming and stupid for them to ever think that telling cock and bull stories will save them in this particular issue. They have boxed themselves into a corner oh! So, let the governor continue whatever peace they are negotiating, the perpetrators are going to be subjected to the court of public opinion in Anambra State.
The answer, the judgment that will be passed on them now from what I am seeing and hearing is total banishment from whatever is of Anambra State. The people are calling for their arrest. First they said the vigilante shot them, that's why they reacted. Now they say it is the governor that set up this, which one do we take?
The candidates you are talking about had earlier said the plan to hold the election from December 18th - 25th, the Christmas day was unacceptable to them and that the choice of Cornel Umeh said to be maternal cousin of Dr. Ngige is a ploy to rig the election in favour for the governor's choice candidates, what is your reaction?
Let me start with the dates for the election. We have conducted elections in this country on December, 26th and 28th. During Dr. Chinwoke Mbadinuju era, the first election was on 24th December and after that the run-off was on 26th December which was done at Ikenga Hotel Royale. So, I left on 24th to do Christmas with my family on the 25th, on 26th I was at Ubatal Hotel where on the 28th there was a run-off primary in Anambra State. So, elections have been conducted during December. These elections, I hope you don't forget, merely demand your going to vote and get back to your house. The politicians are the ones who vote and police their votes. In the particular election we are talking about, the run-off according to the guidelines should hold within seven days after the election. So it may not have to be on 25th. Even there may not be any run-off if clear winners emerge. So to rely on that to cause this kind of mayhem is unacceptable, it is inexplicable; it is unheard of.
That the ANSIEC chairman is maternal cousin of the governor is of no consequence if he, the chairman can do the job very well. Remember in the time of Governor Ambrose Ali of old Bendel State, he located the University of Ekpoma in his own home town, Ekpoma and people were challenging him, then he said, show me another place that is better than Ekpoma my town in the area in terms of availability of land and the environment required for a good university. Now, show me another better than the man chosen for his career and track record to do this election. Wait until he has blundered.
So, they are only searching for where to hook their flimsy arguments and excuses. They don't hold water. Why not attack the man's personality and character and track record.
Politics is a game of number, if you score 2000 votes against 10 votes, it is obvious that you have won, anybody who tries to shortchange you is doing that at the detriment of his life.
Sir, many people are blaming the police command here in Anambra for failing to stop the hoodlums who effected the mayhem while some people are saying that the command could have been working under express orders, how do you place the allegation against the police command?
(Cuts in). Because they didn't tell us whom they took orders from, because we were not there we cannot all the time be pointing accusing fingers on the Inspector-General of Police that he is actually the one who gave them orders. If it was the Inspector-General that gave them the order, why did Hon. Chuma Nzeribe have to come here? This is a case of name dropping. Unless these people are not reasonable enough to know that injustice is a serious sin against God and man.
If anybody gave this order such a person or persons are not qualified to hold any position in this country. I am saying that it has gotten to the level of name dropping.
Some people rush down from Abuja to this state, mesmerize and confuse the people who are here, faking papers and trumping papers that Inspector General of Police told them this or that, they can even put a telephone call and say can you hear the IGP? Then the man will say whatever he wants, do for him. How can an IGP give order that properties of a state should be destroyed? So, I am still querying that story because each time they tell us the order was from above people from the Presidency will wash their fingers off. But where I will want to believe that their hands are not into what is happening in Anambra State is when the culprits and their sponsors and their organisers will be arrested and prosecuted. If the Presidency and the IGP effect that, they would have clearly and unequivocally and crystally shown us that their hands are clean. But where they fail to arrest them as instructed by the National Assembly, by the people of Anambra State as the overwhelming public majority have instructed, I will say oh! truly there is hand of Essau in this thing, there is an unseen hand.
Hon. Chuma Nzeribe is from this state, do you have any more reasons to think he was part of those behind the mayhem or you are basing your allegation on the fact that the lawmaker was sighted in the state?
Yes! Chuma Nzeribe even called a press conference on Sunday after the mayhem. He said he was challenging Ngige to dare come back to Anambra State. Today Ngige walked back to his state in a triumphant entry and Hon. Nzeribe was no where to be found to now stultify and snuff out life from Ngige whom he said if he dares to enter the state he would attack him at Amansea. That's an empty boast. I think he is grandiose and should be subjected to psychological examination to know why he, Nzeribe has violence tendencies.
But we learnt the governor came back through Owerri. Was that a security measure following the threat by Nzeribe?
Well, if the governor came through Owerri route, it is due to flight schedule. He isn't afraid of Nzeribe or anybody, if you look at him you know he is a solid lion, he doesn't fear. There are three things which go well, neigh, four things which are comely in going (Psalm 30 verse 29 and 30), one a lion strongest among the beasts and turneth away for any. This governor is a lion. However, except the Lord keep the city in vain do watchmen do. I know God is behind him.
Most people say that the crux of this matter is that Ngige refused to yield to certain agreements with those who helped him become a governor and that he, Ngige chose to work with technocrats instead of party loyalists.
The whole thing has now passed that level. It is now above political sharing formula. It has gone beyond symbiotic activities of politics. From the beginning Eselu had nursed greed, if not they could have settled behind close doors among themselves. But because they wanted it too soon, too much, the problem broke out. Many things went wrong to the point where they abducted the governor. That singular act of July 10, 2003 destroyed what could have been a symbolic political relationship. Every other relationship that would have encouraged trust, companionship was killed. They murdered sleep and they will sleep no more. Ngige can't go back to such said agreements that were made in the backyard because those who own the yams have learnt of a plot to steal them. They are watching the governor.
What do you expect from the reconciliation committee?
I don't see any reason for the setting up of the committee, because it won't change anything. Somebody burnt your house, instead of first seeing how your properties could be replaced, someone is inviting you to a meeting with the man who set your house ablaze. It is not acceptable.

Original_Search_Title: Police, Vigilance Groups Kill 8 Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Police, Vigilance Groups Kill 8 Robbers
Body_Text:
Ilorin — A combined efforts of crack detectives in Kwara State Police Command, as
well as local communities vigilance groups along Idofian/Koko/
Omupo/Ajase/Omu-Aran federal highway, has paid off with the killing of eight
suspected armed robbers at Oke-Ode/Ora in Ifelodun Local Government Area of
the state.
Following the rising wave of armed robbery on the highway, the police
began collaborative efforts with various vigilance groups in the area, aimed
at arresting the hoodlums as well as their backers.
The state Governor, Bukola Saraki, had enjoined support of chairmen of
the affected local councils, and monarchs at a meeting, with a view to
finding a lasting solution to the menace.
At the meeting, Saraki stressed the need for them to complement efforts
of the police by raising vigilance groups, who shall be paid by the council
bosses, to patrol the highway and nip further atrocities of the robbers in
the bud.
One of them, Alhaji AbdulKareem Adewunmi, Irepodun Council Chairman,
told newsmen in Ilorin that the meeting with the governor had far reaching
effect with the council bosses, resolving to come out strongly against the
armed robbers.
Another Alhaji Tajudeen Aro assured that with the governor's directive,
he and his colleagues have resolved to contribute vigilance groups on the
highway, for a lasting travelling comfort for road users.
A statement by the command spokesman, Marcus Gideon, (ASP) yesterday
said the robbers, numbering about 15, met their waterloo when sighted in a
bush roasting yam.
According to the statement, the men of the underworld belonged to a gang
of hoodlums who had been terrorsing commuters along GanmoAjase-Ipo axis in
recent times.
It said eight of the robbers were shot and killed on the spot, while the
rest escaped with bullet wounds.
It said dangerous weapons such as guns; cutlasses; and other items
including one Nokina 12 band radio cassette player; two pairs of slippers;
clothing material and a water can, were recovered during the incident.
It pleaded with members of the public to assist the police with
information that could lead to hoodlums' arrest.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra: the Example of Anukili
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra: the Example of Anukili
Body_Text:
IT is no longer news that Anambra is under threat from quarters that are
desperate, who no longer even pretend to hide themselves or their intentions
or their methods.
Mr. Femi Fani-Kayode, the presidential Goebel, describes the situation
in Anambra State as a "factional war." It is also no longer a secret which
faction of that war is supplied fully with the apparatus of state power, to
levy war, against the people of Anambra State. Given that the constituted
government of Anambra State, bears the mandate of the people, the faction
that Abuja supports, fights the Anambra people.
It is therefore no longer a battle between the Governor, Chris Ngige,
and this revanchist group funded massively, to destroy Anambra State by the
use of terrorism. It is also very telling that Mr. Fani-Kayode uses the term
"war." This is the first time an official slip of the tongue, indicates
clearly, that a real war, with a real marginot line drawn, is being
prosecuted in Anambra State. The purpose of it, apparently is, to make
Anambra ungovernable, and offer grounds for instability.
With the consequence that the social, economic and cultural life does
not thrive. The most ridiculous thing out of what is happening is the
reported insistence by this faction, with its war room in Abuja, that for
peace to reign, Chris Uba, the minion of the dark forces waging this
infernal battle against the people and government of Anambra State must be
made deputy governor.
They want Governor Ngige to sign his second Carthaginian
treaty. Prominent people, including Chukwuma Onoh, but notably Professor
Chinua Achebe, has pointed at the presidency's collusion in the situation in
Anambra State. Of course, Fani-Kayode, speaking again for the President,
denies this. He says any one who makes that claim, should supply the
evidence that President Obasanjo's is the monkey's hand in the pot of
soup.
Well, indeed, how does anyone supply evidence, when all the instruments
of law enforcement, and detection are concentrated at the center, in the
hands of the President? Fani Kayode's insistence on evidence linking the
President is a mere attempt to fob off a gathering truth. Meanwhile,
Governor Ngige, like every other governor in the federation, is merely a
sitting duck governor, incapable of executing the laws and mandates of the
state on which he presumes executive authority. He is unable to defend the
state to which he swore a public oath of office, because a fundamental flaw
in Nigeria's sham "federal" constitution strips him of any authority to
constitute a police force. I think it is time, at the minimum, for states of
the federation to revisit the issue of state police departments.
If Anambra State had its own police department, and its own constitution
and laws, characters like Chuma Nzeribe, a creature of the Nigerian security
underground, would not be getting away with what he was alleged to have been
doing. I say this, only if allegations of his involvement in the abduction
of the governor, and later the recent criminal incendiary of the property of
the state are true. Meanwhile, even as both Chuma Nzeribe and Chris Uba
constitute themselves into the great powers who allegedly tyrannize the
vulnerable people of Anambra State, I urge seriously that we look to history
for stimulus and action.
One of the things about history is that it offers us a narrative. From
its narrative we may discover some value: grounds for immediate action; a
moral, the basis for establishing an a priori consequence. History offers us
a usable past. In the Igbo past, especially through its stales, we inherit
an important narrative about the power of the collective. It is in the story
of Anukili, the giant. In the days of yore, Anukili was born. He was an
unusual child. He was born with a full set of teeth. He came to earth not
uttering a cry, stolid in his silence, his full gaze fixed almost
unalterably at the world into which he was born. It was a world of oracles
and rituals.
How could a child come into the world with teeth. He was a child of
strength, of the will rather than of the senses. Afa decreed, and the child
was taken to the forest of the spirits, to return to the spirits through a
lonely and quiet death. So it happened with Anukili, except that his mother
would not let go. Ugly or not, a child is the product of a womb. The womb
does not let go easily. Anukili grew, and grew in secret. By the time he was
fifteen, he was already a giant. By the time he came fully of age, he
towered over every man. With his bare hands, he could uproot the most
stubborn of trees. As it happened, his people at Agukwu went to war, and
Anukili led the battle, and wrought a bitter victory over the enemies of
Agukwu. His fame grew across the known and unknown world. He became the hero
of Agukwu.
Songs were made in his name. But fame is like "nkwu-enu." Those who
drink of it know what it does to the head. You become a "godfather." And
soon, it would require the mobile police to clear "jaga-jaga" from your
private road. So it was for Anukili. Soon, his own people at Agukwu became
his victims. He went into barnyards, and took people's yams with
impunity. He took other men's wives and daughters by force, and if they
whimpered, he crushed them under his mighty feet. He took the ofo of the
umunna, and dared anyone to speak before or after him. He silenced the will
of the people. He levied tax, and whoever could not pay, forfeit their lives
or property.
Anukili unleashed terror, razed houses, burned the shrines of the
clans. The people of Agukwu thus began to live in awe of the giant. Wherever
Anukili's shadow was cast, dread and silence settled. His spies and thugs
were everywhere. But one day, the men of Agukwu met in silence and
whispered. The elders remembered the days when the skies were still romping
grounds for squirrels. They decided that they wanted a King, like the people
of Oban'Idu. "A king! That's what we must have! But whoever shall be King in
Agukwu, will only pass one test!" And what was the test? Contenders must be
able to bear the roof of a house from the market square to the great
river. It was a test of strength. And who would be King, but Anukili?
The day came. It was Orie - the day of the ancestors. Anukili arrived
for the test. Agukwu showed him the tatched roof. Anukili quickly heaved it
on his mighty back. To make the roof firm, the rafters were tied on his arms
with ropes. The procession began from the market place. They were
clamorous. Anukili's panegyrics rented the air. "Who, but Anukili, can be
our King? Who but the giant shall be King?!"
The long drums and canons, the voice of praise-singers, the ululation of
virgins, gave Anukili the extra uumph! But half-way to the river, the song
changed. A new fierceness had entered the space. A defiance in the voice of
those who had glimpsed freedom. Anukili's hands were tied to the
rafters. And the voices of the youngmen of Agukwu began to rise: "But, which
child sleeps, who keeps his mother awake?" Anukili's ears heard it: "he who
defies a people, must also eat the broth of their anger!" This was no longer
the ritual of affirmation. From somewhere, bramble and fire emerged.
Half-way to the river, his hands tied to the rafters of the roof on his
back, the people of Agukwu set the thatch on fire. Anukili broke free,
uttering his death-cry, and ran, and ran, towards the river, the flame
consuming him. Anukili became a burnt-offering, to appease the conscience of
a much abused people, half-way to his goal - the great river.
This story is my preface to what is going on today in Anambra State. A
man who sets the shrine of his ancestors on fire, commits a high
abomination, and has symbolically discounted himself from kindred, and the
rights pertaining therefrom. Those who, goaded by forces within and outside
Igbo land, set the symbolic public institutions in Anambra State on fire,
daring the will of the collective consciousness, have taken a public,
symbolic stand against their own people. This is the example of
Anukili. What is going on in Anambra State today is the very beginning of a
civil war. Its purpose is basically, I suspect, to enact a final solution;
and which ever paymasters are instigating the disruption of peace in
Anambra, apparently intends that the place become a permanent ruin.
Anambra opens another front into the Igbo theatre, of a war that still
rages in some heads. I say the Igbo are under a vicious siege, and should
utilize everything within imaginable possibility to resist this continued
dehumanization. Perhaps time has come to remove the kid gloves in dealing
with the Anambra problem. Perhaps it is time for the people in Anambra to
rise, and enact a political "boys oye!" and chase their oppressors into the
river Niger. Run the bastards out of town. Hound them by night, and by day,
so that nothing remains of them to trouble the sleep anymore.
Each one, tell one. It is the right of every people to defend themselves
against aggression. Since the law enforcement agencies have shown themselves
to be particularly partisan against the people of Anambra State, it is
incumbent on the people of Anambra State, now, to raise a parallel police
organization - a properly constituted citizens militia - to defend
themselves, against the recurrent terror of the federal might. The times
call for it.

Original_Search_Title: HIV/Aids to Shrink Sub-Saharan Africa's Labour Force
Headline: Africa: HIV/Aids to Shrink Sub-Saharan Africa's Labour Force
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — By 2010, Sub-Saharan Africa's total labour force is expected to have
shrunk by nine per cent due to HIV/AIDS, a meeting in Ghana heard last
week. Labour losses may top 20 per cent in the worst affected
countries.
By 2015 the losses could reach up to 12 per cent overall, reducing the
labour supply by as much 30-40 per cent in the highest prevalence countries,
a press release sent to The Daily Monitor stated.
Over one hundred representatives from the public and private sectors
concluded a two-day policy dialogue workshop Friday in Accra on the impact
of HIV/AIDS in the workplace.
The workshop, organized by the Commission on HIV/AIDS and Governance in
Africa (CHGA) in collaboration with ILO-AIDS, part of the International
Labour Organization, aimed to provide African policy makers with
recommendations to reduce the impact of the pandemic on the continent's
labour force.
In his welcome address, Yam Barimah, Minister for Manpower Development
and Employment, said: "As AIDS gathers momentum, the workplace can play a
frontline role in preventing its further spread by coping with the effects
of the disease on individuals, their families and communities."
A representative speaking on behalf of Ghana's Vice-President, Alhaji
Aliu, underscored the alarming statistics and also stressed the need for
effective responses.
He said: "AIDS poses the gravest threat to Africa's development. If the
epidemic rages unchecked, the continent faces a spiral of
decline. Nevertheless, this disaster can be prevented. As Africans we must
take greater responsibility for creating a capable environment for an
effective and sustainable response."
Workshop participants called for a greater collaboration between the
public and private sectors. A representative from Sierra Leone stressed that
"although the government has taken the lead in providing treatment, we are
looking for more partners to come on board to bridge the gap in the supply
of treatment and care for our employees."
Participants also emphasized the need for leadership from all major
sectors of industry. Professor Amoa, Director General, Ghana AIDS
Commission, said: "Africa is already marginalized in the global trade and
the digital divide has worsened our ability to compete It is time for all
champions of industry to summon their strength and commitment to respond by
developing appropriate policy frameworks for HIV/AIDS.  "
The issue of access to treatment and care was also one of the key points
of the discussions between representatives of the private and public
sectors.
A participant from Coca-Cola Corporation said: "As one of the largest
employers in Africa, we feel we have to do our part to combat HIV/AIDS. We
offer our employees 100 percent coverage for ARV treatment and extend our
support programmes to the communities they live in. We believe in the
collaboration of all sectors, public, private, UN agencies, the communities
to combat HIV/AIDS."
The Commission on HIV/AIDS and Governance in Africa is a UN system-wide
initiative, involving partnerships with a number of UN agencies, as well as
leading institutions in Africa and internationally on aspects of research,
policy and implementation.

Original_Search_Title: Rehabilitating Roads in the Rural Areas
Headline: Ghana: Rehabilitating Roads in the Rural Areas
Body_Text:
Bad roads have been identified as a contributing factor to the poor development in rural areas in the country. It is in this vein that most of the rural communities are calling for rehabilitation of both their feeder and trunk roads.
The roads in the Central Volta to the Northern sector of the Volta Region, until recently were nothing to write home about. However, people in this area could now count on the government for the recent response to their plight.
The government has responded to the pleas of traditional rulers, including Togbega Gabusu VI the Paramount Chief of the Gbi traditional area, and some religious leaders and now three contractors, including an Israeli firm are working on the 84-kilometre Kpeve-Hohoe-Jasikan road.
The contractors are Gbewa contractors who are working on the Kpeve-Golokwati road, Roliden contractors, an Israeli firm, on the Golokwati- Hohoe road and Somuah and Dapaah construction on the Hohoe-Jasikan road.
The road that hitherto had numerous potholes, manholes and ponds that some drivers called "fishpond" spread over it, is now getting better. Drivers are not meandering on the road as before and activities of unemployed youth including school dropouts on the road filling potholes for tips from drivers are decreasing.
Citizens from the area, who have been leaving their cars at Atimpoku to join commercial vehicles to their hometowns, are now seen driving in their cars to funerals on Friday evenings and Saturdays.
Joe Kamasa, a restaurant operator in Accra who hails from one of the villages along the road, praised the government for the on-going rehabilitation of the road but called on the authorities to install road signs in some areas.
"Areas like Woadze must get a sign that would order drivers to reduce their speeding, while Nyagbo and Avatime junction should get a sign warning drivers of the dangerous curves.
Komla Akpo, a driver, was however not happy with the slow pace of work by the contractor working on the Kpeve-Golokwati road but expressed his happiness for the good work being constructed by the contractor.
Commercial drivers at Jasikan and beyond have also expressed their gratitude to the government. Kojo Seidu, a driver of a truck, which conveys tubers of yam from Nkwanta to Accra, exclaimed that the rehabilitation of the road has come to save drivers from buying spare parts.
Drivers plying between Golokwati and Hohoe were not happy with the condition of roads in the area until the district chief executive for Hohoe, Mr James Dogbe, announced the presence of the Israeli firm, which was coming to work on it.
The contractor has moved to the site at Avega near Gbi-Godenu to commence work on the road.
Mr John Asamoah, Hohoe district director of Feeder Roads, said a total of 200 kilometres of feeder roads were being maintained in the Hohoe district, while the road from Leklebi Dafor through Leklebi Duga to Hohoe to Logba would be tarred.

Original_Search_Title: Speaking Out: Godfathers And the PDP's Anambra
Headline: Nigeria: Speaking Out: Godfathers And the PDP's Anambra
Body_Text:
How could anyone have imagined that it would all just end like that? That a governor would be kidnapped after his election by the police acting in concert with some 'small boy' godfather. That a police boss would be fired, a little noise would be made, mainly by the media; that the president would be blamed for doing nothing about it. And that would be it?
On second thoughts, it is not so hard to imagine how anyone could have imagined it would end just like that. We live in Nigeria, where money truly answereth all things and anything can happen. Still, it is only inevitable that the Ngige/Uba fiasco would resurrect and play itself out to its natural and logical conclusion.
The sins of the players of the Anambra infamous debacle -- the one to which Chinua Achebe referred recently, as one of the reasons for turning down a highly coveted National Award -- are many against their people, the most heinous being that of the governor who had sold out a mandate even before it had been given to him by his people. There is the godfather, who understandably holds no allegiance to the people, and whose only concern is to appropriate to himself as much as possible of the resources meant for the whole state.
Then there is the party which sits there, wringing its hand, while doing nothing even as its reputation goes to the dogs. Of all involved parties, the party is the one most deserving of sympathy. On the worst of days, the PDP is well established in its reputation as a godfather party, one which it has borne with a gracious calm until the birth of the Anambra godfather brouhaha.
It becomes less and less easy for PDP to confront its moral dilemma, particularly as the party's blinding success over the years has pushed all other matters to the background, particularly moral ones. As far as the PDP and its leadership is concerned, the trouble with Anambra is simply a godfather case gone wrong, and taking a definite stance against it would be like taking a definite stance against Dariye in Plateau. It would be like throwing the baby out with the bath water, and a threat to the particularly peculiar liberties members of the PDP enjoy, particularly the ones with the purses.
But as Dariye is humbled into begging, taking a definite stance is what the party leadership would have to do, because it is not democracy per se which is at stake here. What is really at stake is even more crucial, and that is the type of democracy which will be practiced in Anambra state, and in the nation as a whole. Will it be one in which the people rule, or would it simply be a "demonstration of craze?"
With falling oil prices
As world oil prices come crashing, it is time for us to talk, for the umpteenth time, about the prices of fuels in Nigeria. A lot of noise has been made in the very recent past, and is still being made, about the fact that the president did not know the local prices of kerosene until about a couple of weeks ago.
In actual fact, the president is not the only person who does not know the price of kerosene. Millions of Nigerians who have some other source of cooking fuels do not either, and no one appears to be bugging them about not knowing.
The difference is that the president ought to know, particularly given his obsession with the prices, particularly where it concerns price increases. Not knowing would give the impression that it is true what his 'detractors' say, that he doesn't really care about the people who use the products he has such a great interest in.
But his mouthpieces are quick to correct that mistake. The presidency -- or, for that matter, the government -- has little interest in increasing the prices of fuels. What they have an interest in is liberalization, a process they have taken great pains to inform us is irreversible.
It is also a process which, so far, has produced not a little pain and anxiety, and it is this pain and anxiety which government has used against the people -- or persuaded the people to use against themselves -- after the most recent increases in local fuel prices.
And so while labour and civil society groups were working out their strategy for the November 16 strike action that never held, government was hard at work as using divide and rule tactics to persuade other capitalists to speak against the action. This had lead to the calling off.
The advantage of this to the Nigerian society is that the line has finally been drawn. Finally, there is a clear line between civil society and the capitalist society, and the interests of the two are different.
In the mean time, world oil prices are falling, and we can not help but hope it falls more, since changes in our national incomes do not affect our people one way or the other. At least domestic prices should also come down, or government would be proven to be lying.
The woman's value
Everyone has a favorite, and one of my favorite Hollywood movies of all time is the one in which Sandra Bullock played the 'ugly duckling' secret service agent whose way of getting actress was sending a jaw-numbing blow. In Miss America, Sandra Bullocks' character had yams on her legs, never went out on a date and never even thought about fancying her (male) partner) until a few unusual turns of events.
No doubt, Miss America inspired good laughs, but it sent a deep, underlying message which, while not being subtle, could easily have disappeared into the humour.
Bullocks' character looked down on 'soft' women, whose idea of achievement was competing for a beauty queen crown. She thought such a woman fickle and empty headed, with less than average intelligence, until she found herself in just such a position and found that being so fickle minded and empty headed was not necessarily such a bad thing after all.
But that was only side theme. The real theme, which was inextricably linked to it, was the classic case of a woman working against the cause of other women.
History is filled with such cases. But fortunately, while there is so much of the latter, there is also so much of women working for women.
One of the numerous in Nigeria is Lagos State's Funmi Tejuosho. From the beginning, she has made it clear that she would, amidst her other duties, use her position to fight the cause of the battered woman, and she has not stopped since. And one of the ways by which she has attempted this is by trying to get a law passed by the state legislature, which would protect victims of domestic violence.
As would be expected, there are mountains to be crossed, and so while she, with much support from numerous NGO's which deal with women affairs, are anxious to see their wishes come to fruition, the male dominated house is not, for obvious reasons. In the mean time, thousands of Nigerian women suffer silently in their homes.
Hopefully such a law, when it is eventually passed in all its male-patronizing glory, will be taken advantage of, as it is only the first step to a whole renaissance in the area of gender based violence in Nigeria. This renaissance, if it has the good luck of coming to pass, will come in form of a whole social restructuring in the way men see women, in the way women see themselves and in the way government sees the disadvantaged of the nation, one of which is the battered woman.
To achieve this, more women would have to be drawn into the campaign, and these women do not have to be bright lawyers or big politicians or NGO directors. All they would have to be are women.

Original_Search_Title: Sharia Convict Pleads for Amputation
Headline: Nigeria: Sharia Convict Pleads for Amputation
Body_Text:
Lagos — A 28-year-old Shariah convict, Adamu Shu'aibu, sentenced to amputation has challenged the Bauchi State government to amputate his hand in line with the principles of shariah. Shu'aibu was in February last year convicted for theft and sentenced to amputation by an Alkaleri Upper Shariah Court in the state but imprisoned since then.
He told the Director-General, Legal Aid Council, Hajiya A'isha Hassan Baba, during a visit to the Bauchi central prison that he preferred being amputated to "remaining in endless imprisonment". The self-confessed convicted thief said he had made his position clear to both the police and the court which found him guilty of theft.
"If there is anything the Legal Aid Council can do for me, it is to accelerate the amputation of my hand so that I can leave the prison and carry on with life," Shu'aibu told the visiting Legal Aid Council boss. Responding to the request, Baba said she would not encourage amputation but promised to seek other options to assist the convict.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports that there are over 31 Shariah convicts awaiting amputation and stoning at the Bauchi prison.
Twenty-nine of the convicts are awaiting amputation while two were sentenced to death by stoning.
Prominent among the eligible amputees is Hussaini Maidoya, a 45-year-old yam seller who chopped off the leg of his wife, Amina Maidoya over alleged infidelity Maidoya had been imprisoned since 2002 when an Upper Shariah Court sentenced him to amputation without the application of anaesthesia.
Under the code which has been in forced in the state since September 2001, only the state governor has the legal right to ratify all categories of sentences passed by the various Shariah Courts in the state.
Not a single of such sentences has so far been ratified by the state government since the introduction of the code in 2001 in the state in spite of pressure on the governor by the state's Shariah Advisory Council.

Original_Search_Title: Taste of Albums to Come
Headline: Zimbabwe: Taste of Albums to Come
Body_Text:
SUPERSTAR Oliver "Tuku" Mtukudzi and township jazz veterans, Cool Crooners, gave their fans a foretaste of their forthcoming albums at a joint concert at the Harare International Conference Center last week.
The show, organised by Alliance Francaise and Presdom Promotions, was in a way a homecoming occasion for Mtukudzi and his backing Black Spirits. They had returned the previous weekend from the five-concert Into Yam Zvese Zvangu Winterfest Tour of the UK with South African Afro-jazz crooner, Ringo Madlingozi.
Tuku treated his Harare fans to hits from his long discography and gave them a preview of tracks such as Nhava, Dzokai Nahwo, Tsvaga Chikonzero, Hope Hadzina Ndima and Tiri Mubindu from his forthcoming album.
Mtukudzi said Tiri Mubindu was "a tribute to our departed colleagues" (from the Black Spirits).
Local songstress, Prudence Katomeni-Mbofana of Jazz Invitation, who features on several tracks on the Cool Crooners' album, Isatilo, due for release early 2005, joined the Cool Crooners who opened the show with familiar songs from their Blue Sky album. They then played Bafana Bafana, an Aids awareness song and Dhlomo Thatha, a vibrant wedding celebration song from Isatilo.
The popular Tumbuka Dance Troupe made a welcome return to the capital with breathtaking choreographic stunts.
One of the most popular jazz acts in the country today, the Cool Crooners have toured extensively in France and North America while Tumbuka are also well known in France.

Original_Search_Title: RSF Urges Police to Announce the Charges Against Managing Editor of Weekly 'San Finna'
Headline: Burkina Faso: RSF Urges Police to Announce the Charges Against Managing Editor of Weekly 'San Finna'
Body_Text:
Paris — RSF has called on the Burkina Faso police to announce the charges
against Mathieu N'Do, managing editor of the weekly "San Finna" and one of
the leaders of the opposition party National Union for Democracy and
Development (Union nationale pour la dÃ©mocratie et le dÃ©veloppement,
UNDD).
N'Do has been held incommunicado at a special police centre since his
arrest on 5 November 2004 at Ouagadougou airport.
"This journalist is a leading member of the opposition, but if he was
arrested because of his articles, we must condemn this abuse of authority by
the Security Ministry. If not, we call on the government to make this known
and, whatever the charges against N'Do, to grant him the right to a legal
defence in accordance with the law," RSF said.
N'Do was reportedly detained on the orders of the police high command
upon his arrival at Ouagadougou airport, from Abidjan, on 5 November at 3:00
p.m.
(local time). He had just spent two weeks in CÃ´te d'Ivoire and had sent
his newspaper several dispatches from the country.
N'Do was taken from the airport to the "Sector 28" police camp, where he
is still being held. He has not been allowed to contact his family or his
lawyer. Staff at his newspaper said they tried to send him food, clean
clothes, a towel and soap, but the police agreed only to take the food and
the soap, without further explanation.
Reached several times by RSF, police headquarters refused to explain the
reasons for N'Do's arrest and incommunicado detention. Staff at the offices
of the attorney-general and state prosecutor said they had nothing to do
with N'Do's arrest, which was solely the responsibility of the Security
Ministry.
Known both for being a critical journalist and an opposition leader,
N'Do has been detained previously. His family believes his latest arrest to
be linked to that of UNDD President Hermann YamÃ©ogo, a lawyer and member of
Parliament.
On 29 September, Hermann YamÃ©ogo was detained as he got off a plane from
Abidjan. He was questioned for several hours by police, along with UNDD
activist NoÃ«l YamÃ©ogo. Earlier that day, Security Minister Djibril BassolÃ©
said in an interview that Burkina Faso was the victim of a "plot" in which
"illustrious" citizens were accomplices.
BassolÃ© alleged that the accomplices included Hermann YamÃ©ogo, who along
with others, was "traipsing around certain capitals trying to sell
information about supposed training camps operated by foreign putschists" in
Burkina Faso. The minister also said the recipients of this information
included RSF, a claim that was categorically denied by the
organisation.

BassolÃ© alleged that the accomplices included Hermann YamÃ©ogo, who along with others, was "traipsing around certain capitals trying to sell information about supposed training camps operated by foreign putschists" in Burkina Faso. The minister also said the recipients of this information included RSF, a claim that was categorically denied by the organisation.

Original_Search_Title: Memories of the Ofala
Headline: Nigeria: Memories of the Ofala
Body_Text:
Lagos — On the eastern fringes of the Niger River, just on the bank of the
popular river that identifies Nigeria lies the city of Onitsha. The city is
a melting pot of human activities.
It is also a city of commerce, unarguably, the commercial heartbeat of
the West African subregion. But, on Thursday, October 7 2004, the business
activities quietly laid low, and paved way for the ascension and celebration
of culture. It was the day, the Obi of Onitsha, the traditional monarch, His
Majesty Igwe Nnaemeka Achebe, celebrated the annual Ofala Day. It was the
third such ceiebration since he assumed the throne of his forefathers.
The two-day festival captures the cultural and traditional essence of
the Onitsha people, and distinguishes the throne of the Obi. The event has
traversed many centuries to modernity, and it marks the re-emergence of the
venerated Obi of Onitsha from an enforced isolation of communal mourning. On
this day, the Obi makes a symbolic reappearance to demonstrate to the world
that he has survived another year and he is hale and hearty to witness
another New Year. By this ritual, the Obi re-enforces the fact that he has
atoned for the sins and misdeeds of his subjects, while looking forward to
the near future. It is also a demonstration that he and his subjects have
been filled with strength and fullness for what lies ahead.
Three weeks ago, the Obi and his subjects, in the presence of a coterie
of visitors from far and near, celebrated this ritual with a lot of pomp. It
was a Day Africa re-enacted its lost glory. Dressed in a flamboyant cultural
costume, and escorted by his chiefs, the Obi appeared on the scene in
symbolic display of his emergence from an enforced hibernation. In this mere
act lies the mystique that surrounds the Obi throne. To the people, the Obi
is superhuman, with a larger-than-life personality.
To be able to appreciate the Ofala and its spiritual and cultural
import, one must resort to the rich Igbo cultural heritage, remarkably the
historic New Year festival. As a unifying festival, the Ofala marks the 15th
day of Onwaji, the eating of new yam, which is celebrated over two days. The
first day is mainly for the Obi and his red cap chiefs and Ndichies, while
the second day called Azu Ofala allows for wider participation by the
subjects, groups, Agbalanze, etc. In both ceremonies, everyone pays homage
to His Majesty and pledge loyalty to him. The natives, both home and abroad,
have grown to love this culture.
Thus, on the day of the event, all roads led to Onitsha. From the
Amanayabo of Opopo who came with his entourage from Rivers State to the
Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria, it was a congregation of men of
honour who are at home with culture. From the Ime Obi venue of the ceremony
on Awka Road to the far-flung corners of Onitsha, it was a celebration
galore. There was music, dance and a sort of movement that bore the
semblance of a renaissance. As early as sunrise, guests began to troupe into
Onitsha, many of them wearing colourful cultural costumes. Drummers trailed
their steps, with rustic rhythms that heralded the main ceremonies. And by
Noon when the venue had been fully occupied, the tune of the drums had
turned upbeat, in anticipation of the Obi's entry to the arena. Barely one
hour later, the Obi made his triumphant entry into the arena, welcomed by a
charged audience. He danced regally, stopping at intervals to receive wild
applause and changing to new steps as he danced. The audience cheered in
unison. After this initial display, the Obi retired to his stools.
When he re-emerged to give his address, the Igwe expressed gratitude to
God for another year of guidance over his land and welcomed his visitors for
making it to the ceremony. He appealed to both Labour and the government to
resolve by dialogue their differences over the increase in the prices of
petroleum products. "It is important for the government to bring the message
of the reforms programme down to the ordinary people through stakeholders
groups and opinion leaders," he said, adding that, "frequent strikes only
serve to prolong the economic recovery that we all yearn for."
The monarch also highlighted on the inconveniences being encountered by
traders in Onitsha over the recent ban on certain imported items. He said:
"This setback should give our traders and businessmen the distinct message
that it is time to begin to devote more attention to creating wealth through
manufacturing rather than just buying and selling." He lamented that Anambra
State appears to remain in limbo as effects of the last elections continue
to debilitate the lives of the people and affect their place at the national
level. "I call again on the people of Anambra State to rally together, sink
our differences, forgive one another and join hands to restore our dignity
and sense of community and common weaith. We must look inward to ourselves
for solution not to others."
The Igwe's address also indicted the Federal Government over the state
of infrastructure in Onitsha, particularly the Onitsha-Owerri Road, which he
said "is literally abandoned and constitutes a national embarrassment." He
announced his continuing efforts at building bridges across other lands, any
time he travels to certain parts of the country. He said that "the
re-assignment of traditional administrative responsibilities in the entire
kingdom has been highly applauded as giving a sense of belonging to all who
reside in it."
Igwe Achebe commended the cooperation between the community vigilante
service and the Police for the improved security in his domain. "Onitsha,"
he said, "is in the throes of change and transformation. We need to run fast
enough to stand still. We appreciate the need to change with the world
around us whilst at the same time preserving pure and innovative norms and
features that mark us distinctly as a people. "Our external challenge is to
constantly seek the balance between our customs and tradition and the
process of modernisation. We are not unique in this quest."
After the Obi's address came dances by Ndichie Okwa, his second outing,
and Egwuota dancing by Ndichie Ume, followed by greetings, presentation of
gifts and courtesy by special guests. The day's activities ended with the
Igwe's third and final outing.
The second day - Azu Ofala - followed the same pattern, with a parade of
the different age grades and paying of homage to the Igwe. It attracted more
dances and drumming. The Onowu Iyasele of Onitsha and former Attorney
General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, Chief Chike Offodile
(SAN) who was marking his second Ofala appearance in that capacity, also
gave an insight into the significance of the celebration. He went down
memory lane: "In the olden days, our kings were confined to the palace
except for some elderly personal relatives of his and his Ndichies. Ordinary
persons had no access to him and he didn't go out. But whenever he had
serious matters, he invited and discussed with Ndichies at night. So once in
a year, he appears in public for his subjects to see and know him and for
him to see them. On that occasion called Ofala, the chiefs and subjects come
to renew their loyalty to him. Today, the king uses the opportunity of Ofala
to address his subjects, chronicling activities of the previous year and
putting forward proposals for the coming year. He also uses the day to thank
and recognise the contribution of some outstanding sons and daughters of
Onitsha. The age grade groups parade, gaily dressed before the Obi, to
reassure him of their loyalty."
Chief Onyeachonam Aniekweogun Okolonji, Odu Osodi/Nkataukwu, said it is
evident that Igwe Achebe has overwhelming support of Onitsha people, the
Hausa, Yoruba and other residents of the town: "This is why he is referred
to as His Royal Majesty." The Odu also said this year's Ofala clearly showed
again the willingness of Onitsha people and their monarch to build bridges
of friendship all over the country and beyond. "This is evident by the huge
turn out of visitors and guests," he said.
Professor Charles Soludo, Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN),
who was one of the prominent guests, said "Onitsha occupies a very unique
place in Igbo cultural heritage, going back to centuries. This is a
tradition that represents one of the best in Igboland as we can see in
today's ceremony. Onitsha is at the heart of Igbo culture and I am delighted
to be here."
This year's Ofala was witnessed by representatives of sons and daughter
of the town in Atlanta, United States, Speaker of the Anambra State House of
Assembly, Hon. Mike Balonwu, Secretary to the State Government, Mr Alex
Chukwurah, Senator Emma Anosike, Deputy Governor of the State, Chief
Ugochukwu Nwankwo who represented Governor Chris Ngige, Deputy Governor of
Enugu State and Dr Andrew Okolie of the Presidency who stood in for Vice
President Atiku Abubakar. Also in attendance were the Eze Ugbele 1 of Awo
Mbieri in Imo State, Eze Emmanuel Eronini, and the Amanyanabo of Opobo in
Rivers State, His Majesty King Dandison Jaja.

Original_Search_Title: Ohaneze And Ijele Masquerade
Headline: Nigeria: Ohaneze And Ijele Masquerade
Body_Text:
Abuja — Have you ever witnessed an Ofala, a Coronation, or a New Yam Festival in
Anambra State? You cannot imagine the number and variety of masquerades!
They are usually so many! On such occasions you also have the solemn
dignity, the majestic steps and the staid composure of the titled men
(custodians of the core positive values of the society) as they grace the
esplanade. There is a certain nonchalance about these men who look askance
at the ways of vagrants and would-be greats! Ask around and you will hear
"the arrival, or assured involvement, of these elders always gives a solemn
consecration to any communal occasion". I saw all of them at the village
square the other day, during the New Yam Festival.
Everyone was excited. The community was in the heat of the
celebration. Small masquerades were hopping all over the place. They were
chasing people and generally having a good time. Any causal observer would
think that these little masquerades were in charge, as they carried on with
a swagger that was almost impressive. I say "almost impressive", because
those who know masquerade pedigree in the village usually smile indulgently
at their temporary self-elevation. Should the Ijele masquerade suddenly turn
up, these small masquerades would all take to their heels and become part of
the crowd. They can only assume airs before the arrival of the Ijele, (the
masquerade that brings intimations of nobility and honour). If you go to a
village where most people know nothing about the Ijele masquerade you are
bound to meet a village square with a thriving industry of Ijele
mongers. Everyone, or almost everyone, would take the name of the masquerade
in vain. If you listened carefully, you would hear some people saying they
owned the Ijele. Others may well be calling themselves the Ijele.
I was at one such gathering recently, where I noticed that an old man in
the crowd was getting rather impatient with the uninformed talk about the
Ultimate Masquerade. He mounted a tree stump and began to speak, addressing
those who knew nothing about the masquerade. "The Ijele is not like any
other masquerade. In size, in height, in majesty, in "style", in dignity and
in grace, it is a class all by itself. It is everything that would make
those who know its symbolism to stand back, impressed and amazed at the same
time. Its "nature" suggests the radiance of a king. Its "Presence" suggests
the severe clamminess of deep waters. The strange mix of poise, unobtrusive
authority and proprietary nonchalance give the Ijele its royal standing
among masquerades. It is so sufficient unto itself that it does not dance
for money. Like a king, it does not define happiness in terms of always
having its way, even though it must always have its way. It does not
describe authority in terms of force. It does not see itself as superior to
other masquerades. It is simply the Ijele, the embodiment of all that
elevates the consciousness of the people."
People gathered to listen more attentively to the old man. The speaker
continued: "The Ijele that dances for money is a contradiction in terms. It
is simply not an Ijele and it will devalue the choicest gems placed all over
it as a decoration. Everything about it would then be an affront to the
deepest sensibilities of the people. It would be nothing more than a hollow
shell, unable to incarnate the "notion" of the Ijele. Because it does not
represent "the idea" of the Ijele, it will act out of character. In the
absence the Ijele, only the whims of the most dangerous masquerades are the
law in the village square."
Then, someone challenged the old man who was talking about the
Ijele. "Why don't you say something about Agboho Nmanwu, or Lady
Masquerade", queried the voice. "It epitomises the virtues of womanly grace
and maidenly dignity. It has the form of a beautiful young lady. Its manners
are unpretentious. The erect bearing, genteel poise and disarming look of
helplessness retrain even the most debauched of men from contemplating any
improprieties. Even Onuku retreated before the lady masquerade, because this
embodiment of blooming femininity does not suffer coarseness gladly. You do
not push this masquerade around during a stampede. Every effort goes into
shielding it, in the event of an outbreak of violence in the village
square. So why don't you talk about it? The old man smiled, but said
nothing. He knew Agboho Nmanwu does not approximate the Ijele.
Even the Agaba and Okwonma, both of which depict manly courage and
physical strength, beat hurried retreat once there is news that the Ijele is
on its way to the village square. Yet these masquerades, so easily reduced
to nothing by the Ijele, wield machetes and give the impression of a truly
intimidating overflow of power. There is so much force and threat of
violence in their movement. Everyone find out whether the menacing bearing
can translate into violence, as many who have encountered them by chance
came out thoroughly clobbered. If they seize a section of the village
square, everyone, including other masquerades, gladly concede the space in
order to leave the venue of the celebrations alive ... for as long as there
is no Ijele.
"Only the Ijele is an absolute or ultimate category", continued the old
man. "The concepts of beauty and ugliness, virtue or lack of it, strength or
weakness, etc., lose their narrow, defining rigidities before the inclusive
ambiance of the Ijele. People honour it as a symbol of unquestionable
authority. It accepts the exit of other masquerades from the village square
without thanking them. Yet it is not arrogant. It is its nature to live
thus. Just like the lion's mane is part of its identity but also a source of
worry to other forest dwellers, so is the very being of the Ijele a
proclamation of its superiority. It embodies royalty in the form of
steadfast, unbiased authority, which does not get into the gutter in order
to be understood by those in the gutter."
However, let us forget the old man on a tree stump for a while and look
at the Igbo 'village square". It needs the Ijele now, because it appears to
be remiss in its old tradition of mature contribution to national
discourse. It looks like we should remind the people that the Onuku
masquerade is never a source of inspiration. This latter masquerade does not
dance. It goes about without drummers and reminds you of degeneracy. The
very name "Onuku" roughly translates as "the stupid one". The very name
"Onuku" roughly translates as "the stupid one". Lecherous looks and an
unequaled capacity to sidle up to unsuspecting women and molest them in
whatever way it can mark Onuku. Its movement, as it walks, is wobbly and
uninspiring. Onuku has a sagging disposition and drools abominably. Pregnant
women to everything possible to avoid it. Any woman who sees this masquerade
prays not to have a son remotely resembling such a depraved and spiritually
wretched creature. The question: "do you want to be like Onuku" is often
enough to make a mother restrain an aspiring scoundrel of a son from the
path of folly and keep the young fellow on the slippery path of moral
rectitude. It always works like magic.
Ohaneze Ndigbo should rise from the dust where hitherto she had lain,
perhaps for too long. Many casual observers had grumbled incoherently when
the Ikemba summarily "dissolved" the body last year. Those who picked on
Ezigbo may have a point in the matter of how he did it, but the truth is
that he had little choice. It is impossible to order an Ijele out of the
village square, except its Iheleness is open to question. The reconstituted
Ohaneze Ndigbo should avoid the pitfalls that did its ancestors in. It
should not be a platform for ex office holders who lack grass root follower
ship, who make pronouncements on behalf of the people without gauging their
mood and who speak for Ndigbo without being in touch with them. It should
not join public debate by procuring some Igbo journalists on an ad hoc basis
(about whom it cared nothing up till that time) to defend it.
The "oha" component of Ohaneze must include what that word
signifies. Town union presidents, responsible and strong social club,
leaders of traders' association, credible media men and women, etc should
constitute the platform that would enable the people claim ownership and
defend it as a matter of course. Then it would be impossible for anyone to
affront it. But for as long as the body continues to intervene in the
political directives that are largely ignored by the majority of Ndigbo, for
so long shall it continue on the path of irrelevance. An Ohaneze that could
not persuade the south east to leave one person on the seat of the Senate
President, which could not present one candidate or prune the number of
aspirants for a position it spent all of four years "threatening" to grab
should be seen to be different from it recently reconstituted
reincarnation. The former was no Ijele. The latter is threatening to pass
off as one.
The rolling drama in the politics of the South East today says a lot
about political cohesion and dexterity. However, the theatrics and
permutations of that epic scandal is not our concern here. We are more
concerned that the Ijele, or the presumed Ijele, is under the weather. When
will it rise? How will it reaffirm itself? When will the people have the
pleasure, even honour, of helping clear the way, of saying "just the Ijele
is here?" Bated breath! Subdued excitement! The impatient looking hither and
thither! The implicit readiness to make way for the Ijele! All of that! Yes,
all of that and more are missing today. At no time has there been a more
desperate need for these things: for a true Ijele, in every sense of the
word!

Original_Search_Title: Let Our Politics Unite US
Headline: Ghana: Let Our Politics Unite US
Body_Text:
Address delivered by Togbe Afede XIV at a forum organised by the
Agbogbomefia and the Asogli State Council with representatives of the Volta
political party on October 31, 2004
We invited you here this afternoon to share some thoughts with you, make
public our concerns, react to certain developments and explain some of our
recent actions.
But we come to these ruminations after several years of living
continuously in this nation, and experiencing the inherent paradoxes of its
structures.
As we think back and look forward, nothing that we see is simple or
clear, and as salvation does not come through simplicities either of
sentiment or of system, nothing that we see, is without risk.
The gray, grainy nature of our existence and the ragged edges of our
lives as we actually live them, often defy order for a neat, bordered
existence. That suggests that our decisions or actions would not always be
received by a consensus.
It pleases some to say that leadership is the art of the ordinary, but I
choose to view leadership as "the art of the impossible." For leaders must
not only be able to determine where their moral compasses point, but also be
able to convince other thinking people of the rightness of the choices they
make.
We are afflicted now and have been for some time, by solo operators who
think the purpose of democracy is to mandate purity, and not as a means of
discussing among legitimate constituents to arrive at equitable, workable
balances.
But love and hate as Blake said are often fed by the same nerve, that
means that the desire for something more perfect can, in its intensity, deny
the freedom it would affirm.
So as we proceed towards the ideal of a unified nation, we have to be
mindful of the dual nature of the desire, that it can promote unity by its
intensity, but at the same time, that very intensity can alienate some of
the very people it aims to include.
While I would not like to think that elected officials do not take our
nation seriously, several events since independence at least, interfere with
my resolve in that regard. It is not just the Ghanaian citizens living in
the Volta Region that feel marginalized, for those in the North often
complain about being marginalized. But the critical dimension
to this malaise is that Ghanaians that do not belong to, or live in,
these traditional areas often think that we are outsiders, that we are less
Ghanaian, that we are separate nations at best, or that we have separate or
stronger allegiances to our proximate neighbors.
Our nation is at a crossroads. In the run-up to the December 204
elections, it is unfortunate that the appeal to tribalism and separateness
have become a strategy of choice. But we have to be very careful of those
that claim to be outsiders, indifferent people, disinterested people,
particularly when we see them clawing their way inside. Because that should
prompt us to ask ourselves if it is in our best interest to buy snake oil or
for that matter, "tenkalo."
We the Ghanaians of Asogli State are very concerned over this issue, as
all other Ghanaians should be, and naturally so. Between the peace our
nation has enjoyed over the years and the chaos that has engulfed some of
our unfortunate neighbours, the choice is clear. For embracing peace and
every behaviour and action that goes to promote peace is a prudent
option.
In that regard, we should all be very careful as representatives of
authority, particularly, of the national government and of traditional
governments, in our actions and utterances. We have to weigh these very
carefully and use consultation and inclusiveness to fine-tune what we have
to say, in order not to deliver meanings that differ from what we are saying
or doing.
Like all other Ghanaians, we in the Asogli area desire progress, and we
desire development. But these can only take place in an atmosphere of peace,
harmony, and dialogue. Development, and particularly economic development,
that brings hope for the future underpin peaceful societies. It is no
coincidence then that the most developed countries are the most
peaceful.
Our national elections are only six weeks away, and given what I have
said so far, our politicians should probably give more thought to their
conduct and ensure that their actions do not disturb the peace that has made
our nation stand out in a chaotic sub-region.
In our recent visit to the Castle, the President challenged traditional
rulers to stand up and speak against bad politics, and to stand up in
defence of the truth. I commend him for that as I rise to the challenge
because "evil gets done when good men stay silent."
ENSURING PEACEFUL ELECTIONS
In the run-up to, during, and after the December elections, particular
attention must be paid to the following suggestions: First, it is important
that we all endeavour to keep tribalism out of our politics. We should
remember that tribalism is at the root of most conflicts in Africa. Let us
also remember that the adverse effects of conflict do not discriminate among
others.
I want to call on the tribalists and their sponsors to re-examine their
objectives against our shared national aspirations. Let us resolve to put
our Ghanaian identity above everything else at this critical time in our
history.
Second, let us keep insults out of our politics. The sad truth is that
all the political parties and especially the two leading parties, are guilty
of this. Reference to our political opponents as devils, thieves and liars
have become commonplace. You cannot use such language and pretend to advise
your followers to do otherwise.
Third, let us tell the truth and avoid politics of deception. Here also,
all of you are guilty.
Fourth, let us recognize that every citizen has the right to
participate, and that the diversity of opinion confers on each one of us the
right to choose which party to belong to.
Fifth, let us, therefore, recognize the inevitability, and indeed, the
desirability of opposition. To desire that we all think alike is to wish
away an essential fact of life. Opposition keeps us from extreme behaviour,
opposition adds colour to the election process, and opposition makes victory
sweet.
Sixth, let us all endeavour to play it fair, especially on election
day. If everybody tries to cheat, we shall surely be pursuing the path
