towards self-destruction.
Finally, while as politicians you pursue power, you should also remember
that those whom you aspire to lead also have a right to the peaceful
enjoyment of their lives.
True democrats recognize that the desire to lead is not a matter of
life-and-death.
VOLTA AND EWE POLITICS - LEST WE FORGET!
It is not tribalistic nor is there anything wrong to endeavour to see to
the welfare and development of the people under one's jurisdiction. That, my
friends, is moral responsibility but under the law, it is a duty. Ghanaians
in the Volta Region desire peace and progress. Like all citizens, we are
entitled to a fair share of good education and health facilities, and the
hope that a government should bring to its citizens. We want a fair share of
good roads and other socio-economic infrastructure. Above all, we need good
job opportunities.
In that regard, we should receive with skepticism the politics that play
on the forces that divide us, that play on the sentiments that make us
uncomfortable, and instead, begin to welcome endeavours that not only seek
alliances within our nation, but endeavours that attempt to defy the borders
imposed on us by colonial governments and to embrace our brethren on the
other side of the imaginary line.
Brothers and Sisters, Togolese are our people as much as Ghanaians are
our people.
As we have said before, the politics of our region needs to be
interpreted within the context of the dirty and hate politics of the past,
especially during the transition to democratic rule in 1992.
Let us not pretend to have forgotten so soon the tribe-base political
slogans that were coined by short-sighted politicians.
Others took advantage of these slogans to tell Ewe Voltarians that they
would be pushed out of Ghana if a certain group comes to power.
This is the background to the general voting pattern in the Volta
region. Voltarians have become unfortunate victims of the dirty politics
from which they have not benefited much. We have become marginalized, while
some took us for granted, others feel that we do not belong.
BOMB THREATS
It is sad that some self-seekers are attempting to exploit our
unfortunate situation to cause unnecessary confusion, by threatening to bomb
certain parts of our region as punishment for the poor reception the
President received during some of his recent tours.
It is unfortunate that the reception accorded the President in some
places during his recent tour of the region fell short of expectation. But
who is to blame? Has the President's representatives sought to find the
reasons behind this and have they begun to correct whatever it was that
motivated such an unsavory bvehaviour?
As important, what programs have these representatives in the region
implemented over the past four years to correct the unfortunate impressions
created in 1992?
What has been the attitude of these representatives towards the chief
and the people of the region?
One thing for sure, our experience dealing with the President's
representatives over the past year has not been pleasant.
INSTANCES
* Until the President gave the order following our complaint during his
September 28 visit, all our efforts to pay a courtesy call on our President
were frustrated
* Earlier in the year, I asked the Regional Coordinating Council (RCC)
to kindly invite all regional and district heads of department so that I
could meet with them, explain our programmes, and ask for their support. The
RCC wrote back that they could not help us.
* Before the American Ambassador's recent visit to Ho, we had a hint of
the visit and a plot to keep us out of her itinerary. We did not believe it,
but it came to pass that she came to Ho without calling on us. We sent a
protest letter to the Regional Minister, but have since been ignored.
* The President's representatives in the region, including the District
Chief executives, failed to attend the Volta Congress, even though they were
invited.
* I had less than twenty-four hour notice of the President's September
28 visit. I was in my office in Accra on September 27 when my secretary
called me from Ho to inform me that he had just been informed that the
President would pay a courtesy visit at 8.30 am the next day. I left Accra
at dawn to be able to receive the President.
We gave the President a better reception than was possible under the
circumstances.
But our counterparts in Northern Volta were given enough notice to be
able to plan durbars. This is the nature of the politics practiced by the
President's representatives in the region.
Indeed, the President was on his tour during the Volta Congress and just
before our "Yam Festival" durbar at Ho, and the planners of the tour could
have worked to enable the President to attend one of these events.
The above are but a few of my unfortunate experiences dealing with
people who have been mandated to work for the progress of our region.
SOME RECENT ACTIONS
Visit to President Eyadema We went to our ancestral home, Notsie in
Togo, to honour an invitation from Togbe Agorkoli to the Agbogboza
Festival. We were pleasantly surprised when President Eyadema honoured us
with an invitation, at a time we could not see our own President.
The objective for going to Togo was to promote peace and understanding
between Asogli and its neighbours, the same reasons we were at the Ga Mantse
Palace and also visited various Volta chiefs earlier in the year.
VISIT TO THE CASTLE
We visited the President because we want the Volta Region to be
remembered and fairly included in the nation's development agenda.
The region, for far too long, has been a victim of the tribe-base
politics that has become the lot of Ghana. For example, notwithstanding the
very deplorable state of Ho roads, Ho is one of the only three regional
capitals whose roads were not prioritized in the 2004 budget. The other two
are Wa and Sunyani. We are concerned that major corridors that contribute to
trade and industry in regional capitals, have not been prioritized.
As traditional rulers who have a duty to help bring development to our
people, we have a duty to ensure that the region is not forgotten next time
round.
Several of our important roads have been in very deplorable states for a
long time. Examples include Sogakope-Ho, Ho-Kpedze, Have-Kpando and
Have-Hohoe.
I want to emphasise that we did not go to the Castle to thank the
President for sending to us Â¢2 million during our Yam Festival. It cost my
entourage of thirty-five people more than that to go to the Castle.
Nonetheless, on such a visit, it was customary that we acknowledge what
we were given, no matter how little. And in that regard, we acknowledge the
gesture.
During the visit, we expressed concern about the feuding among members
of Ho Central NPP simply because we are not happy about anything that
disturbs the peace in Ho.
OUR PUBLIC PROGRAMMES MONITORING COMMITTEE (PPMC)
We have established PPMC to support government and development agencies
in monitoring their projects in the region to ensure value for money. The
Ho-Kpedze road, for example, has been under construction for the past thirty
years but is still one of the worst in the region.
We want to protect government, the development agencies and the people
against exploitation. We established PPMC in response to several allegations
of misconduct in the execution of public projects in our region. PPMC is
non-political and is relevant no matter which party is in power.
Ladies and Gentlemen, the above presents some of our thoughts and
concerns and explanations of some of our recent actions.
Thank you for your attention.

Original_Search_Title: Telecoms: Challenges On the Pathway of Growth And Access
Headline: Nigeria: GSM :- Telecoms: Challenges On the Pathway of Growth And Access
Body_Text:
THERE is a negative development which popped up a forthright ago that
can even be used as a yardstick to measure the growth and performance of the
telecom industry. Vmobile in an audacious move is asking the courts to wind
up NITEL since the level of indebtedness of the latter to other operators
especially to Vmobile means that NITEL is no longer in a position to do
business.
Were this action taken about seven years ago, doting NITEL subscribers
would have ensured that Vmobile or its officials or both suffered physical
violence or any other action that would have carried its imprints for
eternity.
The reason for this is simple. NITEL was the beginning and the end of
the telecom industry. It was the alpha and the omega and it had the knife
and the yam firmly in its grip that it could measure out punishment to the
innocent subscriber or unplug from its network a business partner that grew
too ambitious. For telecoms, NITEL was the power house and since that power
has turned into an ant-infested pile of papers, another operator has risen
to initiate an obituary process of the former prince of technology
jungle.
The lesson from the NITEL/Vmobile encounter is that even a negative
development has become a useful tool to measure the growth of the country's
telecom industry. But what is this growth rate that people are excited
about? The story has become a global tale of success and heroism.
Here is an industry that hardly existed. Then came a reengineering of
liberalization the process which immediately led to a GSM auction that has
become exemplary to the rest of the African countries. After initial hiccups
encouraged by initial doubts over ability to make payments for license fee,
the story has become a fairy tale of success told by the ordinary man on the
street and in fact the only tangible fruits of democracy that they can
see. Some have argued that these were services that were supposed to have
been there, services shouldn't be used as an electioneering campaign
topic. But the reality is that the services were not there and for decades
the country just laboured to make do with about 400,000 connected lines from
NITEL and about three quarters of that number from the private operators to
serve a country of over 120million people.
This is the story the Minister of Communications, Chief Cornelius
Adebayo, went to tell in London recently. Speaking at CWC conference on the
Nigerian Telecom industry, Chief Adebayo informed that there has been a
sweet turning around of the industry with the number of lines hitting over
six million from a paltry 400, 000 about four years ago while investment has
hit a whopping $4.1million from a shameful low of about $500, 000million
within the same period.
These kinds of figures give an inducing feeling that the telecom
industry is in a cruising altitude at the moment. To this Engr. Ndukwe
during a a chart with Hi Tech, gave an emphatic no, saying that a lot still
remains to be done to solve the country's telecom needs.
In his words: "We are not on a cruise level now. I wouldn't say we are
on a cruise level. We are doing very well by comparing with our standard
before. We are doing extremely well because it is not just the mobile
operators that are attracting investment to the sector, even fixed operators
are attracting some investment. But billions of dollars are required in
order to reach that double digit subscriber base that we are talking about."
And this is where the current boom in the telecom industry really begins
from. When the civilian government came in 1999 after a protracted turbulent
period, the head of that government, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, made it quite
clearly that the telecom sector would be one the areas of focus by the
administration. Two reasons may have informed this; to make communications
easy for Nigerians by putting telephones in their hands, and through the
process attract investment into the country.
Both objectives are being met. But for this to happen the government had
to reboot the NCC through the appointment of a new leader that could bring a
visionary direction to the Commission. Engr. Ndukwe who was already doing
very well in the private sector at the time and also providing leadership to
the entire sector through the platform of ATCON became the perfect
instrument in the hands of the administration. Through him the Commission
was not only reinvented but indeed has repositioned itself to provide a new
direction which the continent is beginning to take as working sample.
What is available in the record book since the year 2000 when
Engr. Ndukwe took over as the EVC of the Commission is that there are four
companies providing GSM services, two national operators, NITEL as the
incumbent and Globacom as the second national operator (SNO), a number of
companies providing Fixed Wireless Services and more numbers providing
internet services. Nigerians have never had had it so good that they can
have a choice of phones few years after they were told by the soldiers that
telephone is for the rich.
In spite of this number however, Ms. Anne Bresnahan, boss of an Abuja-
based telecom company, Startech, told Hi Tech in her office last week that
even though the number of lines range in the neighbourhood of 6, 000,000
actual connections could knock the number to about 3million. And looking at
my hands, she asked: how many phones do you carry? At the time I had two
mobile phones on me and another was in Lagos, bringing the number to
three. The argument was not lost on Hi Tech. Ms. Bresnahan had taken us to
the field of quality which most phone users are complaining about in
Nigeria.
Throughout that week phones were literally dead in Abuja and she had
every reason to be cynical. Yet it is to solve problems like this that the
NCC had put a lot of checks and balances in place. The Commission insists
through the various actions and policies that there must be an acceptable
quality threshold.
For instance nobody can start any telecom operations in the Country
without a license from the NCC. Nobody can bring in any equipment including
handsets without a type approval from the Commission. And just of recently
it pegged an interconnection rate to encourage operators to drop rates. Also
part of the response to the problem of quality although on the long run, is
the establishment of the Digital Bridge Institute (DBI) which is targeted to
provide expert hands to solve various puzzles that exist in the
industry. The Commission has continued to hammer on the absence of skilled
manpower to adequately cater for the sector.
In the whole of the continent, the DBI has begun to play a prominent
role. The Consumer Parliament which brings together operators and consumers
monthly under auspices of the Commission is yet another effort aimed at
working on a common understanding that can impact on quality and the
over-all well-being of the industry. Little wonder that the ITU has hailed
it as a potent mechanism that could be adopted in different parts of the
world to resolve disputes between operators and consumers.
When the meeting opens at the Meridien quality may still remain a
cornerstone problem that has to be tackled head-on.
One other interesting thing is the belief of the Commission that
although Nigeria has been singled out for praise for her progress in the
Telecom industry, the country ought to be doing more on the driving
seat. For instance, the theme of this year's conference is: Connecting next
ten Million Nigerians. What the Commission has accepted through the theme is
that there remains a deficiency of telephone in telephone access in Nigeria
especially in the rural areas, a problem that needs to be handled
immediately. Engr. Ndukwe believes that we ought to get on with tackling the
next set of problems facing the industry.
According to him: "Another important issue is that our progress has been
mainly in voice communication. In the modern world, you talk about
ubiquitous networks. You talk about broadband connection. Very soon, when we
think we are bridging the digital divide and providing voice access to
people what we would be facing would be a world that is facing a broadband
divide."
Apart from the efforts of operators to put phones in the hands of the
Nigerian people, two efforts that may stand out on the side of government
through the instrumentality of the Commission is the Wire Nigeria Project
and the Universal Access Fund. While the Wire Nigeria project is to take
backbone facilities to the length and breadth of the country, the Universal
Access Fund is to provide a financial cushioning for those operators that
may want to take facilities to such remote places even if the operational
terrain may not be lucrative immediately.
Said Ndukwe: There is still a lot of work to be done in our country, and
we need now to start on how best to facilitate it. One of such projects is
our pet project called The Wire Nigeria Project. It is a concept that we say
that it is important that Nigeria has a widespread of fibre optics
infrastructure across the country. The network of fibre that we have today
are only limited to certain roads. We like to see under the Wire Nigeria
Project that every part of this country, no matter how remote is connected
to a backbone infrastructure, not just by radio but by fibre optics."
An operator told Hi Tech that the Universal Access Fund has been in the
front burner for sometime, yet no action has been taken in that
direction. It may be reasonable to hope that if the Commission wants to
achieve the target of 10million lines, this and many other issues will be
periscoped in the two day conference. Perhaps the Fund may feature in the
policy statements and path of growth for the industry in the next one year,
which the regulatory body will release at the forum.

Original_Search_Title: Issue: Govt, Labour Must Embrace Peace to Avert Another Strike, Says Uwazuruike
Headline: Nigeria: Issue: Govt, Labour Must Embrace Peace to Avert Another Strike, Says Uwazuruike
Body_Text:
CHIEF Hyacinth Ohazuruike, the Eze Igbo of Lagos State and the
president-general of Eze Ndigbo in diaspora marks the annual New Yam
festival, and uses the opportunity to speak on certain issues.
State of the nation
I believe that we are still learning the process of
democratisation. What we are experiencing presently is a by-product of what
happened during the unfortunate era of military rule in Nigeria. We
certainly have to overcome some difficulties in order to move the country
forward. I am convinced that the present civilian government under President
Olusegun Obasanjo is trying to build a solid foundation for us all. I
believe that after all these, Nigeria will be a place that everyone will
live and enjoy.
The feats so far
Now, government is talking about deregulation. The communication
eldorado otherwise known as GSM is also here now with us all. We are all
witnesses to the on-going sharp drop in their prizes and all these are
certainly to the benefit of the common man. We have not seen this before and
it is happening now.
I am aware that some people may not like the face of Mr. President but I
am convinced that at the end of it all, we will thank God. My candid advice
is that his advisers should wake up to their responsibilities. They should
endeavour to tell him the truth as it is, no matter how bitter. It is from
knowing the truth that he will adjust or stick to his programmes of action
for the betterment of Nigerians. After all, he is a human being like us
all.
Areas that need urgent attention
Many areas need urgent attention but the one that I will appeal
passionately to Mr. President to intervene in is the rehabilitation of
Onitsha/Owerri road. It is the main artery to major areas in the East ant it
should not have been allowed to decay to this extent. We were told recently
that about N6 billion naira have so far been expended on the road. I don't
think they are saying the truth because the road is the worst road in the
whole federation now and nobody should claim that such a huge amount of
money had already been spent.
On-going face-off with NLC
I believe dialogue is the best answer to the face-off. We have witnessed
so many strikes in Nigeria. We have those we elected into different
positions in the country and they should be able to intervene on our
behalf. That is the main reason why I insist that with dialogue, we will
certainly achieve the best for everybody. It is now incumbent on the
reconciliation committee to come out with quality advice that will
ameliorate these problems.
Igbo presidency
It is now the turn of the Igbos. We have waited for long and have been
patiently supporting other groups. It is now our turn and any ethnic group
that is saying otherwise does not love the country as one.
Fate of Igbos in other lands
As the President of Igbos in diaspora, I can assure you that this is
what we all are working on. We have been going round to other groups to let
them know that we are all brothers, that the Igbo man who has been investing
huge sums of his hard earned money in your land loves you. He is telling you
that he is at home with you. They should not be made scapegoats whenever a
little trouble rears its ugly head. There is nothing that dialogue cannot
do. It is obvious that they are not making trouble anywhere you see them
rather you say they make ends meet.
Festival
This is why we all celebrate our annual new Yam Festival wherever we
are. While doing this, we ensure that our brothers from other ethnic groups
are well represented so that they will enjoy our tradition and culture with
us and by so doing, it will strengthen our bond of brotherhood.

Original_Search_Title: Yobe, Nasarawa Donate N10m to Flood Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Yobe, Nasarawa Donate N10m to Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Gombe — Yobe and Nasarawa States yesterday donated N10 million to the recent Gombe flood victims.
The Yobe State Governor, Bukar Abba Ibrahim, made N5 million donation in cheque personally to the Gombe Stae Governor, Alhaji Danjuma goje on behalf of government and people of his state.
The Emir of Lafia in Nasarawa State, Alhaji Mustapha Agwai also made another donation of N5 million on behalf of Nasarawa State government and the people of the state.
Both Ibrahim and the Emir commiserated with the people of the state over the unfortunate incident.
They said although they were late to bring their assistance, but "It is better late than never."
Governor Ibrahim said Goje had been his friend for several years while his great grand parents hailed from some part of the state.
Apart from the N5 million donated by Nasarawa State, the state also brought five trucks of yam.
The state governor Goje thanked the two states for their assistance to the flood victims.
It would be recalled that about 20 people lost their lives while the families were displaced as a result of the disaster.

Original_Search_Title: Ignorance Bane of Nation-Cleric
Headline: Nigeria: Ignorance Bane of Nation-Cleric
Body_Text:
Lagos — The problems of the nation could best be ascribed to ignorance. Pastor
Emmanuel Eneayan, Senior Pastor, Arise and Shine Bible Church, who made the
submission in Lagos at the Church's seven day convention programme, argued
that the problems of the country were the direct result of ignorance, which
he labelled third slavery.
Eneayan said ignorance had in the first instance caused the black man to
be taken into slavery and to suffer a lot of discrimination, regretting that
when the white man was inventing ships, guns and aeroplanes, the blackman
was ignorantly producing canoes, hoes, bow and arrows.
"What we read on the pages of newspapers revealed that people are so
money conscious. Politicians are not after the interest of those they are
representing. Nigerian elections, when compared to that of America,
glaringly show that they were not the same. You cannot sway Americans with
money. But in Nigeria, people pursue money without thinking otherwise. Look
at the Okija Shrine saga, is it not human beings that were going there?
"It is ignorance too for anyone to think that his best in life would be
measured by the number of houses he had. Those people are not reasonable. To
me, I cannot take what belongs to another person. While I am looking for
more, I am working hard for more. And I am contented by it. I am not in a
race with anybody. That is what I expect Nigerians to emulate", Eneanya
stated, just as he advised that values in the country should be overhauled,
adding that instead of placing emphasis on owning property, emphasis should
be placed on impact.
"The advise I have for Nigerians is that people should first know what
is value. Look at the parastatals, how do you appoint a managing director,
fire him within a short while and hire another one. Ocourse, the in-coming
one knows that you will fire him as he is settling down, so, when he assume
office, what immediately comes to his mind is how to fill his
pockets. Because he has been put at the pedestal of the society as managing
director or director general of a big and well known parastatal, he does not
want to go back to the slum as a poor man. My advise is that our value
system should be overhauled", he stated, advising that the value system was
restructured based strictly on being rewarded for excellence.
On how to solve the present problem of Nigeria, the cleric urged
Nigerians to be prayerful as it was only prayer that can turn around the
heart of human beings.
"It is only prayer that can solve the problem of Nigerians. I did not
believe in revolution or firing. But if we humble ourselves and pray, God
will bless the country", the cleric, who is equally an insurance broker,
charged.
On the Church's decision to recently feed beggars publicly in Iyana
Ipaja, Egbeda and Dopemu areas of the city, he said the exercise, which they
tagged: "Operation Feed the Beggars" saw them cook all kinds of delicious
food, take them to the beggars on the road side, where they were begging, to
serve them.
Added to that, he said they even take raw food to them, including rice,
beans, chicken, turkey, onions, yams, salt, tomatoes, pepper and oil among
others, at Christmas, explaining that the convention was organised by the
Church to impact knowledge on people as well as intensive prayer for the
nation and Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: Elizabeth Ohene Talks Tough
Headline: Ghana: Elizabeth Ohene Talks Tough
Body_Text:
Ho — ... says Volta will fall to NPP
... poised to oust Agbots
THE dream of the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP) to win some seats in
the Volta Region, the "World Bank" of the opposition National Democratic
Congress (NDC) in the December polls is maturing into reality. The CHRONICLE
can report that in the face of the pervading new presence of the NPP, the
NDC can no longer maintain the dismissive posture it had adopted towards the
NPP threat.
A number of constituencies appear resolved to revise the region's
traditional voting pattern this time round "to give the NPP a chance." One
of such constituencies is Ho West where the reality of the revised voting
policy has become discerning.
The constituency, located in the Ho District, has been one of the areas
where the umbrella party had enjoyed exclusive support of the electorate and
is considered as one its strongholds.
But things have changed dramatically over the past couple of years and
our investigations show that the constituency has fallen flat to the NPP's
Elizabeth Ohene, Minister of State for Tertiary Education and one time
Editor of the " Daily Graphic" newspaper.
The incumbent Member of Parliament (MP), Mr. Francis A. Agbotse is
tagged as an under-performer as far as the development of the constituency
is concerned.
"Agbotse has not done anything for Ho West after being in parliament for
the past eight years, so why should I vote for him again?" Eric Numenyadro,
a resident of Kpedze, observed. "I will surely vote for Ohene but for the
presidential slot, I have not yet decided," he added.
Kpedze is the largest town in the constituency where the incumbent had
drawn much support in previous elections. However, the indications now are
that the journalist-turned politician is headed for victory this year. The
town is awash with posters and other paraphernalia of Ohene. Her posters
have been displayed even on commercial vehicles.
One other area where the NPP candidate enjoys considerable support is
Todze where a number of known supporters of the NDC have defected to the
ruling party. They include Richmond Amegboleza, Joseph Numenyadro, Phinolia
Morte and Fred Appau whose defection had come as a great surprise to both
NDC and NPP supporters due to his avowed opposition to the NPP.
Miss. Ohene's strongest support, by far, comes from Kpoeta, where The
Chronicle gathered that over-zealous supporters are determined not to allow
the posters of the incumbent MP to be advertised in any part of the
town. The minister's posters dominate the scene.
Speaking to The Chronicle, the NPP chairman of the Kpedze-Todze L/A JSS
polling station, Mr. Ephraim Appau, pointed out that during the previous
elections in 1996 and 2000, some people in the constituency declared that
even if a goat was made to contest on the ticket of the NDC, they would vote
massively for it instead of an NPP candidate.
He said in those times what mattered for the people, was the NDC and
nothing else. "This time round, we the people of Ho West have resolved that
we need a parliamentarian who will help move the development of this
constituency forward.
This time, we want development and not party, party matter."
Apau said though Miss Ohene was not the MP, it was clear that she had
been able to bring a lot of development to the area and, the people, having
come to terms with what the NPP candidate could do for the community, have
decided to give her their mandate this time round, to represent them in
parliament.
President Kufuor, however, does not appear to enjoy the same support in
the constituency, the NPP activist said, "because the NDC, as a party still
remains the stronger of the two."
It appears Miss Ohene's message has gone down well with the youth and
the womenfolk particularly.
The Chronicle gathered that she is spending quite a fortune helping
people in the area and donating generously at social functions.
At the recently celebrated Yam festival of the chiefs and people of
Honuta, a border town in the constituency, Miss. Ohene is said to have
captured a lot of admirers when she made a personal donation of Â¢5 million
as against Â¢2 million by the incumbent MP.
Surprisingly, Miss. Ohene who, until her political appointment by
President Kufuor, worked with the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC),
appears not to have as much support in her hometown, Abutia, where
Mr. Agbotse is rather said to dominate.

Original_Search_Title: Commodity Prices Hit Roof Top
Headline: Nigeria: Commodity Prices Hit Roof Top
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of foodstuff and other items have risen across the nation, not
even the smallest item in the market is spared.
Consumer Watch findings show that the price increase of these
commodities is causing discomfort for both sellers and consumers.
Market survey shows that the items affected are staple foods such as
rice, beans, groundnut oil, garri, elubo, palm oil, beef, etc. Even prices
of beverages and soaps have shot up.
Apart from the increase in the prices of petroleum products, the ban on
the importation of staple like rice, coupled with some companies' product
promos and the excruciating effect of the economy, are the causes of the
rise in prices of the commodities.
A market survey shows that rice is getting beyond the reach of the
masses.In major markets in the city of Lagos, a bag of various brands of
rice now costs between N5,000 and N5,500 as against the former price of
between N4,000 and N4,500.
The prices of beans is also giving consumers great concern.
Now, a bag of these brands of beans: Drum, Olo, Oloyin, go for between
N6,200 and N8,600 as against their former price of N5,200 and N8,000.
A big of yam flour (elubo) has jumped from N11,000 to N15,000, while a
small bag which cost N9,000 before now, goes for N11,000.
A tin of groundnut oil now costs N3,200 as against N2,700 for which it
was sold a few weeks ago. A tin of palm oil now attracts N3,000 as against
N2,600 it was sold before.Similarly, prices of garri 'Ijebu' and yellow have
increased from between N2,200 and N2,400 per bag.  Butchers, who lamented
that the high cost of cow, coupled with the rise in transportation fare, now
sell beef at a high cost.
Similarly, prices of beverages especially, Peak Milk, Bournvita, Nido,
Corn Flakes, Sugar, among other items have increased.A 400g tin of Bournvita
now costs between N300 and N310 as against its former price of N280. A 400g
tin of Peak Milk is sold for N330 as against its former price of N300, while
a 400g tin of Nido now costs N320 as against its former price of N300.
Also, a bag of Ariel detergent which was sold for N25 now attracts N35.A
housewife, Mrs. Funmilayo Olaniyan told Consumer Watch that housekeeping
allowance is now grossly inadequate to run the household.Most consumers who
spoke with Consumer Watch condemned the federal government for its
insensitivity to their plight, pleading with government to revert to the old
prices of petroleum products.

Original_Search_Title: President Not Insensitive, Says Nwokedi
Headline: Nigeria: President Not Insensitive, Says Nwokedi
Body_Text:
Awka — As the four day warning strike declared early in the week by the Nigerian Labour Con-gress (NLC) to press for the reversal of pump price of petroleum products ended yesterday, the traditional Ruler of Achalla Community in Awka North Council Area, Anambra State and the Chairman, the state traditional Rulers' Council, Igwe Alex Nwokedi, (Uthoko IV) has described as uncharitable the insinuation that President Olusegun Obasanjo is one sadist deriving pleasure from the suffering of Nigerians.
According to Igwe Nwokedi who spoke to newsmen in his palace yesterday in Achalla as part of activities marking the celebration of his community's 2004 New Yam Festival, the Obasanjo he had known over the years is neither selfish nor arrogant but does things for utilitarian good.
He underscored the need for closer relationship and or ties between the presidency and the labour movement in the country even as he noted that the strike didn't so much affect people in Anambra State because they had been buying fuel at exorbitant prices before the strike. What is worrisome, he lamented is the astronomical rise in the cost of a litre of kerosene, adding that with N65 per litre which it sells currently, many poor families are bound to suffer.
"It is not right to say that the presidency is insensitive to the sufferings of the masses. I have worked with him (Obasanjo). I know he is neither selfish nor arrogant.
He does things for the benefit of a greater majority of the people. But, I want to say that there is need for closer relationships and ties between the presidency and the labour movement. If you look around, you will discover that the strike did not so much affect people here because they have been buying fuel at very high prices", he said.
The Monarch noted that the different interest groups who mobilized the peole for the strike had differing agenda and purposes for going into the strike. "While some said, this Obasanjo government must fall, some others simply pursued their objectives", he said, adding that "historically when one embarks on the kind of reformation that President Obasanjo had done, you are bound to step on toes."

Original_Search_Title: That Falcons' Stay Back Protest: What a Colossal Shame!
Headline: Nigeria: That Falcons' Stay Back Protest: What a Colossal Shame!
Body_Text:
It was a bright looking morning as Monday, October 4, 2004
dawned. Ibrahim Galadima, chairman of the Nigeria Football Association, had
just finished his morning prayers and was looking forward to the trip back
to home. Once again, he had only good news to tell when he got back to the
country. The Sports Ministry refused to give the contingent money but to
their eternal shame, what he had on his lap was success and the African
Women Championship trophy was returning to Nigeria - its permanent
home.
Leaving his room at the Johannesburg Holiday Inn hotel, he honoured a
meeting with the leader of delegation and fellow NFA board member, Alhaja
Ayo Omidiran. "How are my girls?" he asked the former football club
owner. "They're fine. They're ready to go", replied the woman. Both
exchanged pleasantries with hotel staff and retired to their different rooms
to prepare for the six-hour flight back to Lagos, where Galadima was already
anticipating a presidential reception for the victorious girls.
Unknown to him, there would be nothing good about the morning. True, the
previous evening, the girls were a terrific delight as they ran Cameroon's
Lionesses ragged in the final of the 4th African Women Championship. Both
teams had played a 2-2 draw in a group phase match, before fate brought them
together again for the final.
The Cameroonians had boasted big, and without fear and courtesy, that
they would ground the Super Falcons to win their first continental
title. Two years earlier in Nigeria, the Lionesses were beaten in the semi
finals by Ghana. This time, they beat Ghana, and duly got the much-awaited
chance to "show Nigeria pepper".
Whatever that meant, it did not take long for the Falcons to turn the
pledge on its head. After 16 minutes, Perpetua Nkwocha, whom Vanguard Sunday
Sports predicted on the day would make Nigeria the perpetual winners of the
championship, scored the first of his four goals, and made it a hat-trick
before recess. Immediately after resumption, she got her fourth, with
diminutive Vera Okolo adding Nigeria's fifth.
"Now that we have won the competition, we can have 'mouth' to talk. We
were promised a certain amount of money and we will collect it here before
we go back to Nigeria", team captain Kikelomo Ajayi told her colleagues as
they left the stadium.
Not even Coach Godwin Izilien was aware of the decision. "I had no idea
what was going on. They didn't show me they were going to take such a
decision", said the coach, who was drafted from the U-17 team for the
assignment.
So, when Galadima met with the girls a few hours before they were
scheduled to leave for the Johannesburg International Airport, it was a
different tale he was confronted with.
"We are going nowhere until all our monies have been paid. The NFA
(officials) told us we would get our money when we get back to Nigeria. But
we refused to accept that because we don't know what will happen there.
"We can't fight them in Nigeria, which is why we are making the stand
here. If they have the money ready in Nigeria, why don't they send someone
over here with it and pay us? If they drive us away from the hotel, we will
stay outside", said Perpetua Nkwocha, as she spoke to the international
media afterwards.
Galadima, a sad man, returned to Nigeria, but would not blame the girls
for their action: "The NFA was not given any money for the tournament. We
sent a budget to the Sports Ministry and no money was given to us.
The girls are holding the nation to ransom and the situation is a shame
for Nigeria, not the NFA alone.
"After they won the Cup, I commended the players and gave them $3,000 of
my own money as a reward, in fulfillment of a promise that I had made to
them. They had not given me any indication that they were going to resort to
the measures they have taken".
But, how many soldiers reveal their battle plans to the enemy before
going to war?
Coming barely three days after Nigeria celebrated 44 years of flag
independence, the incident was the worst possible birthday present for a
nation that loves to style itself 'giant of Africa', though without anything
to concretize that claim.
"If a team is representing Nigeria, I would expect that it is the
absolute responsibility of the Federal government. I feel sad that such
happened. But for God's sake, let us learn to treat our athletes with
respect. It was wrong of the Sports Ministry to have refused to give the
contingent money.
"These girls have done the nation proud. They don't deserve to be
maltreated", said Chief Olusegun Odegbami, former skipper of the national
senior team, a former member of the NFA board and immediate past chairman of
governing board of the National Institute for Sports.
On the face of it, Nigeria has so much money that nobody would expect
such a huge slap on its face on the international canvass. Between 1965 and
2002, the country raked in $350 billion from sale of crude oil. What have we
done with that money, that we can't even boast of stable electricity, good
water, good roads, a healthy healthcare system, an enviable educational
system and surface rail? What did we do with all that money that seventy per
cent of our people can't boast of two round (forget square) meals a day?
The girls must have taken that decision because they knew what awaited
them back home. In an African society with the extended family system alive
and well, the girls knew they would have to 'hand out' to so many people,
relatives and friends and even well-wishers, on returning to
Nigeria. Everybody is hungry, except the fat cats in Abuja. Everybody is
looking for the next meal, and if you have a Super Falcon as friend or role
model, you would be 'salivating' for her to come back.
For God's sake, why was there no provision for the girls going for a
continental championship? Why was a promise of $6,000 each made for them to
win the competition when there was no cash in sight to redeem the pledge?
Why should the girls have been made to leave Nigeria at all without enough
money, in an era when our beloved (don't say cursed) crude oil is selling at
the highest price in the international market, if the Sports Ministry is
truly alive and well to its responsibilities?
Only a fortnight ago, FIFA - the supra-national football governing body-
threatened to suspend Nigeria from international football if the obnoxious
Decree 101, which makes the NFA a tethered goat controlled by the ministry,
is not dispensed with by the end of the year.
Nobody is taking that threat seriously. Or perhaps, the ministry wants
to make its stand clear and its message unambiguous, that the government
holds the knife and the yam, and will always control the NFA no matter what
FIFA desires.
It is unfortunate that in the heat of this embarrassing episode, some
people have been making statements that fail to meet with basic qualities of
common sense. One Pinnick Amaju, who addresses himself as Delta State FA
vice-chairman, claimed that the girls were being influenced by forces
outside the camp: "Honestly, the girls were ready to come back after the
victory... But surprisingly, they succumbed to the lobbying of some powerful
group which insisted that they must remain in South Africa to get what
belongs to them... I lodged in Hilton and the players stayed in Holiday
Inn. The group was all over South Africa and infiltrated the Super
Falcons..."
This is another instance of the immense disrespect that those in charge
of football administration have for our glorious girls. Amaju's contention
here is as hollow as some of his recent utterances. Is he saying the ladies
are incapable of simple deep thinking and deductions? Perpetua Nkwocha, who
won the Most Valuable Player and Top Scorer awards at the championship, is
28. Is a 28-year old incapable of setting up a scheme?
We remember that Jack Yakubu Gowon was 32 years when he became a
military Head of State and Commander-In-Chief of armed forces of the Federal
Republic of Nigeria in July 1966. Mobutu Sese Seko was of the same age when
he organized a putsch that swept him into power in the old Zaire.
How old is Joseph Kabila, the President of new Zaire (DR Congo)? How old
was Thomas Sankara when he became leader of Upper Volta, and changed the
country's name to Burkina Faso? How old was Muammar Gaddafi when he became
leader of Libya in 1969?
It can also be remembered that the girls had to 'shout' loud when they
went to the World Cup finals in USA last year, before the NFA sent two board
members to take their money to America. The duo arrived there on the day of
the last match and were held by security officials at the John F. Kennedy
for traveling with so much cash!
At the Olympics in August, money meant for the Falcons was diverted by
Sports and Social Development chieftains to 'console' their own army of
girlfriends who stormed Greece!
Who would blame the girls for taking such an action? Has anyone
forgotten the case of Prisca Emeafu, former vice-captain of the team, who
was shot by armed robbers at the Games Village during camping for a
tournament? She was made welfare officer of the team because she could not
play again, but she was not even in Greece with the Olympic team.
The earlier we accord the ladies some respect, the better it would be
for our football. The men have never been taken outside the country without
adequate provisions made for their welfare, allowances, bonuses and ticket
refunds. They were not owed money at the men's African Nations Cup finals in
Tunisia early this year. When they won a World Cup qualifier in Harare, last
month, their bonuses, allowances, flight ticket refunds and all was promptly
redeemed. The same will happen tonight in Gabon if they beat the
Panthers. What have the ladies done wrong? Nothing.
In 15 years of the team's existence, the Falcons have won the ticket to
represent Africa at the World Cup on all four occasions that these have been
offered (1991, 1995, 1999 and 2003), have picked thee sole ticket to
represent Africa at the Olympics on the two occasions this has been offered
(2000 and 2004), have won all four African championships held and won the
All-Africa Games gold the only time women's football has been part of the
Games (2003). What else must they do?
It's time we all realize women are not mere play-things; they are our
mothers.

Original_Search_Title: Panic Buying Begins Ahead of Strike
Headline: Nigeria: Panic Buying Begins
Body_Text:
As the stage seems set for an indefinite nationwide strike on Monday to
protest the increase in the prices of petroleum products following failure
to reach an agreement yesterday between the NLC and the government,
Nigerieans have embarked on panic buying of foodstuffs and other essential
commodities to cushion the effect of the strike on them.
P.M.News investigation revealed that major markets in the country are
now a beehive of activities as consumers trooped to them to buy goods whose
prices had risen slightly due to the rush.
At the popular Mile 12 market in Lagos, thousands of buyers were seen
haggling over the prices of foodstuffs like yam, rice, beans, tomato, pepper
and potato.
The same scenario was also observed at Tejuosho (Yaba) Mosafejo
(Oshodi), Mushin and Ajangbadi markets.
A milk cup of beans at the Tejuosho market is now N20 instead of the
former N15. The price of a bag of rice has also shot up to N5,800 from N5500
due to panic buying.
A tuber of medium-sized yam now goes for N150, while a bottle of palm
oil is now N150, at Oshodi and Mushin Markets. Some traders, however, blamed
the high prices on the looming strike and the impending Ramadan fasting
season.
Leaders of some civil society groups, like Comrade Chuma Ubani of the
CLO, had yesterday appealed to Nigerians to go to the markets and buy enough
food that could last them throughout the duration of the strike.
He told the masses to be prepared for a long strike, as it appeared that
the government would not heed NLC's demand to revert to the old pump prices
of petroleum products.
Ubani urged Nigerians to be prepared to suffer for a short period in
order to liberate themselves from the oppressive grip of an insensitive
government.
In his appeal to Nigerians, Dr. Beko Ransome-Kuti of the Centre for
Constitutional Governance (CCG), called on Nigerians to join the NLC strike
as from Monday so as to send a powerful message to the government that it
could no longer take Nigerians for a ride.
Ransome-Kuti said Nigerians must be prepared to fight for their rights,
"and the only way to demonstrate this is by complying fully with the NLC
directive to stay at home as from Monday, until the government reverts to
the old pump prices of petroleum products".
Comrade Segun Sango, Chairman, Lagos State Chapter of the National
Conscience Party (NCP), also appealed to Nigerians to stay at home as from
Monday.
He said the citizens must leave the government in no doubt that they are
opposed to the arbitrary hike in the prices of petroleum products.

Original_Search_Title: Stage And Screen:the Art of Drumming in Showbusiness
Headline: Nigeria: Stage And Screen:the Art of Drumming in Showbusiness
Body_Text:
Drumming is making controlled and intelligent sounds out of the echoes from hollowed chunks of dry wood or from vibrations of leather pieces stretched over woodholes.
In man's beginning there was wood. He built shelter, and made fire to warm his body and cook his meals. With wood and leather he made the drum from which he generates rythmic sounds to make his soul rejoice.
Drum making became a skill as he made the Jimbe, the Bata, Gudugudu, Iya'lu, and fontom from, Gangan. The most wonderful is the talking drum (gangan) controlled by rhythmic movement of the arm and the flexing of the wrist. The other drums are utilised in like manner.
Drummers assembled, communicated, interacted, and made sounds for dance, festivals, rituals, therapy and praise worship in churches.
The African related and communicated within and outside his community, expressing different emotions by the sounds he made with his drum. Drumming became an art and as the society grew, it became a vocation and a way of life. Families of drum makers became skilled in drumming. Drummers like Ayansola Alarape communicated and married his white wife even though he never spoke or write a word of English. Highlife produced skilled drummers like Remi Kabaka and Fela's drummer Tony Allen.
In nearby Ghana performer-drummers like Niapa Fleischer of the Ramblers Band, the Tettey Brothers, and the great Kofi Ganaba also known as Guy Warren toured Europe to propagate the art of drumming. I had the priviledge of sharing the stage with some of them. Soon after the Black Arts Festival of 1977 in Nigeria, I shared the stage with Kofi Ganaba, also in 1976 at the count down club I played with the Tettey Brothers.
The art of drumming developed even among white jazz musicians like Gene Crooper's dexterity on the jazz drums. But essentially drumming is an African art, and the advent of computerized drumming should not negate this.
In 1973 I established the "Calabash Artistes" in London, (An African Cultural Theatre Company) and we had an Afro Cabaret called Drumcall particularly for the exhibition of drumming. I hosted guests groups like the famous Funkees with Chike Madu, Sony Akpan, the late Jake Solo, Harry Mosco, Ofo the Black company, with Larry Ifedioramma, and his brother John, the conga exponent. I returned to Nigeria and re-established the Cabaret Drumcall backed by the skillful Ghanaian Daniel Kuranten at the University of Lagos. Drumcall conveys the poetry of the African backed by Drumming.
The role of Entertainment itself in a growing nation like ours is of great interest to the people and to the entertainers.
As the people tighten their belts and cry for bread, gari or yam, so their souls want to unwind the tension from hunger, ill-health, aggravations leading to temptation and criminal intents and acts.
Stage and Screen offer entertainment therapeutic and profoundly relaxing service to such nerve bombarding experiences, that humans go through every day. Support and encourage more drummers, entertain the nation.
Every weekend the National Theatre area of about 23,000 square metres is crowded with Nigerians whose souls are hungry for laughter, relaxation or just a mental change of picture and thought-frame.
Prominently standing on the landscape is an over 31 metres tall, multi purpose classic and elegant Bulgarian Masterpiece supposedly established for the preservation, presentation and promotion of art and culture which sums up to the establishment of an Entertainment Industry. The industry headquarters is defunct. No secretariat, and nothing has been declared openly.
The people pine for entertaining day outs, the practitioners want to stay alive working, surviving through their practice, and the main arena, the Main Bowl of the building is shot closed, unused for nearly 10 years!
Rumours of transfer deals breeze through the air and settle under the almond trees across the road from the main building... 'Abe'gi', where artists check every day for news about the industry. The latest news came over a year ago that the 'minor repairs' which had grown into 'major repairs' was being taken care of, and that the threatre shall remain a government concern for the masses and no more to be privatised. Artists celebrated the news at 'Abe'gi' I remember I had a beer and joined in the chorus. "We have overcome"! One year later, we are still sitting at 'Abe'gi' waiting to produce 'plays' for the masses. Such services medical or therapeutic should never be suspended all over. The people need it all the time. Visitors into the country want it every time they visit.
Artists want to be part of this democratic dispensation. We want to hear or read manifestos and promises that will be realistic and will attend to the well being, and the vibrant existence of an entertainment industry, and we shall vote and support any such government with vigour and style.
The artist believes in service, and also knows that living is fuelled by money, so he calls on the government to invest in art to build or beef up our economy. If privatisation is the answer to sustain a lucrative entertainment industry, then we the practitioners need to know and adjust to work along with the money-bags to run the affair.
We shall also want to know the lot that will befall the common man.
We strongly suspect that privatisation in show business is traditional, but the nation needs not privatise everything and leave the government with 'empty ministries and the military. The government should run a national theatre and allow the freedom of private companies to promote theatre.
The healthy rivalry will make for a rapid growth of the industry.
Indeed the sooner we begin to export our 'entertainment' the sooner our economy will improve. Why should the government throw in the towel just because a building develops a leakage? Our film industry needs revival. The Big Screen should not go blind, so bring back the cinemas.
The practitioners sincerely wish to work closely with the Lagos state government to turn this state into a "Las Vegas", or giant entertainment port of Africa. Lagos has too many untapped entertainment sources that should not be allowed to remain dormant. What with the islands and the coastal areas from Badagry to Epe and the Ocean beaches.
The main Island of Lagos is an auditorium of immense complexity. Areas of performances stretch from the marina through Nnamdi Azikiwe, Tinubu Square, Campos to Lafiaji Cinema Halls and Recreation Centres can serve.
We appeal to the Lagos state to buy the National Theatre off the Federal Government . FG may insist on building another in Abuja. The theatre will not just be a monument but will definitely give good service to the people and make money for the government.
Taking the capital away from Lagos, does not cart out her commercial importance. Lagos will always swing like a pendulum swings, in control.The subject of the National Theatre has almost become stale but no national issue should remain unattended for the sake of the people. The state of the Nigerian entertainment industry needs attendance for the growth of the nation.
Nigerian artists should swing their votes towards survival and representation to help build a vibrant economy for us all.

Original_Search_Title: Monarch Warns Against Artefacts Destruction
Headline: Nigeria: Monarch Warns Against Artefacts Destruction
Body_Text:
Enugu, Anambra — Adherents of the Christian religion have been urged to stop destroying artefacts and cultivate the culture of mutual respect and understanding with the traditional religionists, to ensure peace, harmony and progress.
Traditional ruler of Umunze, Orumba South Local Government Area of Anambra State, Igwe Promise Eze, made the call during a traditional forum to mark the New Yam Festival, noting that it was wrong for Christians to destroy artefacts, thereby wiping out the cultural relics of the people.
Igwe Eze frowned at the destruction of such artefacts as Ikoro (wooden gong), which has served the people for upwards of four centuries and serves diverse purposes, including communication and music.
He reminded that culture was a peoples' identity, which marks them out from others, adding that Ndigbo should not be in a hurry to lose what they have as their cultural identities.
He, therefore, called for the revival and strengthening of the good aspects of the Igbo culture including the Mmanwu (Masquerade) festival and the Iriji Ndigbo (New Yam Festival).
He suggested that such celebrations should be incorporated into the annual Igbo Day and developed into a tourist carnival, as obtained in some countries such as Brazil.
Igwe Eze used the occasion to appeal to the Federal Government to rehabilitate the federal road that passes through the area, to enhance movement between Umunze and neighbouring communities.

Original_Search_Title: The Agony of 'Becoming a Woman' Through FGM
Headline: Kenya: The Agony of 'Becoming a Woman' Through FGM
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The wound inflicted on Evangelina Kangai 26 years ago, when she was in
Class Four may be healed, but her heart skips when she remembers the ugly
ordeal.
She says: "We were just about to close school when my paternal aunt
forcibly took me to the river early one morning to join a group of eight
other age mates. We were stripped naked and dipped in the cold water to numb
the body and reduce the searing pain of circumcision.
"My aunt and other women escorted us to an open field to face the mutani
or circumciser in the Kimeru language. A single razor was used to do the
operation by people oblivious of the inherent medical and reproductive
health risks."
Kangai, 34, now a mother of three, underwent clitoridectomy.
After the "crucifixion", excited mutanis took us home, blood oozing down
our legs, to signify our becoming a "woman".
On the third day, her minder used leaves to spray warm water on the raw
wound to soften the dry blood, then applied cow milk cream for three weeks
to keep the wound soft. To hasten the healing, Kangai remained in bed, legs
apart.
During that time, she fed on porridge and water only as solid food would
have meant re-opening the wound during long calls. Passing urine was an
ordeal.
During the last week of August 1978, her mother was asked to buy yams
for her minder and friends to celebrate her graduation into womanhood. It
was during this ritual, Kuwata Mwanki, that the newly- circumcised girls,
mwari, were considered total women and allowed to talk to nthaka
(circumcised men). Their initiators also instructed them to reject all male
advances lest they were mistaken to be "cheap".
Before school re-opened for the third term, the girls were bought new
clothes, shoes and headscarves - a sign of recent graduation to
womanhood.
The older girls who had been circumcised earlier welcomed them
happily. The nine initiates stayed in the same group with the older ones as
their mentors.
After sitting her Kenya Certificate of Primary Education (KCPE)
examination in 1982, Kangai joined St Kizito, now named Cyprian High School
after the rape and fire catastrophe of 1991. She later joined Kilimambogo
Teachers' Training College and graduated as a teacher. where, on graduation,
she was posted to Muthaara Primary School where she teaches to date.
The horrors of FGM revisited But the horrors of FGM revisited her when,
in 1989, she got married to John Kaiyongi, an Army officer. in 1989, an army
officer based in Isiolo. "I dreaded sex. Every time we made love, it was as
if the wound was still fresh.
I did all I could to suppress the pain but in vain," recounts Kangai,
adding: "My husband would be upset with me and felt cheated because I was
unable to satisfy him. This frustrated him and for, some time, our marriage
looked set to hit the rocks until we got counselling which greatly improved
our relationship."
Kangai says her loving husband now supports her anti-FGM campaign
because he has first experience of what it can do to women. "He knows how it
deprives women of their natural right to enjoy sex."
But this was just one problem - childbirth was yet to come, which was
the climax of the pain. For all her three children born in 1989, 1991 and
1996, she had to be operated on her vaginal tract had to be cut to widen the
passage for safe delivery.
When in 2000 she attended a workshop on the negative social practices,
sponsored by Plan International, it opened her eyes to the suffering women
go through as a result of FGM. Kangai decided to break her silence, which
paved the way for her psychological healing.
She openly talked to girls about the negative aspects of FGM but the
community was bitter with her for defying tradition.
With the support of three fellow women, Kangai formed Kiambiria kya Weru
(Mwanzo wa Mwangaza) to campaign against FGM. Their crusade has met with
resistance from the community and they have been called prostitutes. But
they are making progress, albeit gradual.
With help from Plan International and Tigania Cultural Development
Association, the group has managed to enlist 80 girls who have undergone an
alternative rite of passage to campaign against FGM.

Original_Search_Title: Police Bans New Yam Festival in Issele Mkpetime
Headline: Nigeria: Police Bans New Yam Festival in Issele Mkpetime
Body_Text:
Asaba — DELTA State Police Command has banned the celebration of new yam
festival in Issele Mkpetime town in a move to forestall breakdown of law and
order in the community following security reports that two warring factions
in the community were spoiling for a showdown.
There had been rumblings within traditional ruling hierarchy over
unresolved issues, which degenerated to the reported suspension of the Iyase
(Prime Minister) of the community by the traditional ruling council but the
Iyase himself refused to bow.
The Police had recently stopped public procession in connection with a
similar celebration of the new yam festival in Asaba.
Spokesman of the state police command, Mr. Victor Obasuyi in a statement
in Asaba yesterday said "the annual yam festival of Issele Mkpetime is
supposed to take off anytime from now. As a result of the conflicting dates
by two factions in the community, there is likely to be a breakdown of law
and order if not properly handled.
"The command will not fold its arms and watch any person or group to
conduct themselves in a manner which is likely to cause a breach of the
peace. Against this background", the statement added, "the disputants are
advised to eat their new yam in their homes if that will create an
albatross. Therefore, public gatherings and processions for the purpose of
this festival al is hereby banned until further notice", the statement
added.
Bans fuel price hike protests
Meanwhile, the state police has allegedly threatened to deal ruthlessly
with anybody caught demonstrating over the recent fuel price hike, saying
that such action would be treated as illegal without obtaining a
permit.
A statement by the state Police Commissioner, Mr. Charles Akaya stated
that "it has been observed that the pump price of petroleum products had
been marginally increased by marketers and Nigerians have been expressing
their opinions from various perspectives.
"The Public Order Act of 1979 provides that public rallies, processions
and meetings will be illegal if a permit has not been obtained. Applications
for the purpose should be processed to the office of the Commissioner of
Police 48 hours before the occasions for consideration and anything done
contrary to the provisions of this act will not be tolerated", it
warned.

Original_Search_Title: Why They Want to Kill Ojukwu And Me
Headline: Nigeria: Why They Want to Kill Ojukwu And Me
Body_Text:
Chief Ralph Uwazurike, the leader of the new Biafra agitation, speaks to Uba Aham on the threat to his life and Dim Emeka Ojukwu's. He reveals the man to be used for the operation, the reason for his agitation and other issues.
What was your relationship with Ojukwu like before 1999 when you
began the Biafran struggle?
By 1999, I had stayed with Ojukwu for eight years.
Closely?
Sure!
As what? Were you his employee?
I was his disciple. I was his boy. I was his aide. I mean, Ikemba
is my master.
That is, you drew your inspiration over Biafra from Ojukwu?
You know, I have three heroes: Mahatma Gandhi, Martin Luther King,
and then Ojukwu. Incidentally, both Gandhi and Luther King have died.
The only hero of mine that is alive is Ojukwu. So, when I came back,
knowing I was going into the Biafran project, the first thing I did
was to get acquainted with him. And, we worked quite closely,
especially, as he is my hero. So, I did everything to get more
knowledge about Biafra from him. I mean, first hand information, not
speculations. I studied the man that handled the first Biafra,
studied if there was any mistake that made us not to actualise it.
But, ironically, he did not know.
What aspect of Ojukwu's life turns you on?
I have written a book entitled: The Struggle for Freedom which is
largely devoted to Ojukwu. If you are close to Ojukwu, you won't like
to miss him for one second. If you are staying with him, you will
find out he is a crowd puller. This is because if you go to him, you
must not go out empty handed from him. You must learn one thing no
matter what time of the day. And, you must be happy throughout. Even
if he is annoyed, he will turn that annoyance into a happy
atmosphere. You learn a lot from his annoyance. So until now, each
day I miss him, I feel it.
Let me give you an instance: One day, I was staying with him. You
know, usually he has an 'Atlantic load' of programs - a lot of people
wanting to see him. But some of us who are close to him would sit
while he attended to his visitors. One day, there was this boy from
somewhere in Umuahia, Abia State. Ojukwu asked his attendants to
bring the boy in. The boy came in. Ojukwu asked him: 'You say you
want to be my gateman, but you are epileptic. How can you be my
gateman?' The boy did not say anything. I was marvelled. So, Ojukwu
pressed the bell. The attendant came.
Ojukwu instructed him:'Take this boy to my doctor. Tell him to take
care of him and send the bill.' While the boy was going out with the
attendant, Ojukwu said, 'My friend, when the doctor must have cured
you, you come back, we talk about your employment.' This is what I am
talking about Ojukwu. When the boy left, Ojukwu told me that he wrote
a letter that he wanted a job to cure his epilepsy, that his parents
were dead. Ojukwu said the letter touched him. Then he asked his
Personal Assistant to invite him, that he wanted to cure the boy
first before employing him. When I was coming home that day, I was
asking myself a question: 'Can I do that?' How many Igbo men can
invite an epileptic boy into their offices or employ him? I never
knew this would happen. I just went to his office, saw it and he must
have been doing this for millions of people without our knowledge.
This happened in my presence.
There must have been other similar kind gestures of Ojukwu you
have witnessed?
Let me tell you again. My association with Ojukwu affected my life
greatly. My wife even realised it. My wife realised that I began to
respect her more when I started moving with Ojukwu.
How?
I give an instance again. The incident that made me start
respecting my wife the more, I learnt if from Ojukwu.
Not that he taught it to me as a lesson or something, I observed it
from him.
The Ikemba is reputed for his great respect for the womenfolk...
Yes, respect for women and, of course, for everybody.
The epileptic was a man, but he had respect for him. This particular
day, I was staying with Ojukwu. That was in Lagos. One of the air
conditioners upstairs was faulty, and, incidentally, that was his
wife's room. Somebody came to repair the air conditioner. But then it
was like that she was having a rest. He asked the technician to wait
until after the wife had woken up. The man had to wait for so many
hours. The man even went and came back. He told him he could not go
upstairs because his wife was having a rest. Incidentally, the man
went home. So, when I was driving home, I was telling myself: 'If it
were me, would I have gone straight to wake my wife? After all, it is
her bedroom, and it is her air conditioner. I was doing her a favour.
But, look at Ojukwu's respect for his wife. Even when I came home and
related the incident to my wife, she said, 'look at how men should
behave towards women!' So, these are things I learnt from Ojukwu -
respect for people, for women, for men.
What was your first contact with Ojukwu?
No, I won't tell you this one.
During the Nigerian civil war, you must have been a small boy. How
old were you?
I was about nine years old.
So, you wouldn't have fought in the war?
I didn't really. I wanted to enrol in the Boys Company, but I was
rejected twice.
In fact, Ojukwu is the only prominent Igbo man who speaks out in
support of MASSOB and its activities. Others do this with hushed
tones. What's your impression about this?
You will understand one thing. Ojukwu is the greatest Igbo man -
dead or alive. Most of these Igbo elites you are talking about, they
still talk to me, but they won't like anybody to see that they are
talking to me . You know, when they want us to meet, they will say,
come midnight. Please, don't come with anybody But, Ojukwu (whom can
he be afraid of)? Okay, to be very honest, whom in Nigeria do you
think Ojukwu should be afraid of? Nobody! Who in Nigeria is equal to
Ojukwu? Let us leave ethnicity, tribalism and all that.
Who in Nigeria today is equal to Ojukwu? Nobody. If you ask an Hausa
man, if he is sincere, he will tell you, 'nobody.' If you ask a
Yoruba man, if he is sincere, he will tell you, 'nobody.' I say this,
not because Ojukwu is my hero.
When you look around, you ask yourself, 'who in Nigeria today is
equal to Ojukwu?' The answer is:'nobody.' Then, has he reason to be
afraid of anybody, to start with? That is why he can come out and say
anything which he feels is right. So, nobody will intimidate him. Who
are you to intimidate Ojukwu? But, other people will fear that
Obasanjo will kill them. All these people at Aso Rock will kill them.
But, all those people at Aso Rock fear the name, 'Ojukwu.' Ojukwu can
be the only person to come out openly to acknowledge support for
MASSOB because he is Ojukwu. And other persons in Igbo land cannot
say it because they are not Ojukwu. Ojukwu says it and goes free, and
if they say it, they suffer repercussions because, one, they are
cowardly. Because of their cowardice, they will even start dying
before anybody thinks of punishing them.
Do you think Ojukwu gets what he deserves from his Igbo kinsmen
and beyond?
I don't think so. He deserves more than he is getting.
At times, I think God just created Ojukwu the way he wants him to be.
When you even talk about it, what can you give to Ojukwu? What can
you give to him that he doesn't have more than you? This is somebody
who was born into affluence. He jettisoned it, and joined the army as
a recruit, even at a time the army was lowly regarded.
But how are you reconciling your conflicting ideology with
Ojukwu's? You believe in non-violence, but Ojukwu believes that
Biafra cannot be attained without some form of violence.
From your perspecptive. I don't see Ojukwu as a violent person.
What happened from 1967 to 1970 was accidental in the sense that he
acted in his capacity as the Governor of Eastern region then. His
people were massacred all over Nigeria, especially, in the North and
in the West. It was his responsibility to safeguard the lives of his
people.
So, he had to take the decision. He had to take the final decision
because the buck stopped at his table. That, to me, didn't mean
actually that if all things being equal, he would have gone to war.
And, remember again the Aburi Accord. Why the Aburi Accord? Because
Ojukwu was looking for all avenues to settle the matter with Nigeria
amicably.
And, if General Yakubu Gowon, Nigerian head of state then, had
accepted the Aburi Accord, there wouldn't have been war. It was a
violation of the Aburi Accord that precipitated the war. So, nobody
should classify Ojukwu as violent. I don't see him as such.
Is your life under threat following the success of the 26 August
sit-at-home protest?
I have told you that each day that passes, I thank God for sparing
my life. There have been threats on my life.
Can you give instances?
There was this last minute security report I got where, in Aso
Rock, they conspired to kill Ojukwu and myself. The conspiracy was
with one of the frontline presidential aspirants now because he was
told he would have problems if he should emerge as president 'arising
from the MASSOB clamour for Biafra.' And he has accepted to sponsor
our elimination. That, also, informed the sudden invitation to Ojukwu
to attend one book launch at Owerri recently.
Because they agreed that this time around it will not be by shooting
of gun, and that it will be by other means. So, there are always
threats.
Have you taken measures to safeguard your life?
I leave everything to God. You see, when you think of taking
measures and all that, ordinarily, if God wants to kill you, he will
kill you. If God thinks that what I am doing is against His will, and
He wants to take my life, there is no problem. But, if God doesn't
will it, no matter what anybody tries, he won't succeed.
You mat have made mistakes in the course of this struggle, what
were those mistakes?
Each time I remember the killing of my men, I feel sad.
That was why I stopped rallies. I saw it was an avenue the federal
government was using to kill my boys. Now that I've embarked on
sit-at-home, it is even working more than rallies. At times, when I
look back, I begin to imagine that if really I didn't call for
rallies, perhaps, I wouldn't have lost so many people. That's the
only regret I have. Otherwise, I haven no regrets.
Recently, the attorney general of the federation said the
sit-at-home protest you organised amounted to treason.
The man should be reminded that, about two months ago, Easterners
and Igbos in particular were massacred in Kano, and we are looking up
to him to charge those who massacred them to court. People were
arrested, and those who were arrested have been released since then.
None of them have been charged to court. These are people who killed
thousands and thousands of Ndigbo. Almost always, they have done that
in the North and gone free. Since they have been killing Igbos in the
north, nobody has charged any of them to court. Or even executed any
of them for murder. I am asking the attorney general to charge the
recent killers of Ndigbo in Kano to court. For the past six months,
MASSOB members who were arrested in different parts of Nigeria and
taken to Abuja are languishing in jail, even when the courts asked
them to be released. He is still keeping them.
The 26 August 'sit-at-home' protest by your organisation, the
Movement for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra,
MASSOB, was a success. How did you do it?
Well, I give thanks to God. It was God who did the magic. Like I
keep on saying, I give the credit to Dim Chukwuemeka Odimegwu Ojukwu
who, in the first place, put together the word 'Biafra'. I discovered
on 26 August that quite unlike what people think about Ndigbo, they
can work together. The only thing is that if they doubt your
integrity as their leader or, sometimes, you have told them lies, or
they see you as a representative of their oppressor, they will not
take instructions from you or obey your commands.
But, if they see you are committed to their overall interests, they
will listen to you. That was what happened on 26 August.
But, beyond this, what mechanisms were put in place to ensure such
compliance?
There are two things there. The first is what I have told you: If
the people believe in you as their leader, and they trust you.
Remember, the stay-at-home' was not compulsory. We made it clear that
it was voluntary. But everywhere was completely shut. The reason is
that we in the struggle are committed.
Secondly, people tend to forget the fact that the membership of
MASSOB runs into millions. When you talk of traders, who are the
traders? They are MASSOB members. You talk of Okada riders, who are
they? They are MASSOB members. I tell you, if there are 1,000 shops
in Ariaria International Market Aba, Abia State, 700 of them are
MASSOB members. If there are 4,000 shops in Onitsha, Anambra State,
700 of them are MASSOB members. So, if you say 'close', you see that
two-thirds would obey. The only people who might pose a problem could
be the three or four executive members working for the governor as
leaders of the market.
Otherwise, others are MASSOB members. We run into millions in the
entire South East and South South. That was why when I called for the
'sit-at-home' protest, I was confident that it would be a success
because I know how much we are on ground.
MASSOB drew up a 25- point programme towards the realisation of
Biafra. Which stage was 'sit-at-home'?
We are still on stage four. You see, like I have said, it is not a
matter of counting one, two, three on the finger tips. No, it doesn't
go that way. A stage can last five to ten years, depending on what to
expect from it. If all that is expected in a state has not been
accomplished, the stage starts all over again. So, there are certain
things we want in stage four. I am happy that, actually, we have
succeeded in getting some of them, but not all of them. We are still
on state four.
So, it is not definite when stage four will be exhausted?
Until we hold our national meeting. During the meeting, we shall
take stock of the successes so far recorded. We shall also examine
areas of improvement.
What level of casualty did your organisation record during the
sit-at-home protest?
No. There were no casualties. Not even a single arrest on that
day.
MASSOB members are regularly clamped into detention. Some have, in the process, lost their lives. Can you quantify the
losses-both human and material - in the past four years' agitation
for Biafra?
My brother, I don't know how to begin answering this question. At
times, it is confounding to see how the Federal Government clamps
down on a nonviolent organisation like MASSOB when we had, throughout
our existence from 1999 till date, not killed anybody. We have not
burnt anybody's house. We have not destroyed anybody's property. We
have not killed any soldier. We have not killed any civilian.
We have not killed any policeman. Still, they clamp down on us. Up
till now, they are holding more than 300 of our members in Abuja,
irrespective of a court order that these people should be released.
On one occasion, the policemen in Abuja even beat up the bailiff when
he went to serve them. Look at the type of country Nigeria is!
You arrested more than 200 MASSOB members who were on a clean- up
service, clamped them into detention. The court ordered you to
release them. You didn't release them.
You didn't charge them to court. Why? Because you want to humiliate a
tribe. You want to see them annihilated.
When we come out for a rally, you shoot at them, kill them without
any reason. You just shoot at them and kill them. I mean, it is
