dumbfounding.
So, how many Biafran agitators would have lost their lives in the
past four years?
It is unimaginable. More than 1000 people would have been killed
by the federal government.
The bodies of your members who were, in 2001, killed by the Police
in Okigwe, Imo state, are reportedly still lying in the mortuary
without burial. What is the situation now?
I learnt that after sometimes, they gave the corpses mass burial
along Aba-Owerri road. They claimed that only eight MASSOB members
were killed when they killed over 2000. We said, okay let us carry
the corpses.
They knew that if we should carry more than 200 corpses out of the
mortuary, the people would revolt. Then, they sneaked the corpses
away and buried them.
About how many times have you been arrested and detained for
Biafran cause?
I can't remember. I have been in and out of detentions.
Have you been intimidated by the harassment?
Intimidation? I don't like the word, 'intimidation.' Nobody
intimidates me. When they arrest me, I have the impression that they
could also kill me. It is just like each morning I wake up I think
I'll die that same day. If I live to the next day, I thank God for
sparing my life. So, intimidation does not mean anything to me.
Dim Emeka Ojukwu supports MASSOB. But he believes that Biafra may
not be realised at the moment. How do you react to this?
Ojukwu supports the cause of Biafra. Biafra is his child. He is
the originator. And, he didn't make any mistake by bringing up
Biafra. That he supports Biafra, I am not surprised. I am sure that,
even in his old age, he will be the happiest person to see that
Biafra has not died.
But he believes that Biafra cannot be realised at the moment?
I think you are referring to a recent interview he granted before
26 August. If you go back to him, I am sure he will sound
differently.
Ojukwu also believes that Biafra will not be handed over to the
people on a platter of gold. Biafra, he thinks, has to be struggled
for?
Well, Ezeigbo is a retired general. You know, if you begin to talk
of nonviolence or all that, he will be laughing at you. But,
nonviolence strategy is very powerful. It is just like when we say:
'The pen is mightier than arms and ammunition.' Let me illustrate
with what happened on 26 August. People were told to either go about
their businesses or stay at home. Everybody stayed at home.
Supposing it was for seven days, what do you think will happen? India
secured her independence through the process that I am following. I
left Nigeria for India to understudy the process of actualising
sovereignty by a peaceful process. I spent close to 10 years
understudying this. So, I think I am an expert at it.
I am not a soldier. I didn't fight in a war front, but I know the
concept of nonviolence. And, I've seen issues resolved by nonviolence
as opposed to violence.
I have seen the workability. I believe it would work for Biafra.
Let's recapture the genesis of the new Biafran struggle. What motivated you?
I felt the war Nigeria waged on Biafrans between 1967 and 1970 was
unjust. And, the way Nigeria executed the war by blockading our
people, subjecting them to suffering was bad. Immediately after the
war, they were raping our sisters and killing our men who were
returning from the war front.
I felt it was unjust and that if I grew up, I would come up with the
Biafran issue again. So, it was burning in my mind all through, even
up to my secondary school and up to the time I was to go for higher
education. I chose to go to India to explore means of bringing up the
issue of Biafra.
After I had read how Mahatma Gandhi used nonviolence process to
actualise Indian independence, a lot of things gingered me. The
climax or the immediate cause was the ingratitude Obasanjo showed to
Ndigbo after the 1999 election. The Igbo have been marginalised in
Nigeria after the war. Since then, it has been primarily military
governments. We said, 'okay, let us try in a democracy and see what
happens.' PDP was formed by Dr. Alex Ekwueme through G-34. Then, the
powers-that-be felt that an Igbo man should not be president.
They went to the prison and brought out Obasanjo who was serving his
jail sentence, rigged the election and made him president. But, to
show how supportive Igbo can be to the so-called one Nigeria, they
voted en-masses for Obasanjo.
The South East gave him the second highest vote to emerge president.
But then, amongst the 12- member Security Council, there was no Igbo
man. And when the Igbo agitated, Obasanjo said that a people defeated
in a war are not supposed to talk for 200 years or so. I said fine,
you think we are still in that war. We better begin it than making
inferences to the war. So, I said, fine, you think we are still in
that war? We better begin it than making inferences to the war. So, I
said, okay, if you people have not forgotten about the war, why
pretending? Let's start Biafra all over again. That's it. I don't
want to talk about the neglect for the past 34 years (the
infrastructural whatever in Igboland, the impassable road; this and
that). I am just telling you the immediate cause.
The Abuja branch of MASSOB recently listed some vital positions
which, if given to Ndigbo, the Biafran agitation would cease...
The person who said this is not even a MASSOB member. At times,
the Federal Government stage-manages this. Like I learnt, they did
one in one newspaper to the effect that MASSOB had surrendered its
quest for a Biafran Republic.
Government does these things. It is just like on the eve of 26
August, they came up with pamphlets, criticising me, castigating me,
publishing so many choice buildings they said belong to me. Imagine
it! It is the handiwork of government representatives and stooges.
So, it is always the case. I don't look at those things. No MASSOB
member can tell you that there is an alternative to Biafra. Any
member that says that is a saboteur. There is no alternative to
Biafra. There will be no time. We shall, even if they give us all the
positions in Nigeria, leave Biafra. Even if they give Ndigbo
president for 100 years from today, we shall not stop the issue of
Biafra. This is because the issue of Biafra means permanent freedom.
We are in Nigeria. Nigeria is a slave nation. I mean we are slaves in
Nigeria. And if they want us to stay in Nigeria, even for 1000 years
without the freedom as a people (the freedom to express ourselves,
freedom to be what we want to be), we cannot be satisfied. We want a
nation, our own country where we will say, 'oh this is our own.' In
this place, our people will not be killed in any part of the country.
In this place, nobody will discriminate against us. There will be no
hatred against us by the mere fact that we are hard working people.
That is the type of country we want, not a cosmetic country.
Let us talk about dissent and internal crisis in MASSOB. You were said to have been suspended from the organisation by the
co-ordinators and the Central Working Committee (CNC) last year. What
is your reaction?
First and foremost, I was the person who formed MASSOB.
When I formed MASSOB, we were not two. I was the only person. It was
around 2 am one morning. I woke up and started putting the words,
'MASSOB' together.
Around 2 a.m?
Yes, that early morning, my wife complained that she wanted to
sleep, that the electricity generator I put on was disturbing her. I
said, 'please I am doing something that will be of immense importance
to our people in future.' So, after I had done this, then I was the
chairman of Igbo Council Council of Chiefs in Lagos. I tabled it
before them, they looked at it, they accepted it. Then after a week,
we went to the streets of Lagos and proclaimed Biafra. Since that 13
September, 1999 till today, we don't have anything like CWC, there is
nothing like Board of Co-ordinators.
We have never had such nomenclature in MASSOB before.
But these days, Abuja has been using some people to cause confusion
in the organisation.
How?
Prince Vincent Ogbulafor, when he was serving Obasanjo as adviser
or whatever, gave N5 million to these two boys who are doing that to
procure arms to kill me. The plot leaked. They bought arms actually:
they bought 10 bazooka (triples). Then, the Chief Security of MASSOB
in Aba whom they wanted to use absconded, and leaked the plot to one
of our members that they wanted to kill me, and that he did not want
to be part of the plan. The boy fled. That was how the plot failed.
What was MASSOB's reaction to the alleged plot?
We are non-violent. What are we going to do to them? Are we going
to kill them? We said, okay you're expelled from MASSOB. Since then,
they have been doing this. You know, if we are a violent
organisation, I know what we can do. My members said we should do
something. I said 'no, no, no, whatever you do now will be contrary
to our principle, and it will be counter-productive.
There is an allegation that you are using MASSOB to enrich
yourself - that you are building five mansions, including a
presidential palace for the proposed Biafra amongst others?
I don't know how to answer you, but I will tell you something. If
even I have been poor or poorer, it is the time I am in this
struggle. Because in Lagos before 1999, I had five houses. From 1999
till date, only three are remaining. I sold two and put the money in
the struggle.
And, all the houses we are using in MASSOB belong to me.
In Lagos, the house we are using as MASSOB secretariat is my house.
In Okigwe, the one they (the police) burnt is my house. I bought one
was in 1989, the other in 1999 or so.
I bought them before I started MASSOB. But I am using all of them for
MASSOB. The five houses they are alleging, I don't even know where
they are, and I am saying, give me the addresses of these houses,
I'll go there and lay claims. I'll tell the landlords of those
houses, people say these houses belong to me. The landlords should
surrender the certificates to me. If you say a house belongs to me,
what is the address so that my members can go there and verify?
What of the Presidential Palace?
Let us resolve the issue of five mansions. You are in my country
home, but have not seen five mansions under construction but only
one. But this one, I have not levied anybody anything to contribute
to the building of this house. And again, I have not borrowed money
from anybody outside, maybe, from any of the Governors or others. I
told you I have five houses in Lagos, and I sold two. Okay! I can use
the money for the two to build a house. Cant I? Why I am telling you
this is to inform you that I had started acquiring property before
MASSOB. And, I told you that all the houses we have been using as as
our secretariat from 1999 till date belong to me personally. I didn't
inherit them from my father or mother. I bought them before I started
MASSOB, and no member of MASSOB has paid me one naira as rent from
these houses both in Lagos and in Okigwe.
When will you rebuild the burnt down Okigwe MASSOB house?
Each time I wanted to start the rebuilding, the police who had
camped there would stop me. Recently, they were ordered out, but I
shall start the reconstruction when money is available. Now that I
don't have money to start work on the building, can MASSOB give me
money to do it?
But, I can levy the members on the alibi that I am using it for the
struggle, but I won't do that because of my morality.
To what extent have these attacks on your person affected the
struggle for Biafra?
No, No, No. They don't. They were making the allegations before 26
August, and you saw what happened. People know these allegations are
not true. Members of MASSOB read all these, but they wave them aside
as lies. I told you about my buildings before now. I did not tell you
about vehicles.
I had five vehicles before MASSOB. I was using three, my wife was
using two. But, I sold all my wife's vehicles, put in the proceeds in
the struggle. Then, out of my own, I sold two, remaining only one.
You can investigate all these things yourself as a journalist.
Young members of your organisation are said to be undergoing
military training in some secret locations in Igboland and beyond.
Could you give us an insight into this?
I don't answer such questions. If you know that they are being
trained in any location, you go there now and arrest them. I don't
speculate and I don't believe in speculative stories.
Your members claim that the United Nations, UN, has thrown its
weight behind the agitation for Biafra. What is the correct
situation?
The first thing I did in 1999 was to take to the United Nations,
the Biafra Bill of Rights. Our people have been following it up. We
have many committees in MASSOB, both in Nigeria and outside. Matters
pertaining to UN are handled by our members outside. They only give
me reports. On daily basis, it is their function, to go to the UN,
updating them with information after some questions and other things.
What is the next stage of activity towards the realisation of
Biafra?
For now, we are savouring our success of 26 August. We have not
met. MASSOB is not like a club or town meeting. We are highly
democratic, though it is a revolutionary body. I have to meet with my
principal officers, cutting across the whole Nigeria. We will sit
together and appraise the August event before moving forward.
There are other pro-Biafra organisations in the country.What is the relationship between them and MASSOB?
We actually have some organisations that are linked to MASSOB
officially. We have Biafra Foundation in America. We have more than
five organisations outside Nigeria that are our affiliates. But in
Nigeria here, we don't have such body. It is either MASSOB or
nothing. But now, when we want to do something, you see some
individuals, they just want to support, but instead of coming as
MASSOB members, they coin one word or the other and say they are
pro-Biafra organisations, supporting what we are doing. You know, the
problem of our people is that everybody wants to be at the head, and
they know that MASSOB is a very disciplined organisation. It is not
where you come to battle for headship or leadership because we have
so many cadres, so many rules and regulations. By the time you come
in, you'll see that even if you are a multi-millionaire or you are a
Ph. D holder, you have your level. That is, you start from scratch. I
mean, before you start talking in MASSOB, you must have spent, at
least, one year (before you even) attend important meetings. So, when
they see all these, what they do is that they form their own
organisations.
When we want to do something, they say they are aligning with MASSOB,
they are pro-MASSOB, Biafra this and that.
President Obasanjo described the 26 August sit-at-home protest as
an Igbo affair. He said Ndigbo were having their new yam (iri ji)
festival...?
Obasanjo was the one who fixed the iri ji festival for us. He has
a way of trivialising a rather serious matter.
Don't mind him. We are forging ahead. Biafra is a must.
There are other groups across Nigeria agitating for sovereignty
for their own tribes. What is the level of networking between MASSOB
and those other groups?
Of course, we network. We meet regularly, and we have areas of
collaboration. You would have noticed such assistance to MASSOB
during the 26 August programme by some ethnic based organisations.
From the event of 26 August, Biafra may be realised sooner than
expected. When do you think this Biafra will be realised fully?
I cannot predict. You see, everything depends on God.
When you even talk of 26 August, it was possible because of God. So,
without God's help, it would not have been possible. That was the
first time in the history of Ndigbo they could come together and take
a definite action. There were some markets which, since I was born,
were never closed under any auspices. But, they closed. Even if they
had rolled out armoured tanks, and lined up soldiers, ordering total
closure, not everybody would have closed his shop in the market. But,
on this day, everybody complied, irrespective of the fact that we
said it was voluntary. In fact, it was an act of God. If I tell you
it is because of me or my powers, it is a lie. It was an act of God.
So, it is God who will give us Biafra, and He knows when.
The Federal Government, in this dispensation, appointed Ndigbo
into some sensitive positions such as Finance Minister and Governor
of Central Bank amongst others. Don't you see this as an effort to
appease the people and restore their confidence in one Nigeria?
Normally, all over the world, that's how it is. It is just like if
you are a businessman and you have many apprentices, and you have a
very hard-working one who naturally after his service year, wants to
be free. If you want to retain him, you will seem to be kinder to him
so as to make him stay. That's what they are doing. But, the issue is
not all these. The issue that bothers me more than everything is that
there is no security for my people in Kaduna, Kano, Zaria, I mean the
entire north. All these being minister of this, minister of that does
not, in anyway, secure the lives and property of our people in the
north. They do not stop the burning of markets of our people in the
west, in Lagos and looting of our property.
It does not stop the hostility of the government towards our economic
interests. If an Igbo man imports a 'tokunbo', the next day it
becomes a banned item. If fridges come into Nigeria, and they are
imported by the Igbo, it becomes a no-go area. Do you understand me?
Hatred is hatred. So, all these gestures, they are using our people
who do not know.
After using them to get what they want, the authorities dump them.
Because of the successful and hitch-free nature of the sit-at-home
protest, some people suspected a subtle conspiracy between the state
governments, especially, in the South East, and the MASSOB
leadership. The logic was that if there was no such thing, the
Governors would have invoked the state apparati to frustrate the
protest.
What do you mean? There was nothing they did not do to stop the
sit-at-home protest. On that day, mobile policemen jumped over the
fence in my compound. They jumped into this compound through the open
windows. Unfortunately, I was not around. Before that day, most of
the governors were saying all sorts of things. The Governors were the
people sponsoring all these pamphlets and leaflets criticising me.
They openly condemned us, but they couldn't stop the protest.
So, the Governors were helpless?
Yes, the Governors were helpless. You know, I am happy about the
role they played - how they came out and condemned MASSOB, condemned
the sit-at-home initiative and so on. If they had kept quiet, they
would have taken the glory. They would have boasted: "Oh, because we
kept quiet.
That is why people didn't go out. But, that day, they said people
should go out to attend to their businesses." But, MASSOB said,
"People should sit at home." People sat at home. Today, the Governors
are the ones in shame because they know that they are in control of
nobody. And, they know that the people have realised whom they are;
that they are the servants of our oppressors, and that they have no
agenda other than their selfish motive to become vice president in
Nigeria. All of them want to be vice president at the same time. So,
the people have chosen whom they want and what they want.
What of the Ohanaeze, the Igbo apex organisation? What level of
support do you get from it?
Leave Ohanaeze out of this.
How rich is MASSOB? And, how do you administer the foreign funds
alleged to have been coming from foreign countries sympathetic to
your cause?
What do you want me to say? If they say I received money from all
the countries of the world, I say what about it?
If I receive money, so what? I am doing something! And, if any
country of the world sees what I am doing as a positive thing, and
wants to support me, so what? Why must I deny that I am receiving
money from all the countries of the world? I won't deny. Why should I
deny? So that they would love me or hate me? What difference will it
make? I won't deny anything.
But, you get funds from countries abroad?
Yes, I get remittances from all countries of the world, and I am
happy. I'll continue to do it!
Does MASSOB intend to have a permanent secretariat?
In Nigeria or outside?
In Nigeria.
You see, all these things are not important to us. On 26 August,
we were everywhere. And, I said, look at our principles:
non-violence, non-exodus. Non-exodus means: 'stay wherever you are as
Biafrans.' Stay in the North, stay in the West -stay everywhere you
find yourself as a Biafran. When we want to do anything, do anything
there as a Biafran. Stay at home. Our people stayed at home. No shop
opened in Kaduna, Kano, Zaria, Funtua, Maiduguri, everywhere, Lagos,
Ibadan. This is not the time you clamp the people into one axis and
barricade them, and suffocate them, and use your fighters and bombers
to kill them. There will be Kwashiokor and all that. No, there is no
Kwashiokor now. Every Biafran is existing side-by-side with
Nigerians, saying he wants Biafra. Until Biafra is actualised, we
shall be staying together in Nigeria. We shall be eating the same
sugar, the same salt, the same meat, enjoying together until we get
Biafra. We shall never co-operate with Nigeria until we get Biafra.
That is the magic as opposed to all the Igbos (all the Easterners)
coming to the East. Then, you use your army, you barricade all of us.
You stop relief materials from coming to us. We shall be dying of
Kwashiokor. Your children will be going to school, and our children
won't be going to school. You will be using your planes to bomb us.
No, no, no. This is a new dimension, and you will see how Biafra will
be actualised.
Watch out and see what will happen. This is something I have studied,
mind you. So, it is not magic. It is something I am doing out of the
experience I have acquired over the years. Before now, nobody could
believe that Igbos could close their shops, though the hands of God
were there. It also shows that we, the Ndigbo know what we are doing.
You said we should watch out and see what will happen. What will happen?
What will happen will just be like 26 August.
The Biafran map, flag and currency have long been in circulation.
When will the currency be in use? Must you wait until Biafra is
realised before using the currency?
Like I have told you, we have 25 stages to realise Biafra, and we
are in the fourth stage. All these things will come into play. There
are so many things that will come into play. Like you have asked when
shall we realise Biafra? We are in Biafra. When we directed people to
close their shops and stay at home, they obeyed. For what did they
close their shops? For Biafra, of course (for Biafra holiday). Now
you are talking of Biafran currency. Thirty four years ago, you
wouldn't talk of Biafran currency. But, you are talking about it
today. Somebody asked when Biafra would materialise when he is living
in it, talking about it! It is a matter of nomenclature. After some
time, certain things will be clearer than before. That's how it
happens.
By 1999 when you started this agitation, not many people took you
serious. But today, you are a celebrity. How do you feel? What is it
like being celebrated?
I give the whole thanks to God. You look at it. By 1999, who was
Ralph Uwazurike? So, the attitude of people was what it was supposed
to be. Some people had one illusion or the other about Biafra, and
about the person crusading for Biafra. It is like, Biafra again? I it
the one that will come now, and we will start fighting? Is that the
one that will come now, people will start fighting? Is that the one
that will come now people will start running away from their homes?
Then after some time, one year, two years, I started acquiring some
kind of credibility. People thought I would accept money from Aso
Rock. Even but I refused.
People saw it. They killed my men, we didn't stop. They killed my men
severally and the number continues to increase. They burnt my house,
I said 'Bull Shit.' I didn't kill a goat. So, I started acquiring
credibility as people started seeing sense in our cause. Because the
humiliation continued. The marginalisation continued.
People started seeing our cause as theirs (if it is by non-violence).
That is it. I am happy.
Since the August protest, what reaction greets your appearance in
public?
The other day, I casually went to Owerri, Imo State. One person (I
don't know what happened), knew I was Uwazurike.
Before five minutes, the area had been occupied by people waiting for
me. My host had to call me, saying, 'look at what is happening.
People who want to see you have taken over this place.' Do you know I
abandoned by vehicle there because I had to sneak out through the
back? My host guided me out of the place. But still, few that saw me,
chased me up to a distance. So, I give thanks to God. And, I give the
credits to Ojukwu who started Biafra.

Original_Search_Title: Callers Claiming to Be SSS Could Be 419 Men - Ojukwu
Headline: Nigeria: Callers Claiming to Be SSS Could Be 419 Men - Ojukwu
Body_Text:
Enugu — Says he's not under house arrest
THE Ikemba Nnewi, Chief Chukwuemeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, yesterday, said he
received several phone calls from those who described themselves as SSS
operatives, saying: "I have to come and obey their summons," adding that the
entire thing could be part of the "419 syndrome" in the country.
He also dismissed insinuations that he had been placed under house
arrest, and asked the President Olusegun Obasanjo-led government to find
urgent solutions to the armed conflict in the Niger Delta rather than
antagonising citizens expressing their rights of speech on national
issues.
Ojukwu, who reacted to media reports that security had been beefed up
around his residence and at the Enugu Airport to restrict his movement, told
newsmen that the reports were false, as he was free to go anywhere even
though, according to him, nothing had changed since the rift between him and
the State Security Service (SSS) began about two weeks ago.
The ex-Biafran warlord advised President Obasanjo to drop his military
reflexes and adorn the garment of a civilian president to enable him relate
better with the citizenry. "He must take that uniform off and try to be a
citizen, the first citizen, not commander. He left that when he was in the
army, now he is a civilian and we Nigerians should see him as a citizen," he
said.
Ojukwu said that instead of taking up arms against the opposing voices
in the country, the Obasanjo administration should, as a matter of urgency,
address the "armed revolt" in the Niger Delta, adding that there should be
more discussions with the aggrieved people of the Delta towards a peaceful
resolution of the problem.
"I feel this matter should be taken with the urgency it requires. I feel
actually that there should be more discussions taking place all over our
country. Those people should be talked to. I prefer this than talking about
war, war. Talk to them, they are citizens and equal owners of this
country. Nobody owns the country more than the other person," he said.
Excerpts from Ojukwu's interaction with reporters:
There were media reports that you had been placed under house arrest by
security operatives to prevent you from escaping arrest. How true is this
and what is the present situation of your rift with the SSS?
It is a wishful thinking. I have not been restricted in any way or
form. I live here and I can go out anytime I want, by nature I am not an
outgoing person, so nobody can really say I haven't been going out or
anything like that. Nothing really has changed for the past three or four
days and as far as I am concerned, my hope is that perhaps government itself
is having a rethink about the whole thing.
I am a law-abiding citizen; I recognise that I will respect the
government provided they act properly. What I mean by that is this, if it is
an "invitation," the English I speak interprets "invitation" to be a matter
of convenience. If you invite me to come and eat fried rice next Sunday in
your house, I can say yes, I can say no. So, if you insist that it is an
invitation, then they should grant me my right of refusal. On the other
hand, if the answer is that it is an order for me to go to their office,
then let me see a document that says that I should go there.
We have been like this for virtually over a week, nothing has
changed. The only paper that has got into my hands which is now famous is
the "one-way ticket." So, I don't really know what the problem is. I have
also said that anybody can interview me here. Anybody can even have the
benefit of my own guarantee that once they come in, they can go out. I am
not fighting the government, all I am saying is if it is an invitation, let
it be casual, if it is not, let me see the documents. That's all. When they
come, once I am under arrest, I will follow anybody. That is the way I feel
and I haven't changed on that. We are waiting for the next step by the
government.
The thing I think Nigerians should realise is that a number of things
have been going on in this country. The worse, of course, is the 419
syndrome. It behooves everybody to be very careful. As far as I am
concerned, this could be a big 419, I don't know. The person who came to me
said he is from the State Security Service (SSS).
I don't know if he is or not. Those who have been phoning me and all I
remember each time the phone rings is that 419 in Nigeria always begins with
telephone calls. So, I must confess, I don't believe anybody, unless it is
somebody I know and recognize his or her voice. So come out of hiding
whoever it is, make yourself clearly known and I will react
accordingly.
You talked about phone calls, has there been any phone call?
I get phone calls all the time. Sometimes my phone is cut off for quite
sometime and suddenly a phone call comes through to tell me that it is from
SSS and I said, Oh yeah, and I am told that I have to come and obey their
summons and I will tell them my position is very clear on the subject and I
will put down my phone.
Are you in any way worried by this development?
Naturally for Nigeria, I am very worried. I had hoped that the change
that we went into, the civilian government, that we will be running our
society in a democratic manner, but I regret that what I see does not hold
good things for Nigerians.
This is a country where executive lawlessness seems to be the order of
life. It is not my own phrase; it is somebody in the judiciary who said
so. Nigerians on the other hand, actually are beginning to react much better
than I thought. In the newspapers, people are making comments, they are
rising up and making their opinions felt and that is when it is said and
done.
The beginning of freedom for a people is for them to insist on their
rights. Once they do that it will be more difficult for other people to
oppress them. For example, my good friend, Obasanjo, all his reflexes are
military and when I see so many things, I smiled and laughed. He must take
that uniform off and try to be a citizen, the first citizen, not
commander. He left that when he was in the army, now he is a civilian and we
Nigerians should see him as a citizen.
Why do you think government is taking exception to the comments you made
in a recent interview?
In fairness, I don't know what government thinks and what government
doesn't think. They haven't told me anything. I really do not know. I wish
they will tell me and we will discuss it. I am satisfied that no comment I
make in a democratic setting can be objectionable.
But SSS says you have a case to answer. What case do you think you have
to answer?
I haven't seen any and there are a lot of people who can say
anything. There are these N20 papers you buy from the market and they can
say anything. So, I really don't react to things like that. If they say I
have a case to answer, put it on an arrest warrant and I am prepared to
answer. And talking about cases and answering, I really feel very proud of
the effect of the solidarity of the sort of legal practice in
Nigeria. Everyday I am inundated with volunteers wanting to stand behind me
in this case. They don't even want to see it as Ojukwu's case, they look at
it as a whole question of democracy and that is the way it should be looked
at.
There is the feeling that the silence of President Obasanjo is ominous
in this matter. How will you react to it?
It could also be that he doesn't know what to do.
Don't you think he (Obasanjo) should say something?
Every man has his own style. I grant that the fact that General Obasanjo
might think it is better to say nothing. And don't forget what I said
earlier, this could be a whole 419 and it might be that it is not even
him. Look at it properly, what has happened according to him, "Ndigbo went
on yam-eating festival." I said I like the ones who organise a yam-eating
festival. How can there be a crime? I don't see anything wrong in that and I
imagine him not going to take exceptions to people who went to enjoy their
yam festivals.

Original_Search_Title: Festival Ban: Youth Clash With Police
Headline: Nigeria: Festival Ban: Youth Clash With Police
Body_Text:
Asaba — Youths in Asaba defied an evening rainfall and the ban on the
celebration of the new yam festival yesterday to catch fun.
In the process, they clashed with anti-riot policemen who fired tear gas
into the air. The pungent fumes gas filtered into homes in the densely
populated Umuagu areas of the city, forcing many residents to quickly cover
their faces with napkins soaked with kerosene.
As at the time of filing this report, it was not clear if there were any
casualties or arrests.
The Delta State government had last week placed a ban on this year's New
Yam festival when security reports indicated that the rivalry between the
highest traditional ruling body, the Asagba-in-Council, and some age grade
socio-cultural groups over the date and manner of the festival would lead to
a break down of law and order. A meeting involving the state Acting
Governor, Chief Benjamin Elue, Director of the State Security Service,
Commissioner of Police, Mr. Charles Akaya, representatives of the Asagba-
in-Council and the group Otu-Ihaza had agreed to abide by the ban and to
maintain peace in the city.
Since last week Tuesday, armed police have been patroling the city in a
convoy of 10 vans led by an armoured tank, ostensibly to disuade those who
might want to foment trouble.
State commissioner of police said the patrol was to enforce the ban on
the celebration of the new yam festival, noting that if the festivities
would lead to violence and destruction of lives and property, then it was
better that people celebrated in their homes.

Original_Search_Title: HUNI Absorbs 100 Porters for Training
Headline: Ghana: HUNI Absorbs 100 Porters for Training
Body_Text:
The Human Needs International (HUNI) an Accra-based non-governmental
organisation in Agbogbloshie has started an intensive six-month vocational
training for about 100 street children and porters - 'kayayei'.
The porters who were drawn from, Agbogbloshie, Kantamanto, James Town,
Tudu and the surrounding areas would undergo training in any of the
following vocations; catering, basket weaving, carpentry, dressmaking, tie
and dye, kente weaving, hairdressing and auto mechanics among other
employable skills.
Mr Ernest Cosby Saka, Chairman of the Human Needs International told the
ADM, HUNI had adopted the Hallmark Professional Institute and the Accra
Technical Training Centre (ATTC) aside HUNI Training Centre for training and
re-training of those who would want to upgrade their knowledge and also
acquire new skills.
He said the beneficiaries are first taken through counselling, basic
education in their chosen career to know the pros and cons.
Mr Saka said the porters would be resettled in their various districts,
as most of them live very far from Accra, trained to be self-employed, and
urged to start their own businesses.
He said a monitoring and evaluation programme would be organised as a
follow-up to ascertain if the people were coping with life after their
resettlement.
He said these porters and street urchins had been victims of many abuses
in Accra, their newly-adopted home for greener pastures.
He described the number of people who had trooped into the offices of
HUNI to be put on the scheme as overwhelming. "Even though the project would
be carried in phases, its target is 100 for now. But as of now, over 100
people have come to register, which HUNI finds difficult to absorb due to
financial constraints at the moment. I would appeal to diplomatic missions
and other financial entities to assist us to achieve our aim."
One of HUNI's objectives is to reduce the number of potential 'kayayei'
through sensitisation on the dangers associated with migration and lack of
employable skills.
This would be started with the training of one hundred potential street
urchins and to sensitise the various District Assemblies in the districts to
design a programme to curb this phenomenon.
Unlike Hannah, who was brought here in Accra from the North to be a
porter when their father passed away; Enoch Limudal, who also hails from the
North has completed secondary education. Both named persons are
beneficiaries of HUNI's project.
In a short chat with Limudal, he said, "I have nothing to do in the
North so I came to Accra to find myself a job to do. I have been helping in
offloading tubers of yam from trucks at the Kokomba market but what I earn
does not suffice me in any way - very meagre and so I am unable to save as a
result."
Hannah and Enoch are learning sewing and carpentry respectively.
Mr Maxwell Kudoto, Head of Catering Department said most of the porters
he had talked to are willing to learn a trade and abandon the 'kaya'
work. He described them as very determined.

Original_Search_Title: 107 Returned from Cameroon
Headline: Nigeria: ... 107 Returned
Body_Text:
Owerri, Imo — Imo State government has offered relief materials to returnees from
Cameroun camped at the fsp Interna-tional Primary school, Ugwuorji,
Owerri.
The materials, which were given to them yesterday by wife of the state
governor, Mrs Theresa Udenwa, included 27 tubers of yam, over 20 bags of
rice, two bags of beans, blankets, mats , clothes and bucket, with cash
ranging from N4,000.00 to about N10,000.00, Mrs Udenwa said government was
excited by the decision of the returnees to come back home, describing the
state as a safe place for them to live in.
She said government would make adequate arrangement to enable them
re-adjust to the local environment.
"We are happy that you are back to help contribute your quota to see the
state grow from strength to strength. Having stayed in Cameroun for so long
and decided to come back home, you didn't make any mistake. We'll definitely
not treat you as refugees because you are now in your own home.
The returnees who returned Thursday last week included, 7 Abians, 5 from
Ehime Mbano, 27 from Aboh Mbaisem 12 from Ahiazu Mbaise and 18 from
Ikeduru. Others are 1 from Oru West, 2 from Isiala Mbano, Oru East and Njaba
had 2 and 7 respectively.
Others were 14 from Obowo, 6 from Ihitte Uboma and 7 from Ngor
Okpala.
Representatives from each family received materials from the governor's
wife, who noted that the orphans among them would be kept in rehabilitation
homes, while the youths and able bodied ones among them would be sent to the
Songhal Redemption Centre at Nekede near Owerri.

Original_Search_Title: Vendors Forced Out of Wetland
Headline: Uganda: Vendors Forced Out of Wetland
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Kampala-Jinja highway is a busy road. It gets busier at Banda, a sleepy archaic suburb, where there are brisk countless odd jobs and businesses.
Between Banda and the nearby trading centre, Kireka, is a wetland. To its left edge facing Kireka township is a warning sign, which reads: 'Wetland. No encroachment.'
Near the signpost, makeshift stalls stand in an expanse of water logged canals and yam gardens.
Buyers file in and out, and you could hear the noise made during bargaining from afar. This is an extension of Banda market.
Vendors relocated to this gazetted wetland after an investor fenced off their original place opposite it.
Reminders that they had settled in a wetland fell on deaf ears, as is the case in many other parts of the country. After several notices were ignored, an ultimatum was issued to vendors requiring them to vacate the area. The National Environment Management Authority (NEMA) was intent to enforce this.
The wetland is vital for its water purification role. It is connected to a network of other wetlands which discharge water into Lake Victoria.
Hell broke loose when a team of Kampala City Council (KCC) environmental bodies and the Police viciously evicted the encroachers last week and destroyed temporary shelters erected in the wetland. "We have been thrown off the frying pan into the fire proper," Sarah Mbabazi said. She added that they were vending food stuffs for survival.
The encroachers including more than 100 market vendors, who two months ago, invaded part of the swamp between Banda and Kireka, both of them Kampala suburbs, and established a market.
Dr. Aryamanya Mugisha, who heads the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), said encroachers would have to vacate.
"We want you to leave this place because it is a wetland," said Mugisha during an inspection of Kampala's wetlands about a month ago.
However, encroachers backed by politicians, including Mukome Lukoya, the deputy RDC Wakiso and Mamerito Mugerwa, the Kiira Town Council Mayor, defied orders to vacate.
"They should have given more time to the vendors to remove their merchandise. There was no need to do this in a hurry," said Mugerwa.
But Mugisha said a restoration order was issued almost two months ago and the vendors were informed about an impending eviction.
In a letter to The New Vision, Wilfred Banabakintu said they issued a restoration order to Joseph Nsubuga, the leader of the vendors, on June 28. The restoration order is a provision under the Environment Act, which requires an encroacher to vacate the wetlands.
However, when a team from NEMA visited the wetland on July 1, they discovered that the number of encroachers had tripled.
"No progress in complying with the requirements of the improvement notice was noted," NEMA's release said.
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in the Wetlands Inspection Division in the Ministry of Water, Lands and Environment, said the occupation of wetlands is illegal according the land Act.
"They should have used the wetland after securing a written permission from NEMA," Mafabi said. He added that wetlands are ecologically sensitive.
He said the Constitution provides that wetlands are held in trust for the people of Uganda, and that the wetlands policy outlaws large-scale reclamation of wetlands.
Environmental activists at a policy dialogue convened by Environmental Alert, a local NGO, said a cocktail of factors were frustrating protection of wetlands.
Godber Tumushabe, who presented a critic of the proposed wetlands bill, blamed politicians saying they were overruling government institutions.
He said politicians have set a bad precedence, and environmental institutions are losing ground for evicting wetland encroachers.
Other sources attacked KCC for the lack of planning.
"How can they establish a market in a waterlogged area without a toilet?" a source asked.
There is another market outside the Banda wetland, but vendors argue that their number is big and the market could not accommodate them all.

Original_Search_Title: Big Trouble Looms in Volta!
Headline: Ghana: Big Trouble Looms in Volta!
Body_Text:
Ho —  ... As dark clouds gather over Regional House of Chiefs
Dark clouds, coupled with an uneasy calm, are gradually taking over the
usually quiet atmosphere of Ho, the Volta regional capital, due to moves in
the pipeline aimed at preventing the paramount chief of the area, and
Agbogbomefia of Asogli State, Togbe Afede XIV, from partaking in a meeting
with his colleagues in the regional house of chiefs today, a decision some
of the people in the capital consider as an affront and provocation.
And by this Wednesday morning, when the various 'Asafo companies' from
the 33 traditional areas in the state would be marshalling in readiness to
present their 'king' to the august house 'at all cost', as The Chronicle
reliably learnt, there is the high possibility of an unfortunate scene being
created in a region that is not only considered politically volatile, but
also a security hot spot. And the outcome of such a scene, God forbid, could
be anybody's guess.
It may not only deal yet another serious blow to the chieftaincy
institution, but also has the propensity to further push the region's
conflict status to a different elevation; probably without equal in the
country, as a sizable number of members have confided in yours truly their
disgust at the decision.
Even though the real essence of the decision not to admit Torgbe Afede
XIV to take his place in the house of chiefs, nearly a year after his
installation, is still unclear, contradictory documentary evidences on The
Chronicle desks, and inside information gathered from both the Agbogbomefia
secretariat and other places close to the regional house, point to the fact
that there could be more to it than meets the eye.
According to what has now become known as 'disturbing revelations', the
Asogli Traditional Council, following the successful installation of a new
paramount chief, formerly accepted him as its president on June 28,2004, and
subsequently informed the regional house.
And based on that, and other reasons, the region, in a letter dated 13th
August 2004 and signed by the registrar, Mr. A.K. Ahiabah, congratulated
Togbe Afede on his new position in the house and invited him to partake in a
swearing-in ceremony and other roles in the house.
"I am directed by Togbega President to inform you that the Regional
Electoral Commission has informed me of your election to serve a term of two
years as a member of the Volta Region House of Chiefs. Please accept our
congratulations," it read.
The letter, with reference number VRHC.129/Vol.3/26, continued; "I am
further directed to inform you that arrangements are being put in place for
the swearing-in ceremony at a General Meeting of the House slated for 1st
and 2nd September 2004. You are therefore respectfully invited to attend
without fail".
Copies of the invitation letter were sent to the director of Chieftaincy
Division at the State House in Accra, the Regional Director of the Electoral
Commission at Ho and all elected rotatory members of the House.
This information, certainly put smiles on the faces of the Asogli
people, and feverish arrangements were put in place for the maiden
socialization of their chief with his contemporaries, but that joy was
short-lived.
For two days to the gathering, another letter, signed by Ahiabah, the
registrar, withdrew the house' earlier invitation to the Agbogbomefia,
citing what it called 'some legal and technical reasons'.
The second letter, with reference number VRHC.3/Vol.8/38, and dated 30th
August 2004, read in part; "Togbega President deeply regrets to inform you
that owing to some legal and technical reasons you may not be admitted into
the House at this time, so the invitation is withdrawn". Interestingly,
unlike the first, this one was only written to the Agbogbomefia and his
Assogli Traditional Council, without any indication of copy or copies sent
to any other member or institution. Apart from that no part of the
Chieftaincy Act was quoted to clarify the legal and technical reasons in
question. But all said and done, it is not clear whether the second letter
will have any bite as the Asogli people have vowed to 'flout' whatever this
latest message was all about, and damn the consequences.
Speaking to The Chronicle in his office on the developments, the
personal secretary to the Agbogbomefia, Mr. John Kukah, said in accordance
with Act 370 of the Chieftaincy Act 1970, the Asogli people have a permanent
seat among the comity of eminent chiefs in the house, and needed no gazette
or any other clearance from any quarter before having access to it.
He contended that as head of the state, the Agbogbomefia has every
customary and statutory power to represent his people at any forum and speak
on their behalf.
Mr. Kukah, however warned, that drastic measures would be taken against
anyone who would venture to stand in their way on Wednesday, saying; "It is
our right, and we would not let anybody take it from us".
The personal secretary hinted that ever since his installation, attempts
had been made from various quarters to frustrate the laudable visionary
ideas of the new ruler, especially those who feel threatened by the
personality and charisma of Togbe Afede.
When asked to cite an instance, he said on the 19th of July, he was
directed to write to the house for the use its premises to host Regional
Heads of Departments to a buffet dinner on 5th August, under the auspices of
the Agbogbomafia. The gathering was, as part of the new vision, a forum to
rub minds on how to push the region forward, but the registrar, on the
instructions of the president of the regional house of chiefs, turned down
the request on the premise that such a forum could not be held there.
"Many individuals had been allowed to organize weddings, birthday
parties and funerals there. Even the Lions Club and students of Ho
Polytechnic had used it for some social gatherings, but when a prominent
member of the house like our king wants to use it then they find a problem
with it", he fumed.
Several efforts to contact the registrar to for an interview have failed
as his driver received all calls to his office.
Some of the visionary ideals of the Agbogbomefia, is the holding of a
Volta Congress for all citizens of the region, both home and abroad, at Ho
on 30th September and 1st October this year.
The congress is expected to rake in some Â¢10 billion into the regional
kitty, with another $20 million foreign investment expected from some
international contacts the chief is making.
He had already discussed the idea with 12 out if the 15 paramount chiefs
in the region, many of whom have embraced the idea, and have thrown their
weight behind it.
"We are far behind many regions in terms of development in the country,
and we need to put aside the archaic tradition of chiefs merely acting as
custodians of land and leading their subjects to fight wars to show power
and wealth. What we need in this age and time is the education, health and
well-being of our people", one prominent Volta chief intimated to this paper
on telephone.
A divisional chief in Ho charged that he did not understand why chiefs
from other parts of the region are allowed to partake in meetings on their
soil, while the 'boss' of the capital, in whose domain the structure is
located, is not accorded the respect he deserves, calling it a recipe for
conflict.
It is the view this traditional ruler, who would not like to be named,
that as chiefs, making pragmatic efforts to better the lot of the people
they rule is far more important than merely playing symbolic roles and
elbowing one another from becoming part of a house.
"If this matter is not well addressed, it may well add up to the long
chain of disputes related to chieftaincy, land and ethnicity in the region",
he concluded.
Some of the 'trouble spots' already catalogued in the region, and for
which security experts are having sleepless nights are at Nkonya, Alavanyo,
Peki, Awudome, Krachi and Anlo. Will Asogli be the next? In a related
development, Asogli has warned all 'dissident groups' based in Ho Dome to
desist from making announcements and publications to the effect that they
were performing rights and celebrating the Yam festival.
A letter with reference number AS/9/42, dated 30th August, and signed by
the president of the state council, reminded those groups that only the
Agbogbomefia, who is the custodian of all traditions and customs of Asogli,
has the authority to celebrate the festival, and this is done once by all
traditional divisions.

Original_Search_Title: Igbos Shun New Yam Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Igbos Shun New Yam Festival
Body_Text:
Lagos — Igbos resident in Lagos State and other parts of the south-west, who in
the past always travelled home to celebrate the New Yam Festival, are
staying back (in Lagos) due to the uncertainty that characterises eastern
Nigeria currently, as MASSOB embarks on a campaign of disobedience that
could draw the ire of the federal government.
P.M.News gathered that the Okija shrine issue and lately the activities
of MASSOB, which has vowed to declare a sovereign state of Biafra, are
responsible for the failure of many Igbo people to travel home as they used
to do.
A member of the Ohaneze Ndi Igbo in Agege area of Lagos State told
P.M.News this morning that he could not travel home because of "the negative
signals I am getting from home".
He added that a good number of Igbos resident in Lagos State, who never
missed celebrating the New Yam Festival in the countryside, have decided to
jettison the idea and would rather celebrate the festival at the level of
their unions in Lagos.
Uncertain about how the MASSOB campaign will end, they fear that they
may be stranded in the East if they go there "in these difficult
times".

Original_Search_Title: I Dress Simple At Home & Smashing On Stage - Yinka Davies
Headline: Nigeria: I Dress Simple At Home & Smashing On Stage - Yinka Davies
Body_Text:
SHE has come a long way, as a dancer with the Tropicana Dance theatre troupe, back up singer with Alex O, Lagbaja and to the music industry's next big thing.
To Yinka Davies, dance was a craze, dance was a drug, it was her own cocaine. There was only one way to tip her off, get her to dance and she will sing for you. But "if anyone told me I would become a musician, I'd look at them and laugh. I was having fun dancing, to become a musician was the last thing on my agenda." But today she is thrilling her fans with her Afro-centric music and Angelic voice. Her theater attributes reflects in her kind of music, which she calls fusion. "I just fuse all sorts of style, pop, jazz, rock soul as long as it sounds great and feels good."
Whatever it is, charisma, don't give -a dame attitude, exhibitionist streak - Yinka has it in spades. She oozes attitude and you can see it in her dress sense and feel it in her songs. Though she looked plain in long double slit ankara top on fitted jeans trousers, she admits. "When it is Showtime I must look my best. It has to do with the job. I don't make up everyday or go to the sauna but when it comes to going on stage, I don't settle for anything less. Any outfit I have to wear must be smashing, traffic stopping kind of thing.
"I am not a very fashion conscious person but I am very fashion conscious, again, I just choose to be plain at home and be great on stage. I can wear slippers with my ankara and jeans to US and I'll be very comfortable in it. I am a jeans person I wear mini skirts too. I don't go with the trend, I go with me."
Asked if her style has evolved with her music. "No, frank Oshodi used to sew for me, he still does. And Diane Oputa makes clothes for me now because, Frank got too busy. They know what suits me, because, I believe what you wear sometimes affects how you comport yourself. If you wear the heavy lace or damask, you have to find a way to sit gracefully so that everybody will notice you. That's very true about style.
"I was singing at the Nicon Hilton for three years and there was a particular position I was supposed to take. Frank made me some beautiful dinner wears and it fitted exactly what I wanted to do, be by the piano, sing by the piano and addressing your guests as men not boys. You put yourself in a of position of authority, confidence and poise, they will keep you there."
Yinka's personal style reflects the qualities of a true African woman. "I don't perm my hair. I have never used relaxer on my hair, I wear my natural hair, I am too native. I don't have suits and tie. My mini skirts are in Ankara and Adire or in plain guineas, I wear them over a Kampala. Even the ball dresses I have are in African fabric.
"The world has gone Afro, there's lace with adire on it, there's chiffon with incredible adire ideas on it. I am too black in my head. I love things that make me look very native. My hair is mostly done in shuku, didi , basically African hairstyles that makes me stand out.
" I love beads, I have beads that reach my cleavage and I just put something to cover myself up. It's not to attract any man. I do not wish to offend people with my outfits. I can wear anything as long as I feel good in it, it's very important that I feel good in anything I wear. The only thing I flaunt are my legs and my arms. Sometimes I make my dresses down to my cleavages so that you can enjoy what you see and I to enjoy what I see too.
"I can't wear nail extensions it destroys what we have and I don't want that to happen, so I keep my nails short. I hope I can get to grow my nails again and use real good nail cutex on them, with very bright colours. I like colours a lot, plum, pink, fusia, purple, rich baby pink etc, these are the colours I look out for when I go shopping.
There's some lushness that I look for. All of these has a lot to do with your person, how you feel or what you want yourself to be. That's what style is all about. It's life, who you are everyday, what you do what drives you, that's style.
As for skin care she has found solace in Amelia. "I have searched for so long for something that will keep my face smooth and I have not seen. I stumbled on Amelia through a friend. Scrubbing my face with soap and water is not good for me but, I still do it. For the first time in my life, in the past two months I've been using Amelia and it works for my skin. It has a cleanser, toner etc. All I need to do is use the skin toner at night and sleep, I don't need to put water on my face the next day. I don't even steam my face anymore. If I don't use the cleanser at night and I want to go out in the morning I just use the cream cleanser, that keeps the oil in check. Every other week, I use the scrub or the clay mask, that's all the beauty routine I engage in.
"For make up I go for Mary Kay signature eye liner, it feels good, the powder I don't want, I would rather go for fashion fair, foundation, before applying my white powder. I use the Christian Dior eyeliner and lip gloss. A lot of times, I go out without make up except when it's really necessary.
"I love Must de Cartier" she says about her taste in perfume. "Its not so sharp. It has a sweet fragrance.
And Jewries - "Silver Jewries are my all time favourites, more white gold, than yellow gold. I have white bracelets that go up to my arm, I love it that way, that's my style.
Her idea of relaxation "to just see the world move, go out see things and people that do not connect to your place. Reading books is boring for me, even though I read books, but I won't call that relaxation.
Her eating habit she says is horrible. "I love eba, amala, fufu. I can eat eba three times a day, but I don't take pounded yam, I don't take milk too, but I take ice cream.
The 34-year-old singer looks up to God for the best. "As long as the sun rises and sets everyday and I can still sing it's ok."

Original_Search_Title: Silent Crippler Stalks Women Over 30
Headline: Namibia: Silent Crippler Stalks Women Over 30
Body_Text:
Cape Town — OSTEOPOROSIS has beco-me one of the major diseases that affect women in the Western world. So much so, in fact, that it is now more widespread than cancer and heart disease, say medical experts.
But it is a myth to think that osteoporosis is part of old white women's natural ageing process. The disease can and does hit young people as they adopt a more Western lifestyle.
Since 1998, every year South Africa observes a national osteoporosis week campaign to raise awareness of the disease, which thins and weakens the bones, making sufferers susceptible to fractures.
The best way to avoid osteoporosis is to arm your-self with knowledge.
It has been called the silent disease because it creeps up on a person, developing without warning signs or symptoms, and is usually not detected until a fracture oc-curs, says Professor Stephen Hough, chairman of the South African National Osteo-porosis Foundation attached to the University of Stel-lenbosch.
After the age of 30, when peak bone mass is achieved and the skeleton is at its strongest, we start to lose calcium from bones so that the skeleton becomes weaker and more fragile.
However, latest research shows that some myths need to be dispelled.
Osteoporosis is not a natural part of ageing for women, nor does it strike only old women.
One in three women and one in eight men will have an osteoporosis related fracture in their lifetime. In fact, the roots of the disease set in long before old age. The way we eat, exercise and safeguard our health in our teens and twenties sets the scene for bone health in later years, Professor Hough says.
Research also shows that as black women adopt Western lifestyles they are increasingly under threat. As more women adopt a sedentary lifestyle, not exercising and drinking alcohol and smoking cigarettes, the risk grows. According to medical research, even 14 units of alcohol a month interfere with bone rebuilding.
You cannot feel your bones getting thinner and weaker. You may not know you have osteoporosis until your bones have become so fragile that a strain, bump or fall causes a fracture, Professor Hough says.
It is therefore important to do the one-minute bone density test, now widely available at many (South African) clinics, hospitals and gynaecologists' rooms.
While some bone loss can be expected as part of the ageing process, osteoporosis occurs when bone loss is so severe it causes bones to become porous, brittle and likely to break.
The bones most likely to break are the hip, spine and forearm.
If fact, medical experts say that a woman's risk of hip fracture alone equals her combined risk of developing breast, uterine and ovarian cancer. Half of all hip fracture survivors require assisted living and 20 percent die within one year of the fracture, according to the South African National Osteoporosis Foundation.
South African educational and counselling psychologist Dr Glenda Hicks says the non-physical effects can be even more debilitating than the obvious physical aspects.
"It is an isolating disease that can cause loneliness, depression and a feeling of helplessness," she says.
Sufferers may have to avoid even the simplest daily tasks such as shopping or laundry, taking care of the family or simply hugging a grandchild. It affects inter-personal and sexual rela-tionships and can diminish a woman's self-esteem.
The good news, says Professor Hough, is that osteoporosis is preventable and treatable, but only if women take the initiative to do something about it.
The best way to improve bone status is to make sure you exercise regularly and have a balanced diet, rich in calcium (about 1 000 mg a day).
Beneficial foods include fish, sardines, shellfish, milk, yogurt, cheese, broccoli, kale and almonds. Consider taking calcium and vitamin D supplements if you don't get enough of these foods.
Exercise regularly. Walk-ing, jogging, tennis and other weight-bearing exercises that put pressure on the joints are particularly beneficial.
Hormone replacement therapy is the most effective method of preventing bone loss before and during meno-pause, according to most doctors.
There are also several drugs available to treat os-teoporosis and prevent it from worsening. These include "Serms" (Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulators) and biophosphonates, which pro-vide practical alternatives to oestrogen therapy.
The positive development of "Serms" is that it does not increase the risk of breast and ovarian cancer the way oestrogen does in some women.
If you do not want to interfere with the natural endocrine system of the body, try the alternative route.
Some research supports health guru Leslie Kenton's theory that rubbing the cream of wild yams into the inside of the elbow serves to decrea-se the effects of the meno-pause, of which osteoporosis is a symptom.
The homeopathic prepa-ration "sepia" also helps com-bat menopausal symptoms.
Risk factors:
- You are thin, small boned or light skinned.
- You are ap-proaching or past menopause.
- You do not drink milk or have a low calcium intake.
- You are a smoker or drink alcohol in excess.
- You take thyroid medication or steroid-based drugs.
- You have a family history of osteoporosis.
- You have a sedentary lifestyle with little or no exercise.
- You have never given birth.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers Confess, Beg for Forgiveness
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers Confess, Beg for Forgiveness
Body_Text:
Jalingo — Six persons, who specialied in armed robbery, cattle rustling and theft
were currently seeking forgiveness from police authorities, traditional
heads, government and members of the public for the atrocities they
committed before and during the Tiv/Jukun crisis in Taraba State.
This followed a strategy perfected by a concerned group in Gassol,
Headquarters of Mutum-Biyu, during a meeting of cattle rears that a forum be
opened to cattle rustlers and armed robbers mainly of Fulani extraction to
come forward and surrender themselves in peaceful so that they could be
forgiven.
It was gathered that it was though that process that the six men came
forward and confessed all the activities they had previously been involved
in in within and outside the state.
But in what appeared as jungle justice, instead of prosecuting the self
confessed robbers, they were asked to pay N10,000, the money the Fulani
leader popularly called Ardo (real name, Ardo Magaji Mutum-Biyu) received
from the six men with the intension of forgiving them of their robbery
escapedes.
One of them, Bude, 29, told journalists before the mamonth crowd that
gathered to mark the 100 days in office of the executive Chairman of Gassol
Local Government Area in Taraba State, Alh. Iliyasu Salihu Sansani, that he
had stolen countless numbers of goats, thousands of tubers of yams and other
items, belonging to the Tiv-people during the Tiv/Jukun crisis in 2001.
But Mohammed's story was different. According to him, he and his gangs
specialised in cattle rustling besides robbery. He spoke of an incident,
where he and his gangs went and confronted a Fulani herdsman and demanded
huge suns of money from him, but rather than oblige them gave them a hot
chase.
Asked of the where abouts of their firearms, they all denied using such
weapons,just as they also denied killing human beings during such robbery
operations.
Both the council Chairman, Alh. Iliyasu Sansani, and the Ardo Magaji
Mutum-Biyu war-ned the six self-confessed armed robbers not to return to
their old ways of and that they would be held responsible for any robbery
incident in Gassol Local Government Area.
The robbers were later handed over to the Divisional Police Officer
(DPO) in Mutum-Biyu for further integration after promising to disclose the
identifies of their other colleagues.

Original_Search_Title: Deltans Bid Majemite Final Farewell
Headline: Nigeria: Deltans Bid Majemite Final Farewell
Body_Text:
Lagos — Personalities from all walks of life recently thronged Ovwevwe town,
Agbarha-Otor, in Ughelli North Local Government Area of Delta State for the
interment of the remains of a business tycoon and a royal member of the
community, Madam Margaret Jitobor Majemite.
Among those present at the occasion were the Delta State Governor, Chief
James Onanefe Ibori, Speaker Delta State House of Assembly,
Barrister. Y.D. Igbrude, Secretary to Delta State Government (SSG),
Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, Senator Felix Ibru, Deputy Speaker, Delta State House
of Assembly, Hon. Pius Ewherido, and the Deputy National Secretary, People's
Democratic Party, (PDP), Mr. Efe Akpobi, among others.
The event became more of a festival as Ovwevwe became host to visitors
from within and out side the country like never before.
Musicians of all sorts were at hand to dish out the best of the latest
in Urhobo and other music to all, who came to bade their final farewell to
her.
If the dead were ever conscious of happenings, Madam Majemite would have
been more than appreciative and perhaps surprised at what she got as a final
honour as she left for the great beyond.
Delta State Governor, Chief James Onanefe Ibori, at the occasion
described her as a wonderful and great mother to all, adding that she will
live in the heart of all even after her death and she will die no more
because she died a noble death.
"Truly, Mama was great and wonderful because she learnt to accept and
love the truth about humanity. She is the kind of person, who has the
obligation to love and appreciate herself", Ibori said, adding that she was
a woman of principles and a disciplinarian, who after making up her mind
about an issue never changed.
Igbrude, on the other hand, said they will miss her but that they have
solace in the fact that she has ascended into high heavens to sleep on the
right hand of God while Akpobi described her demise as painful. His
consolation, he said, lies in fact that she will live forever in the heart
of Urhobo people in particular and Nigerians as a whole.
Her elder son, the Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
of Jitobs Nigeria Limited, Barrister Fred Majemite, said her mother, who was
the eldest among the family of three, was born into the royal family of
Ugheghe, Ovwevwe town, Agbarha-Otor in Ughelli North Local Government Area
of Delta State in 1927. Her beauty, he said, in her younger days was the
admiration of all.
"When her first marriage was not fruitful with children after several
years, she has no other choice than to leave and got married to another man,
Mr. Idodo Majemite, whom she bore two children for. They were myself and my
junior brother, Mr. Samuel Oturayebure Majemite. She was a very courageous
woman, who did not lose heart in her difficulties, tribulations and trials
of life. My mother, who thought she will never have a child of her own, was
very happy when she gave birth to me. That was why she named me Okiemute,
meaning there is time for every thing", Majemite said, adding that her joy
knew no bounds after a period of three years, when she was blessed with
another male child, whom she named Oturayebure, meaning they are now
many.
"Mama was hardworking and has no idle moment. She was always busy,
toiling and moiling. From early morning till in the evening, she was busy
doing one thing or the other. She started life as a trader, dealing in garri
and yam produce. She was equally a market leader until she veered away from
trading and became a foremost contractor among women that were registered
with the then Eastern Urhobo of Nigeria. She was the first woman in her
village and Ughelli town to own a White Ridge Bicycle at a time when that
brand of bicycle was a luxury", Majemite said, stating that her mother was
among those, who believed that the vital interest of the country was at
stake if the youths were not given sound education.
That, he said, she demonstrated in ensuring that her children pursued
their studies, in the hope of being useful to the world in their different
spheres of calling, by working and studying hard with the ultimate aim of
becoming useful citizens.

Original_Search_Title: In Adamawa, End of Battle for Ezeship
Headline: Nigeria: In Adamawa, End of Battle for Ezeship
Body_Text:
Lagos — The controversial Eze Ndi-lgbo contest in Yola, the Adamawa State
capital, which has torn the Igbo community apart over the years may have
come to an end following a recent Yola High Court ruling, which restated the
resolve of the Ndamawa Emirate Council to recognise Chief Solomon Ohechukwu
Ebis as Eze Ndi-Igbo II of Adamawa state. The battle for the coveted stool,
which has caused a lot of economic and social set back of the Igbo community
in the state has been of great concern to many Igbo leaders in the Northern
region and Abuja. Adamawa State Government cannot be said to be unruffled
over the issue especially as the long-standing disagreement constituted a
serious threat to the much treasured peace in the state.
It all began about two years ago when the leaclership of Igbo community,
under the umbrella of Igbo Cultural Association (ICA) Adamawa state chapter,
decided to select a successor of the late Eze Nidi-Igbo I of Adamawa state,
Chief Anthony Anyika, who died several years ago. The official announcement
of the vacant stool and the need to fill it triggered interest among the
most commercially oriented 1gbo people of the state.
As the interest for the coveted stool narrowed down to only two
contestants of the two major actors, Chief Solomon Okechukuu Ebis and Chief
G.N. Obute, found it very difficult to run the battle of leadership, a
position that has no remuneration other than the usual honour bestowed on
such traditional rulers .
The supporters of the competing business gladiators in this struggle did
not help matters as it was evident that they were willing to let their last
blood for the success of their candidates, a situation that rendered a
round-table discussion by stakeholders very difficult.
The Chief Ebis group believed that their fathers the legitimacy of
occupying the vacant stool following a (victory?) pass mark awarded to him
by a constituted ICA screening committee and subsequent endorsement by the
Adamawa emirate council. the Obute faction insisted that their candidate
must be the only and truly recognized Eze Ndi-lgbo, saying that Adamawa
emirate council lacked any right to impose an Eze Ndi-lgbo on them,
emphasising that the Igbos are republicans who must be allowed to have
things done their own way.
Armed with the report of the screening committee which allegedly gave
Chief Ebis an edge over his opponent the screening exercise, some Igbo
elders wrote a letter to the emirate council seeking for approval of
appointrment of Chief Ebis as the Eze Ndi- lgbo II of Adamawa state. The
request forwarded through the District Head of Jimeta, Dan Amal Adamawa,
Alhaji Hamman Ngabdo was approved but not without the council confirming the
said request from the Igbo elders. Few weeks after the approval, the Adamawa
emirate council performed the coronation ceremony of Chief Ebis as the Eze
Ndi-lgbo II of Adamawa at the Ribadu Square, Jimeta-Yola.
This however, did not go down well with the Obute faction under the
leadership of the ICA President, Chief Ofo Eze and Mr. Obioha Greggs, the
association's scribe, as various methods were applied to vehemently oppose
the emergence of Chief Ebis as Igwe Anyika's successor.
The situation became heightened with another coronation ceremony of
Chief G.N. Obute as Eze Ndi-lgbo II of Adamawa. The coronation, which defied
a high court order allegedly, held under the pretext of celebrating annual
yam festival with Igwe Ogbonna, from Enugu State as the officiating royal
fathers. He described the ceremony as an end to the demand of the majority
Igbo group in Adamawa state. But unfortunately, the exercise was never the
anticipated lasting solution to the power tussle.
Only recently, a Yola high court ruled in favour of the State Board of
Internal Revenue in a case between the board and Obute over a special
(private) vehicle number plate bearing Eze Ndi-Igbo II of Adamawa
state.
Addressing newsmen over the verdict, the secretary of the State Board of
Internal Revenue, Barrister R. Christopher, stated that in November last
year, Chief Obute applied, booked and paid for the special number plate
.
Barrister Christopher stated that the Board wrote and asked for
clarification from the Adamawa Emirate Council in order to enable the
issuance of the special number plate . In the process of waiting for the
reply from the emirate council, Ebis also applied for the same titled
vehicle number. plate. The secretary further explained that the Adamawa
Emirate Council wrote and confirmed the title of Eze Ndi-lgbo of Adamawa
state to Chief Solomon Okechukwu Ebis, thereby leaving the Board with no
other alternative but to return the money for the special plate number to
Chief Obute since not more than one person is entitled to the use of the
titled number plate.
Chief Obute apparently not satisfied with the decision took the board to
court, and the case presided over by Justice Nathan Musa, ruled in favour of
the State Board of Internal Revenue. By this judgment, it is quite apparent,
even to any doubting Thomas that the power that be has spoken. The Lamido
Adamawa has reaffirmed his decision; the Adamawa Emirate Council has no
dissenting opinion from its former resolve; and so the people of Adamawa
have spoken.

Original_Search_Title: Erastus Akingbola: My Day is Incomplete Without Pounded Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Erastus Akingbola: My Day is Incomplete Without Pounded Yam
Body_Text:
HE sprinted down the staircase of the Murtala Muhammed International Airport Ikeja, Lagos with the agility of a youth. Even as
he swaggered smartly towards a waiting car, nothing suggests that
Dr. Erastus Bankole Oladipo-Akingbola, Vice-Chairman/Chief Executive Officer
of Intercontinental Bank Plc, is 53 years. He looked trim, agile, smart and
well groomed; Akingbola attributes his youthful look to God and to the
strict health regime he had maintained for a long time. "I try to keep a
good health regime."
Says the banking icon. "I watch what I eat. But above all, I take things
easy and I have strong faith in the Almighty God as a Christian and as a
church deacon. Probably, my attitude tells on the way you see me."
Chief Erastus Akingbola whose name rings a bell in the nation's banking
sector, is the big boss at the Intercontinental Bank Plc as well as chairman
and director in several other banks within the Intercontinental Bank
Group.
Fresh from what is today known as First City Monument Bank (formerly
First City Merchant Bank), Akingbola in 1989, teamed up with other big boys
in the bank like Chief Raymond Obieri, Chris Alabi and Hyacinth Enuha to set
up what was formerly called Intercontinental Merchant Bank. Expectedly, this
initiative was tough and discouraging, but the team headed by Akingbola was
undaunted.
Within 10 months, the young bank had made an impact, posting the sum of
N18 million; an unprecedented feat in the Merchant Banking sector at the
time.
With this effort, coupled with Akingbola's rare mix of innovative
insight, dynamic leadership, Godliness and teamwork, he successfully
launched himself into a new career phase in the bank. As a testimony to his
leadership acumen, Erastus Akingbola was honoured last Weekend in the city
of Port Harcourt, Rivers State with the Ziks prize for leadership by the
Public Policy Research and Analysis Centre.
Childhood
As a little boy growing up in Ilesha, Osun State, Erastus Akingbola knew
what it is to work hard. He knew where he was going and what he wanted to be
and he resolved to work harder to realise his dreams. He shunned the
frivolities of local boys and took himself seriously. "I was not born with a
silver spoon so, my parents made me to work really hard," Akingbola
said. "They usually drum it into my ears that the only way to success in
life is hard work with dedication.
So I was conscious of all these admonitions and that accounted for my
not being frivolous like some of my childhood friends." Akingbola who hails
from Oke Igbo in Ondo State stayed at Ilesha for 10 years with his parents
