who were working there before they moved to Ife, their ancestral home
(because the people of Oke Igbo migrated from different areas to settle at
Ife).
"It is at Ile Ife that I spent my childhood until I became an adult," he
said.
His banking career
Chief Erastus Akingbola started off as a banker in January 1971 after
his secondary education at Christ Secondary School, Ado-Ekiti. He joined
Union Bank then known as Barcklays Bank. Seven years later, he crossed over
to NAL Bank formerly NAL Merchant Bank. He was in NAL for about 10 years
before he again crossed over to First City Merchant Bank (FCMB). He was in
FCMB for about two years before he resigned to team up with fellow bankers
in 1989 to found Intercontinental Bank where he is currently the chief
executive.
Chief Akingbola tells the story of how he became a banker thus: "Let me
say that my becoming a banker was not my decision. It was just God directing
my ways with His invisible hand. I was encouraged to sit for a banking exam
by one of my mentors, Chief Akinbami. At that time I had told him I wanted
to go to the university to study International Relations, because I wanted
to be an ambassador. That was my ambition."
"He said well, take this exam and when the time comes for you to go to
the university, it will not stop you. So, I took the banking exam and passed
while I failed the GCE. And they gave me a prize of N20 for passing
exams.
Then I said may be God was really talking to me and that was how I took
to banking. So, as I said, it was just God's own doing that I became a
banker". Reminiscing on the good old days of banking. Akingbola says when he
started his career, Information Technology, was low, though it was still
really fun to be a banker as others then held bankers in high esteem.
"As a junior banker, we really enjoyed the time. Bankers were really,
respected then. Even as a young man, elderly people defer to you because you
work in a bank. This was because not many people were in the banking
industry and there were fewer banks then."
"Secondly, we were working with very experienced bankers who were able
to train us in banking operations and that solidly made us to be grounded in
banking operations. Also, we used to work very late into the night because
we had no computers, everything was done manually. Sometimes we stayed in
the office up to 10.00 p.m. And until your books are balanced, you can't go
home. But this made us to learn a lot, how to balance accounts, how to count
figures without the adding machine and things like that. I think I really
enjoyed the times."
Intercontinental Bank dream
When Akingbola and his three friends conceived the idea of starting
their own bank, they had no briefcase filled with money, so they had to make
very inconveniencing personal sacrifices.
"I was the pioneer MD. Myself and three other friends conceived the idea
of Intercontinental Bank. We obtained the license and they asked me to come
and run it. We started at Beckey Street here in Lagos. We inherited a
building that had not been used for three years. So, we first actually had
to wash the building before we could pain it. And we had to use some of the
chairs we brought from our homes and our personal cars for some time."
"So, it was very rough at the beginning, but we just had that
determination. When I look back now, I ask myself how did we manage? That
made me to believe that whatever God has decided to do, He will do it. So,
God decided that Intercontinental Bank is a bank that will stand and will be
great."
Akingbola perhaps is the longest serving Nigeria's MD in the banking
industry. And his long years at Intercontinental have shot up the fortunes
of the bank to an enviable position.
"I thank God, we started from the scratch. So, whatever Intercontinental
is today, I'm part of the team that made it to be so. Number two, we started
with a staff of 31 in the first one-month, and today we are 2,600. That is a
lot of achievement. We also started with one office here in Lagos and today,
we have about 55 offices plus the head office making it 56. And we have
about six subsidiaries; these show we have moved."
"Also, we started the bank with a capital of N7 million, but today, this
has shored up to about N8.6 billion while our deposit is about N70
billion. These show we have actually moved quite some distance."
The bank also recorded other firsts, among them, the Securities &
Exchange Commission (SEC's) ranking as the Top Issuing House in the first
post-deregulation of the capital market in 1994. This was in recognition of
the bank's frontline status in capital market operations.
Dr. Akingbola has also successfully steered the bank from its transition
to a commercial bank in 1999 to its successful Initial Public Offering (IPO)
in August 2002 that concluded its transformation into a Public Limited
Company (PLC). He also built Intercontinental into one of the largest and
most diversified financial services institutions in Nigeria today, with
thriving interests in banking, insurance, finance, discount house operations
and real estate.
Akingbola's academic and professional background is equally as
impressive as his career accomplishments. Having joined the banking industry
as a school leaver, his insatiable quest for knowledge drove him to qualify
as a Chartered Banker in 1975, and to subsequently bag an MBA in Management
from the University of Lagos.
He became Fellow of the Chartered Institute of Bankers (UK) in 1988 and
was also awarded the Fellowship of the Nigerian Institute of Management
(1996) and the Institute of Chartered Secretaries & Administrators of
Nigeria (ICSAN) in 1998. A board member of several companies, Akingbola is
an alumnus of both Harvard Business School and the Lagos Business School and
holds an array of professional qualification from internationally acclaimed
institutions across the globe.
Management philosophy
Intercontinental Bank has a tradition of all-blue attire. Even CEO,
Akingbola wears the Corporate uniform that it would be difficult to
distinguish him from the others. Ask him, he will tell you he is one of them
and, therefore is carrying everybody along, a philosophy that has kept them
together to achieve the huge success.
"I think my management philosophy is to continue to carry my people
along. To listen to everybody, believing that each fellow has his or her own
ideas and anybody can get inspiration from God and give the right opinion. I
think our major strength is that we move as a team in
Intercontinental. That's why it has been very difficult for us to be
defeated. We move as a team and believe in common or general wisdom."
He says their chief marketing strategy is doggedness that must culminate
into good result.
"Our marketing strategy is that we don't turn back until we weigh the
bill. We tell our people that at Intercontinental, there are no excuses for
non-performance. You must achieve it. So, when you are coming back home with
a laurel you know you worked hard for it. And I used to tell my people that
there is no teller for effort, there is only teller for cash. So, if you
really want to pay money into the bank, then make sure you achieve your
goal.
So, our people are very determined, they are noticeable in the market
and they never give up. This is because of our training."
Akingbola says that the success of Intercontinental Bank was not a
chance happening, but a result of many years of research and well baked
products which the bank offers.
We have over six now. There is the Intercontinental Diamond Fund (IDF),
there is the Intercontinental Instant Cash Transfer (I-CASH), there is the
Intercontinental Consumer Leasing (I-LEASE) there is also the
Intercontinental Valuable Yield (IVY) Account, there is the Intercontinental
Premium Savings Account (IPSA) and the Intercontinental Classic Account
(I-CLASS).
There is also the Valucard and now the Western Union.
"The products have performed very well in the market. The IDF as you
know has been a leading; product in the banking industry right from day one
and this is its 11th year in the market and we have over N5 billion in that
particular product. The I-LEASE which is the one that came not too long ago
is really attracting a lot of patronage because university lecturers, union
officers and others are encouraging their people to come and take loans to
buy various household appliances. So, that one too is moving very well.
On N25 billion capital base
Intercontinental Bank has been running a thriving group structure;
believes that it can scale the hurdle with its group structure. Members of
the group include Equity Bank of Nigeria Limited, Associated Discount House
Limited, Gateway Bank Plc, WAPIC Insurance Plc, Intercontinental Capital
Markets Limited, Intercontinental Securities Limited among others. Global
Bank Plc is also an associate of the group.
The group consolidated capital/shareholders' fund as at end 2003 was
N16.6 billion and it is estimated at N20 billion June 2004. A consolidated
group shareholders fund by end of this year would have met the minimum N25
billion required.
But Akingbola says the group is aiming at surpassing the minimum
requirement substantially. "So we may have to go for a post-consolidation
Initial Public Offer to raise the funds to about N35 billion before end of
2005".
He said the Nigerian economy has been blowing hot and cold, adding that
the management of the bank had been able to tame the ugly tide.
"Our economy has been on a roller-coaster, let me put it that
way. Sometimes, things are good, but the task of the management is to be on
its toes to be on sure it can give it what it takes. Each time, we have to
analyse our environment and then ask ourselves, what do we do? The task of
management is regardless of what is happening around you. You must keep
achieving success".
Recreation/Favourite food
For Erastus Akingbola, all work and no play makes jack a dull boy. He
has his peculiar ways of unwinding after a hard day's job. "When I get back
home, I listen to the news. I listen to some local plays; Yoruba plays, Igbo
plays and others. Then I go to chapel to pray after which I go to bed. But
on weekends, from Saturday morning to Sunday night, my time is mostly
consumed in the church because I have various activities in the church and
my mind is off banking completely during these two days. I believe this is
how I manage to cope."
The intercontinental boss has his favourite food as pounded yam and Okro
soup which of course inspired him in his job as a banker. He said: 'pounded
yam is my favourite food. "I don't joke with pounded yam and okoro or
Efo.
"Don't forget that they have a way of doing pounded yam abroad and now
and it looks and tastes like the real thing. So I also eat it there. In
fact, anytime I go to the UK, my wife is kind enough to make sure that I
feed on Nigerian food, either on Eba or Amala, pounded yam, rice with dodo,
that's what I eat. Don't forget UK and Nigeria seem to have fused together
now. We have a lot of people carrying this food there to sell. And some
Brazilians are bringing food there. So, I live in UK as if I am in
Nigeria. And it is only when I go for business lunches and dinners that I
eat the Western food."
Books
Akingbola reads a lot of books too. "I will say I read a lot because I'm
lucky to be chauffer-driven.. So, I'm able to read while I 'm moving to
places. And when I leave between cities, I read a lot. And what I read are
leadership books, management books, investment books."
Anytime I travel out either to UK or US, I buy a lot of management and
leadership books. I have a big library. I buy a lot of books and there are a
lot of them I have not read."

Original_Search_Title: Interior Minister Urged to Protect Traders From Robbers
Headline: Ghana: Interior Minister Urged to Protect Traders From Robbers
Body_Text:
Madam Leticia Tieko, Market queen of the Yam Sellers Association at Nsawam in the Akuapem South District has appealed to the Minister of Interior to protect members of the Association from armed robbers.
Madam Tieko made the appeal when she addressed yam sellers at a meeting to find out how best they could protect themselves and their property from robbers.
The meeting followed an attack on a group of yam sellers about a fortnight ago by armed robbers at Atimpoku, Worawora and Apesekubi all in the Volta Region where they had gone to purchase tubers of yam.
"Trading in yams is the only business some of us engage in to pay for our children's school fees and to look after our families," she said, and appealed to the Minister to take steps to protect the traders to enable them to go about their businesses in a peaceful atmosphere.

Original_Search_Title: 'Farewell, My Angel Gabriel'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Farewell, My Angel Gabriel'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Gabriel Ndubuka, retired as an executive director with the
National Electric Power Authority (NEPA). On July 5, he died after battling
with stroke for five years. He will be buried on August 14. The following is
his widow's tribute.
Your departure, like a writer once wrote, is like that of a man who has
sailed into mortal history like an actor who was taking a bow, with
deliberate flourish, from life's stormy stage. Yes, you did just that when
the children and I thought you had recovered well enough to travel home, to
see your people, and to celebrate the new yam festival with them as you had
requested.
Before making this request, you had taken a look at yourself and felt
good and proud, that at last you had been able to outstage the effects of
stroke, which has left you devastated and speechless for sometime. You felt
you had fought a long and hard battle to regain, not only your good looks,
but also your strength and your speech from this wicked phenomenon called
stroke.
As if you were having a premonition of death, you also instructed us to
arrange for a photographer to come and take a family portrait of us,
something that was not in your character; most of your photographs were
taken at functions, but this time you saw the necessity of a family
portrait. Unfortunately, you were gone before we could arrange it.
Daddy (we fondly call ourselves Daddy and Mummy- courtesy, our
children), I did nor prepare myself for this because, I though that somehow
you would pull through as you had always done. I did not know, and could not
imagine you bowing to it this time. No wonder I felt so devastated,
heart-broken and lost when you breathed your last.
Most times, people would say to me that since you had been ill for
sometime, I must have known that this day would come and prepared my mind
for it. But the truth is that, no person in love looks forward to this day
to come, even when your partner is ill. You never dream of it, you do not
anticipate or hope for it, instead, you pray and hope for he best.
My husband was my Angel Gabriel, my friend, my confidant, my love and my
all.
Darling, we got married when I was too young and inexperienced and you
taught me most of the things I know today. You also taught the kids and me
the strength of character, determination, patience and courage. For you said
they were the only weapons that could make a man from humble beginning like
you reach the height you reached. You took on the task of acquiring western
education with vigour, making electrical engineering the mainstream of your
scholarship. Government College, Umuahia became the bedrock for the
development of a determined and disciplined mind like yours. At the end of
your training in England, you came home to work for ECN, now NEPA. In
service to your country you displayed your dedication to duty, your
resourcefulness, honesty, transparency, humility and hard work, yet you
never compromised standards in anything you did. By the end of your career
you had garnered conceivable, achievable, and conferrable encomium. In your
profession, you stood tall among your colleagues - I always felt proud of
you, my love.
As you lie dead today, you have become a lesson in humility and a big
challenge to us. You have been a very gentle, loving, considerate,
understanding, compassionate, sacrificing, and dedicated husband, brother
and uncle. Darling, there will be none like you. You are an excellent study
in humility. We shall miss your hearty laughter, your practical jokes that
always kept us reeling in laughter, the way you repaired almost everything
around the house, because you were very good with your hands, and never
waited for other people to do things for you. My love, there is this gaping
vacuum. Already words cannot describe my feelings. You were particular about
details and good records, you taught us that hard work does not kill anyone
but can make one grow. You encouraged us, giving us the confident hope that
was so infectious- you encouraged me in my academics, and encouraged me even
in Herbert Mccaulay foundation, a Foundation set up to honour hard work,
dedication, transparency and achievements of our past leaders.
You attended every public lecture and conference we organised, and you
ogonised with us when funds were not available for us to carry our
programmes. If you had money you would have sacrificed it for us to continue
the good work we were doing. You told us to always remember that a life well
lived is better than life engaged in some mundane activities like petty
rivalry, intrigues, hatred, blackmail, injustice, enmity, etc. That it is
better to make our living sublime in love, peace, fairness, friendship,
understanding, and service to humanity, so that on departing, our eulogies
may resound of fulfilled life on this planet. This is what you have also
achieved for yourself, because you are leaving as a fulfilled man with
pleasant memories even as tragic as your departure may have done. You are
leaving behind a proud family and many devoted and loyal relations and
friends.
Your exit has created a big vacuum and has left a big hole in my heart,
yet I have been asked to accept it and give thanks to the Almighty. This is
what I am doing, even as I feel lost, abandoned, and left in the cold, taken
to the height of passion and then dropped to the depth of human
emotion. But, God is my strength, and I also have to draw from your
legacies, and take courage, for you said that courage is one of the most
important virtues of life. This is why I am bearing this cross with
dignity.
We believe that your gentle, loving, and humble spirit, which is resting
in the bosom of the Lord, will be our Guardian Angel. We hope and pray that
our aspirations and efforts on this earth will at one point or the other
cross paths with your spirit.
You are man of great service and peace to people. You may not have had
money but you gave love and service and you were recognised. That is why the
people of Akokwa a conferred you with the title of Aka na-enye Ihie 1 of
Akokwa (the hand that brings light). You were also honoured with the title
of Iyi Oha 1 of Ndikelionwu (the flowing stream that the people drink from)
of Ndikelionwu etc.
Daddy, my darling, the love of my life, I wish you safe journey to
heaven where you belong. Sleep on my beloved, and take your well-deserved
rest in peace, till we meet to part no more.

Original_Search_Title: How to Save Our Public Schools
Headline: Nigeria: How to Save Our Public Schools
Body_Text:
Polity — Late Chief Obafemi Awolowo was one politician who believed that anybody who chooses to criticise a policy, programme or point out the inadequacy of a situation should not stop at that. He should move further and suggest his own solution. Incidentally, the man practiced and lived this belief.
This philosophy of one politician I admired most in the history of this country has always haunted me each time I write to point out certain problems in our polity. I have always strived to do more than just criticising. I seek to do so constructively and giving suggestions which I believe will be subject to criticism by those who choose to examine it critically.
Thus, after my last piece on this page on November 20 titled "Who Will Save Our Public Schools?", I have been disturbed that I only sought to use that piece to direct public attention to an endemic crisis in our society in my own special way of celebrating the 70th anniversary of one of our national icons, Professor Oluwole Akinwande Soyinka. But since then two incidents have helped me to sharpen my perspectives on one good solution to the problem I raised in that article.
First, my brother, Barrister Dokun Abolarin (Doksy) called on the phone the day the article was published and after the necessary pleasantries, he repeated the title of the article to me. I eagerly asked him what he thought about it, he then answered the question by saying "it is the duty of you and me to save our public schools." He then asked when last I visited the primary school I attended and what I have ever done to help the school. It was a question which threw me off guard.
But even then, I recovered immediately to tell him that several times I had discussed with my wife about my willingness to visit that school founded some 104 years ago on the base of the rocky area of Abeokuta and donate N10,000 to the school. He said, I should stop thinking of doing it I should go ahead and act. He added that I should not only give the money, I should monitor how it is spent. And really, I did not lie to Doksy. On more than one occasion, the idea of going to the primary school I attended had been dominating my thoughts. But the issue of when I will be chanced to go there during week days has been my problem. But now, I can see that I have no option, if I want to be in the rightful position to canvass the idea that you will read about further down this piece.
Now, to the second incident which helped to firm up the idea I want to canvass on this issue. It is that picture of St Paul Primary School, Ake, Professor Soyinka's alma mater published last week in The Sun newspaper. That picture moved me close to tears. I was so depressed at the image of the old, dilapidated buildings in which Professor Soyinka's ingenuity was baked. It was a terrible sight which confirmed the fears I expressed in that last article that perhaps the school will not be in a position to produce the likes of Soyinka again.
I know that until 1978 when I left primary school in Abeokuta, St Peter's Ake was still a good, popular school whose pupils turned out fine when we all would compete during march past on Independence Day anniversary. To think that buildings in that school are now looking like yam barns is a source of sadness for me. It kept me imagining what would have happened to my own school. I am sure all conscientious Nigerians must be worried by the crises of infrastructure, staffing and funds plaguing our public schools. It is for this reason that I believe that we should all rise up and join hands together to solve the problem. The point I am making is that every Nigerian who can afford it, particularly those gainfully employed, those doing well in private businesses, those occupying public offices and those abroad with good jobs should join efforts to save our public schools.
What is expected of us all is that we should all return to our bases. I enjoin all Nigerians who know that honestly God has been good to them and they are relatively financially balanced, to pay a visit to their alma mater, particularly the primary and secondary schools which gave them their foundation in life.
As you visit the school, try to relive the period you were a pupil or student in the school. Then, make a mental note of the differences, particularly where you saw some negative changes. Then, think of what you can do to help the school. Hold extensive discussions with the school authorities and be sharp with your observations. Then, before you go, decide on what you want to do to help the school and announce it there and then.
During the visit, I enjoin you to spend some minutes giving inspirational lessons and career development talk to the present pupils. If we all try to embark on this 'Return To Our Root' journey at least once in a year, I bet you that we would have taken a major step in saving our public schools. Imagine the millions of naira that will be re-invested in our alma maters if all Nigerians who earn more than N500,000 a year decided that one per cent of their annual earning will be ploughed back into the development of the secondary or primary schools from which they graduated.
What this means is that for everybody which qualifies to participate in this scheme, a minimum of about N5,000 can be donated to their old school in cash or kind. With the success of this campaign, those schools will not lack dusters, good black boards, good buildings, good libraries, good laboratories and such other facilities which made them good enough to produce today's successful professionals, bureaucrats, politicians, private sector leaders and businessmen.
We can even take this idea to a higher level. As Doksy told me, on his 40th birthday, he chose not to organise a lavish social party to host friends, family and well-wishers as it is the fad on this shore. The man simply decided to embark on this therapeutic trip back to his root. He paid a visit to his old primary school and donated N100,000 worth of chairs and desks. Isn't that a wonderful and unique way to celebrate a special occasion like the man's birthday?
This effort is commendable. It is a style of celebration which is solemn, ennobling, fulfilling and in my view, Godly. I know that if all the flamboyant celebration of birthdays that I view on television or read about in the newspapers can include as part of the elaborate ceremonies a visit to the celebrant's primary or secondary school and the assessment of the area of needs which is backed up by immediate moves to solve the problems, then we will have better public primary and secondary schools across the country.
For example, look at all the efforts made by Soyinka's friends to celebrate the man's achievements as a multi-talented artist. If the organisers of those ceremonies had included a visit by the professor and his team to St Peter's School, Ake as a part of the schedule, the Nobel Laureate and his friends would have seen how one of the legacies that made the Soyinka personae had been dying gradually and they would have done something which I know will inject life into that school. Remember, St. Peter's is not only Soyinka's alma mater. His father who was fondly called 'Essay' became popular as a teacher in the school. That school also produced Soyinka's other siblings who are world rated, successful academics and professionals. So, no better birthday gift could be given to the renowned playwright than to rebuild and re-equip his old primary school.
Well, it is never late. Professor Soyinka and his friends can still make efforts to visit St Peter's School and do that which will help that public school. In the same vein, all of us whether during our special moment or at any other time should make it a compulsory yearly ritual to help our old schools out of their distress situation. Let us all make it part of our compulsory civic, spiritual and societal duty to give to our old schools.
From President Olusegun Obasanjo, whose old primary school I presume is also decrepit, to Vice President Atiku Abubakar down to the other top guns in the executive, legislative and judicial arms of government down to their equivalent in the states and LGAs, the need to champion the cause of saving our public schools through individual and private efforts should start. We - every capable Nigerians - should make it a compulsory engagement to seek to help our old schools.
Those of you who will read this piece should carry the message to others who may not read it. Let one person at least speak with two other persons about this idea. We need to join the train and move rapidly to restore dignity, quality and utility into our public schools. Let the fortunate ones in our society, no matter what level they are, contribute whatever they can to make the public schools better.
The effort should be privately-driven this time around. Let us not wait for government to perform this task for us. I know that N5,000 can at least put two black boards in place in a secondary or primary school. The same five thousand can donate five bags of cement to a classroom building project or buy four asbestos roofing slates. That money can equally buy one or two books on different subjects for the general use of the students. Or better still, it can fund the supply of two newspapers for 25 days so that the students can start improving on their reading culture and knowledge of contemporary issues.
However, it is important that we should all heed Doksy's warning. When you donate in cash or in kind, ensure you monitor the use of the money and material. Ensure that the money and material are put to good use.
As a result of the importance that I attach to this campaign for us all to start giving financial, material and moral aides to our public schools, I intend to do a reminder to all the readers of this column every forthnight.

Original_Search_Title: Suspected Plague Kills 58 in Ituri
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: DRC: Suspected Plague Kills 58 in Ituri
Body_Text:
Kinshasa — Health officials in the Democratic
Republic of the Congo said on Thursday 58 people, suspected to have caught
the plague, had died so far this year in the northeastern district of
Ituri.
They were among the 1,042 suspected plague cases recorded since January,
according to a joint Ministry of Health and World Health Organization
mission sent to the area from 18 to 26 July.
"Ituri remains the area in the DRC most affected by plague because of
incessant population displacements caused by the continued fighting between
rebel groups," Dr Vital Mondonge Makuma, deputy director of the
Epidemiological Surveillance Unit at the Ministry of Health, said.
The mission reported that most of the cases occurred in Mahagi
Territory, 140 km north east of Bunia, within Ituri District.
With 517 suspected cases of the disease recorded, the health zone of
Rethy is the hardest hit: 31 deaths were registered there from January to
May. During the same period, the Logo zone registered 340 suspected cases
and 15 deaths; Rimba 183 suspected cases and 10 deaths; and Nyarambe two
deaths. There seems to be no let up.
"Even if we can no longer call this a severe outbreak, cases continue to
be reported with a 5.5 percent mortality rate," Mondonge said.
Dr Jean-Marie YamÃ©ogo of WHO Kinshasa told IRIN on Thursday: "Even
though the region has been a hot spot for the plague since 1928, the absence
of proper laboratory facilities in Ituri has made it impossible to confirm
scientifically that those infected are indeed suffering from plague."
The mission's report shows that 90 percent of the cases are suspected to
be bubonic plague, 7 percent pulmonary plague, while 3 percent are suspected
to be a variant that affects the blood.
Local committees have been set up to sensitise the public to the problem
and to sterilise the homes of those who have fallen ill.
Dr Tarcis Elongo of WHO Bunia told IRIN that one of the factors
encouraging the epidemic was the climate. "The high humidity during the
rainy season forces [people] to store their food indoors, and this attracts
rats and fleas that are carriers of the disease," He said. "The fact that
people are forced to live in the bush during fighting has also contributed
to its spread."

Original_Search_Title: Vista:I Fear Nobody, But Pray Hard -- Bello Lafiaji, Ndlea Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Vista:I Fear Nobody, But Pray Hard -- Bello Lafiaji, Ndlea Boss
Body_Text:
Notorious drug dealers in Nigeria live in constant fear of Alhaji Bello Lafiaji. Ever since he took over the helm of affairs as Chairman of National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA), Bello Lafiaji has discharged his duty without fear or favour.
In the world of drug dealers, he has a fearsome reputation as a crusader who is bent on chasing them out of town. His anti-drug campaign has won him a lot of accolades. But in the underworld, people like Bello Lafiaji are not regarded as saints, because in a state of lawlessness, to be law abiding is illegal and a sin. Doesn't he fear for his life? Or that of his family? And has he ever been offered a bribe to compromise his position?
I caught up with him recently in his office to find out the answers to these questions, amongst many others, and of course, to find out what makes him tick.
How was growing up like?
"Life has been great right from the beginning. Though I lost my father at a very tender age of 6. It has also been challenging, but I had a very good aunty, who took over when my father died. I had a very good training both in character moulding and a sufficient education. I was always among the first five in my class.
I also represented my state in sports like hockey, basketball, cross country running. I was the North Central State champion, I really had a very good time growing up. I was well disciplined. I never committed any offence in school and thank God for it. That was why I ended up being the class captain from primary school to secondary school. So, my leadership quality started manifesting from a tender age".
What are the challenges he faced?
"Let me say it quite clear here, because I had very good moulding and sufficient knowledge, I tried to encourage my peers to do the right thing and you know when you do that, you come across a lot of enemies. I remember back in Class IV, my classmates all went on strike, but I refused to join them, because of that, some of my classmates wanted to come and deal with me in the hostel".
What are the unforgettable moments of his life?
"Despite the fact that I was very well disciplined, there was a time my class in secondary school was punished, though I didn't break the rules, I was included, that period I felt so bad because I knew I wasn't an offender. I felt bad about it".
Did his environment influence his choice of career?
"I think my environment did to a certain extent. I was brought up by a policeman, the late Alhaji Lafiaji. In those days, policemen looked smart. They wore smart caps, smart trousers and shoes. And I liked them. So, nobody really influenced me, I think I would say it is God that influenced me into going into the police force".
How was the police force when he joined?
"The Police Force was quite successful at that time, because for you to have a career in the police force as a cadet officer, you must be self-disciplined. In Police College, I exhibited the same traits from secondary school that I was nicknamed "O.C. Works", the man who is always working".
During the time he joined the police force, it was quite attractive, compared to now. If given the opportunity with what obtains now, would he still join the police force?
"I would still join the police if the discipline that I met is still part and parcel of the police. The way I see things now, I don't think I would, unless we bring back the discipline that we had in the police force. Then, the mere pride of wearing the police uniform was something. But things have changed. But I think our era was good and you'd be proud to be a policeman".
What were the things he did differently then from his contemporaries?
"When I was in Police College, I was neither drinking nor smoking, nor running after women. They are not my passion. My passion is to do the right thing. So, I don't find the aforementioned attractive. Infact, some of my friends wondered what kind of human being I was since I didn't do all these things. But that is how I was brought up. My passion then was sports and reading. So, I was not distracted by social vices".
But even at his age now, he is still good looking, while younger, he must have had a bevy of beautiful women seeking his attention?
"On a lighter mood, I can kill the dames, but I didn't. Dames were running after me. But I tell you, my life has been well moderated. When you do things in moderation, then you cannot exceed bounds".
His job is quite a dangerous one because of the people involved. Doesn't he sometimes worry about his safety and that of his family?
"I think my career right from time has been dangerous. I was not only a policeman, I was also in the State Security Service (SSS). I remember one of the greatest operations I carried out was the evacuation of President Shehu Shagari when he was to be taken away by the military during the 1983 coup. I don't carry a gun. I believe what I am doing is for humanity and God would protect me. So, this is not the first time I am taking a dangerous assignment. I have carried out lots of operations, undercover operations. And now that I am here as chairman of NDLEA, I don't fear anybody except the Almighty.
Even when my family was being harassed by the military when I evacuated the President in 1983, I still took them out of Abuja even though I was not there. I also evacuated them in absentia. So, I don't fear anybody. Pray hard, that is my philosophy and God would protect you and guide you. And this is what I believe and has guided me for over 31 years of service. And I have been guided and protected, and would continue to be protected and guided till the end of my life. I feel so fulfilled that God has answered my prayers to protect me and guide me throughout my life.
My children are doing well, my first son, sooner or later would be a captain in the Nigerian Army. He has passed his exams. My second daughter would graduate as a medical doctor next year, the third is into information technology, the fourth one is dong well. So, tell me what do I need in this world? I think my life has been fulfilling and I thank the Almighty God. Anything that has come my way, it's from God".
What's his attraction to his job as chairman of NDLEA?
"My mission is to do my best. If God has chosen you to lead, you must lead very well because, I want to leave a good legacy in the agency, that I have been dedicated to, I have worked hard to sanitize the society, to protect human beings from being destroyed, to repair Nigeria's image, to serve the fatherland, that is my attraction to this job".
Drug business worldwide is big business, and drug lords would stop at nothing to keep the wheels of the business turning, one of their strategies of ensuring this is through bribes. Has he ever been offered a bribe to compromise his position?
"No. I have never faced it, I don't pray to face it. Like I said, I am a very disciplined man, I am a hard worker. I put the nation first, not self interest. Before I came, the country was decertified, but since I assumed office, Nigeria has been certified for three consecutive years. And when I came I initiated the West African Joint Operation, and I was first chairman. Because of my effort, the international community elected me as the president of the European Drug Enforcement. The first African to be so elected. I have held the position for two years now. Is there anything more than this to bring out the image of the country? The first African and the first Blackman to hold that position. It is fulfilling".
What attracted him to his wife?
"Her beauty and simplicity, because I take life very easy. So, when I met somebody who is like that too, it was easy to have the chemistry".
Was it love at first sight?
"Yes, it was. It didn't take me one month to marry her".
Considering his busy schedule, when does he find time to read?
"I find time to read when I am travelling. That is when I don't get disturbed by calls. I just returned from Indonesia, which is about fourteen hours from London, so that is my best time to read or write. I have about three of my books in the pipeline. They would be launched soon".
What are the books about?
"They are about drugs. And surprisingly, about the press, because the press has been a good partner in my campaign".
What kind of clothes does he like?
"I like to wear clothes that are smart. I tell people, you don't have to buy expensive clothes, as long as they are neat and smart you can use them."
What are the things he does not like about people?
"Hypocrisy. It turns me off completely. I like to be straight forward"
What gives him the drive?
"The joy to see that I have performed my job creditably. I derive satisfaction from that"
Since he became the chairman of NDLEA, which would he say has been his most challenging moment?
"There are quite a number of them. But for me, the most challenging is turning around the agency from what it was before. I know what I did. It took me a lot of time to refocus the agency. When I came, for instance, there was no criteria for disciplining people.
You commit an offence, you are asked to go but now, I had to set up a disciplinary committee for both senior and junior officers. And I have to put the guidelines for appeal when necessary. There was no training policy. I had to do that too. When and where should officers be trained? I also drew up a promotion policy. How many years are you supposed to stay in the cadre before you move and what do you need to do? So, these are some of the things I have done to sanitize the place.
This place was like a market, even in the market, there is sanity, because you have tomato sellers in one place, yam sellers in another. But when I came here, it was not like that. No direction, no focus. But I came with a focus. I achieved all these with the staff here. I didn't bring any expert from outside. We have the talents here. All they need is motivation".

Original_Search_Title: Writers Plan Big for Resort
Headline: Nigeria: Writers Plan Big for Resort
Body_Text:
Writer's Resort will, come July 24, celebrate the 17th Anniversary of its founding. The occasion ,which includes the exhibition of books, craft works and paintings, will hold at the Afimo-Agoro road, Ijoko Otta premises, starting from 2pm.
According to a release signed by the founder, Bode Osanyin, who is equally an artistic director, the resort since its inception has been a veritable instrument to a vibrant literary, theatrical and artistic tradition in Nigeria.
A lecturer of Literature and Media Art at the Department of Theater Arts, University of Ibadan, as well as an award-winning writer, Dr. Mathew Umukoro will be present as the chairman, along side with Dr. Ahmed Yerima, the guest writer and reader.
The latter, an artist of many parts rolled into one, is the artistic director of NTN (National Troupe of Nigeria). Most of the books to be featured in the exhibition are written by him.
Dr. Yerima, who is regarded as a cultural ambassador of Nigeria to the world, brought NTN to the limelight and has thought theater arts in reputable Nigerian Universities, such as OAU (Obafemi Awolowo University) and Ahmadu Bello University. Not only that, he has defied his tight schedule to pick up a teaching job at the University of Lagos's Department of Creative Arts. But as an associate lecturer in Theater Arts.
All these, his hosts have added to his credibility. In fact, he is expected to have an interactive session with other participants. "The guests and visitors at this year's celebration of 17th anniversary of Writers' Resort will therefore be privileged to encounter one on one, a formidable and well-rounded artist and intelligentsia and hear him read and discuss his writings, ask questions from him, and see sketches and experts of some of his theater productions for this is indeed the hall-mark of Writers' Resort to bring the writer and public closer, in an intensive artistic and creative exchanges, in the relaxed, informal and romantic environment of the Resort, which is a befitting retreat for all art lovers."
Yerima is also expected to come with the NTN. This is, of course, to treat the participants with his directional work - Wole Soyinka's Death and The King's Horseman, produced to celebrate the Nobel laureate's 70th birthday.
Besides the cultural displays and literature readings, the resort will also make provision for African dishes, such as, pounded yam, akara, eko, jollof rice and Ijoko palm wine, among others.
The track record of Writers' Resort, as stated, is fully documented in its international quarterly publication - WORD
Writers' Resort is said to have come a long way in its regular literary, theatrical and artistic activities, having featured well-known writers and artists. Such names as Femi Osanfisan, Niyi Osundare, Olu Obafemi, Kola Omotoso, Ken Saro-wiwa, the veteran TM Aluko, Abubakar Gimba, actress Karen King-Aribisala, Tolu Ajayi,Taiwo Ajayi-Lycett, veteran performer Funso Alabi, and the famous soprano classical singer Femi Akigbua, Akachi Ezeigbo and a host of others.
Prominent persons in the art community have also chaired and listened to some of the Resort's previous readings. They are names like Ambassador Segun Olusola, Dr Lateef Adegbite Segun Sofowote, Dr Duro Oni Professor Mrs Akoke Mohammed, Mr Rasheed Bgadamosi, , Professor Mrs. Ebun Clerk, Professor Jelili Omotola, Professor Theo Vincent, the American diplomat Mrs Ayanso.
Others include Chief Charles Adebiyi, Anthony Soetan, Nze Mark Odu, Professor Peter Omoluabi and Dr Adebgoyega Salami.

Original_Search_Title: The Igbo And Democracy: a Play of Dogs
Headline: Nigeria: The Orbit: The Igbo And Democracy: a Play of Dogs
Body_Text:
In a civilized society, political leadership normally flows from an
awareness of a people - their values, their human and material culture and
their aspirations. This empathy with a world emphasizes the relationship in
which the highest trust is built between the individual who seeks a mandate
and the people from whom he seeks the mandate.
Trust is not easily built: it comes from observation; it is based on
evaluation of character, of the purpose of power, of the value of the
individual and of his moral worth and weight in the community. The community
-- let us call it the electorate -- must be aware that it is exercising its
highest authority when it casts a ballot and hands the community"s mandate
to an individual, trusting that the individual will represent their will and
exercise the mandate with authority and integrity. Authority is of course
not coercive, it is arrived at not by indirection but through mediation, and
through consensus.
It does not express itself with horsewhips and sirens. It does not ride
in bullet-proof Humvees, nor does it advertise itself with
caprice. Authority is seen and hailed because it belongs to the people. It
does not belong in the private bedrooms tin gods. I speak of course about
the kind of authority that comes from democracy. Authority that is
enlightened; that is wrought upon the covenant between a free people and
their ministers. And we must remember that the minister is the servant, and
the ministry is service. The chief minister of a modern, sovereign state is
the president. He is no King. He is the chief servant of the land.
This knowledge makes it impossible for anyone to rig elections. In other
words, elections are meant to guarantee credible representation. But in
Nigeria, this axiom does not hold. Although we say that it is, Nigeria is
not a democratic state. It is a backward, neo-colonial state, with neither
head nor tail. It is an organized Mafiadom. Political representation flows
from a conclave of this local Mafiosi, who them organize sham
elections.
Nigeria is a make-believe; a harsh, brutish, Hobessian and Dantesque
Hell. It is the musical chair of pretender-Generals, many of whom are
half-literate; many of whom cannot spell the word "democracy." Of course,
these half-baked Generals have always been supported by pseudo-intellectual
types, who spout values that stand no taller than their primitive
instincts.
Nigeria is a classical affirmation of that truth: take a pig to the
Waldorf Astoria, it is still a pig. That is what many of these chaps
are. Primitive. Inelegant. Years in power have never really smoothened their
rough edges, because frankly, they belong to the gutter from which they rose
in their hordes to "keep Nigeria one" for violent and serial
carnalisation.
We already see them lurking in the brushes along the road to
2007. Nigeria"s democracy is the type called "nascent." It remains
nascent. It never ascends, nor does it have much prospect of ascension. It
is paraplegic. The current Vice-president, Atiku Abubakar once said of
Nigerians and democracy "we are learning." That may be true about Atiku
Abubakar, that he is trying seriously to learn some democracy. I think it is
a great virtue that he learns, for as a Fulani, the concept of democracy may
have been strange and alien to him. The Fulani society is a hierarchical one
of Kings, and serfs and commoners. It is not a democratic culture. The
Yoruba culture is also given to the monarchical ethic -- in which the
"kabiyesi", the king, is all.
That is why I suppose, we currently have the kabiyesi presidency; for
Obasanjo grew out of an undemocratic culture. These habits of overlordship
have dominated Nigeria since independence. Two factors account for this
aspects of Nigeria's political character: first is that the structures of
democratic participation did not take firm roots across Nigeria in the
decolonizing years before the soldiers struck.
The Northern Peoples Congress retained a very anti-democratic political
culture, and did not even brook opposition. The modernist, and democratic
forces in the north like Aminu Kano, Raji Abdalla, Saad Zungur, Patrick
Dokotiri, Joseph Tarka, Abubakar Imam etc. were constantly violently
neutralized because they were anti-monarchical, believed in the notions of
democratic participation, and constantly fought the corrupt traditional
institutions which the NPC prodded as its power base.
The emirs remained minor suzerains who supported the actions and
policies of the state actors under the NPC government. In the West, Obafemi
Awolowo struggled between democratic modernity and entrenched monarchical
authorities, and walked an ideological tightrope up till 1962 when the
Action Group adopted a forward looking programme of social democracy, and
Awo never survived it, because he had moved against these entrenched
interests.
The East of course was different because it was made up of cultures that
had remained vigorously republican and democratic -- the Igbo, the Ijaw, the
Ibibio etc -- who had no monarchical institutions until the rise of the
colonial warrant chiefs whom the Eastern women in fact fought and destroyed
in 1939. But the Hausa/Fulani and Yoruba cultures of power became dominant
in post-war Nigeria, and these came to bear upon the character, the
tyrannical authority of the military dictators who arrived and usurped the
powers of the state, and have remained at the epicenter of Nigeria"s
political culture. For the most part, Nigerians have been cowed into
accepting the notions that whoever is president is "king of Nigeria" and
must have all the unquestionable authority of the monarch. The president of
Nigeria must have the knife and the yam. He cuts and gives to whom ever he
wants.
The rest of Nigeria do not matter, and can really go to blazes. Although
this idea of presidential power is unacceptable to enlightened people, but
it is already at the core of the debate about transition, 2007, that the
current ethnarc, Olusegun Obasanjo will choose whoever will be the next
president of Nigeria.
That there was a "gentleman's" agreement about power moving back to the
North and so on. Mark, that the assertion was made, that the current
president will determine who succeeds him, by Anthony Anenih, who is the
putative chairman (of the board) of the People's Democratic Party.
It leaves us with the question: where are the people and where is the
democracy in that party? It leaves me with an even more important question,
because as an Igbo, with a long and very ancient tradition of democratic
practice in my culture, one anchored on the inexorable truth "Oha ka" -- the
people are supreme -- I often wonder if the Igbo people are on the same boat
with the rest of Nigeria. It is true that the very backward Nigerian
monarchical ethic is creeping slowly into the Igbo culture area with all the
fake and invented "traditional rulers", paper tigers created by obnoxious
edicts that seek to break the Igbo culture of autonomy; the real basis of
Igbo norms of industry and adventure; but the Igbo remain fiercely
republican.
They have no need for any blinking kabiyesi to make choices for
them. Because true Igbo people do not bow their heads to either man or
deity, they retain the ability to speak and act with autonomy and
conviction, with scant regard for the herd.
That is why on this matter of the Igbo presidency, Chukuwuma Onoh speaks
that way, Ochiagha Achuzia speaks the other way, Orji Uzo Kalu speaks one
way, Sam Egwu speaks another way, until they come to the Assembly of Umunna"
and dialogue, trade positions, quarrel openly and agree, and some one raises
a voice to say "kwenu!" and everyone says: "Yaaah." Even then, it would be
"ndi kwe, ndi ekweghi" -- some would accept, some will not. The Igbo cannot
function any other way. It is the basis of their democratic soul. It is a
very sophisticated way.
Of course, other Nigerians who do not understand the meaning of
democracy will say the Igbo are fractal and divided, and do not speak in one
voice. Obi Nwakanma, I say, may the day never come when the Igbo will fail
to speak from the core of individual conviction.
The Igbo have survived by knowing when to align, and by knowing when to
cut an individual swath in this forest of a million daemons. So, Atiku
Abubakar and possibly the rest of Nigeria may just be learning
democracy. But the Igbo are not just learning democracy. They have practiced
it for at least ten thousand years. And they are waiting for the rest of
Nigeria to grow up. I grew up watching and observing the most sophisticated
forms of democratic performance through the clan Assemblies -- the "Otu
Umunna" -- and its method of concentric representation. I have seen it work
in the village councils, the Community Unions, the guilds, where men of my
grandfather"s generation, brokered the most complex political questions with
savvy.
Nigerians do not understand the Igbo: the world to the Igbo is
performance. It is like the play of dogs: "you fall once for me, and I fall
once for you, that is play. But I fall once for you, and you do not fall for
me, that is a battle." When the Igbo want the presidency of Nigeria, as a
matter of course, not even God himself will stop it, because the Igbo also
have the saying: "onye kwe, chi ya ekwe." May 2007 come.

Original_Search_Title: Akande Accuses Oyinlola of Witch-Hunting Over Alleged N126m Fraud
Headline: Nigeria: Akande Accuses Oyinlola of Witch-Hunting Over Alleged N126m Fraud
Body_Text:
Lagos — IMMEDIATE past governor of Osun State, Chief Bisi Akande has alleged that the Osun State governor, Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola is trying to witch-hunt him as a result of alleged N126m fraud.
In a letter addressed to the Osun state governor, Chief Akande stated that "very recently, the Federal Government published the lists of failed contracts without asking any head of government (past or present) to refund lost funds resulting from such abandoned contracts. To that extent, it is curiously suspicious that, in your own judgement, you would tie any failings of the contractors and consultants of the 'Bola Ige House' round my neck even as it is obvious to you that the project was never abandoned. It was substantially completed at the time we left office".
Chief Akande, however, stated that rather than concentrating on improving the standard of living of the people, Governor Oyinlola was creating a 'problem of esteem and dignity' for his (Oyinlola) office. He said: "I will like to state, once again, that rather than achieve your motive in this perfidious enterprise, you are creating a problem of esteem and dignity for the office of Governor, even for yourself and for the future, because every claim made against me by you are based on your personal assumption. For instance, your illustration in the hypothesis using the example of 'yam and oil', in the concluding part of your address, is absolutely hollow and bankrupt although you claim to be a lawyer."
Chief Akande while faulting Prince Oyinlola's claim as an Awoist pointed out that "your reference to me as a 'failed Awoist' makes it necessary for me to educate you and those who constantly deride the Yoruba leaders of Awolowo's school of thought. The simple lesson for you, at this point, is that "Awoism" is a body of philosophy which places the welfare of citizens above the comfort of those in government at reasonably reduced cost. That is exactly what I stood for as governor from May 1999 to May 2003. The verdict of the people is clear on this."
In addition, he said "may I say, with deep sense of responsibility, that it is an insult to the sensibility of Obasanjo's policy and the authority of the National Assembly, who created the Independent Corrupt Practices Commission (ICPC), for you to publicly insist that a matter of this nature will be handled by one of your Magistrates, an employee of your government and a public servant who can be compelled to do your bidding."
In furtherance to this, Chief Akande maintained that there were untrue facts in presentations which Governor Oyinlola issued to the media. His words: "While ignoring the irrelevances in your address, some of them bothering on pettiness, it will suffice, for now, to cite only a few of your claims to prove the totality of the falsehoods contained in the presentation to your 'World Press'.
First, your excellency claimed that you never received my letter which, incidentally, was sent by courier. Perhaps, you have forgotten that courier companies maintain a record of delivery and receipts. May I let you know that the receipt of letter was signed for by an officer in your office. Secondly, you stated that I approved "the augmentation of N230 million for additional sub-contractors on May 19, 2003" but you failed to reveal to the public that it was you who revalidated whatever I approved and authorized payment even when, according to you, the accounts remained muddled. Also, you claimed, falsely again, that certain purchases were made which included some items whose values were inflated. Do I need to tell you as the governor that a governor has no business purchasing any items of whatever description? While it may be the style of some governors to do so, purchase of individual items of furniture, fixtures and fittings was not my indulgence."
It would be recalled that Chief Akande was recently declared wanted by the Osun state Police Command over the alleged fraud of N126m.

Original_Search_Title: Wallahi, Show-Yin-Ka Ne!
Headline: Nigeria: Wallahi, Show-Yin-Ka Ne!
Body_Text:
'This is the second part of the excerpt from Ifowodo's account of
Soyinka's detention under the Abacha regime, a work in progress aimed not
only to give the anti-military, anti-dictatorship context of Soyinka's
life-long activism, but also to have the point of connection to him that
would also put it in the mould of a personal recollection.The concludng part
will be published next week
I URGED him to deliver it at the address on the obverse of the card
should we be arrested as I feared we would. I had given the one hundred and
twenty pounds I had on me to him in Accra. Akeem had handed over one
thousand, seven hundred dollars, savings made with admirable self-denial
from his stipends during nine months of writers' residencies in Los Angeles,
USA, and Graz, Austria, with the hope of finally making a two-year
down-payment for a cheap Lagos apartment. We had arranged to travel together
by phone.
He happened to have called Ike Okonta from Graz while I was spending an
extra week in London with him after the end of the Commonwealth Heads of
Government Meeting in Edinburgh. I had attended the meeting as part of the
CLO's delegation to press for the expulsion of Nigeria from the body in
fulfilment of the threat issued two years earlier in Auckland when Nigeria
was suspended due to the judicial murder of Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight of his
fellow Ogoni minority and environmental rights activists.
Glad for the opportunity to speak to each other again after nearly a
year, we had agreed on making what we thought would be a stealthy road entry
into our own country together. Unfortunately, he was to depart Graz four
days before I would leave London, but the opportunity was too good to let
go. Twelve good hours of updating each other on what we had been up to in
that time! He elected to wait for me in Accra. In those four days, he would
try to discover more of the city where once Kwame Nkrumah had sought to
unify a continent turned against itself by the thriving legacy of
colonialism.
As we walked to the hut, my mind revisited the Auckland summit. The
Abacha regime had chosen the high noon for cold-blooded murder, when the
Commonwealth meeting was holding to hang the Ogoni 9 in defiance of the
pleas that came from every nook and cranny of the world. The show trial
preceding their conviction was so irredeemably flawed that everybody had
thought they could not possibly be executed. Not, at the very least, until a
proper trial in a regular court of law with free choice of counsel for the
"convicted" persons.
Their execution had given the clearest and most unambiguous statement
from General Sani Abacha to the world concerning what he thought of "the hue
and cry about human rights violations in the country." In his terse and
blunt coup-day broadcast which set a record for lasting precisely three
minutes, and that long only because of the great difficulties he had reading
it, he had vowed to "deal decisively with any person or group of persons
that chooses to test our will."
He had certainly chosen his moment well, the many other atrocities that
marked his three-year rule so far having failed to deter "subversive
elements and their external collaborators from threatening the peace and
security of the country in the guise of human rights and pro-democracy
activism." Indeed, in his rush to spit in the face of all the pleading faces
from around the world, he had not bothered to wait for the report of the
proceedings of the special tribunal as the enabling decree
stipulated. Neither had he felt constrained by a pending appeal challenging
the jurisdiction and procedure of the tribunal.
Suddenly, all the heads of state who had been assured by phone, whose
emissaries had been given "solemn promises" in person, and so, had implored
quiet diplomacy as the safest way "to lead the bull out of the china shop,"
began to speak in undiplomatic language. Prime Minister John Major of
Britain described the executions as judicial murder.
President Nelson Mandela who had sent a veteran crusader, the Reverend
Archbishop Desmond Tutu, as his personal emissary was so outraged he
described Abacha as a man with a heart of stone. Tutu himself, his anger of
Mosaic proportions, was moved to curse his brother in disregard of the
biblical injunction "whosoever calleth his brother fool is damned" by
calling Abacha something worse: "an evil man."
As we entered the hut, led by Lanky in front and another spook who had
emerged from the shadows behind us, the prospects of a very long night
appeared even more real than they had been all evening.
Two battery-charged night lamps lit the hut. There was a table in the
centre, a bench on the other side of it and two metal chairs at the
corners. A man in a brown French suit, still wearing sunglasses, sat on the
chair to the left; a pregnant woman in an ill-fitting gown that only served
to make her stomach look like a diabolical punishment sat on the one to the
right. As we entered, she spat in a plastic cup which she covered with a
folded newspaper. Akeem's two big suitcases containing mostly books and
sundry newspaper and magazine clippings were searched first.
There was nothing hazily close to contraband in them, but photocopies of
newspaper articles on, and interviews with, Ken Saro-Wiwa were gleefully
impounded. Akeem tried in vain to point out how old the materials were and
the fact that they were not even written by him. As the contents of Akeem's
suitcases were being stuffed back in, haphazardly, I was ordered to open my
suitcase. I weighed the odds against me, and still thinking we were in the
hands of Customs, said, "You won't find anything there worth your time."
"We'll know that soon enough. For your information, we are from the
NDLEA. This is a combined security services checkpoint. If we see anything
that is the business of another agency, we will hand you over to them. But
we will also search very carefully for drugs," Lanky explained, in the
patiently cold tones of power.
The NDLEA. I was relieved and felt for a moment that we might after all
resume our interrupted journey, after they were done with a disappointing
search. But something in his manner had suddenly urged me not to be deceived
though I couldn't name it. Perhaps checkpoint-wise and wily, he knew he was
bound to come up with something to reward unhurried efficiency? Perhaps he
had recognised me? No, I assured myself; my face was not that frequent in
the newspapers as to be recognisable by a security man at a border
post.
"In that case," I said, happier, nevertheless, with the prospects of
dealing with the NDLEA rather than the SSS, "you might as well let us
complete our journey. I don't have anything there with even a trace of
nicotine." "Shut up and let us do our work," the rear-guard spook barked. He
wore a black kaftan over a matching sokoto. There was a wristwatch-sized
badge pinned to his right breast. It bore the portrait of General Abacha in
babanriga and a hand-woven cap.
"Where is your passport?" Lanky demanded, adding that henceforth, I was
only to answer questions and not volunteer a word of my own. It was getting
darker and any hope of completing the journey growing thinner. "I don't have
it," I replied, throwing myself on the ropes. How was I going to explain I
got through immigration? I remembered the subterfuge for avoiding
immigration at SÃ¨mÃ¨ on my way out.
I had arrived at the Mile Two departure point at about 5.30 a.m. only to
discover that my ECOWAS pass had expired a couple of months ago. Too late to
do anything about it, I accepted the option suggested to me by the driver of
the Accra-bound vehicle. "That one no be problem. I go give you person to
cross you for borda," he had said, with the confidence of one who dealt with
that kind of problem every day. I looked unconvinced and vexed him into
disclosing his credentials, repeatedly beating his chest as he spoke.
Bleak options
"Ah, Oga, leave that one for me. Na me be Man Leke, alias O-Hay-Yu -
Organzation of Afrika Yunity. Ask anybody in this Mile Two, dem know
me. Ai-yam the only in-ta-na-shion-al dryvah to cross every borda for
Afrika, even go Libya. Is Gadafi crown me O-Hay-Yu himself in Treepoli. Oga,
if I no fit cross you, call me bastard."
My options were bleak anyway, and one really could not dismiss the road
savvy of these trans-border drivers. So I put my fate in his hands. We set
out in a light blue Peugeot 504 station wagon which did not look like it
could endure the long hard drive to Accra, but I had used the public
transport system enough to know that Nigerian drivers' art of coaxing
unwilling cars to their destination and back was nothing if not magic of the
first rate.
At Agbara, Man Leke aka O-Hay-Yu stopped to pick up a man in customs
uniform, standing knowingly apart from the horde of commuters at the nearby
bus stop. There was no space for him in the car, which was already chockfull
and I got ready to launch a vigorous protest if O-Hay-Yu attempted squeezing
him in beside me in the front seat, but he killed my protest by explaining
that this was the man to "cross me for borda." For that, I was prepared to
perch with one half of my buttocks on the now divided seat and to bear the
pain that came from having to sit with the other half on the handbrake
lever.
A few poles before the gate at the Nigeria side of the border, O-Hay-Yu
pulled off the road, parked, and the customsman got out. O-Hay-Yu asked me
to come down too and after explaining the task to be performed to his man,
drove off to join the slow line of cars waiting to be searched and extorted
before being cleared to leave Nigeria.
"Do you have any documents on you?" the customs man asked, turning to
me. "No," I said, but looking in my briefcase all the same to make doubly
sure I had not forgotten any "incriminating" scrap of paper there. It was a
constant threat, this matter of documents, and over time, rights activists
had learned to travel or move about in the country bare, that is, without
anything that could fall into the catch-all category of "subversive
documents" on their person.

Original_Search_Title: I'm Not Witchhunting Ex-Gov Akande - Oyinlola - Gives Details of Alleged Fraud
Headline: Nigeria: I'm Not Witchhunting Ex-Gov Akande - Oyinlola - Gives Details of Alleged Fraud
Body_Text:
Osogbo — GOVERNOR Olagunsoye Oyinlola of Osun State yesterday described how his immediate predecessor and political opponent, Chief Bisi Akande allegedly defrauded the government of about N127 million from Government House contract
Gov. Oyinlola also declared that his administration was not witchhunting the former governor as claimed, explaining that he paid two visits to Akande's Ibadan home in a bid to prevail on him to help sort out the scam.
Addressing a crowded press conference in Osogbo, the Osun capital, Gov. Oyinlola said his administration discovered on assumption of office that the commissioned Government House "had no supply of electricity, functional toilets and water supply; some of the toilets in the governor's office had no doors and windows and were yet to be painted.
The governor recounted how the alleged fraud was uncovered thus: "The embarrassing situation naturally called for explanations, hence the contractor was invited to brief the government on the position of things.
"We found out that the total payment to the contractors did not tally with the expenditure recorded in the books of government A staggering sum of about N127 million was discovered to be missing and not accounted for.
"We progressed further by inviting the consultants to the project, Messrs. Tropic Consult who volunteered the information that the difference in figures recorded represented instructions from some officers of the then Osun State Government for additional sub-contracts even though there were no contractual agreements to that effect.
"My senior brother, Chief Bisi Akande, approved the augmentation of N230 million on May 19, 2003, shortly before the expiration of the term of his administration
"Consequent upon the observed contractual abnormalities, a technical committee was raised to investigate the circumstance leading to the sub-contracting of these jobs.
"The committee sat and submitted its report on November 2003. One of the highlights of the report is the purchase of a 14-inch television set for about N120,000... Another startling revelation is the award and payment for door name tags at a sum of N2 million Nothing of such was done at Bola Ige House (the name of the Government House).
"We equally discovered full payment to the tune of about N20 million to horticulturists for Bola Ige House. Again, nothing of such is on the ground. Still baffling is the supply of non-existing furniture that gulped N30 million."
Governor Oyinlola alleged that from the above and related incidents "it is obvious that Chief Bisi Akande was eating the boiled yam and oil through somebody's hand. He did that to avoid the tell tale of oil and crumbs of yam on his fingers."
"However, an x-ray has revealed the final destination of the yam and oil, and that place, from all intents and purposes, is Chief Akande's stomach," he further alleged.
Oyinlola revealed that efforts were made by him to reach out to Chief Akande to resolve the issue without any media hype.
"On my part, I made sure that whatever our government's observations and reactions are about the conduct of his (Akande's) administration, which are considered to have fallen short of, expectations are kept away from the prying eyes of the media."
"It might interest you to note that nothing happened between November 2003 until February 2004, when I notified the former governor that the consultant, Architect Lanre Oladeji had notified me in confidence that the missing N127 million was used for electioneering campaign."
Oyinlola further stated that the duo of Chief Azeez Aresekola Alao and Dele Ige were aware of the moves made by him to ensure that a workable arrangement was made with Chief Akande for a refund of the alleged loot.
He described as cheap blackmail the allegation of Chief Akande that he is being witchhunted.
According to Oyinlola, "It is absolutely unthinkable and unimaginable to suggest that I would want to blackmail Chief Akande."
Akande was alleged to have on May 19, 2003 approved the payment of N114,480,060 to "avoid any mess".
Oyinlola then vowed to ensure that the law takes its course in bringing all the perpetrators of the alleged scam to book.
Meanwhile, the former SSG in the Akande administration, Mr. Sola Akinwunmi and the former finance commissioner, Chief Lere Adebayo, earlier declared wanted as Akande, were spotted at the police headquarters yesterday morning.
The duo later had a chat with the Assistant Commissioner of Police in charge of investigations, Emmanuel Obiakor and are expected to report back on July 14 this year.

Original_Search_Title: N127m Contract Scam: Akande Not Telling the Truth, Says Oyinlola
Headline: Nigeria: N127m Contract Scam: Akande Not Telling the Truth, Says Oyinlola
Body_Text:
Osogbo — OSUN State Governor, Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola has described his predecessor in office, Chief Bisi Akande as a pathological liar for not saying the truth about his involvement in the N127 million contract scam in the state.
Addressing newsmen in Osogbo yesterday on government's position on the alleged "Bola Ige House" scam, Governor Oyinlola denied that he was on a vengeance mission to tarnish the reputation of the former governor.
"May I not succeed if I told lies against Chief Akande over this contract issue, but if he has lied against me, I leave him in God's hands for judgment," he said.
"It is obvious that Chief Bisi Akande was eating the boiled yam and oil through somebody's hand. He did that to avoid the tell tale of oil and crumbs of yam on his fingers. However, an x-tray has revealed the final destination of the yam and oil, and that place, from all intents and purposes, is Chief Akande's stomach."
"It is not surprising therefore, that Chief Bisi Akande's outburst of recent, which clearly portrays him as a confusionist and an incorrigible peddler of falsehood. Since the former Governor has blown the lid off the drum of rot, let me therefore quickly throw more light on the project, Bola Ige House, which has been constructed to serve as the new Governor's Office.
"It is absolutely unthinkable and unimaginable to suggest that I would want to blackmail my brother, Chief Bisi Akande, on account of his attack on Mr. President. I must confess that Chief Bisi Akande must have been put through the due process but for Chief Obasanjo who counseled on the need to handle the issue with utmost respect.
"I believe that it is extremely naive and mischievious for anybody to associate Chief Olusegun Obasanjo with the self-inflicted travails of Chief Adebisi Akande, who appears totally confused by the devastating discoveries perpetrated by the once most prudent governor in Nigeria as painted by the media.
"The burble burst long before the last local government elections and I guess that the issue is one veritable arsenal that could have nailed whatever reputation Chief Akande ascribes to himself if it was deployed.
"My belief in decent politicking precluded me from deploying a weapon I know he would not have hesitated to use in blowing me out of political existence. Having expressed this, all I can advise is for Chief Bisi Akande to muster up his defence to clear up his reputation before the arbiters at the law courts. Heaven knows that I did all I could not to get to this destination, without fear or favour, ill-will or affection.
"Chief Bisi Akande has himself stated that the whole episode appears messy. The construction engineers, quantity surveyors and architects were at variance with their figures and the records are in a muddle. Since the buck stops on his table as chief executive of Osun State at the period the funds got missing, by acts of commission or omission, it should be naturally expected that he should clear the air on this matter, which involves public funds.
"All I have done are consistent and are in line with our policy of running an open administration that is accountable to the citizenry who have entrusted us with the task of managing their affairs for a constitutional period of four years."
Governor Oyinlola said he did not believe in probing what occurred before his tenure because "probes don't yield any dividend we are witnesses to so many policy measures put in place. The same policy measures are subsequently reversed in later years. What have we gained by putting resources that would have been positively utilized elsewhere into the aspect of probing and that is why I have sold a manifesto to the people of the state.
"I will only focus on those issue that I promised the people and I will continue to fulfil them. There is nothing like sentiment in this matter. If Chief Akande didn't open it to the media in his attempt to blackmail us, there would have been no need for me to address this press conference because I believe that governance is a continuum and that we must be able to give reference to the office of the governor.
"And that is why since November last year for God's sake that I brought this to his attention, he seemed to be willing to make up for the losses but his (Akande) ego, his false reputation prevented him from wanting to reach an agreement with me because he was adjudged the most prudent, the most effective governor."
Meanwhile, former secretary to the state government (SSG), Alhaji Sola Akinwunmi and former finance commissioner, Chief Lere Adebayo reported to the police at about 9:30 am yesterday accompanied by their supporters. The duo said they were ready to face prosecution if the government has evidence to prove their allegation.

Original_Search_Title: University of Lagos: Dress Code for Decency
Headline: Nigeria: University of Lagos: Dress Code for Decency
Body_Text:
NEWS that the authorities of the University of Lagos planned to introduce a dress code for its students to check the prevailing trend of indecent and provocative dressing, especially among its female students, did not come to many as a surprise. If anything, popular opinion has it that the action is coming a little too late because having for long been exposed to a permissive setting that enabled them to imbibe a fashion or dress culture that has succeeded in eroding every norm of decency and modesty, it will be difficult for these students to accept the imposition of a dress code at this time.
Indeed before the present move by the UNILAG authorities, a visitor to the school or any other higher institution in Nigeria could not help but notice the alarmingly increasing population of scantily clad female students prancing seductively about the campus.
