The authorities presently consider this form of dressing inappropriate and provocative because it indecently exposes sensitive parts of the human body which can be temptingly distracting as a result of the strong sex appeal it conjures.
This is why under the new dress code, both male and female students are forbidden from wearing all clothes which reveal sensitive parts of the body such as the bust, chest, belly, upper arms and the buttocks. Examples of such dresses are given as transparent clothing, spaghetti tops, wicked straps, mono straps, show me your belly and waist low trousers.
Also banned are all tight fitting clothes including skirts, trousers and blouses as well as outfits such as knickers and mini-skirts and dresses which are not, at least, knee length. Equally not permitted for wearing by students are T-shirts and jeans which carry obscene and subliminal messages, just as party wear, beach wear and slippers are not to be worn to lectures by any student.
Speaking to Vanguard Features on this development and how the ban will be enforced, UNILAG Dean of Student Affairs Division, Professor Dele Olowokudejo informed that as a first step in this direction, "lecturers have been empowered to exclude students who dress indecently from their lecture rooms and laboratories." He did not stop there. "Administrators also will not render service or attend to students who are indecently dressed," he said, adding that the ban covers the wearing of such dresses or clothes both within and outside the lecture rooms and other places within the campus. According to him, "they can go to Abule Oja and strip themselves naked if they want, but they should maintain decency in dressing while on campus."
Sanctions
But beyond exclusion from classes, what other sanctions await students who flout the new dress code requirements." Part of the punishment is identifying them publicly and disgracing them openly. If that happens more than once, then they have their records endorsed, which means we can exclude them from some privileges they're normally entitled to. We can say they may no longer stay in their hostels and we may not recommend them for scholarships or bursaries, or we wouldn't allow them to be with other decent students when they're being interviewed for jobs or placements," Prof. Olowokudejo informed.
Given the wide scope of the dress ban and the sweeping sanctions that its infringement attracts, the question maybe asked whether this is not an overkill or using a sledge hammer to kill a fly. Mrs. Ifueko Fadaka, the Faculty Officer of the Faculty of Law, does not think so because the problem according to her is serious enough to warrant whatever punishment or sanction applied to curb it.
"Let us be sincere, why should a female student reveal three quarters of her breast and then go to a male lecturer's office to talk to him? And if the man touches her, then she will start shouting sexual harassment. We also went to university and we also wore mini during our time. But it's not like what we're seeing today where somebody wears the kind of mini that she cannot even bend down.
"You don't follow fashion blindly and stupidly. You follow fashion if it fits you. If the fashion requires you to expose your legs and you don't have fine legs, then please don't expose them. It can even discourage a man. If you have legs as fat as yams, then you want to wear mini because people are wearing mini. That is stupid! If you have a bow leg or K-leg, it doesn't make sense to wear mini. And that is something these students don't seem to know," she said, adding that beyond the demand of fashion, the dull ones, dress provocatively to tempt or seduce their male lecturers into passing them during exams.
Some outgoing students VF spoke with agreed with Mrs. Fadaka that the problem basically can be traced to the fact that some students have become slaves to fashion that they don't know what is decent for them to wear at any point in time. "Yes, I think we need a dress code here. I totally support it because the way some of us dress here is really terrible that I as a girl I will look and say ah-ah. It was really bad, I must tell you," confessed Miss Oluwakemi Sola who has just completed her degree programme in French. "I think the problem is that some people follow fashion blindly. They see these things on TV and copy them blindly. Other girls dress that way to tempt lecturers because they know that some male lecturers are weak and can easily fall for them. That is what has been going on, and so we need the dress code," she said.
Also in support of the dress code is Mrs. Patricia Agbakwu-Ajegwu, the President of Fashion Designers Association of Nigeria (FADAN). An alumnus of the University of Lagos, she said she is in support of the code because indecent dressing has become synonymous with fashion in most Nigerian universities.
"I'm totally against indecent dressing. You can project your assets without being indecent. Why should a young girl going to a lecture hall or library to read put on a bustier that will expose her breasts? Is she going to study or is she going for a fashion parade or night party? Or is it the case of a female student in spaghetti wear that barely covers her breasts approaching a male lecturer in his office. I'm a woman for Christ sake and I know what this provocative exposure of this sensitive part of the body can do to a man. So, the authorities of the University of Lagos are very much in order in taking step to check this ugly trend," said Mrs. Agbakwu-Ajegwu who also lamented that "the last time I visited Moremi Hall where I stayed as a student, I discovered that it has been turned into a den of prostitutes."
Also speaking more or less in the same vein, another popular Lagos based fashion designer, Folake Martins of Schon Afrique averred that it was unacceptable for women, students inclusive, to expose their bodies indecently. "The problem is that most of these female students are young, impressional adults and they are easily carried away. Unfortunately, the fashion world is moving towards undue exposure of sensitive parts of the woman's body. You can see this in most of the adverts today as the models are dressed to expose as much of their bodies as possible to attract attention. This is unacceptable and we must not encourage it," she said, adding that was why she has refused "to design clothes that expose the private parts of women."
Indecent exposure
But there is already an indication that the enforcement of the dress code at UNILAG might be resisted as some students have started expressing opposition to it. One of such students, Amina Bakare, says the code is okay only if its enforcement is restricted to the classrooms, adding that any attempt to do otherwise would be seriously resisted.
"They shouldn't tell us not to dress the way we like. Some people express themselves by the way they dress. They shouldn't say we should not wear pants and tights. It's wrong for them to tell us not to wear these things. They say we dress to tempt our male lecturers; that's not true. We dress to express that we feel good," she stated, her voice rising excitedly as some of her female colleagues hailed and applauded her.
Reminded of the indecent exposure that goes with this type of dressing, she replied thus: "Oh, for crying out loud, wearing tight pants is modest enough, it doesn't show anything, except maybe my tummy. In any case, if you have cause to flaunt anything, please go ahead and flaunt it."
However, some male students are disturbed by the indiscriminate way and manner their female colleagues have been "flaunting their "assets" which they (male students) find highly distracting, especially during lectures. "The way our female students dress is highly disturbing. Sometimes we'll be in the lecture room and you'll see them strolling in in very skimpy and transparent dresses and distracting everybody including the lecturer," complained Olalekan Oladokun, a third year mathematics student.
"I believe some of them do this to attract and seduce the lecturer because they need something from him. But some others dress like that because they want to look good and at the same time attract the attention of the guys (male students)," he explained.
Another male student, who simply gave his name as Joseph of the Department of Biochemistry described the dress habits of the female students as "alarming." He said the introduction of the dress code was a welcome development as it will help to bring sanity in the way students, both male and female, dress in the institution.
Apart from blaming the female students who indecently expose their bodies in the name of fashion, Mrs. Fadaka also holds their mothers responsible for the immoral dressing habit of their children in school. Some of the mothers of these children, she claimed, encourage their daughters to dress this way to seduce their male lecturers for special favours.
"We had a case here of one of them who encouraged the daughter to go and sleep with a lecturer because the lecturer refused to pass her. The woman said the daughter should go ahead to do it and pass, after all the thing no get metre. That is how bad the problem has become," she informed.
Her sentiment in this respect is equally shared by Mrs. Agbakwu-Ajegwu who said the problem persists because some parents not only encourage their daughters to dress immorally, they also defend them when people raise eyebrows at such scandalous dressing.
"Can you imagine that a so-called influential woman went to LASU (Lagos State University) to tongue-lash and threaten a lecturer for walking out her indecently dressed daughter from class. It happened and that is a woman who should have known better," she said disapprovingly.
Meanwhile, the introduction of the dress code is catching on fast in some other higher institutions since UNILAG first mooted the idea. At the last count, institutions such as LASU, University of Calabar, Bayero University Kano, University of Maiduguri, Enugu State University (ESUT), Othman Dan Fodio University, Sokoto, University of Ado-Ekiti (UNAD), Olabisi Onabanjo University, Ago Iwoye and the Federal University of Technology, Akure (FUTA) have all joined the train.

Original_Search_Title: Soldiers to Quell Riots, Police Take Over States
Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers to Quell Riots, Police Take Over States
Body_Text:
Lagos — APPARENTLY leaving nothing to chance, Federal Government may deploy
soldiers to quell any violence that may arise from today's planned
nationwide strike over hiked fuel prices.
President Olusegun Obasanjo met with the Service Chiefs including
Inspector-General of Police (IGP), Mr. Tafa Balogun, behind closed doors,
yesterday, to discuss the security implications of the impending
strike.
Ahead of the industrial action, scores of heavily armed mobile and
regular policemen patrolled the major streets of Owerri, the Imo State
Capital, Makurdi, Benue State, and Lagos.
Police authorities in the naiton's former capital said they have ordered
their men not to shoot at protesters even as use of horsewhips and sticks
was banned.
Defence authorities said the military is waiting in the wings to join
the police in quelling any violence that may arise from the planned
strike.
Defence spokesman, Col. A Adewale told newsmen in Abuja that the
military had been placed at alert to quel any violence that was beyond the
capability of the police to handle.
He said what the military needed to roll out tanks was a presidential
directive to the Military High Command to act.
Details of meeting between Obasanjo and the service chiefs which held
behind closed doors at the Presidential Villa, Abuja were not made
public.
The extra-ordinary meeting which lasted over an hour, commenced at about
2.00pm and it was said to be convened by President Obasanjo.
It was gathered that national issues requiring urgent national attention
were discussed, particularly on the need to ensure security of lives and
property should the strike hold.
At the meeting were the Chief of Defence Staff (CDS), General Alexander
Ogomudia, Chief of Army Staff (COAS), Lt-Gen. Martin Luther Agwai, Chief of
Naval Staff (CNS), Vice Admiral Samuel Afolayan and Chief of Air. Staff
(CAS), Air Marshall Jonah Wuyep.
Anti-riot policemen in Owerri in their hundreds trooped out as early as
8.00 a.m. and patrolled almost all the major streets and communities near
Amakohia.
Armed with guns and teargas cannisters, the policemen chanted war songs
as they marched while a fleet of siren-blaring police cars and trucks
escorted them.
A large number of senior police officers were in the procession.
Police spokesman, Chike Nwabuwa was said to have travelled to Umuahia,
Abia State, for a meeting when Daily Champion called his office but another
officer, who did not reveal his identity confirmed that the procession was
to warn trouble makers against violence from today.
It was a difficult experience for workers and travellers due to
unprecedented traffic hold-up in the morning occasioned by long fuel queues
in Owerri.
Vehicles stayed in line for hours and many workers could not get to
their offices early.
There was a rush to purchase fuel by taxis and commercial motor cyclists
resulting in huge traffic hold-ups on the roads.
Mobile policemen in Makurdi also kicked off patrolling the streets of
the town in apparent preparation for the strike even as the state branch of
the NLC agreed to commence the industrial action as directed by the national
secretariat.
Daily Champion learnt that mobile policemen were engaged in a dress
rehearsal to confront persons involved in unlawful acts today.
But some workers read the presence of the policemen as an intimidation
to prevent them from participating in any rally in the state capital.
But the state chairman of the NLC, Mr. Boga Abuul, told reporters in
Makurdi that workers have unanimously agreed to commence strike by today
should the Federal Government fail to reverse to the old fuel prices.
According to Abuul, the NLC had gone a step further to mobilise other
social organisations like the motorcycle hirers association and market women
and traders to join the strike, adding that Labour was only providing a
platform for the masses to resist the fuel price hike.
He said the presence of policemen in the streets would not prevent the
strike, adding that police personnel were also victims of the Federal
Government's policy of fuel price increases.
Calling the Borno lawmakers' attention to the impending strike, the
Majority Leader, Hon. Abdulsalam Abdulkarim noted that the industrial action
is likely to disrupt the proceedings of the assembly and also because of the
security implications, the assembly should adjourn to June 15.
The motion was subsequently seconded by member representing Dikwa
constituency, Hon. Mohammed Zakariya.
Heavy human and vehicular traffic were recorded yesterday in the various
markets and filling stations in Asaba, Delta State Capital, as customers
jostled to purchases the essential products in readiness for the
strike.
The commissioner for Local Government and Chieftaincy Affairs, Mr. Moses
Odibo expressed support for NLC's action.
The state council of Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), Monday said the body
had mobilised for the exercise and insisted that all filling stations,
banks, markets, schools and work places (both government and private
institutions) would be shut down from today.
Customers had as early as 7.45 am besieged the known filling stations
and markets in Asaba to fill their fuel tanks, jerry cans as well as
purchase essential food stuff in line with the NLC's directive and fear that
the strike would last for 21 days.
Traders at the Ogbe-Ogonogo and Mammy markets in Asaba as at Monday
consequently increased the cost of their wares as customers made bulk
purchases.
Daily Champion checks revealed that a cup of beans and rice which before
respectively cost N15 and N20 went for N20 and N30 yesterday while a tuber
of yam sold for between N250 and N350.
Also, butchers and fish sellers make brisk business as customers flood
their tables to get the items even above their normally purchased
quantities.
At the wholesale section of Ogbeogonogo market, some top government
functionaries and their wards were seen loading bags of rice and several
tins and gallons of assorted tomatoes and groundnut (vegetable) oil into
their cars.
The development forced most hawkers and those without shops to line up
along the Nnebisi road of the market in a bid to sell their wares following
increase in demand.
Kerosine sellers in gallons and bottle at the market have increased the
cost per bottle of the product to between N120 to N150 from about N90 as
customers besiege them to get the item.
At filling stations long queues of vehicles were noticed following the
high demand for petrol which sell for N55 (petrol), N1,500 for 20
litres.
In Benin, the Edo State Capital, thousands of anxious customers besieged
banks to withdraw money in preparation for the strike.
The development coincided with the increase in prices of consumable good
and services in all markets in the city.
In order not to be caught napping, many depositors were seen in banks
making frantic efforts to withdraw money, make purchases and stock pile
foodstuff and other household weeds.
The situation has also compounded cash flow in nearly all the banks as
they found it extremely difficult coping with the demands of their
depositors.
Traffic situation along Akpapava Street which houses the Central Bank of
Nigeria (CBN) office and other financial institutions was hectic as
customers scrambled for available parking spaces.
Addressing members of the police force-during his official tour of
police formations in Lagos State, the new commissioner of Police, Mr Israel
Ajao cautioned policemen against flogging protesters, adding that they are
human beings.
Urging his men to comply with the Inspector-General of Police's (IGP)
order banning use of live bullets during the protests, Ajao noted that any
officer who shoots indiscriminately while dispersing crowds would face the
wrath of the law.
Nothing that the use of firearms must be in strict compliance with the
Force Order, Rule 237of the Police Act, the commissioner reminded the
officers that the police was meant to protect the citizenry as well as
enforce the law, but not to kill the people.
"Don't cork your gun unless you are ready to shoot. Remember, if you
make a mistake and kill human beings, you cannot create one. Even when you
are pursuing armed robbery suspect, it is better to let them go than to
shoot innocent soul," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Besiege Banks, Filling Stations - Senate Moves to Avert Action
Headline: Nigerians Besiege Banks, Filling Stations - Senate Moves to Avert Action
Body_Text:
Lagos — BULK purchase of food items and fuel, panic withdrawal of money from banks and crowds at filling stations were the hallmarks of preparations across the country for tomorrow's planned nationwide strike over the fuel price hike.
In Lagos, an unusual surge of customers was recorded at the major markets such as Sandgrosse, Iddo, Otto, Mile 12, Awolowo market at Mushin Olosa and others.
There was no noticeable increase in prices of food items, however.
A customer at Sandgrosse, who identified herself as Mrs Hassana Adewale, said she came to the market to buy enough food items to last her family of eight for at least two weeks.
According to her even if the government and Labour resolved the fuel price matter within the two-week period, the purchases would be to her family's advantage.
Most of the customers were seen buying items such as garri, yam flour, yams, rice, beans, palm oil, vegetable oil and so on.
Lengthy queues snaked out of some banks in the nation's former capital and also in Abuja, from as early as 7.30 am.
Both the old and new generation banks were similarly affected by the trend as people reacted to the NLC call to stock cash and food, given that the strike may be prolonged.
Many motorists in the country, spotted at various filling stations yesterday, said they were filling their tanks in anticipation of the strike.
Jenrycan-carrying residents were spotted at the different stations purchasing mainly kerosine and petrol.
Adamu Bello, a resident of Sokoto, told Daily Champion at the Mobil filling station that he was there to fill up his stock of kerosine for his family as the immediate future is uncertain.
Residents told Daily Champion that they could not predict what the situation would be during the strike and therefore had to prepare for the rainy day.
There was also panic withdrawals of cash from banks.
However, less than 48 hours to the expiration of the NLC ultimatum to the Federal Government to revert to the old fuel rates from the present N53 per litre of petrol, Senator Maccido said the Senate is working to ensure the strike does not hold.
"We will by the grace of Allah avert the strike. Senate leadership is still negotiating with the NLC and I believe that a political option will be reached before Wednesday (tomorrow)," he said.
Maccido said the negotiations which began last week had in attendance the "Minister of Labour, the NNPC, PPPRA and the independent marketers."
He, however, admitted that the meetings have not been smooth as both the NLC and the representatives of the Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA) have "stuck doggedly to their different positions."
The senator noted that while the NLC condemned the increment, the PPPRA said it was necessary and inevitable given the fact that deregulation had come to stay.
Senator Maccido regretted that the disagreement came up few days to the end of the year recess of the Senate, but promised that the "Committee on Labour and the Senate leadership will not go on recess until the strike is averted through a political solution."
Consequently, he said, the "negotiation with Labour and the Presidency is still on and it is spearheaded by the Senate leadership and Committee on Labour."

Original_Search_Title: Up, Up Go Food Prices
Headline: Nigeria: Up, Up Go Food Prices
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of essential commodities like rice, beans, yam, garri, onion,
vegetable and yam flour have gone up by as much as 10 per cent in
major markets in Lagos, in response to last week's hike in the prices
of petroleum products.
P.M.News market survey at the popular Mile 12 Market, Iyana Ipaja,
Ketu and other Lagos markets this morning, showed that the prices of
these goods have been going up since last Friday when marketers
raised the prices of petroleum products.
A litre of the Premium Motor Spirit (petrol) now sells for N49.70 as
against N39, at which it was hitherto sold, while the prices of
kerosene and diesel are now N50 and N57 a litre respectively.
The increase in fuel prices has led to an increase in transportation
cost, which has directly affected the prices of commodities.
Investigation by PMNews at Mile 12, Ketu and Iyana Ipaja markets in
Lagos revealed that prices of foodstuffs have risen astronomically in
the last few days.
A bag of rice (Caprice) at Mile 12 Market now sells for N5,000 as
against the former price of N4,500. A bag of beans (olo) is now
N7,500, as against the N5,000 which it sold for last Thursday.
Garri, a staple food consumed by majority of Nigerians, is now N2,200
per bag. The same bag was sold for N1,9000 last week.
A bag of yam flour (Elubo Egbodo) is now sold for N14,000 as against
N10,000 which it sold for last week.
A tuber of medium-size yam now goes for N200 as against its former
price of N100.
Prices of Tomato and pepper have also gone up. A basket of pepper
(Rodo) at the Mile 12 market is now N2,500, while a bag of Tatase now
goes for N4,000. Last week Rodo was sold for N1,000, while Tatase was
sold for N1,500.

Original_Search_Title: 20 Wedding Guests Die in Auto Crash
Headline: Nigeria: 20 Wedding Guests Die in Auto Crash
Body_Text:
Osogbo — A WEDDING ceremony in Osogbo ended on a tragic note Tuesday after 20 people, half of them guests, lost their lives in a motor accident on the Ilesa/Akure road. The victims, according to sources, were returning to Akure after the wedding in Osogbo when they met their untimely death.
It was learnt that the passenger bus conveying them rammed into another vehicle in front of it which had just overtaken a truck before suddenly running into an on-coming car. All the 10 occupants of the bus were said to have died immediately. Gift items and tubers of yam believed to have been received by them littered the scene.
Ten occupants of three other vehicles involved in the multiple accidents were also said to have died in the mishap. Those who sustained injuries in the auto-crash were taken to an Ilesa hospital. Corpses of the victims were also evacuated to the Wesley Guild Hospital mortuary.
An FRSC personnel who spoke on condition of anonymity said the state command's men had gone to the scene of the incident.

Original_Search_Title: NEEDS And Higher Education (2)
Headline: Nigeria: NEEDS And Higher Education (2)
Body_Text:
As we observed last week, we congratulate the authors of NEEDS for
reflecting bright ideas in the document. But like every critical Nigerian
knows, the problem of the country for a long time now is not the absence of
bright ideas, good budgets or good plans, but mainly that of translating all
these into action (ie making them to work and achieve the expected
results). So lack of commitment to effective implementation of public
policies remains the greatest hindrance to our national development. This is
where the inextricable link between the constraints of the prevailing social
order and the history of consistent government failure in the implementation
of fundamental reforms and policies comes into play.
Nigerian politicians (both military and civilian) and the bureaucrats do
not understand what good governance entails. As far as the dominant faction
of these officials are concerned governance means private enrichment
Q.E.D. (as we say after a geometrical equation has been proved
correctly). And with this goal in mind, every other thing becomes
secondary. This is why government good plans, policies, programmes and
projects are not strictly followed and implemented. All that the majority of
the implementers do is to look for: ways to rip off; ways to subvert the due
process for private gains; ways to create bottle-necks in order to maximize
their private gains; ways to deny members of the public their due service so
as to convert the funds into private use; ways to connive in order to
embezzle large sums of public funds; ways to use fake companies in order to
secure contracts for themselves in their offices; ways to plan intrigues and
jostle for lucrative positions so as to embezzle all that are embezzlable;
ways to secure 10% and more kick-backs from contracts without being ashamed;
ways to use arson as an instrument of covering frauds; ways to print their
own (fake) receipts and deny the government its due revenue; ways to have
ghost workers as means of diverting public funds into private pockets; ways
to obtain fake receipts in order to make false claims; ways to hide official
files in order to get a bribe and make the magic of the file's reappearance;
and all the ways of A -Z of public funds embezzlement, mismanagement,
misapplication and misappropriation. And this cuts across all groups or
types of public officials (both politicians and bureaucrats), all positions
or categories (both those in top and lower positions), all ethnic groups and
all religious groups. In fact, the summary is that corruption has become (a)
classicized, (all classes are involved, some for accumulation and some
others for survival, e.t.c.), (b) ethnicized (all ethnic groups are involved
and they use it to protect the accused or guilty members of their ethnic
group), (c) commonized (it is common with both politicians and public
servants) and worst of all (d) religiousnized (not only that members of all
religious groups are involved, they also hide under religion to protect
their accused or guilty members as in use of ethnicity). (Bear with us in
the use of some of these seemingly strange words). And all offenders or
accused use any of these categories to escape. This is our prevailing social
order deeply rooted in our crude and exploitative type of capitalist
system. The big question remains: what measures do the authors of NEEDS have
on grounds to prevent NEEDS from being messed up by these constraints
imposed by the prevailing social order?
Our experience in the past demonstrates that the consistent
inconsistency of government policies (which derives from the above) is a
factor to fight against apart from the non-commitment to new reforms after
they have been launched (this also derives from the above). For example, how
does one reconcile the fact that a government that wants to implement NEEDS
is cutting grants to, and enlarging short-falls in, the universities as was
reported few weeks ago. Who is responsible for the cut? What is the person's
motive? Recently, the Honourable Minister of Education, Prof. Fabian Osuji
promised the universities enough funds. He noted that higher education
supervisory agencies 'have been inundated with complaints of shortfalls in
personnel cost from institutions under them'. He went further to say that
'the question of shortfalls is something we have been pursuing for a while
towards the end of last year. It was realized that NUC, NBTE, the NCCE, all
complained of some shortfalls in their salary grants. That was immediately
taken up with the ministry of finance but of course as you would expect by
the time the year ended, the ministry of finance could not meet its promises
and commitment' (see
http://www.gamji.com/New_Folder/budget23042004.htm). Our big question now
is: who in the Ministry of Finance cut these grants? Under whose and which
authority? What was the reason? What was the motive as it may differ from a
faked reason? If they could not keep their promises and commitment, where is
the money since the shortfalls are now to be accommodated in this year's
budget? What happened to the one in last year's budget? Did any body use the
money for Christmas or Sallah celebration after the December fasting period?
Concerned Nigerians would like to know. Is the Ministry of Finance above
probe? Why is it that a place where everybody knows that officials demand
and get bribe before government establishments get their rightful budgetary
allocations goes on as if nothing is wrong? Perhaps because, the victims who
should have complained are themselves guilty? The mess should stop if NEEDS
is to succeed. By the way, where is President Obasanjo- the Nigerian
Corruption Crusader? Where is our Mallam El-Rufei- his able assistant in
this fight? Where is Dr. Oby Ezekwesili- Our Due Process Chief Whip? Where
is our Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala- the 'hoped hope' for sanitizing the mess in
Nigeria? Where is our Prof. C.C. Soludo- the quintessence of bourgeois
intellectualism and a Chief author and proponent of NEEDS? The constraints
of the prevailing social order are first and foremost the real challenges
before you people. And with done, it will be easy to talk about the
substantive issues involved in getting NEEDS to achieve its laudable goals
in the universities.
We are afraid this subject will not end today as we had hoped when we
started it last week. We shall therefore conclude the subject next week in
part three of it.
Congratulating Dr. Abdullahi Sule Kano & the New ASUU Executive
This column welcomes with great joy, the news of the election of
Dr. Sule Kano of UDUS as the new ASUU President and congratulates him for a
well-deserved elevation which this column had long predicted and anticipated
bearing any shift of the leadership to the Southern minorities especially to
the Cross River axis.
The column also congratulates the entire members of the new executive
and wishes them what it calsl system-transformative success. We will in the
next two weeks write on the lessons that the politicians can learn from
ASUU's non-rancorous methods of leadership succession since its existence
over two and half decades ago in spite of its multi-ethnic and
multi-religious membership especially in the light of those who may think
that ASUU is not adding value to our system other than strike.
On the same note, this column congratulates the immediate past President
of ASUU, Dr. Dipo Fashina, his crack strategists and tacticians like
Dr. Ogban Ogbani-yam and the entire members of the past executive for being
great and worthy ambassadors of ASUU and rare patriotic Nigerians who along
with their predecessors this columnist called ASUU Heroes and all of whom he
dedicated one of his books in their honour. We shall come back to these in
the next two weeks.
Dr. Obasi writes from the Department of Political and Administrative
Studies, University of Botswana, Gaborone, Southern Africa

Original_Search_Title: Tycoons' Taste
Headline: Cameroon: Tycoons' Taste
Body_Text:
A discreet group of women who live above the standards of their peers.
It is not enough to define a prostitute as a woman or man who dresses in sexy attire and sits by the roadside or bar, with sparkling eyes, itchy for a man's money (or for a woman). It may not also be apt to exclusively associate her with baseness and poverty. A hidden and more delicate class exists in urban areas. They appear responsible, rich, gentle and discreet. These ladies have as major characteristics no jobs, live on the pockets of tycoons and often initiate divorces.
Many city dwellers have often cast doubts on the occupation of some of their female neighbours who occupy decent flats in reserved neighbourhoods, ride in flashy cars but own no offices, and at times claim to do "business". These women put up very unique attitudes. They neither visit their neighbours nor accommodate any relative. Their regular visitors are mechanics, plumbers and electricians who are sometimes called to do repairs. It is believed they remain isolated to conceal some "dirty linens".
Their activities generally arouse curiosity. Usually, they rise from bed as late as 11a.m and remain in the bathroom for over an hour, painting and recoating their lips, nails and eye brows. Such women also take time off to clean their surroundings, carefully watering from one flower pot to another, dusting and readjusting pictures and dolls in the parlour and bedroom. Next, they drive to the market where they go for the largest fish, vegetables and other delicacies. What the market women admire about them is that they pay without haggling.
"High Class" ladies rank among the best cooks. It is said good food is the charm with which they maintain the "sugar daddies." This, because they devote time to prepare it. Someone observed that they use about three hours to prepare yams and chicken stew. In such homes, no meal goes without fruits or salad, sometimes accompanied by cream and other delicacies.
At dusk, a convoy of cosy cars usually queue in front of such residences, letting out tycoons who sometimes carry expensive wines and champagnes for supper. One will hardly determine who among them maintains the lady. A woman who divorced her husband for infidelity, two years ago, said such men often visit the concubines in group to conceal the identity of the real master of the house. "When someone, very close to me, hinted that my husband maintained a lady in town, it was difficult for the truth to surface because he kept telling me that the lady belonged to one of his friends with whom he drinks," the woman lamented.
It is said such ladies take more than half of a man's monthly household budget. They are also noted for causing divorces in many homes.

Original_Search_Title: Teryima Aboashule: the Forgotten PDP Warrior
Headline: Nigeria: Teryima Aboashule: the Forgotten PDP Warrior
Body_Text:
April 19, 2003, (the presidential/governorship election day) may have
come and gone but for some its memory will remain with them up to the end of
their life.
One of such people is Zaki Biam born Teryima Aboashule who lives behind
Atakpa clinic, Zaki Biam in Ukum local government area of Benue State.
27 years old Teryima obtained his Senior Secondary School Certificate
Examination (SSCE) in 1994 but could not further his education because his
parents could not afford it.
However, in order not to let his mind become a safe haven for the
devil's playground as its said of idle minds, Teryima joined Zaki Biam Yam
market Association, an association responsible for the smooth commercial
activities in the largest yam market in Nigeria, to keep the mind busy and
put bread on the table.
So when democracy came calling in 1999, like most youths, he couldn't
resist the temptation. He joined active politics under the platform of
Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), little knowing that politics can be, as is
most often, brutal and dirty in Nigeria.
That ignorance has turned him into an invalid and may perhaps remain so
for the rest of his life.
But for Governor George Akume of Benue State and president Olusegun
Obasanjo for whom he worked through thick and thin to ensure their victory
in Ukum local government area, it has been celebration ever since while
people like him wallow in their misery.
"I was confident George Akume and Obasanjo will win considering our
level of campaigns. Of course, we planned celebrations after the returning
officer declares the results, celebration that was not to be for me," he
narrated.
The reason for that according to him is that on the
governorship/presidential election day, at about 2:30pm, he along with his
uncle, Mr Torhile Orkaa (a.k.a Kemba) and two others were travelling in a
white Toyota starlet car when they ran into some vicious youths of the
opposition party which attacked them.
"Of course, we have been on their hunt list because of our intimidating
and campaign capabilities. On sighting them, our driver sensed some trouble,
and tried to reverse but, he was too late for their bullets. They let loose
their guns on us, spraying our car with bullets.
"In a split second, Peaceman, yelled in anguish. I saw pain on his
face. He grabbed his right knee cap, blood gushing out, he was shot. I heard
another shout from my uncle, who grabbed his legs too in a futile attempt to
stop the blood from gushing out of the shattered knee cap with his
palm.
Before I could bring myself to appreciate what was happening, I felt a
sharp pain in my loins. Obviously, I was shot. I went unconscious, I only
regained consciousness at NKST rehabilitation hospital Mkar.
"As you can see, I am confined to the wheel chair now, I'm
paralysed. The doctors say they are afraid, I may not be able to walk again
especially, since I can't afford expensive and complicated surgical
operations. The doctors say my spinal cord is affected too. As you can also
see, I pass urine through a pipe," he further narrated his ordeal of 419,
2003.
He said his uncle was more fortunate, as the injury on his legs was less
severe, while his friend, Peaceman had his knee cap damaged and he is
crutches bound now.
According to him, for the five months he stayed in the hospital
undergoing treatment, his uncle and yam market association picked part of
the bill while Honourable Emmanuel Udende, now Benue State commissioner for
local government and chieftaincy affairs also picked part of the bill which
amounted to about N250,000.
Summing up his frustration Abuashule said; "what pains me the most is,
the PDP, which I'm suffering this for, has not shown any reasonable
concern. My governor, Akume, for whom I was shot has not demonstrated any
kindness. He cannot claim ignorance of this incidence. He is aware I'm very
sure. What could be more cruel? He asks rhetorically. But please they should
come to my aid. The doctors assured that if some damaged parts can be
replaced, I may walk again."
The story of Teryimas presents a lot of lessons for Nigerian youths who,
with their youthful exuberance and energy work tirelessly for
politicians. Teryima is now condemned to the wheel chair. While those he
claimed to have sweated for (President Obasanjo and Governor Akume) have
abandoned him to his fate.
It also presents to Nigerian the need to redefine the art of politicking
devoid of the madness presently practiced. Alhaji Waziri Ibrahim of blessed
memory gave us a good standard before he died. He preached "politics without
bitterness," and the axiom of "live and let live." But will our present day
politicians ever appreciate these refrains?
Those who wouldn't should be prepared to take responsibility for their
supporters in the event of situations like Teryimas?

Original_Search_Title: Expert Calls For Reappraisal of Management of Acid-Related Disorders...As Astrazeneca Launches N
Headline: Nigeria: HEALTH:- Expert Calls For Reappraisal of Management of Acid-Related Disorders...As Astrazeneca Launches Nexium
Body_Text:
A Nigerian medical expert and healthcare provider has called for the
reappraisal of the treatment of acid-related disorders (ARDs) such as
indigestion, peptic ulcers and upper intestinal tract infections. Making the
observation in Lagos last week during the official launch of Nexuim triple
therapy from the stable of Astrazeneca -- a multinational pharmaceutical
company represented in Nigeria by Reals Pharmaceuticals Ltd., the expert
noted that diagnosis and treatment methods of such disorders have undergone
critical changes in recent years.
In a paper entitled "Management of Acid Related Diseases: The Nigerian
Experience", guest lecturer at the event, Prof. A. Malu, Consultant
Physician /gastroenterologist of the Dept.
of Medicine, Jos University Teaching Hospital (JUTH), said it is now
standard practice to treat peptic ulcer patients with triple therapy while
those with reflux disease require higher doses of proton pump inhibitors for
longer duration. He said: "Even though Nigeria and most developing countries
were said to have low prevalence of peptic ulcer disease, on the whole, the
situation seems not to be so." According to him, the belief that ARDs are
not common in Nigerians is a myth that needs to be discarded.
"Management of acid peptic disorders have come a long way in
Nigeria.
In a review, he stressed that for several years, diagnosis of ARDs in
the country relied almost wholly on clinical impressions, that had no
reliable background information. "These methods are totally unreliable and
cannot stand scrutiny," he asserted. Continuing, Malu observed that "It is
obvious we need stsndadized methods of carrying out endoscopy in this
country if we are to determine the frequency of various categories of
acid-peptic and other diseases. It is a shame that in a country of about 120
million people, there are probably less than 20 endoscopy units that provide
services for many years at a stretch.
Lamenting the cost of equipment, paucity of trained personnel and lack
of facilities for proper maintenance and repair of damaged equipment, he
said"it is an even greater shame that even where these facilities are
available, doctors, including physicians and surgeons still rarely refer
their patients for the procedure."
Continuing, he stated: "Here there is need for reappraisal. Many doctors
and other healthcare providers have advised patients to avoid alcohol,
coffee, bread, yams, fried foods, gari, beans, rice, vegetables, etc. One
wonders what the person should eat. Diet has very limited role in the
management of acid related diseases."
He said management of acid peptic disorders has come a long way in
Nigeria. "We have moved from the point where the disease was said to be
uncommon in most parts of the country to the position where we know it is
common.
"We have moved from reliance on clinical diagnosis to where specific
investigations can be done to determine the cause of upper GIT
symptoms. With the rest of the world we have moved from acid neutralization
through acid suppression to possible cure of peptic ulcer diseases with
triple therapy."
He however noted that the nation was yet to move from the myth of the
importance of diet in treatment to rational use of diet. "We have varied
experience with endoscopic findings in our patient populations, and this is
more likely due to patient selection and quality of facilities and personnel
available to various centers. There is thus the need for us to standardize
our equipment, methods and training of personnel.
Further, he added that the cost of investigation and therapy for acid
peptic diseases is still out of reach of the great majority of Nigerians,
and the awareness for referral to specialists is very low among medical
colleagues in the health care sector.
Nexuim, (Esomeprazole) underwent series of multicentre trials in the
country prior to its launch.

Original_Search_Title: Ngige: I Laugh When People Say I Had No Kobo Before I Became Governor
Headline: Nigeria: Ngige: I Laugh When People Say I Had No Kobo Before I Became Governor
Body_Text:
ABOUT two months ago when I visited Anambra State in the wake of the
order by Justice Stanley Nnaji of the Enugu High Court, directing the police
to withdraw the security details of the state governor, Dr Chris Ngige, I
took time off to visit Alor, his country home, with our correspondent in the
state, Mr. Anayo Okoli and picked up some vital information on the man in
the news from his kinsmen. But because of the axiom in journalism, "leave
out when in doubt", I suspended the report , particularly as his special
assistant (media) , Mr. Fred Chukwuelobe, could not pull through an
interview with the governor to enable him comment on the issues due to the
prevailing tense situation then.
I kept my gun powder dry waiting for an opportunity to meet one on one
with the petite and stormy petrel who not only gave his estranged political
godfather, Chief Chris Uba, the battle of his life but took the fight to the
doorsteps of President Olusegun Obasanjo, the police, judiciary and all
those who had one thing or the other to do with his predicament.
Between that time and March 8 when the opportunity came after yet
another failed effort, coordinated this time by his chief press secretary,
Mr. Chuks Akunna, my list of questions had grown to include what makes the
"Igbo Leader", as some people prefer to call him, tick.
The interview was scheduled for Sunday evening but when I arrived the
Governor's Lodge amidst tight security, Ngige, spotting a milk colored linen
attire and clutching a walking stick, had just finished a meeting . Pleading
that he was tired and needed some rest, he postponed the interview to 8.30
pm and at the appointed time, I was at the Presidential Lodge, where he is
currently putting up.
He had to excuse himself again to take his bath when he saw me. He left
his walking stick on the chair and staring at me as I took a wide look round
the room were the pictures of his wife, Dr (Mrs.) Evelyn Ngige and another
in which he posed with the Jigawa State Electoral Commissioner,
Prof. T.I. Eze, on May 22, 2003 when he collected his election victory
certificate from INEC headquarters, Abuja. Hanging behind the seat he just
vacated were the portraits of President Obasanjo and himself. He came back
about an hour later to resume another meeting.
He betrayed no tension as he chatted with his guests and at about 1.00
am , he was ready for dinner and told Chuks to "tell the Vanguard editor
that food is ready". Ngige sure loves "ogbono" soup with vegetable ,given
the way he dispatched the balls of pounded yam home. Not for him the
niceties of using fork and spoon as I and some others did and when I helped
myself to the "ukwa" delicacy of the Igbo man, he asked "Emma , you don eat
ukwa before" to which I replied that I started eating it from my mother's
belly.
I did not want to ask him why he was staying at the Presidential Lodge
because somebody had told me that the Governor's Lodge was vandalized by
some people when the former governor left the place and because of the money
required to put the place back in shape, the governor felt the money should
be used, for now, to attend to other vital needs of the people.
The interview finally started at about 2.00 am and I looked the governor
straight in the face for about two minutes, imagining what kind of man is
this that launched himself into national limelight within two months in
office and has sustained it till date with the people of the state
developing more interest in him than they had before the crises. I told him
that I visited his home town in January and that my findings about him would
form part of our interview. That raised his anxiety as he wondered what I
was up to.
Why he joined politics:
"I entered politics because, first and foremost, I am somebody who likes
to do things that will touch the lives of people. At the same time, I am
somebody who is interested in service and I normally like to look at people
on regular basis and I asked myself, what can I give to these people? So I
started from giving medical services to people free. When I was in the civil
service, I never worked in any clinic that is commercial, I did not charge
money , so I have worked in government clinics and offered free services to
civil servants and to various families that are around my establishment. At
home, when I came back on weekends or was on leave, I observed my own clinic
and I offered services and drugs free. So at a point, I decided to break
away from the conference of medical services and start offering political
services so that I can give a type of diversified service to people in terms
of medical services, water supply, education and other social services. That
was when I now started taking interest in politics. I was not partisan then
but I started going into it from the angle of organization of structures
like the Rotary, my town union and other bodies like the Aka Ikenga, a socio
cultural organization of the Igbo elite in banking, law, business and other
professions.
So I started there, trying to give services to our people. I was not
quite satisfied with that too, so I left my job in the Federal Civil Service
and joined politics. I wanted to be a Senator, I did that in 1998, helped in
the formation of the PDP after the death of Abacha as a foundation
member. From Anambra, we all went, people like the former Vice President, Dr
Alex Ekwueme, Prof A.B.C. Nwosu, Chief Joseph Okonkwo, my former deputy, Dr
Okey Udeh, Chief Edwin Ume-Ezeoke and others. After the formation of the
PDP, I tried my hands on the senatorial seat for Anambra central, I was not
successful then , I went to join the party structure, contested and won
election into the office of zonal secretary of the party for South East. I
served in that capacity and also as a member of the National Executive
Committee till 2003 when I vacated my position to vie for governorship.
So you can see that it is the desire to serve my people, to serve the
Nigerian people; the desire to serve the down trodden that actually prompted
me into partisan politics. I have told you I was the president of Aka Ikenga
for about six years. And during that time, I participated in the formation
of Nkpokor Igbo, an organization we formed with Izu Umunna in the North and
some other organizations in Enugu. The organization was essentially to
prepare the Igbo for the 1999 Constitutional Conference. We had a
pre-conference in Enugu in 1995. So to answer your questions directly,
service was the propelling thing".
I was told you are a strong willed and stubborn person, can you please
tell me about those stubborn things you did while growing up?
Did they say I am too strong-willed?
Yes , that is what they said.
Is that so? Well I do not know, I thought they say I am a very
principled person, I read a newspaper where they said I am too principled,
saying that I am very strong willed and stubborn. You see when people say
you are stubborn, it means you are not dancing to their tune, some times,
they will know that what they are asking you to do is wrong and once you do
not do it, they would attribute it to stubbornness. Strong willed, yes, I am
and that is borne out of conviction on issues. I cannot really remember my
strong will being displayed as a child. But I do know that I attended
primary school in Enugu, I was born and bred in Enugu. And when I was small,
my father used to send me out to live with teachers, he believed I was not
reading my books enough, not as if I was not passing my examinations but you
know my father had a civil service background, he worked as a supervisor at
OPWD. So he will wish his children to be coming first in the class, I was
not coming first neither was I in the bracket that was not doing well. I was
always in the eighth, ninth position and the man would want his children to
be first so that he can be assured.
He used to send me out to teachers to live with them but I did not like
living with teachers, I like my independence. So about three times, I came
back to the house. The first teacher, I stayed with him for three months, I
returned to the house, then my second teacher, I came back and later my
uncle who was working with the Railways said I should come and live with
him, I came back again. So my father gave up. When I was in secondary school
before the war, I wanted to be a lawyer and I asked for the courses that
would make one who wants to be a lawyer, they gave me all the courses, there
was no mathematics in it and I said okay, I can be an archeologist, a
historian or the lawyer.
But, that is not what my father wanted me to be and all efforts to make
me pick up in mathematics failed and I was not convinced in any other
course. As a matter of fact, I dropped mathematics in class four and my
father was very upset. Then, the war intervened. It wasn't until after the
war that I was convinced about the course called medicine but I was told
that even though I was good in biology and chemistry, that I needed physics
and mathematics, so I was at a loss on what to do. Of course I had not done
physics so when I joined the class, I became the only new person in physics
and of course, mathematics, having packed it up in class four as it
were. But I persevered and was determined that I needed to pass mathematics
and physics in addition to chemistry and biology where I was good in order
to be a medical doctor. So in no time, I picked up in mathematics which I
had left in class four and physics followed. So by the time I got to class
five in the first term, I was among the first five sent to pick the Federal
Government College entrance form for post secondary class six and I was one
of the three chosen from my school to go to the Federal Government College,
Warri for Advanced Level and did physics, chemistry and biology. So it was
my determination that made me say now , I wanted mathematics and I wanted to
do physics. Some people tried to discourage me to say it is too late , you
left this thing in class three, you cannot make it, it's too late , I said
no because I needed it to be a doctor.
*Dragging family to court In your village, some people gave me the
impression of you being a very mean person; that you took your father and
uncle to court over a land matter and even hijacked a transformer sent to
the village, some years ago, how true is this?
This is what you have been keeping since as your special Alor
findings. Do not mind the stories, I will tell you the story. You see, I
never took my uncle to court, I never took my father to court in Alor, that
is not true. My father and I are friends, but when I differ with my friends
on principles, I will differ completely. What happened was that I have a
land given to me by the extended family for me to build my house in 1987. I
think you saw the house when you went there, that is why I laugh when some
people say I had no kobo before I became governor. That house, you will
think it was built yesterday because the design came from Italy and the
builders did a very good job at that time. So I built on a portion of the
land and left some.
When my junior brother decided to develop a house in the village, he
consulted me. I said okay, no problem. I went and bought another land
adjoining my own and said you could have this. But my father told him not to
go and build on that land, that he did not like the source of the land, that
it was for some people that were regarded as slaves even though the land was
adjoining my own compound and was very big, big enough for him to build his
house. So my father went while I was abroad and gave my brother a portion of
that my land that was remaining, the one I did not immediately build on
then. Of course, he had asked me when I was about traveling to America what
I thought, I said no problem, I can give you the small land I bought, I can
give it to my brother to build on, my father said those people were slaves ,
I said it did not matter, that we have passed that age of talking about
slavery and people being sacrificed to the gods. Any way, when I traveled,
before I came back from America, I was going on holidays for two weeks or
one month, my father had gone into my land, the remaining portion,
partitioned it, took what you can now call a normal land plot of 100 / 70 or
that kind of measurement. He and my brother squeezed in a house there. Of
course, I told him that what he and my brother had done would not benefit
him, to build on a land that is less than 1,800 square metre to 1,000 square
metre and a thing I bought about 1,900 square metre and they were building
on a land that was about 900 square metres, so I was naturally angry, I am a
human being and blood flows in me . Moreover , my father started making a
case that, after all, the land in question belongs to him, I said how, he
said because the land belongs to me and it is his own. I said no father, it
is wrong. That was the cause of the quarrel because I said he did not own
everything I own because I am of age and have my own family and even if it
his own as he tried to say, he should get my permission before using it. So
we did not go to court but I had to show my displeasure because he is my
friend and he took me for granted, that was what happened. We never went to
court.
Why they call me Igbo leader: When they were calling me Igbo leader was
when I was leading Aka Ikenga as Aka Ikenga was the most thought of as
vibrant of all the Igbo organizations at that time. This organization is a
leading Igbo organization. So, leading it , indirectly, I am an Igbo
leader. So they call me the name as an alias, that is all about it.
My beard: I have worn this beard since the medical school days. At that
time , it was uncommon for medical students, especially in the clinical
class, to wear beard. And the dean of the School of Medicine at one time
threatened that if I did not shave my beard, I should forget my final degree
examination. That was Professor Udoh. I refused, insisting that it was my
right; that this had nothing to do with my being a medical student or doing
my final year examination in medicine. So, we had a very big row in one of
the clinical surgical classes. And, of course, I told him I could not shave
because I suffer from bumps that come on when one shaves. So, he said if I
knew that was the situation, I should be ready to get a medical paper to
prove it. I went and got the paper and gave him and he still insisted that I
will shave but I refused.

Original_Search_Title: Troubled Thrones
Headline: Nigeria: Troubled Thrones
Body_Text:
Lagos — Igbo leaders of thought look set to abolish Eze Ndigbo titles outside
Igboland, EMEKA IBEMERE reports Eze Igbos (Igbo kings) outside Igboland may
spend the remaining part of this year fighting to keep their titles.
An indication to this effect was recently dropped by Dr. Orji Uzor Kalu, Governor of Abia State.
Some months ago in Yola, Kalu told newsmen at the South East Governors
Forum that the Ohanaeze, the apex Igbo socio-cultural organisation and
prominent Igbo leaders of thought will this year scrap the coronation of any
Igbo as a traditional ruler of Igbo people outside Igboland.
"Let me be honest with you, in the next Conference of South East
Governors, we are going to abolish all these Eze Ndigbos. They have to go
back to what they used to be, because it is a mockery of the traditional
stool," contended Kalu in Yola.
A leader of another Igbo socio-cultural group agrees with Kalu.
According to the leader who pleaded for anonymity, tussles over Eze Ndigbo
stools have polarised the Igbo communities outside Igboland.
P.M.News learnt that Kalu's anger with kingship outside Igboland was
sparked by reports of intractable crisis over the stool of Eze Ndigbo in
Yola.
Also, a member of the Mkpuke (Kitchen Cabinet) of the Ohanaeze, told
P.M.News that the abolition of the title has become necessary because crises
arising from tussles have brought disgrace to the Igbo race.
"The title has to be abolished," the Ohanaeze leader asserted.
To achieve that desire, P.M.News gathered that there are already plans
to pressurise governors outside Igboland to stop recognising Eze Ndigbos in
their states. Pressure will also be mounted on town unions, Igbo social
organisations and market unions to shun events organised by Ezes outside
Igboland.
Igbos are at daggers drawn over the title in Adamawa State.
The Adamawa Emirate Council was said to have approved the appointment of
one Chief Solomon Ebis as Eze Ndigbo II, following the death of Igwe Anyika
some years ago. But the turbaning of Ebis did not go down well with other
members of the Igbo community in Yola. This led to protests.
In Ibadan, the Oyo State capital, the situation is the same. The tussle
is between Dr. Alex Anozie, Chairman and Managing Director of Conac Opticals
and Dr. James Oramadike. Anozie is claiming that his authority is derived
from an election by the Igbo community in Ibadan, while Oramadike claims he
has the approval of the Olubadan of Ibadan.
In their case, Chief Emeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, (Eze Igbo Gburugburu), and
Chief Chukwuemeka Ezeife are said to have vested interest in the tussle that
has lasted over five years. Ojukwu is said to have given his blessings to
Oramadike, while Ezeife is backing his brother and kinsman, Alex Anozie. As
the war rages in an Ibadan court, Igbos there are re-echoing the Israelites
song of "To your tent oh Igbos."  In Lagos State, the tussle over the throne
is the same.
But here it is a one-sided war.The influence of Igwe Omereoha N.H.
Ohazuruike is very visible as Ezendigbo of Lagos, because of the seal of
approval he has received from prominent Igbo personalities including the new
Ohanaeze leadership and the Lagos State Government.
But even as he is enjoying the throne, another Ezendigbo has
emerged.
He is Chief Ochiagha Uche Chukwu Nwachukwu. It was gathered that for
years, these duo do not see eye to eye over who reigns in Lagos as Eze
Ndigbo. Both Ezes claim their authorities from Igbos in Lagos, the late Oba
Adeyinka Oyekan II and the Lagos State Government.
Besides, there are cases of Ezendigbo tussle in Abuja, the Federal
Capital Territory, Osun, Kebbi, Sokoto and other states. But despite these
'wars' over the Igbo kingship outside Igboland, there are people who still
think that Ezendigbo's in "diaspora" have not done badly to deserve being
scrapped.
In a swift reaction to Kalu's statement, an Igbo socio-cultural
organisation under the aegis of Igbo Youths Association of Nigeria, has
condemned the move by some prominent Igbo leaders to scrap the coronation of
any Igbo person as a traditional ruler of Igbo people outside Igboland.
The Secretary-General of the association, Nnaemeka Ozegbo, said "the
institution of Ezendigbo outside Igboland is a welcome exercise. "It is one
of the wisest ideas developed by an Igboman towards fostering unity, peace,
love, protection, concern, promotion of culture, and it debunks the belief
in some quarters that Ndigbo (Igbos) have no leader, no focus and direction
as misconstrued by some people," he noted.
Those who voted for Eze Ndigbo outside Igboland claimed that the royal
fathers discharged a lot of responsibilities which included preventing and
controlling crisis, promotion of peace amongst the Igbo on one hand and
between the Igbo and other tribes on the other.  They also claim that the
throne fosters unity and facilitates solid advice for Ndigbo during periods
of commotion, confusion and crisis.
Ozegbo is also of the view that the Ezendigbos promote and showcase Igbo
tradition and culture such as the New Yam Festival in which other members of
the communities where the Igbo live partake.
Like Ozegbo, the royal fathers are also unhappy about the statement
credited to Kalu. When P.M.News visited the palace of Igwe Omereoha
N.H. Ohazuruike, Eze Ndigbo Lagos, the royal father said he had not been
informed officially and that Kalu was speaking for himself.
He said he would not join issues with the young Governor of Abia
State.
Igwe Omereoha told P.M.News that he was at the inauguration of the new
Ohanaeze leadership and was highly welcomed at the event.  However, he
promised to react at the appropriate time.
On his part, Chief Ochiagha Uchechukwu Nwachukwu told P.M.News that
Governor Orji Uzor Kalu was on his own and he has no right to stop him from
parading himself as Eze Ndigbo of Lagos or abolish the title anywhere.
He further said Kalu is a governor and has no power to do what he was
talking about.
For now, the situation looks uncertain, at least until a ban is formally
placed on the conferment of Eze Ndigbo outside Igbo land.

Original_Search_Title: West Tries to Bail Out of Ugandan Meltdown
Headline: Uganda: West Tries to Bail Out of Ugandan Meltdown
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The passionate 17-year-old affair between the Kampala government and
Western donors has run into troubled waters.
The past two weeks have witnessed bitter exchanges, with donors pressing
the government to end the bloody rebellion in the north by the rebels of the
Lord's Resistance Army through political negotiations.
The government has in turn accused donors of abetting the war by
bringing pressure on it not to increase defence expenditure. And ministers
and army officers have rounded on the head of the EU delegation in Uganda,
Sigurd Illing, telling him to keep his nose out of the country's affairs,
and to stop commenting on the war.
This is a strange flap, because if Uganda were a company, it would have
filed for bankruptcy long ago. Donors, who pick up the bills for nearly 90
per cent of development expenditure, also fork out over 50 per cent of the
government's recurrent expenditure.
Technically, Uganda doesn't have the means to fund the war in the north
from its own resources. If it chose to do so, and didn't have donor money,
virtually the whole budget would go to defence, and other governments would
be closed and civil servants sent home to grow yams and raise goats.
However, to see this as just another case of an African leader who's
turning into a despot falling out with Western donors urging him to remain
on a civilised path, is to miss the point.
The row in Kampala is ultimately about the terms of disengagement
between the donors and the Museveni government.
For many years, the position that the donors are now taking on the
conflict was pushed by many voices. The donors took the view that the
Museveni's government's push for a military solution was the best option,
because the LRA was nothing more than a terrorist organisation, and it had
no clear political agenda.
The second view was that, while the LRA didn't have a political agenda
and used terrorist methods, it was not too high a price to pay to talk to
them to see what would be their terms for ending their rebellion.
A third, more sophisticated view held that the LRA war was only a
symptom of dysfunctional politics. That the one-party state of the Movement,
and the government's hostility to the north, created an undercurrent of
resentment for the Kampala regime which the LRA, like other rebel groups
before it, exploited. It held that opening up the political space in the
country would allow northern grievances to be channelled into constitutional
politics.
The bulk of the donor community wasn't impressed. Most took the position
that with Uganda's chaotic past, there was a case to be made for keeping a
lid on democracy, in order to avoid a relapse into ethnic strife. With its
impressive economic growth, the country soon became a "success story" for
the donors.
Some accordingly developed a vested interest in an illiberal political
system. They believed that a more open and quarrelsome political order would
reduce Museveni's power to continue carrying out unpopular economic
reforms.
Today, years of repression have radicalised sections of the
opposition. A government long used to uncritical support has allowed
corruption to become so entrenched, it can't uproot the vice. The war in the
north has gone on for so long, it has taken on a life of its own.
As a strategy for re-legitimisation, Kampala now backs a return to
multiparty politics. As a tactic for the personal security of its top
leaders, it wants to lift term limits so that they can continue in
power. Having supported the actions of the regime all these years, recent
criticism by donors of the war, defence expenditure, and the bid to scrap
term limits are seen by the hardliners in the Movement as an attempt to bail
out and hang the government out to dry, hence the deep resentment.
If the donors thought they could just turn their backs on all this and
walk away, they are dead wrong.
Charles Onyango-Obbo is managing editor in charge of media convergence
at the Nation Media Group.

Original_Search_Title: Without Justice Eso's Influence, I Would Not Have Been a Judge--Justice Ilori
Headline: Nigeria: Without Justice Eso's Influence, I Would Not Have Been a Judge--Justice Ilori
Body_Text:
JUSTICE Kayode Eso, no doubt, is one of Nigeria's greatest jurists. He
is one of the nation's jurists who retired from the profession
unblemished. This great jurist, however, is not the focus of this story as
the focus would be on somebody he unknowingly influenced into becoming
another great judge.
Justice Samuel Ilori is certainly not a new face in the legal
profession. Until three years ago when he retired from the public service,
he was the chief judge of Lagos State. He is presently into private
practice. But for Eso, Ilori would have been contented with being a
lawyer. As a young man, Ilori practically worshipped Eso because 'he was the
first lawyer I ever saw in my area at that time.' "He really had a great
influence in my life. I am sure you know that he is Nigeria's foremost
jurist.When he returned from England, he was the only lawyer around at that
time. His family and mine were very close because his father married my aunt
and I was frequently visiting their house. We got carried away by everything
around him, his profession, the way he parted his hair, the way he walked,
virtually everything about him attracted us and we started copying
everything about him so much so that we parted our hair too and we even put
chalk across just to make it look like his own. Brother Kayode, that is what
we called him, really affected my life and that is why I decided to study
whatever he studied that made him to be like that, I will also do", he
reflected.
Apart from Eso, one other thing that lured Ilori into law was the
ceremony that usually accompanied his late father's visit to land
disputants. His late father, High Chief Arapate Ilori of Ilesa, was a native
court judge. "As a young child, whenever there was a visit like that,
disputants would make a lot of preparations for the visit of the court. They
would kill cows, prepare pounded yam, it was celebration for us whenever
daddy was visiting and it was always on Saturdays. My late father was a
native court judge because the courts were called native at that time. He
was a judge of a court that sat on Wednesdays. In those days, only the high
chiefs were made judges of different courts, that was why his court sat on
Wednesday and his court was only on land matters. Whenever he sat in court,
the children were not expected to go to school, we had to go and sit in
front of the court and fanned him while the case was going on. It was the
court clerk who recorded everything that transpired in the court. From going
to court with him, I had extreme interest in law. So after the case is heard
in court, there was usual invitation for the judge to come and visit the
land or boundaries in dispute which must have element of settlement. Those
were the things the judge would have to see to determine the real ownership
of the land.
So it was the practice of my father to go on to the land and see physical boundaries. So I said wao, if this is the kind of food that
they would be preparing for judges, then, it is good becoming a judge. I was
actually hoping to become a high chief so that I could take my father's
title and be able to sit like a native judge and hear all these funny
stories and more importantly visit the boundaries".
Ilori bagged his law degree from Leeds University in1963 but he never
attended secondary school. Why? He gained admission into Edo College but his
father would not allow any of his children to go to school far away from his
environment because 'he had problem with my elder brother who went to
secondary school away from Ilesa and came with a sickness that led to his
death. So, he said he was not going to allow any child of his to go to
secondary school far away from him'.But he claimed that the primary
education he had at that time can only be compared to the university
education of these days. He explained further:
"After my primary education, there were several secondary schools
around, like Ilesa Grammar School but my ambition was to go to Edo College
into which I passed entrance examinations in 1950 but my father would not
hear of it. Having missed the school, I now went out to teach. In those
days, education in primary school was superior to what you have now in
secondary schools and can even compete with the university education now
because it was real hard learning and teaching.
When I finished the Grade three teacher training, my mind was set on
going abroad for further studies. I said to myself, I must become a
lawyer. So I left Nigeria in 1956 with a Grade three teacher certificate in
my pocket. By 1959, I was already in the University of Leeds, reading law. I
came back home January1964, I did the three months compulsory law school
before I ventured into private practice in my cousin's chambers. It was from
there I went to the Federal Ministry of Justice. I became a state counsel in
the Federal Ministry of Justice. But when Lagos State was created, all of us
in public prosecutions were zoomed by decree to Lagos State, so, I became
one of the very few foundation staff of Lagos State Ministry of Justice
under the leadership of the late Justice Agoro.
Public prosecutions
Then in 1976, I became the director of public prosecutions. I was in
that post until 1978 when I became the solicitor general and permanent
secretary in the Ministry of Justice. I was appointed a judge September
1980".
That month, September 1980, Ilori claimed, was his happiest in life. "It
was a dream come through when I was appointed judge that day. There was
nothing like dreaming something and achieving it. I feel very much
fulfilled. I am one of the very lucky people who found their profession. I
don't think I would have been happier being in any profession other than
law." As a judge, how does it feel sentencing an accused to death, I asked
him. He said that when he was a judge, the authority and he also thought it
was not right for him to be a judge in the criminal division of Ministry of
Justice.
Said he: "The authority thought it was not right to put somebody who had
been a director of public prosecutions into the criminal side of the bench
because they felt as a prosecutor, you already have a prosecuting mentality,
you are already hand in gloves with the police, you are more or less
directing the police in their investigations. To now go from that area and
become a judge of the criminal division, it is not fair on the society and I
believe too that it is not fair on society and even the accused persons
because you already come with that convicting mentality. So, I never worked
in criminal division. All I had to do with those sentenced to death was, I,
as the secretary of the Committee on the Prerogative of Mercy for eight
years, saw to it that nobody sentenced to death is hanged without ensuring
first that his case is being reviewed by the committee. After the accused
had exhausted all his appeals to the Supreme Court and the death sentence
has been confirmed, the papers are now sent to the secretary of the
committee who now starts his own investigation, not on the merit of the case
but on the merit of the accused. You contact a reverend or an imam who knows
him.
You call in the social welfare officers to go and investigate him, what sort of person had he been to the society. When all these data
are gathered, I now write a brief for points against and for the accused. It
is then you come to conclusion on whether the accused deserves to die or not
based on which points weighed in his favour.I remember two cases in
particular, there was a man whose wife had a boy, it was the only child they
had. There was a fight and the wife said 'do you think you are the father of
the boy? You are not. I knew you wanted a child and I brought him to
you'. This man loved the boy but in fist of anger, he said if the boy didn't
belong to him, he would not belong to anybody also and threw him into a
well.
Of course the boy died. As far as the law was concerned, he had
committed murder. The case came to me and I had to present it to the
prerogative of mercy committee.
We had a look at the life structure of the man, his behaviour in society
and we found that he was not the type of person and that he was thoroughly
provoked to that situation, so we recommended that his death sentence should
be commuted to a small term of imprisonment and he should be
released. Another one was during the civil war. There were soldiers guarding
Carter Bridge which was the only one at that time.
Argument over bread
For fear of being bombed, the government mounted soldiers on the bridge
but there was this drunken man who went to one of the soldiers on duty and
said this is loaf of bread for you, I give it to you to eat but the soldier
rejected it, they started arguing from there and the soldier took his gun
and shot him dead. As far as the law was concerned, he had committed
murder.
But when it got to the prerogatives committee, we looked at it and asked
ourselves, suppose that bread had been poisoned and he took it, ate and
fallen dead, the whole of the bridge would have been left without
protection, no security and we decided that he should not be killed, his
death sentence should be commuted to some years.
Where it is decided that the law must take its course, that the man
ought be hanged, the council meets, sits and if they all agree that this is
a case we cannot allow to go, the law must take its course, it is then you
write the prison authority conveying the decision of the committee, it is
not automatic that once somebody is sentenced to death, that is it".
Ilori has eight children from two women. His first marriage crashed
shortly after his return from England due to pressure from his in-laws . "We
got married before I went to England but when I came back, things were not
easy for me. People expected so much from you by the time you return from
abroad. But here I was, there was nothing to show for my trip abroad. It was
