the car of the closest person to me on earth, my cousin, Prof. Bolanle Awe,
I was using. When my former wife's parents saw the way I was living, it was
contrary to their expectation. There was no problem

Original_Search_Title: Frankly Speaking: All the President's Men
Headline: Nigeria: Frankly Speaking: All the President's Men
Body_Text:
If a man is known by the company he keeps; a company is also known by
the people it keeps --Anonymous
NIGERIA Plc is everybody's business and the Chief Executive Officer,
whether we like it or not, is Olusegun Obasanjo. But the CEO, whether he
likes it or not, must recognize the fact that he is there at our pleasure;
not to please himself. And like shareholders in any enterprise, we must keep
a sharp eye on the characters the helmsman has appointed to assist him in
the performance of his duties. Keeping bad company is always a prelude to
calamity which already stares us in the face from every angle; political,
social, health, education, infrastructure, and most importantly economic. A
great deal of blame for the current state of affairs has been laid, quite
rightly, at the door step of the President, but the CEO is not God, even
though he assumes a higher degree of omniscience than most mortals with
ingrained modesty would. Despite his ill-disguised contempt for his
advisers, he still nevertheless listens to them, if only to convince himself
that they are really the yam-heads he thought they were. Which reminds me of
that quip by the late American comedian Will Rogers (1879-1935): "Mr
President, can I tell you the latest joke in town?, I asked. "You don't have
to tell me Will, I appointed many of them into my cabinet and as my
advisers".
Or the one about late President Anwar Saddat of Egypt who was holding a
cabinet meeting when a messenger rushed in to announce that the Presidential
palace had been robbed by unknown robbers. "But that is impossible" said
Saddat, "all the biggest robbers in this country are here with me." A
President who holds meetings with "come and eat people" and comedians will
always find it difficult to deliver anything but jokes. Today, I bring you
three of the President's men. Two are amusing in various degrees; the other
is simply peculiar. Permit me to introduce Dr Tunji Abayomi, one of the
President's lawyers and friends; Chief Femi Fani-Kayode, one of the zillions
of advisers to President Obasanjo. Those two are amusing in various ways as
I intend to show shortly. The third man is Engineer Adeseye Ogunlewe, the
Minister of Works from Lagos State. He has been uttering some words lately
about the Onitsha-Owerri road and the sooner we pay attention to this man
the better because the lives of too many travellers depend on his
performance. For a start, I can state categorically that the federal
government road leading to Ogunlewe's village, the Ikorodu-Itoikin-Epe road
is such a death-trap it never fails to claim at least two or three victims a
week. Three weeks ago, a bus-load of people including three infants were
roasted to death on the road. Thus, when the Minister talks about other
roads, we should listen carefully. But first, the amusing people.
Dr. Abayomi, in The Guardian of Wednesday, February 4, 2003 writing under the title: "The error of NLC's endless strikes" made various
points but the most important from my view are the following: "We need to
make it clear that the NLC is not a trade union nor among the registered
trade unions under the Trade Union Act. Not being a trade union, under our
laws, but a creation of a federal statue, NLC cannot in the first place
declare a trade dispute under the Trade Dispute Act with any employer". In
another section, he went on to assert that : "The NLC has become an
unregistered political association that appears committed to wasteful
reactionary ends, instead of the well-being of workers in work and
after".
Because I happen to agree with much of what Dr Abayomi wrote and because
of the mature language he has adopted, my response will be just as
measured. My reason for being amused by what Dr Abayomi wrote stems from
what I regard as the typical lawyer's submission, which invariably states
one side of the case - the client's side. That might be acceptable in a
court of law, but it is not permissible in the court of public
opinion. Politics and socio-economic situations of countries are not based
only on law. But more to the point, even if one accepts Dr Abayomi's
assertion that the NLC has no legal right to declare a trade dispute or to
negotiate on behalf of workers, one is at a loss where the legal luminary
has been when President Obasanjo had been negotiating wage increases with
the NLC and reneging on the agreements. Government itself, when it suited
the political interest of the President, had accorded the NLC the right
which Abayomi now denies it.
And if NLC has become a political organization, the question is when did
it become that? The NLC openly supported the President for his first and
second elections; the same NLC was the first organization to oppose Buhari's
call for mass action. Apparently, it is permissible for the NLC to be
political when it suits the President and his counsel, but not when it
annoys them. Dr Abayomi, like most defenders of Obasanjo, almost invariably
gets entangled in his own under wears simply because the product he is
trying to sell is stale and the Nigerian people are no longer buying. It is
really amusing when so much intellectual power is wasted on such a
project. Finally, the NLC did not declare a trade dispute; it threatened a
national strike which would have affected all employers of labour and even
those self-employed. And any organization, including the NLC can do that, it
is part of their rights as citizens of this country. I thought the learned
gentleman knows the difference; or was he just pulling our legs? Chief
Fani-Kayode brought torrents of abuse on himself because he has been trying
too hard to keep his job. It must be quite a tough way to make a living. He
even dared anybody to attack his President; declaring his own version of
"Operation fire for fire" in the sort of language that only Don Quixote,
made famous by the great Spanish writer Miguel Cervantes, could have
employed as the blind and partly deaf knight went about battling windmills
which he mistook for giants and flocks of sheep which he thought were
dragons. With the Presidency crawling with idle hands; it has necessarily
become a place where anything is expected. So when Fani-Kayode left Col Umar
to throw a challenge to all and sundry as if intending to intimidate us, I
couldn't at first make up my mind whether to laugh or cry. I read the piece
again and involuntarily, the tears came co-mingled with the wildest
laughter. For comedy, the guy will be difficult to beat this year. He works
for a man already famous for his rib-crackers; he must obviously love
comedians to have appointed so many. For me, with my deficient education
acquired from Jankara university and brought up in the Campos Square area of
Lagos, I don't know grammar; but whatever needs to be supplied by way of
Area boys approach will be provided. I feel sorry for the fellow already; I
feel more sorry for his employer.
Now to the serious one, because among other things, the man comes from a
village close to mine, and among those being sacrificed daily are my
relatives, that is, Engineer Ogunlewe. Last week, the Minister announced
that the Onitsha-Owerri road will take ten years to complete at the rate
funds are being released to his Ministry. In that one sentence, the Minister
has exposed the lies told by his President and the former minister for
Information and National Orientation, the ubiquitous Professor Jerry
Gana. First, the President's whopper which was delivered in Enugu when he
went to launch his re-election campaign. Said the President: "The
Onitsha-Owerri road, I dualise am." When several people including myself,
wrote to object to that bare-faced untruth by the President, Professor Gana
was on hand to offer an explanation which turned out to be just as
false. According to Gana, the funds had been secured, that is, set aside for
the project and the contract has been awarded.
Work on the project ground to a halt shortly after the 419 elections
understandably given the empty promises that preceded it and the failure to
redeem the pledge is a clear testimony to the integrity of those who lead
us, and now Ogunlewe is asserting that the road cannot be completed unless
we swallow the poison pill of fuel tax or road use tax and God knows if they
will not add breathing tax to the list. So, where were the secured funds?
And where is the dualised road that the President announced to those who
made the gross error of believing him? The implications of Ogunlewe's
utterances for the Onitsha-Owerri as well as other death traps are obvious:
there will be no improvement on roads unless we pay with our lives for
it. Now that's the kind of language straight from hell because it portends
grave danger to the populaceÃ¢-oe.
To be continueded..

Original_Search_Title: Toll Gates, No Longer Busy Centres
Headline: Nigeria: Toll Gates, No Longer Busy Centres
Body_Text:
Lagos — Andrew Ahiante who has been following developments emanating from the
fuel tax and subsequent demolition of toll gates across the nation, writes
that the usual bustling and hustling of commercial activities at the toll
centres are over
Travelling from Lagos to Benin City, Edo State one sunny morning, the
only interuption was from roadblocks created by the police and gullies by
erosion. The same applied to a journey from Lagos to Ibadan, Lagos to Ogun,
among others, across the nation.
The introduction of the fuel tax, which has been suspended by a law
court and the subsequent Federal Government's decision to remove all toll
gates across the nation's major roads has therefore created free flow of
traffic without the usual commercial activities. Except for some few ones,
other toll gates are now ghosts of their former selves with little or no
business activities going on there.
At the Ore toll gate, THISDAY inquired from few people still hanging
around how business has faired without the tollgates in place.
"Since the day government asked the toll collectors to vacate, we hardly
sell anything", a woman who hawks banana, groundnuts, and other fresh fruits
items, lamented. She explained that the toll gate used to be her major
selling point, stressing that since demolition, she had to resort to hawking
her wares across the city. Life, she said, has not been the same for her and
her children who are employed to help hawk the goods.
"You see my brother, life is getting tough for us. This has always been
my source of livelihood. And since the Federal Government destroyed this
place, I no longer see customers nor feel comfortable staying here", she
said, emphasising that travellers no longer stop by nor, do motorists care
to look at what we have to offer.
"You see, before by the time drivers buy their ticket we are able to
sell one or two things to commuters. Now, motorists no longer stop", she
further lamented, stressing that those who now stop there either do so to
fix their vehicles or just to ease themselves.
"This government does not consider the poor people out there", she
further said, expressing that she has been looking for an alternative busy
place like the Toll Gate, but has not been successful.
At the Benin toll gate, another woman who had erected a big shop where
she sells food such as rice, pounded yam, among others, lamented that since
the place was demolished, she no longer sells.
"You see, we no longer see customers. You know our customers were mainly
motorists and other commuters undergoing long distance journey. Most times,
they stop by to buy food. This used to be a good business for us. Now the
government has destroyed this place. All we now do is to watch the vehicle
speed away", apparently referring to how the vehicles now drive past the
place on high speed.
She said since activities began after her Christmas and New Year break,
her loss was incalculable as food go bad because they cant finish selling it
due to lack of customers. "How am I going to pay my children school fees"
she queried rhetorically?
With the development, THISDAY observed all through the journey that some
people have shifted base to areas where motorists have difficulty in moving
fast. Some of these new places of settlement used to be areas occupied by
lepers. At the end of Benin City, lepers and traders struggle for space. One
of such persons has this to say..
"You see, we have no other place to go. We use to stay at the toll gate
there", he said, pointing at the direction. He however explained that with
the introduction of the fuel tax and the subsequent demolition of toll gates
across the nation, he has since observed that he was no longer making
it.
"You see, my brother, I have to survive. Am I not a human being?
Everyday, I must eat. How do I eat, if I don't have the money", he queried?
He explained that the search for a new place has made them pitch tent
with the lepers since for now that is the only alternative place for
them. "Here, motorists slow down like the toll gate. We can still do our
best to run after them, entice them with our wares and God willing, we still
sell some things", noting however that there was still a big difference with
that of the toll gate.
He explained that some of his colleagues have shifted their base to the
city. He explained that he has remained on the road because he was yet to
identify any suitable place in the city where he would not be harrassed by
social miscreants popularly refers to as Area Boys or the local government
officials.
As the journey to Benin City, Edo State, last, the feelings of commuters
were obvious. While, many did not understand the direction of the nations
economic policies, some faulted the federal government's fuel tax and the
demolition of toll gates.
"We are talking about privatisation and deregulation. Why did federal
government not hand over collection of the toll fees to a private body", a
passenger queried perhaps ignorant that the tolls were collected by private
individuals.
To him, if federal government has considered the plight of Nigerians
whose daily sources of livelihood stem from income generated from trading
activities at the toll gates, it should have taken measures that would see
these people remain in their various places.
According to him, it was true that government was not getting enough
from the toll fees, it should have handed it over to a private group and set
a target for such group. In that wise, he explained, all these Nigerians who
have been rendered jobless will remain in the mainstream of their economic
activities.
Although, another commuter reasoned that such category of workers would
easily be absorbed into federal government ministries, he enthused that if
other people had applied to such places, government will claim that there
were no vacancies.
However, another passenger condemned the speed with which the federal
government demolished the toll gates. He argued that the N360 million used
in demolishing the toll gates across the nation could have been used to
checkmate the lapses noticed by the federal government.
Corroborating such idea, another passenger condemned the spate of road
accidents on the federal high ways and the helpless situation the government
found itself and not been able to checkmate them. He explained that such
amount of money spent on demolishing the tolls gates could have been used to
convert them to emergency hospitals and accident centres to respond to the
various highway accidents occuring on a daily. The passenger urged that the
federal government should have borrowed a leaf from Governor Bola Ahmed
Tinubu of Lagos State who in the bid to respond to road accidents had
stationed mobile clinics across the metropolis, especially on top of the
bridges.
"Government should have considered putting the toll plazas to other uses
among which are converting them into high-way emergency accident rescue and
first-aid centres, rest-stops for motorists, security post for Police,
Customs and FRSC personnel.
"You see, this government will continue to tell you that it is for the
masses. It will continue to claim that it has welfare policies for the
masses. Where are these policies?", he queried. Most of the passengers
however, did not see any reason why the government will spend N360 million
just to demolish toll gates that consumed even more fund to construct.
One of the passengers observed that the toll gates should have been left
for future purposes, as it was possible that another government would want
the toll gates back in some other times. Some passengers however opposed
this thinking. To this group of people, leaving the toll gates unused will
constitute a trap for motorists. They observed that armed robbers would use
the places as hideouts for motorists and commuters, mostly those travelling
during the night.
Federal Government, in August 2003, said it would henceforth channel the
N65 million monthly income from the toll gates nation-wide into road repairs
and maintenance. It also disclosed that three additional toll gates would
soon be built, to bring the total number of toll gates throughout the
country to 34. Minister of Works, Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe who disclosed
this in Abuja while addressing the press stated that there were 85 on-going
projects in the six geographical regions of the nation.
Nigerians were however, appalled when President Olusegun Obasanjo, while
presenting his 2004 budget proposal to the National Assembly, announced his
intention to abolish toll gates and in its place introduced fuel tax,
claiming that the revenue generated from toll gates were not enough.
According to report, since its introduction at the Niger-Bridge end in
the present Delta State in January 1966, by the Abubakar Tafawa Balewa
administration, the policy to use tolls collected from the nation's roads
and bridges for their maintenance have proved problematic due to
mismanagement, embezzlement and lack of accountability.
For instance, from that single toll gate at the Asaba end of Niger
Bridge, toll plazas grew to 25 in 1987 and constructed at the cost of N74
million. Twenty more were erected in October 1988 at the cost of N54 million
with a further N20 million thrown in for support services and provision of
security to the operators.
Yet again, a further 10 plazas were built in 1997. Together at their
best, the tollgates were said to have only generated about N432 million per
annum. However, the rationale for their numerical increase had been to use
the proceeds to repair, maintain and secure the federal highways. But far
from living up to its billings and public expectations, the toll-gate
transformed into veritable cash-cows for fraudulent government officials and
later private operators called in to take over their management in the
1990s.
Matters were so bad in 2003 when the Minister of Works and Housing,
Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe referred to the amount generated monthly at the
toll gates as disgraceful. But during the presentation of his 2004
Appropriation Bill to the joint session of the National Assembly last
December, the president had announced to a surprised Senate and nation that
a new petroleum tax of N1.50k per litre of fuel purchased within the country
would now replace the dwindling revenue from the toll-gates, which was
scrapped on January 1, 2004.
The petroleum tax has since taken effect to the consternation of many
who deemed the tax illegal. It was however relief when the law court last
week suspended the tax as a result of a looming strike by the Nigeria Labour
Congress (NLC). However, while they existed, the tollgates had acquired a
sub-culture of there own with landed structures, economic and service
activities and as isolated hubs of development. And yet, it is doubtful that
their demolition would achieve the security and crime-control aims of
government after they have been pulled down.
According to history, the earliest tol1 gates were in Pennsylvania and
were erected in 1835 by the State Commissioners. One of the purposes of
erecting them was to clear the roadway of immense droves of cattle, sheep,
hogs and large flocks of turkeys that were being driven to the eastern city
markets.
Since there was no other way through the mountains, the marchants were
compelled to pay toll as a small tax per head, stage coaches and conestoga
wagons with steel rims. Little wonder, Nigeria patterns its system in
similar ways.

Original_Search_Title: Cholera Outbreak Confirmed But Under Control - WHO
Headline: Chad: Cholera Outbreak Confirmed But Under Control - Who
Body_Text:
Abidjan — An outbreak of cholera killed 11 people in southern Chad earlier this month, but the outbreak has been brought under control following repairs to the water supply system in the town of Moundou, the World Health Organization (WHO) said on Thursday.
Dr Abdoulaye Yam, the leader of a WHO team which investigated an outbreak of accute diarrhoea in Moundou, an industrial town 600 km south of the capital N'Djamena, said the disease had been confirmed as cholera.
He told IRIN the Chad Ministry of Health had recorded 131 cases of the disease in Moundou since early January after the main pump at the town's waterworks broke down forcing local people to drink polluted well and river water instead.
So far 11 people had died of cholera, he added.
However he said the number of new cases was decreasing rapidly. While 118 cholera cases were recorded between 4-12 January, only 13 cases were detected the following week.
The WHO official said local wells and latrines had been chlorinated, a public hygene campaign had been launched and Moundou's water supply system had been repaired, removing the main source of infection.
"The water system was repaired last week", he told IRIN by telephone from N'Djamena.
Yam led a WHO mission to Moundou, which lies in the cotton growing region of southern Chad, from 9-12 January.
The team conducted a public information campaign with the help of local churches and mosques to urge local people to improve their hygiene.
They urged residents to wash their hands regularly, disinfect kitchen and drinking utensils and fetch water from potable sources to prevent the spread of cholera.
This highly contagious water-borne disease causes a rapid loss of body fluids and can be fatal unless treated quickly.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Strike: Aviation Workers to Shut Airports
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Strike: Aviation Workers to Shut Airports
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA'S airspace would be shut throughout the impending nationwide
strike that begins tomorrow.
Consequently, the National Union of Air Transport Employees (NUATE) has
warned air travelers to beware as flying during the industrial action would
be at the risk of those involved.
The strike is being called to protest the N1.50k fuel tax and the
reversion to N34 per litre of petrol even as fuel queues returned to Lagos
ahead of the impending strike.
President of NUATE, Mr. Imonitie Itua, and the general secretary,
Mr. Abdulrasaq Seidu, told Labour reporters at the Yaba annex office of
Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) that all aviation workers will join the strike
as a loyal affiliates of the Congress.
They said the union had consequently written the government and her
agencies, private and foreign airlines, embassies as well as all aviation
parastatals, on the decision of the workers and the need for them to be
prepared for the consequences of the planned shutdown of the airspace.
The Labour leaders said they were aware of possible moves by government
to negotiate with some airline operators to open shop even when none of the
vital operational equipment would be handled by the NUATE personnel.
They warned foreigners to be cautious and endure the situation "since we
all have to make sacrifices to make the country change for the better."
The NUATE leaders said there was no basis for their union not to join
the strike against anti-people policies of the government because their
members, being employees of the government, also suffer from policies of the
administration on a daily basis.
The workers who also highlighted a catalogue of perceived injustices
meted out to them by the government warned that "beginning midnight of
January 21, NUATE shall not guarantee air safety both on ground and in the
airspace as a result of our full involvement in the national strike."
Also, National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW) has directed its
members to join in the nationwide action, saying their support would enable
the strike succeed.
National president, Gidado Hamman, who met with the leaders of Lagos
State council said the impending struggle was directly those of the
members.
He said if the drivers refused to join in the fight to stop alleged
extortion by government and save democracy, there will not be an end to the
incessant hike in fuel prices.
Meanwhile, the NLC has zoned Lagos State into 10 areas to be headed by a
co-ordinator.
Assistant general secretary Mr.. Owei Lakemfa said in Lagos that there
would be protest rallies in each of the zones and that the Congress
leadership would ensure they are peaceful.
He said monitoring teams had also been set up to maintain peace and
attend to immediate logistic needs.
But a group, Nigerian Aviation Safety Initiative (NASI) yesterday raised
alarm about disruption in the ongoing airlifting of pilgrims to Mecca,
notwithstanding the earlier exemption of Hajj flights from the strike.
The aviation watchdog was reacting to NUATE's decision to shut down the
airspace as part of efforts to make the effect of the strike total.
Airlifting of pilgrims from Nigeria for this year's hajj commenced in
different parts of the country since last week and it is expected to
continue until next week.
Following an earlier appeal by the Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs
(SCIA), NLC assured that all hajj flights would not be disrupted.
But Capt. Jerry Agbeyegbe, the Executive Director of NASI, who commended
the NLC for exempting hajj flights from the industrial action, billed to
commence tomorrow, however, noted that the exemption would be meaningless if
the support structures which guarantee flight safety were not
available.
NASI, in a statement signed by Agbeyegbe therefore, called for the
exemption of critical sectors of aviation needed for smooth hajj
operations.
Meanwhile, ahead of tomorrow's nationwide strike workers in Anambra
State yesterday held a rally at Aroma junction in support of the action just
as commercial banks in Benin, the Edo State capital, ran out of cash due to
mass withdrawals by their customers.
Addressing the workers yesterday, Chairman of the Anambra State Council
of NLC Mr. Charles Onyeagba, said "the rally is two folds, one, to
demonstrate our condemnation of the political crisis in the state and two,
to prepare the workers for Wednesday (tomorrow's) strike."
Onyeagba said there was the need for traders, commercial motorbike
operators and other people to participate in the strike "so as to let
Federal Government knows that we are against the fuel price hike."
The state president Town Union Association (ASATU) Chief Ben Ezeagboogu,
urged people in the state to support the NLC in its moves to check the
Federal Government and to improve the welfare of Nigerians.
In Benin, several customers besieged the banks yesterday to make last
minute withdrawals ahead of tomorrow's proposed strike.
At the Akapapakva Street which housed greater number of commercial
banks, endless queues of anxious customers were seen in front of the
banks.
The situation was more fearful at one of the banks along Mission Road
where customers fought themselves to get a number that would enable them
withdraw money.
The timely intervention of private securitymen saved the situation from
degenerating into a major fracas.
A manager of one of the banks who pleaded anonymity, told Daily Champion
that the bank had attended to more than 500 customers before noon
yesterday.
"Only two percent of them or less, came to deposit. Others stormed here
to withdraw huge amounts of cash that will sustain them throughout the
period of the expected strike.
"I am sure that you will agree with me that this type of situation is
not good for the economy of a developing nation like Nigeria.
"Something urgent should be done to avert the strike because its effects
on the nation would be more than the controversial N1.50k fuel tax," he
stated.
Meanwhile, people have started stockpiling foodstuffs and other
essential goods to cushion the effects of the envisaged prolonged
strike.
A visit to some of the major markets within the Edo State capital
yesterday revealed a mad rush for the purchase of foodstuffs and other
essential things.
At the popular Oba Market, buyers who were mostly women were seen buying
items like rice, beans, garri, yams, palm oil and even iced fish, in large
quantities.
In the same vein, several filling stations were seen yesterday making
frantic efforts to dispose off their old stock of fuel.
Kwara State council of NLC has expressed its preparedness for the
impending strike, calling for a total support for the strike to make it a
success.
In a communique after a Special Emergency State Executive Council
meeting signed by its chairman, Mr. Emmanuel Ayeoribe, the council frowned
at the alleged unpopular policies of the present administration.
Meanwhile, fuel queues returned to filling stations in Lagos yesterday
as motorists engaged in panic buying in anticipation of scarcity from
tomorrow when the nationwide strike is expected to begin, barring any
unforeseen circumstances.
Daily Champion observed long queues of vehicles snaking out of fuel
retail outlets into major roads in the city as most of the fuel service
stations were overcrowded with desperate buyers.
Besides, the unwelcome reality of fuel hawkers clutching jerrycans of
the product along major roads has returned. The hawkers often sell at
unbearable prices.
The panic buying broke out following statements by president of Nigeria
Labour Congress (NLC), Mr. Adams Oshiomhole, warning Nigerians to stock up
food supplies and enough cash to last the period of the impending
strike.
Daily Champion surveys in Ikeja, Apapa, Mariland, Ikoyi, Marina and
Victoria Island, showed that fuel marketers were having a field day as they
turned on all their dispensing pumps to contain the pressure from crowds of
buyers.

Original_Search_Title: At Least Nine Die of Accute Diarrheoa in Southern Town
Headline: Chad: At Least Nine Die of Accute Diarrheoa in Southern Town
Body_Text:
Ndjamena — At least nine people have died from an outbreak of accute
gastro-enteritis in Moundou, the main town in southern Chad, following the
breakdown of its water works which has led people to drink polluted water
from wells and the local river, the Ministry of Health said in a
statement.
The ministry said 90 people had fallen ill with accute diarrheoa and
vomiting since the town's main water pumping station broke down in late
December.
The World Health Organisation sent a mission led by Dr. Yam Abdoulaye to
the industrial town 500 km south of the capital N'Djamena last Sunday to
investigate the outbreak and determine the exact nature of the disease
Its victims suffer a rapid loss of body fluids. The government has so
far avoided any suggestion that the disease may be cholera.
The river Logone which flows through Moundou is heavily polluted with
the effluent from a local abattoir and factories that make soap, cooking
oil, tobacco and beer.

Original_Search_Title: For Okpodu's Falcons, It Was Over Before It Was Over
Headline: Nigeria: For Okpodu's Falcons, It Was Over Before It Was Over
Body_Text:
FOR coach Okpodu it is talk, talk, talk, never quiet nor modest. Now all
the alibis, excuses, boasts et al, are over, silenced and conquiered by
stack realities of the tournament.
We are among the first few nations to pack our bags and baggages waiting
for the last mandatory game before boarding our plane to come home for more
eba, amala and pounded yam. We have nothing more at stake except to make or
mar our last opponent's fortune for 'he who is down needs fear no fall'. If
our coaches would realise their personal technical handicaps and limitations
as well as those of their players, they would never boast of ability to
demolish opponents or rule the world. If they considered the financial and
logistic constraints of NFA which in turn affect the duration, quality and
mode of their preparation, they would have been able to minimise the high
expectations of Nigerians about their hopes, vision and targets. By the time
this piece is published, the Falcons would have played their last match and
hurry away from that seat of shame to their happy homeland for the All
Africa Games. If they remain African Champions again, the glamour of
continental victory may attempt to eclipse the trauma of the USA 2003
disaster, but the scar cannot be bleached for a long time. I am of the
opinion that what happened to the Super Eagles after Mali 2002 would have
happened to the Falcons if they were not going to participate in the All
Africa Games.
Now that we are out of USA 2003 and we are convinced (I hope so) that
our female football has suffered a very serious set back and international
humiliation, let us accept defeat and full responsibility and face the
realities and programme of the future.
If we must reverse the present trend, we must look at causes, effects
and remedies. Like the former Falcon's coach Ismaila Mabo said, "let us
accept our failure in good faith" as sportsmanship demands that we should be
humble in victory and noble in defeat. However, I would like to remind Mabo
that Nigeria is making its fourth appearance at the World Cup and if we
still lack experience, then we must be a nation of perpetual
experimentalists always fielding guinea pigs. That a team can participate in
four or three consecutive World Cups suggests that it must be a good team by
the standards of the zone from which it qualifies but if such a team
comprises mostly the same players, then it must be an ageing team with
diminishing productive possibilities.
If in 1999 we got to the quarter-final and 4 years later, we could not
survive the first round and not even to score a single goal against the
eleven we conceded, then of course, something serious must have happened to
our football. By the time I was about writing this paragraph, my grand
daughter broke the news that Sweden compounded the Falcons woes into another
catastrophe of 3-0 defeat. So, for Okpodu's Falcons, it was well over before
it was really over and they are welcome back.
Inept, outdated and unfocused technical crew: If one considers and
analyses what it takes to be a good team, one would be right to rate the
technical crew and the players low. From selection to substitution of
players, one could not see sufficient evidence of technical expertise. Basic
skills, tactics and strategies were lacking and so were endurance, speed,
possession, commitment, national pride, mobility, effective marking et
at. Goals were conceded in irritating quick succession and in some cases in
identical manner.
Poor Selection: Although Coach Okpodu is advancing belated excuse about
his having a poor team, I am of the opinion that the selection of a national
team is the joint responsibility of the technical committee of NFA and the
coaches. After the AWC competition, Coach Okpodu had all the time to plug
all the loopholes in his team. Unfortunately, instead of planning for the
World Cup, precious time was spent on squabbles and temper tantrums that
drew adverse media criticisms. If he and NFA looked or planned ahead from
the glory of AWC, some of the girls currently being coached by Ismaila Mabo
could have made the team as replacement of aged players.
Lack of advance planning and programme: Our culture, after the
promulgation of Decree 101, of fire brigade approach and last minute
stampede for major international and even national tournaments does not
favour productivity. Sports development demands knowledge that the end of
one tournament is the beginning of another and so we must have a progressive
programme which guarantees adequate preparation. By bow, after full
technical analysis of what went wrong in the last tournament, we should know
the nucleus of our team to the next AWC and World Cup.
Ministry's continued interference with and control of sports governing
bodies: Sports governing bodies are not allowed sufficient measure of
initiative and autonomy to pursue their goals and source for funds. That the
ministry can over-rule NFA in matters of sponsorship as in the case of
Globacom is scandalous and repugnant interference. If the NFA is granted
enough autonomy, it will be in a better position to plan better and produce
result. Even though the sports ministry or government funds the association,
it should limit its control to accountability through audit.;
Personal interest and lack of commitment:
The foreign exchange factor and the desire for personal gains appear to
set a limit to the type of commitment that places national interest and
image above those of individual. From the players to the coaches to the NFA
to the ministry to legislators, the craze is for estacode to the detriment
of honour. Even our girls went more for fashion and hair dressing and
weaving than for their mission.
Over-confidence and lack of respect for opponents: Inspired by the chief
coach's boasts and loquaciousness, the players on their own became so
immodest that they predicted surprises and defeat of opponents they had not
confronted. In the end, the results were 0-11.
For Okpadu's Falcons pride went before their fall.

Original_Search_Title: Orji Uzor Kalu: You Got This One Wrong!
Headline: Nigeria: Orji Uzor Kalu: You Got This One Wrong!
Body_Text:
I once advised on the kind of assistants coach Adegboye Onigbinde
deserved to do well at the last World Cup.
I talked about Onigbinde as an intelligent man who would have ideas on
how a team could be organised.
But I also harped on the need to assign assistants who were practical
coaches to him. I meant coaches who could demonstrate and put in practice
some of their drills and tactical approaches. I strongly suggested that some
players who played in Europe and who were still current with modern ways in
team preparation and match execution be considered.
Well, we all know how it went at the last World Cup. Henry Nwosu was not
even in a position to contribute the little that he knew and the system did
not help Onigbinde to succeed. The damage had been done before the World
Cup.
When they appointed Christian Chukwu coach of the Super Eagles, I wrote
of Chukwu being a good trainer who needed assistance in the management of a
team. The former Eagles captain is one of the best trainers around. But you
may question his managerial competence in many cases. Again, I looked back
to some of the teams Chukwu trained in the past and pointed his great
ability to organise the defence and midfield reasonably well but I said that
most of his teams lacked aggression in the attack. Generally, Chukwu's teams
lacked the aggression of today's top teams. His gentlemanly nature could be
responsible.
A coach who is naturally aggressive would not, in training, smile at a
striker who receives the ball in a situation that deserves a one-time strike
but who decides to trap it and eventually lose the chance to make hay. He
quickly corrects that even in an aggressive manner. This impacts on the
player some attributes of aggression if repeatedly done not only in an
attempt to score but also in general attack especially on approaching
opponents' goal area. An aggressive coach will not accommodate players who
cannot play one-touch and accelerate with or without the ball. A coach who
is conscious of aggression will not accommodate a player who slows the pace
of the team. He will not accommodate a player who is not a fighter. Kanu
Nwankwo is a fine player whose nature lacks aggression. And in a match where
you need fighters, where finesse may not be your priority, you may ask him
to observe certain things on the bench, preparatory for a half time
chance. Coaches also have their discretion but I used the Kanu analogy to
paint a picture of how their minds work for one to understand why the
Nigerian finds himself on the bench these days. He may still be very good
but the absence of just one quality may explain his place on the
bench. There could be other reasons unknown to us but coaches admire
attackers who have aggression because the way teams organise their defence
now, you need aggressive and fast attackers to dislodge defenders. Chukwu
lacks aggression in his approach and I'm worried about Tunisia. His
assistants appear to be in the same mold. But the good thing is that Chukwu
knows about this and promised to do something about it. Tunisia will
judge.
When a coach knows where he is deficient, he complements. I wrote before
the last World Cup, that the moment a person like Kevin Keegan is appointed
coach of any side, he looks for an assistant who is defence conscious. Left
alone, Keegan can line out 11 attackers(including the keeper) for a match
and they will be running against opponents' goal like dogs just let out of a
cage.
Keegan does this because he knows where he needs help. It is so because
no person is perfect in all areas. Complementing in other to build a
standard team is a modern approach. It appears our people are yet to know
this. No one a person is perfect in all areas. I have drawn all these as
analogies to condemn the way Governor Orji Uzor Kalu has handled the Kadiri
Ikhana case.
I would have been harder on the now smooth-riding governor but for his
comments last week when he admitted that Kadiri was a good coach and his
friend. Orji Uzor Kalu is arguably the most popular governor in the country
today largely because of his achievement with Enyimba of Aba and his
grassroots programmes in many areas that have touched the lives of his
people. While his people score him high in his performance, Nigerians know
him for three major things - his attacks on bad policies of Obasanjo's
government, marginalisation of the Igbos and now football and Enyimba. It is
football that has now given him almost larger than life
projection. Nigerians at home and abroad are still celebrating Enyimba's
great victory on December 12 when they won the CAF Champions League,
becoming the first Nigerian club to win the title in its 39-year
history. Winning the cup was one thing and the commitment and touch of Uzor
Kalu was another factor. He was the force behind the victory and now, the
holder of the highest football office in the continent and one of the
custodians of the game in the world, Issa Hayatou is not only singing Orji's
name but also predicting a bright future for Nigerian football if the likes
of the governor are running the affairs of the country.
Orji is getting all the praises, becoming popular all over the country
but I strongly condemn the way he rubbished Kadiri Ikhana, the coach who led
Enyimba to victory. Even from the semifinal stage where he sat on the bench
and later explained to Nigerians that the need to compel Kadiri to make both
tactical changes and substitutions took him to the bench. They were 1-0 down
against USM of Algeria before they equalised and won in a dramatic way
during stoppage time. The governor has always said that Kadiri is a
fantastic trainer but maintains that he doesn't read matches well and says
that's why he hardly makes changes or when he does, he makes the wrong
ones. He rightly says that coaches sometimes need a second opinion and since
Kadiri was a difficult person who only listened to him, he was compelled to
go to the bench when necessary. Anyway, it is clear that the governor has
tremendous passion for the game. He has proved that it is not only pumping
money into the game that makes it thick and viable. If good planning,
commitment and good administration are lacking, there will be no
results. Orji Uzor Kalu is my man of the year for what he has done and what
he thinks of sports. But he did not impress me when, three days after the
feat in Egypt, he was telling the press that Enyimba had signed a foreign
coach. What followed were controversies and speculations on the sacking of
Kadiri, Kadiri's resignation from Enyimba etc. until the governor cleared
the air last week. That's certainly not the way to celebrate a winner, not
the way to treat a coach who toiled with the club till they made the
greatest history any Nigerian club has made. Kadiri cannot be good in all
areas. If he was not talented in reading matches, he must have been good in
some other areas. Orji Uzor Kalu was wrong. I was happy when the governor
said that Kadiri was his friend and was free to stay. The way he spoke about
Kadiri made me believe that he must have rushed into getting a foreign coach
out of excitement and his ususal passion for the game. He was in the
euphoria of the great victory. But that's the more reason they should have
at least allowed the celebration to die before announcing the engagement of
a foreign coach. The man would not resume at least until January. So why not
allow Kadiri to savour the greatness of Eyimba's feat. That's where my
friend, Orji Uzor Kalu got it wrong. But he remains my man of the year. I'm
proud of this man who means so well for Nigeria and sports which is my
constituency.
READER'S CONTRIBUTION:
ATTENTION KALU: WHAT IS GOOD FOR THE GOOSE IS GOOD FOR THE GANDER
Dear Onochie,
I had difficulty uploading the picture we snapped together during that
chilly weather in Ismaili when Enyimba broke the 38 year jinx. I had held
you so tight and felt so excited touching the man with the most incisive and
thought- provoking pen. I remember extolling those virtues any regular
reader of your column would have. If you did not download any pix in the
attachment, know that it is scan error. I will send the picture to you. I
have adorned it in my album. While I congratulate you for being part of the
team that made history, I make bold to say that you took the crusade to
break that jinx personal. You were most thoughtful and articulate in your
analyses. I am sure the players and crew of Enyimba adhered strictly to
every beat of your advice. Anybody who doubts this should read your piece on
Lessons from Algeria and see how you averted the cold weather implications,
borrowing from what happened to Iwuanyanwu. Now that the Cup has been won,
Enyimba should not relent.They should start the campaign to defend it
successfully. So, I urge you to continue your campaign along that
direction.
Please, make a strong case for the Supporters Club. Enyimba
International FC Supporters Club endured till the end of time. What with the
rickety vehicle that abandoned them somewhere at Qhale in Delta State, the
trip to Enugu on a 911 truck conveying yams to Enugu and the numerous vigils
in prayers ahead of matches. Football is a team work affair and every member
is very relevant. Government of Abia State should provide a good bus (they
have never had even a rickety one though) for the Supporters Club. That way,
they would have less problems going to various cities to support
Enyimba. There are dedicated members of the Club who are willing to give
everything to secure victory for the Club. While the Abia State Government
takes delivery of 22 KIA vehicles and 2 Jeeps, they should vote for a BUS
for the Supporters Club. That, which is good for the goose is good for the
gander.
BEN UDECHUKWU,
Secretary, Information and Strategy Committee
ENYIMBA INTERNATIONAL FC SUPPORTERS' CLUB,
ABA

Original_Search_Title: Who is Tereza Mbire?
Headline: Uganda: Who is Tereza Mbire?
Body_Text:
Kampala — The name Tereza Mbire has for long been associated with the Uganda Women Entrepreneurs Association (UWEAL). But Tereza Mbire the person has remained a paradox, and an unknown person. Finally we have a book, written by Tereza Mbire, thus providing a crucial insight into this woman who has come to personify so many things.
She was born after her father had died.
Mbire, also called Nkabahita (meaning I'm better off than you), would have been considered cursed by the gods. As a child, she twice had a brush with death -- the first time was when she nearly drowned in a well, the second time when her brother, Tadeo, hurled a spear at her, which she dodged. Her brother's vicious conduct towards her is very shocking. He one time seriously beat her for sneaking off to school.
Life has not been very rosy for Mbire. She talks about her dreary life in a Kitooto -- a home for the poor and elderly set up at a Catholic mission, where the principal menu comprises sorghum, ugali and Irish potato-leaf stew, and where a meal of plain sweet potato is deemed a rare 'delicacy'. She is brought up by her uncle's wife, a vicious woman, who deprives her of a comfortable life.
However, from all these experiences, Mbire emerges not just unscathed, but tough.
It is not all agony and wretchedness though for Tereza, for in the swirling confusion of her young life, there are pillars to support her -- her cousin Elisa Bashungura, her mother, sisters, friends and school. After junior school, she goes to boarding school. But despite the tough life she finds there, she is happy and enthusiastic about school. Her ambition to become the first female graduate from Kigezi is, however, thwarted when she is denied admission to Nabbingo Secondary school by the Kigezi Education Board, which refers her to a teacher training college.
However, not all hope is lost.
After college, Tereza becomes a headteacher at a young age of 19. She also gets involved in politics and is appointed a Regional Service Commissioner. She buys her first car, a Volkswagen Beetle, and becomes the first Mukiga woman to drive. She also trains as a hotelier in Israel, where she has to frequently dive into a bunker for safety during the 1967 six-day war between Israel and Egypt.
upon her return to Uganda, she is appointed executive housekeeper at the then Apollo Hotel and a trainer with the Uganda Hotels. She gets married and takes on new roles -- wife and mother. Having acquired experience in running a business, Mbire decides to strike out on her own. She sets up a garment factory, where she employs Chinese and Russian expatriates, and sets up the first modern bakery in Uganda. Owing to her success in business, she is named International Businesswoman of the Year (1997), and consequently invited to Monaco, where she meets with some of the world's celebrities.
This detailed account of Mbire's growth into a successful businesswoman is living example that hard work, hope and determination pay off.
There is the palpable force of patriarchy which assigns duties and responsibilities, determines values, and delineates boundaries in terms of work and ambition. While few restraints are imposed on men, the women have to tread very cautiously in all areas of life. For instance, education is largely considered to be a preserve of men, while arranged and early marriages are a must for women. Then there is the force of destiny -- being an orphan, family poverty, blood relations and inherited social customs which Tereza has to encounter.
Tereza's struggles to overcome difficulties occasioned by patriarchal practices and attitudes make the book suitable in the concerns of gender relations. Tereza's struggles also point to the possibility of the opening-up of opportunities through sheer force of will, a lesson that we should all consider crucial.
Tereza Mbire: Shaping of a Destiny is a remarkable story simply told, that has a vital lesson for everyone.

Original_Search_Title: Giving 'Lost Life' Back to Girls
Headline: Kenya: Giving 'Lost Life' Back to Girls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Minutes to 10 pm and the metropolis is abuzz with night activity. It is party time as usual at a popular nightclub on Moi Avenue in the heart of Nairobi. The mood is exhilarating, the music and drink intoxicating, with noisy guttural voices and high-pitched sounds from the old jukebox. The crowded bar is choking with cigarette smoke.
The young women wiggle, wriggle and gyrate suggestively to the music, and the male patrons are clued in. Clad in tight see-throughs and wearing a come-hither expression, these girls are commercial sex workers.
At a far corner of the room, a hefty and visibly irritated woman shoots up from her seat and starts banging the table, shouting at the man seated next. Standing close to the jukebox, another seemingly calm one is strategically placed. From her vantage point, she floats around a cunning gaze, sampling and winking at prospective prey.
Then there is this petit one, probably 15, staggering and vomiting as she is led to the toilet. She could be one of the "latest arrivals" from the village.
But perched at another vantage point in the midst of all this buzz is a rather elderly woman. Clad in a T-shirt, a jacket and a cap to match her trousers, she is keenly watching the goings-on in the noisy pub. Here on a different mission, this university professor has been on this beat for the last 15 years.
"The surest way of understanding the dynamics and intricacies of prostitution is by being on the ground and mingling with those who participate in it," Prof Elizabeth Ngugi of the University of Nairobi's Department of Community Health explains her presence in this unlikely choice of a bar.
Ironically, just like the men and women crammed in the noisy room, Elizabeth is on a hunting mission. She established, in 1992, an organisation to fight HIV-Aids among commercial sex workers and that is why she is here tonight - to recruit girls and young women into her reform and rehabilitation programme.
It is a window of opportunity for those who wish to kiss goodbye the world's oldest profession, and a chance for those resolved to carry on to learn more about the health risks and hazards and how to protect themselves better. Even as Elizabeth calmly keeps vigil over a soft drink, there are amusing moments as some men try to make passes at her.
Well, she is above that level, and the look in her eye says it all. Instead, she beckons to the girls close to her, one at a time.
She explains to the girls why she is here. She wants to talk with them about prospects of a better life, the way out of prostitution into a more productive and enjoyable occupation. She would like to fix an interview with each one of them in her office the following day.
Those interested submit their contacts and suggest the date and time suitable for an interview. Then she leaves them to enjoy the night and moves on to the next nightspot.
"There is no major nightclub in Nairobi that I have not been to over the last 15 years," she says. But need she dress like the girls? "No. I do not wear the trousers as a camouflage but rather to protect my legs from the cold of the night. Besides, I do not want mosquitoes to make a meal of them."
"I have to be myself," Elizabeth explains, "to win confidence of the girls. If you pretend to be who you are not, they will quickly recognise that and dismiss you as fake."
Elizabeth is happy to do this difficult task and even jokes about it: "It gives me great joy to mingle and offer help and hope to the young women. It makes me feel like a sweet sixteen."
Indeed, the professor of community health is no ordinary individual. Her exceptional and selfless efforts received global recognition two months ago when she was declared the United Nations Person of the Year, 2004. The medical researcher was recognised for her "outstanding contribution to the natural response to HIV-Aids, with a particular focus on commercial sex workers in Kenya." Her name was proposed for nomination by the executive director of UNaids, Dr Peter Piot, whose office is in Geneva.
Dr Piot has many times met and interacted with Elizabeth in the course of her spirited crusade to convert and rehabilitate commercial sex workers. The award constitutes a certificate and a beautiful plaque, with no monetary value. It is simply given out as recognition of an individual's outstanding efforts or contribution to the wellbeing of mankind. Elizabeth is the first Kenyan to win it.
She set up the Kenya Voluntary Women's Rehabilitation Centre (K-Vowrc) in Nairobi in 1992 to help mitigate HIV-Aids impact among girls and young women.
"I am simply helping these women to be responsible practitioners - to protect themselves and their male clients against HIV-Aids," she adds. "In this war, one has to move in all directions like a spider".
Elizabeth says the HIV-Aids scourge has "completely destabilised players in the sex industry." A host of commercial sex workers have already refined their trade techniques to operate safely.
The women have designed "friendlier" and "less risky" methods to hold on to male clients who are increasingly shying away. At a popular discotheque on Moi Avenue, male patrons are allowed to only caress, fondle and kiss at an agreeable fee.
"Babe-sitting", or lap-dancing, is the hottest item on the menu. In this procedure, a half-nude woman sits on her client's laps, and both cuddle as they rock to the disco music.
"Some of the men are willing to pay as much as Sh2,000 for this service," discloses Maureen, an 18-year-old girl who dropped out of school and is learning the ropes as a commercial sex worker.
Some of the girls bluntly ask for food and financial assistance, forcing themselves onto the company of men in bars. "The truth is that I'm not interested in sex as such," says Julie, another operator at the club. "My aim is to make money, and I would be so much happier if I could earn an honest living without having to jump into bed with strangers," she adds. And the women do not necessarily depend on the unwritten "menu"? Some earn their cash the smart way?
Noting that most men appreciate the company of women while having an evening drink, Julie says that countless times she has been bought food and drinks and paid money by a male customer just to keep him company in the bar.
However, she explains, often there are strings attached. "Some of the men want to have a good time by engaging the girls in a vulgar conversation, fondling our bodies and even touching the private parts."
"Sometimes it feels good but at times it can be annoying or embarrassing, depending on the quality of customer at hand; but we girls have to bear it to make an extra coin," adds Julie. But the men are crying foul. One male client says he has often been given a raw deal by the girls. "I have at times been left alone by a girl after buying her food, drinks and even cigarettes; she picks up the drinks and disappears into the drunken crowds," explains John, a regular night club patron.
"Some of the girls exchange the drinks for money at the counter and slithe out of the club into the next pub. It's a very bad experience for the male customer." In even more cunning approaches, the girls get into an understanding with the barman, who constantly serves them plain water (strictly sipped from the opaque beer bottles) for each drink that is bought for them by a client.
The girl and barman share the spoils when the man staggers to the loo or out of the club.
Maureen explains that even with such tricks, a commercial sex worker still earns little to boast. "While these games sometimes work, they don't pay much cash. Besides, the regular male clients know all the tricks and are hard to cheat". In light of this, medical researcher Elizabeth is perhaps the sole individual offering the girls a most desired remedy - vocational training and credit facilities to start them off and change their lives for good.
Currently, her organisation is working with more than 1,000 women on the training and loan schemes. In essence, K-Vowrc gives each woman a small amount of money to live on, for transport and food. In the meantime, it finds them a work training placement with a business in occupations such as hairdressing, dressmaking or basic motor vehicle mechanics and repair work.
The tale of Judy Mwangi, 48, now working as community mobiliser at K-Vowrc, is perhaps the most inspiring. When she quit the world's oldest profession in 1991, Judy was given her first loan of Sh12,000 and she set up a business venture that would also benefit some of the women she left behind in the pubs and clubs.
Having been in that trade for 20 years, Judy had been nicknamed "Queen". She knew the girls had difficulties accessing affordable meals from their lodging rooms, especially after a night of indulgence in drink, dance and sex.
"What I used to do," Judy recalls, "I would prepare meals like porridge, githeri and njahi (black beans with mashed potatoes), which I sold to them at the doorstep of their rooms in a popular lodging house on Luthuli Street."
The business idea proved lucrative, earning her a profit of well over Sh500 a day. She made money and seized the opportunity to win over a host of former colleagues from the flesh-peddling business.
Today, Judy is the envy of not just her former colleagues but people in business, formal and informal employment and even in the professions.
From her small loan and mentoring by K-Vowrc, she made enough money to build two houses, each with eight rental rooms, in Dandora Estate.
However, the going is still rough for some of the young women recently recruited from the streets by K-Vowrc. Owing to economic hardships, some concede the temptation to return to the clubs is high.
"Ujue saa zingine kunakauka mpaka inabidi nichomoke hadi tao kutafuta angalau shoti moja nipate chapaa (You know, sometimes the going is hard without money, and I am forced to go to town looking for a male client)," confesses Sheila, who months ago made up her mind to quit the sex trade.
The girls under rehabilitation, some as young as 15, initially got into prostitution after dropping out of school for lack of fees, peer pressure or after being kicked out of their homes by parents because they were pregnant or had a baby.
Some of the girls are being trained in hairdressing by two former twilight girls, Joyce Wangari and Catherine Wanjiku. The two initially got the small loan (Sh12,000) to start up hair salons and are doing well.
"The profit margins may not be that much compared to what we used to get from the streets," explains Wangari. "However, the cash flow here is constant and the job is socially respectable and not risky in terms of health and hygiene."
Elizabeth says many girls she restored from the streets now have stable families and businesses. "Some even got married in the end and have happy families," says Elizabeth. "We are talking about life that was virtually lost that has been dramatically recaptured."
The UN award winner is convinced she has a good cause to brave the cold nights on the streets and nightspots of Kenya's capital city in search of lost lives to restore.

Original_Search_Title: Pandhari Lodge: Little Nyanga in Harare
Headline: Zimbabwe: Pandhari Lodge: Little Nyanga in Harare
Body_Text:
Harare — PERCHED on a hill in the rolling, woody hills of Glen Lorne suburb in an area that resembles the beauty of the Eastern Highlands, is a lodge providing a rare combination of secluded luxury with a sense of peace and serenity.
If you thought you had to travel hundreds of kilometres to Nyanga for such a beautiful view of mountains and a peaceful environment, you were wrong.
Just at your doorstep, here in Harare, that place exists.
And its just a 12 minutes drive from downtown Harare along the Nyamapanda Road and you turn left into Glen Helen Way to number 3 Barlow Close, Glen Lorne, where you find Pandhari Lodge - a place to refresh yourself.
As the name implies, Pandhari is a meeting place where people drink and eat taking themselves away from the daily hassles.
I instantly fell in love with the gauzy beautiful landscape and the green environment which reminded me of the imposing resort town of Nyanga.
The fresh, clean and very peaceful atmosphere sunk me far away from the stressful bustles and hassles of the city.
After walking around and still amalgamated by the thick woods and sweet scent of the fresh air that swept my breath away, I elected to check what was on offer inside.
The lodge's manager Ms Linda Chikonzi ushered us in with her homely smile.
Long after I left the place, it dawned on me that Ms Chikonzi's smile was part of the staff dress code - everyone wore a broad smile.
Just at the entrance of the lodge are two huge hippopotamuses standing still but majestic as if they were waiting for my arrival, yes, true two nicely carved hippopotamuses.
We went downstairs first to a carpeted 100 square metre Chiefs Suite sitting room close to an inner dining room.
These are decorated with various quality African wood carvings of different wild animals.
Here and there are carvings of Zimbabwean people, paintings as well as mats that give one an authentic African feeling.
While I was shifting my eyes from corner to corner like a chameleon, everything in that joint impressed me and I thought to myself, this was just the perfect place that cuts across racial and age barriers.
And I mean family, friends, businesspeople and lovers, especiallythose wishing to spend a quiet, fun-filled and peaceful time at a nice hideout in the mountains.
A wide range of Western and traditional foods is on the menu and served by smartly dressed chefs whose quality of service and food is exceptional.
Offered at the lodge which opens from Monday to Sunday from 6am to 11pm are ala carte meals different from buffets in the sense that they are cooked per order and served by chefs.
The variety of dishes available ranges from appetisers to desserts.
They give diners a wide mouth-watering choice of what to eat at reasonable prices.
Starters include traditional mushroom soup, lightly curried butternut soup, roasted tomato soup, and potato and cheese soup.
A wide range of dishes makes up the main course that includes steak azeka, steak prepared the Portuguese way and served with either pepper sauce or choron sauce, chicken roulado, bream menier, cracked pepper steak among others.
Also on offer are traditional meals that include tripe and sadza, beef stew and sadza, stewed chicken served with peanut butter rice and many other dishes.
Famous chef salad, which comprises of mushroom, cucumbers, lettuce and tomatoes, Greek salad, boiled eggs and bacon, asparados with mayonnaise sauce and other salads, make up the salad dishes.
Wide ranges of desserts are also on offer for a grand finish.
Well I had a treat to a delicious meal that included rice mixed with diced tomatoes, onions and carrots, roasted chicken, braised steak with potatoes and mushroom sauce.
However, there was not much of a choice at the bar where they had the usual soft drinks, no fruit juices and a narrow variety of wines and whiskies which I think most people would want to have at such outings.
Apart from the inside dining, Pandhari lodge also has an outside dining area that overlooks a sparkling two-pier pool, floodlit tennis and basketball courts that people can enjoy themselves.
What a good way of relaxing!
All the 21 spacious conditioned bedrooms are fitted with Orthoking beds and ceramic tiled en-suite bath or showers, cocktail fridge, satellite colour television and tea or coffee making facilities.
The lodge that has a kitchen that caters for 70 people is also convenient to full conference and business meetings.
"We also offer outdoor catering and we have been hosting weddings and parties of up to 60 people.
"Our aim is to provide the best quality of food and service in a tranquil environment that accommodates all classes and where people can refresh and relax," said Ms Chikonzi.
So why not go and have a wonderful and memorable special Christmas lunch with your family and friends at the little Nyanga - Pandhari Lodge.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rate Up
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Rate Up
Body_Text:
THE inflation rate for November rose by 0.3 per cent to 18 .3 per cent from 18 per cent in the preceding month, the Central Statistical Office (CSO) has said.
This is according to CSO latest edition of its Monthly Bulletin publication released in Lusaka yesterday.
CSO acting director of census and statistics William Mayaka attributed the rise to increases in food prices, which accounted for nine per cent while non-food items accounted for 9.3 per cent
The annual inflation rate also increased from 16 per cent to 16.8 per cent while the index for food increased by 2.6 per cent this month.
But compared with November last year the annual rate has declined by 0.8 per cent from 19.1 to 18.3.
The bulletin reports that relatively higher prices were recorded for most food items, including maize grain, roller meal, cereal products, fresh meat, dried kapenta, fish, fresh vegetables and shelled groundnuts.
Others were sweet potatoes, dressed chickens, eggs, cooking oil and other processed food items.
Meanwhile, the total exports from September to October increased by 0.5 per cent from K560,289 million to K563,264 million.
It states that the main products exported were manufactured goods, refined copper, cobalt, precious stones, cotton yarn and Portland cement.
These commodities accounted for about 69.2 per cent and 71.7 per cent in total exports of manufactured goods in September and October, respectively.
The imports also increased from K930,547 million in September to K943, 949 million, which represented about 1.4 per cent in the total value of expenditure on imports.
And Chirundu has accounted for about 32 per cent of value of imports, which left Zambia in October followed by Lusaka International Airport which accounted for 17 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: How Wavamunno Out-Competed Fate
Headline: Uganda: How Wavamunno Out-Competed Fate
Body_Text:
Kampala — "An average man of my age who was born in Rugaaga, Isingiro was destined to lead a rather limited and miserable life. He was bound to live, work, marry, raise children and eventually die within the confines of Rugaaga. He was not likely to go school," notes Mr Gordon. So just how did Wavamunno outmanoeuvre fate?
Although he grew up in a small town, Wavamunno liked to think big. He was determined not to let the remoteness of Rugaaga limit him. He knew there was a larger world beyond the horizon.
His home was in a rural trading centre and that made the difference. Compared to his contemporaries who lived in isolated villages deep in the countryside, Rugaaga was a relatively urban atmosphere.
"The place where I was born was a small trading centre; one of the first ones in Isingiro. My dad had a shop, which was selling sweets - we used to eat a lot of sweets from a big twenty-litre tin - soda, beer, matchboxes, sugar, and salt. I think it was the only shop around. Apart from trading, my mother would sit in the shop with us young ones," recalls Wavamunno.
Gordon Babala Kasibante Wavamunno is one of 13 children of Maliza and Yovani Wavamunno. He was born in 1943. Rugaaga, the place of his birth is one of six sub-counties of Isingiro County as it used to be in the colonial Ankole district. By the time of Wavamunno's birth, his father was just retiring from his position as sub-county chief of Rugaaga. At this time, Wavamunno's dad, a migrant trader from Kooki in Buganda, bought 100 acres of land near the sub-county headquarters and became a permanent resident of Rugaaga.
"I was born in Mbarara. My dad migrated to Ankole for trade. He was one of the first people to start trade between Ankole and Buganda. He was dealing in cattle and produce," recounts Wavamunno.
When he was growing up, most of the prominent people in Isingiro were Muslims and the area's traders were almost exclusively people who originated in Buganda, settled in Ankole and operated the businesses.
From what Wavamunno remembers, his father wasn't wealthy but compared to the majority of their neighbours, his family was above average. They lived in a big house with two wings, one for each of Yovani Wavamunno's wives with his father's shop in the middle.
"As children, our accommodation was congested, our furnishings were Spartan, to say the least; and our clothing was hardly adequate. Nevertheless, compared to other families in Rugaaga who lived from hand to mouth, our family was rich, privileged and enlightened," he says.
In the Rugaaga of those days, a bicycle was a status symbol and the Wavamunnos had one.
"From tender age, we were privileged that our dad had a bicycle, then he started a bus service," says Wavamunno. In reality, the bus he speaks of was merely a pickup truck of the Ford make, with benches at the back. They called it 'Foda Kasanga'.
"We used to go to school on bicycles. We had two bicycles; one carrying my elder brother Eliab and Elidad, then the other one carrying my other brother and sister. We used to ride the bicycle from the side," he says. Among Wavamunno's contemporaries at school were Hon. Dr. Nkuuhe and Peter Bibangambah while his elder brother Eliab went to school with former ministers Richard Kaijuka and Eriya Kategaya, Mr George Abola, Mr Akabway of URA, Prof. Lwanga Lunyiigo and Prof. Stanley Tumwine among others.
On their bicycles, Wavamunno and his siblings rode six kilometres to school from home in Rugaaga to Kihanda, one of the area's four primary schools, none of which went beyond Primary four. After P.4, the children went to school in Gayaza, some thirty miles away. "There's another Gayaza in Isingiro," Wavamunno hastens to add.
Not only did his family own one of the few bicycles in the village, they had porters at home and could afford shoes in the 1940s.
"We had shoes right from the beginning. Our dad bought us these 'lusegyera' (canvas shoes) from Indian shops," he says.
"I think electricity first came to Mbarara in 1962." And with it, the black and white TV, another thing that the Wavamunnos acquired before most of their neighbours. When he wasn't watching television, he took part in outdoor games like football.
"I was a football player to the school level. Up to now, I love football. When we grew up, we used to go dancing at Kakyeeka. There's a big hall there. There's also another one called Nganwa hostel. Later on, I was a proprietor of Nganwa hostel, bringing musicians from Congo and Kampala to perform in Mbarara. I loved dancing so much and up to now, I still love to dance," says Wavamunno.
Wavamunno, the teenager joined Mbarara high school briefly before leaving school altogether to work with his dad. "He said that what I had learnt was enough. He needed me to help him in his business. My dad was a very strong man, average businessman. He used to buy coffee and bring it to Masaka. From there it was my duty to escort lorries of coffee to their destination. I did this right from the age of nine and when I dropped out of school, I became more actively involved in the buying of coffee and beans. We travelled through many parts of Ankole, Masaka and we even got to Kabale to buy cabbages and Irish potatoes for sale in Kampala," he says.
Here was a small boy trying to run his father's business, didn't the older workmen try to take advantage of him? "I was the boss' son and my dad's men respected me very much and they had to make sure they protected me and we remitted all the money made. By the way, people were very trustworthy in those days," he says.
"Trading and travelling with my dad gave me the exposure. Eventually, I found that I had left Mbarara and was now in Masaka and after Masaka, I was in Kampala." "By 1969, I'd started seriously considering leaving Mbarara for the city," he says.
In 1972, Wavamunno succumbed to the lure of the big city. He left Mbarara for good and went to the city where (together with a few others) he started a company called Uganda People's Transport Union.
Although the Wavamunno family still have some properties in Mbarara, when it's time to go back home, he takes his children to Kooki in Buganda. Although he was born and bred in Mbarara, home is elsewhere.
"We have built a school in Isingiro. My father started it and we finished it. We have also built them a church," he says. And that's as much attachment as Wavamunno has to Isingiro. On the few occasions that he has returned, it is the people's low standards of living that struck him.
"It is still under developed and it's not just Rugaaga but all the villages we moved through while trading coffee are still lagging behind. Nothing has changed. If anything, I believe that the people there are worse off than we were. The population has increased but it has only increased the burden."

Original_Search_Title: Deo Releases an Eight-Track Album
Headline: Zimbabwe: Deo Releases an Eight-Track Album
Body_Text:
Harare — Just a year after releasing his chart topper Magetsi last year, Langton Deo is back with "Ndokuchaisa Ngemheni."
It might seem fast, but it's certainly not unprecedented. In fact, it follows the pattern set by other luminaries like Alick Macheso and Oliver Mtukudzi who release an album every year.
Deo was doing well with Magetsi and Ndokuchaisa Ngemheni is expected to do equally well.
The eight-track album comprises beats ranging from Kwaito to jazzy stuff.
This is the first album by Deo, which he self-produced at his Goromonzi Studios and it sold more than 500 copies last week after its release."I produced this album myself at my state-of-the art studio and it is a source of pride for me. I am going to record a couple of new artists as part of my youth policy and have already purchased a Harare building to house my equipment," he said.
The title track features high riding artist Betty Makaya who is oozing with confidence following the success of her first album Ndichange Ndiripo.
On Ndokuchaisa Ngemheni, Betty's vocal clarity makes the album enjoyable. The song warns those who try to lure Deo's woman using money that he will answer by sending a bolt of lightning to strike them. She also features on the cut titled Mentor Yangu, a love track in which Deo showers his loved one with praises.Other tracks on Ndokuchaisa Ngemheni include Foromani, Share Yenyu Hapana, a track that condemns irresponsible fathers who refuse to take care of their families, MaJealous and London, a satirical social commentary of people leaving Zimbabwe to work in the United Kingdom.
Gramma Records are distributing the album.
Deo released his first album in 2001 Sweet Potato. The title track made a huge impact in the club scene although that did not reflect in sales.
Later, Deo released his second album Magetsi which ushered in a new era in his quest for stardom.
The title track was produced by Fortune "Mac Daddy" Mparutsa at his "Wofman Studios".
Magetsi was a masterpiece and it featured the talents of Malvin S as well as Fortune himself.
The album also featured tracks like Awuya Muroora, Huya Pano and Ndinosara Nani.
Deo says he drew inspiration from his personal life when penning such songs as Magetsi and Ndinosara Nani.Deo makes every album different from the other as a result of his collaboration with different musicians. "I have realised that it is important to give other artists a chance and that chance should be availed to them without having to go through a struggle," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Ngugi Meets in-Laws
Headline: Kenya: Ngugi Meets in-Laws
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It was another hero's welcome for Professor Ngugi wa Thiong'o when he
paid his in-laws a visit for the first time in the Kikuyu traditional
fashion on Wednesday.
The literary icon was accompanied by his family members and friends as
he made his inaugural formal contact with his in-laws, in a practice
