commonly known as kumenya mucii, (knowing the home) at Mitero village in
Mang'u, Thika.Right from the local trading centre, Gatukuyu, the villagers
were agape as they watched the convoy of cars snake its way along the dusty
roads.
Prof Ngugi wa Thiongo and his wife Njeeri (centre) are hugged by his
mother-in-law Magdaline Wambui during the visit in Mang'u, Thika. Pic by
Hudson Wainaina
And on arrival at his wife's ancestral home, Ngugi - this unrivalled
lover of culture and everything African - could not have felt greater
joy.
Women welcomed the delegation with song, dance and the traditional
ululation (ngemi) as they were ushered into a makeshift tent made of gunny
bags.
Here, they were served with lunch as a mob of villagers and dusty
children mostly in tatters joined in the celebration and kept their eyes
glued on this enigma.
But always the peasants' advocate, the writer patted the youngsters'
heads affectionately and inquired their names and after their well-being as
he passed them on his way to an adjacent pit latrine.
The visitors were served with the Kikuyu traditional delicacies of irio
(a mashed mixture of maize, beans, potatoes and wild greens) sweet potatoes,
njahi, and njugu (variety of legumes) and roast goat ribs. And as is
anybody's guess, the writer ate the meal voraciously.
Ngugi, who spoke fluent Kikuyu, then proceeded to greet his
mother-in-law, Magadaline Wambui, in the deeply traditional Gikuyu version,
that is almost extinct and connotes a lot of respect, "Wakia Maitu (how do
you do mother)," to which his wife's mother promptly responded: "Wakia Awa
(how do you do father)."
And as per the custom, after the food and refreshments, the inner circle
consisting of Ngugi's family and close friends from Limuru, who included his
elder brother Kibiruri wa Thiong'o, were ushered into his mother-in-law's
the house. According to Njeeri, this was not an occasion to pay dowry, but
for the two families to get to know one another. Bride price negotiations
are scheduled to start later this month. The party went on into the night
before the visitors finally left for their homes.
Meanwhile, it emerged that between 2001 and 2002, Ngugi dispatched his
two children to a nondescript rural school in the area to learn Gikuyu.
Mumbi and Thiong'o were enrolled in Standards One and Two respectively
at St Francis Primary School, a typical rural school with dusty classrooms
and missing window panes.
Ngugi explained that his children initially spoke their mother tongue
when they were growing up in New Jersey, but they later forgot the
language.
"We wanted our children to have roots, so we brought them to stay with
their aunt and study in this school for one year," said Njeri. Today, the
children alternate fluent Gikuyu with English with amazing ease.

Original_Search_Title: This Is How Federo Does Museveni Good
Headline: Uganda: This Is How Federo Does Museveni Good
Body_Text:
Kampala — Those who have persistently articulated the quest for federalism under
President Yoweri Museveni's regime have over the years been treated more or
less like restless youngsters. How has Museveni's strategy worked? First,
you must assure them that they are among your best friends; that you will do
almost anything to make them happy. And most important, that you are the
only person on earth who will go that distance.
Two, while they should never be allowed to stop hoping, they must be
kept guessing what or when your next move will be. Three, when it comes to
policies on which you intend to make good, it helps to delay and draw out
each delivery for as long as public patience can hold. Remember that if you
give ten sweets to a child all at once, they will be finished in a day, and
he will ask for more. If you hand out one sweet every Sunday, they will last
several weeks. So every Sunday the child will have to (if grudgingly)
acknowledge that you deliver.
This kind of benign meanness keeps many sons and daughters under
otherwise leaky parental roofs for several years. And that is how the
country (apart from the north) got its sleep from the Movement. That is how
traditional rulers were restored, albeit without any power. Plus the 1995
constitution and the "democratic" 1996 elections.
Then the 2000 referendum that seemed to give the Movement yet another
lease of life; that is, until the judges said all the time keeping was
wrong.
How many years have gone by? Oh, eighteen! Well, that is also the way
you will get your multi-party system, making it twenty.
I believe that federalism was programmed to be President Museveni's
joker, the trump card to crown all concessions, coinciding with his quest
for yet another stretch of years in power.
But to give some powers to the regions means to surrender those powers
at the centre. Museveni, a control freak, would live a nightmarish life
devising ways of retrieving the powers so given away.
The Odoki report presented Museveni with a problem; the majority of
Ugandans wanted a federal arrangement. But the report also opened an
opportunity; the support for federalism was significantly stronger in
Buganda than elsewhere. If that difference in the levels of fervour could be
nurtured to appear to be a conflict in positions, making federalism a
concept that Buganda had to "sell' to other regions, then the president was
well advanced in the design to keep his turf.
Isolate... isolate... isolate them. Even demonise them. As recently as
last Sunday, after forces from various regions appeared to close ranks with
Buganda, Museveni moved very quickly. And Mengo officials slipped into the
same old trap and accepted an invitation to attend a hastily arranged
meeting at State House to discuss their concerns and grievances with the
president on their own.
The result was entirely predictable: one inch of progress, which can be
reversed whenever it is expedient for the president to backtrack, and a good
measure of irritation among Mengo's "new" allies, the sting of which has
prompted Mengo to improve on its footwork.
Look at the president's meanness towards Kampala mayor, Ssebaana Kizito,
who against so many odds has tried to make some improvements to the city. It
is difficult to see Museveni appreciating the work of regional governors,
who might after all usefully reform service delivery in areas where the
present myriad of LC councillors and chiefs have become little more than
partisan parasites sponging on an impoverished populace.
But one must have some sympathy with the president. If you have been
used to blessing or kicking anyone around for so long, in any corner of the
country; and you have gamblers, conmen and investors pretending to be under
the spell that you are the only god this side of the earth, and you are in a
year when your Cabinet's idea of constitutional reform was to give you even
more power, you would not cede any of that so easily.
And that must be one reason why Museveni, who lectures and offers wisdom
on every topic under the sun, has never committed chalk and time to explain
to his peasants how federalism works. If he did, they would probably be
wiser on the holes in the blend of his philosophy of bigger regional blocks,
his dismissal of federalist aspirations and his fragmentation of the country
into unviable postage stamp-sized districts.
For now, their ignorance is his strength, especially if they are
encouraged to confuse federalism with monarchism, and to increase the tint
of ethnic and tribal sentiments in their attitude instead of emphasizing
"developmental" concerns.
This ignorance has even become respectable. It would be fine to be
opposed to the system, but a good number of our legislators now actually
claim to be too potato-brained to understand the main features of
federalism!
Before they built their mansions in Kampala, the NRA/M lords used to
mock their predecessors as "primitive". And yet these new brilliant men and
women cannot see beyond Buganda's dated glory songs, nor beyond their
obsession with the charms of this little metropolis called Kampala, with its
car-jammed winding tracks masquerading as streets.
They cannot visualize the emergence of new centers; new institutions of
learning, research, industry and health care powered by the engines of
federalism, tapping the huge reserve of human potential out there; the
growth of new cities; cities which would make posterity wonder what the fuss
about Muteesa I's dusty town was all about.
And they can't see in that distance because they are totally
self-regarding. They are now very rich, but in spirit they are
"hand-to-mouth" gatherers and predators. They cannot invest their faith in
communities and cities that would reach their splendour after they are dead
or have left power.

Original_Search_Title: Previously Disadvantaged Chefs Head for the States
Headline: South Africa: Previously Disadvantaged Chefs Head for the States
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Two previously disadvantaged chefs are heading to New York to serve up a
taste of freedom in a weeklong series of events hosted by South African
Tourism at the prestigious James Beard House.
During a series of in-store food and wine events leading up to the main
event, New Yorkers will have a chance to sample a rich variety of tastes of
South Africa's 'Rainbow Cuisine' at special food demonstrations and tasting
sessions.
The event will be held at the new Williams-Sonoma store in the Time
Warner Center at Columbus Circle.
According to the South African Tourism president in the US Prudence
Solomon, Cape Classics will be providing South African wines, featuring some
of the country's finest vintages, pairing selections with each course from
the James Beard dinner, as well as hosting the wine tasting at the in-store
events.
Ms Solomon said the two are Sun International's pastry chef Earl King at
the Table Bay Hotel, and Johannes Mokae, sous chef from The Palace of The
Lost City.
They had been chosen by the hotel group to represent South Africa for
the James Beard House dinner.
They will be accompanied by team leader Grant Cullingworth, when they
prepare a five-course-meal demonstrating the eclectic ethos of African
cuisine to some of the most discerning palates in the world.
"We are honoured to be bringing these talented South African chefs to
showcase their talents at one of the world's most prestigious food
organisations, and to continue our celebration of ten years of freedom in
our nation.
"It is after all thanks to this freedom that our people are able to
share their remarkable creativity and abilities with the world and, in the
process, place a deserving spotlight on South Africa's fabulous fusion
cuisine," said Ms Solomon.
Mr King became a chef after studying hotel management and turned his
talents toward pastries after realising that he was "not too fond of sweaty,
hot kitchens".
"I am proud and happy to be selected for this honour," said Mr
King.
"It makes me proud to see how far our country has come in ten years, and
to be part of that renaissance. I look forward to representing my hotel and
my country," he said.
Meanwhile Mr Mokae discovered his passion for fine cooking at a young
age, joining the hotel group when he was only 20 years old.
"Democracy has brought a lot of opportunities, now I will go to the
United States for the first time in my life. When we lived under apartheid
we never could have dreamed of an opportunity like this," he said.
The menu for the James Beard House dinner has been designed to be a
celebration showcase of South Africa's 'Rainbow Cuisine'.
Highlights will include a Curry Spiced Butternut and Sweet Banana
Emulsion with a Chermoula Roasted Langoustine, Toasted Mossbolletjie Brood,
Coriander-Sugared Pineapple, Venison Loin Wrapped in Morogo (wild
vegetables) and Pancetta with Corn and Mielie Pap Tert, Pistachio Nut
Crusted Potato, Tomato Sheba and Coal-Baked Pearl Onions. Desserts will
feature a Frozen Melktert with Ginger Nut Biscuit, Koeksisters and Naartjie
Konfyt.
South African Airways will be providing a roundtrip airfare for the
chefs to participate in the celebration.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Goes Down
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Goes Down
Body_Text:
THE Central Statistical Office (CSO) has announced a 17.4 per cent annual rate of inflation for the month of May, representing a 0.4 percentage point lower than the 17.8 percent recorded last month.
Director of census and statistics Buleti Nsemukila said however that between April and May the Consumer Price Index (CPI) went up by 0.7 per cent.
He said the 17.4 percent annual inflation for May was a composition of increases in food prices which accounted for nine percentage point while the non food items in the CPI accounted for 8.4 percentage points.
Dr Nsemukila said annual food inflation stood at 16.9 per cent, gaining 0.6 percentage points on the April rate of 16.3 per cent.
The CSO said generally, decreases occurred in the prices for maize meal, maize grain, fresh fish, dried Kapenta, fresh meat, eggs, sweet potatoes and fresh fruits among others.
Price increases on the other hand occurred in fresh vegetables, chicken oils and fats, milk and milk products, sugar and other processed food commodities.

Original_Search_Title: Song, Dance And Hope As SA Marks Decade of Freedom
Headline: South Africa: Song, Dance And Hope As SA Marks Decade of Freedom
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — PRESIDENTS, monarchs, state representatives and tens of thousands of
locals gathered under a blazing sun yesterday to watch President Thabo Mbeki
being sworn in at the Union Buildings for his second term as head of the
country.
At least 38 heads of state from Europe, the Americas, Asia, the Middle
East and Africa attended the occasion, which coincided with the celebration
of SA's tenth anniversary of democracy.
Mbeki's inauguration kicked off nationwide celebrations on Freedom Day,
a holiday that commemorates the day in 1994 when South Africans of all races
voted together for the first time, bringing a remarkably peaceful end to
nearly half a century of cruel and often violent white minority rule.
The tone for the day was set by the arrival of former president Nelson
Mandela, who was greeted with wild cheers. Ululations and impromptu
renditions of freedom songs rang out from the lawns of the Union Buildings,
which hosted an estimated 60000 people from around the country and, indeed,
the world.
The official part of the programme was conducted with precision timing
starting from the freezing early hours of the morning when the media, chefs,
ushers and cleaners, as well as other logistics staff gathered at
Centurion's cricket park to go through security checks.
The meticulously planned celebration, with a total budget of R90m,
doubled as a showcase for SA's bid to host the Fifa football world
championship in 2010.
SA is bidding against four north African countries for hosting rights,
and several members of football's governing body attended the celebration,
including Fifa president Sepp Blatter of Switzerland.
The crowds began gathering as early as 5.30am, arriving in buses from as
far afield as Mpumalanga and North West. Families had to walk at least a
kilometre from designated parking areas around the city to the Union
Buildings' lawns where huge screens were set up on the state of the art
stage for artists and musicians to perform after the official swearing-in
ceremony.
After the president was sworn in, a Cheetah fighter jet in the colours
of the national flag roared overhead and a 21-gun salute was sounded.
The president reviewed a military parade, which involved about 4000
members from six defence force units.
But it was the arrival of African leaders that really set the crowd
alight. At least 23 African heads of state, three heads of government and 14
ministerial delegations represented the continent. Among them was Botswana
President Festus Mogae, Democratic Republic of Congo President Joseph
Kabila, President Sam Nujoma of Namibia as well as President Olusegun
Obasanjo of Nigeria.
The second-largest delegation came from Europe and the US with 11 heads
of state, three heads of government and 27 ministerial delegations. Most
countries outside Africa sent lower-level delegations.
Alphonso Jackson, secretary of housing and urban development, led a
delegation from the US, while the UK sent Deputy Prime Minister John
Prescott.
SA's key trading partners sent high-level delegations, with Brazil
represented by President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and India by
Vice-President Bhairon Singh Shekhawat. Vice-presidents from China and Iran
were also present. Together, these continents had four state leaders and a
total of 12 ministerial delegations. A special envoy, Archbishop Edmond
Farhat, represented the Holy See.
Mbeki also recognised the African Union's commission chairman, Alpha
Oumar Konare, who sat near Commonwealth Secretary General, Don McKinnon and
Pan African Parliament President Gertrude Mongella. Also present was FW De
Klerk, SA's last white president, who shared a Nobel Peace prize with former
president Nelson Mandela.
After the swearing-in of the president it was time for the crowds to
dance to music from South African icons such as Hugh Masekela, Jonas Gwangwa
and veteran Letta Mbuli.
There were mountains of food with local chicken franchise KFC
distributing 40000 food parcels and Coca-Cola providing free drinks.
Guests were treated to South African dishes, including umngqushu (samp
and beans) and sweet potatoes, leg of lamb and mogodu (tripe).
There were 60 tents to cater for, among others, police, paramedics,
media, vendors, VIPs, artists and technicians. With AP and the Financial
Times.

Original_Search_Title: From School Dropouts to Graduates, Everybody's Becoming a Trader
Headline: Kenya: From School Dropouts to Graduates, Everybody's Becoming a Trader
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  Kampala's enterprising men and women - and children too - in the
informal sector sell almost everything under the sun. special Correspondent
LILIAN NSUBUGA reports.
They are often semi-literate, ignorant, desperate and in financial
problems. Their incomes fluctuate between $10 and $50 a month. They are
barely able to make ends meet and are so ignorant of their rights that they
always prefer to bribe their way out of trouble with government agents.
Despite being pivotal to the country's economic performance, informal
sector workers rarely attract much attention from the public and policy
makers. To the casual observer, they are simply part of the general street
background. They easily blend in with the buildings, roads, cars and human
traffic.
Uganda's informal sector workers engage in all sorts of
businesses. These enterprising men and women - and children too - sell
almost everything under the sun, from second-hand undergarments to luxury
goods made in China, Taiwan and India. They peddle scratch cards at street
corners, sell tiny portions of fruit salad in plastic containers to civil
servants and rent shelves in Indian-owned shops to sell merchandise from
Dubai.
Children pace up and down dusty streets, weighing scales in hand, hoping
for some weight-conscious women to give them business, while others have
taken to displaying their drawings and paintings at street corners. And of
course there are those courageous women who daily brave the sun under tiny
umbrellas to sell newspapers, sweets and envelopes.
University graduates are joining the informal sector in increasing
numbers as job opportunities become scarce and the cost of living shoots
up. The drastic fall in investments by foreign and local investors, partly
due to the rebel insurgency in the north and widespread poverty, the radical
downsizing of the civil service during the 1990s and a generally poor
business climate in the country, have all conspired to force Uganda's
graduates into the informal sector.
Civil servants and politicians have also joined the bandwagon, mostly
bringing in merchandise from Asia and Europe such as clothes, shoes and
jewellery and getting their spouses or children to take care of the
shops.
But is Uganda's informal sector sinking or thriving? The economic
situation in Uganda has worsened, with growth falling by almost two per cent
in 2002-2003, from 5.6 to 4 per cent.
Paul Wasswa, a metal workshop owner in Kalerwe near Kampala, says his
business is thriving because he does not pay rent or taxes. "A woman friend
sold to me this little piece of land many years ago, so I built this mud
structure to use as my workshop," Wasswa said. Outside were displayed
kerosene stoves, suitcases and cooking pans. "My neighbours are having a
hard time. Monthly rent is high and they are always being ambushed by
Kampala City Council (KCC) officials, who are almost always after money," he
added.
Ever so suspicious of strangers in case they happen to be tax officials,
informal sector workers are never keen to discuss their businesses. "I don't
have anything to discuss, but my neighbour may have something to say," most
traders on Market Street said evasively.
But a cobbler on William Street confessed that he often plays
cat-and-mouse games with KCC law enforcement officers, who often demand
money and threaten him with imprisonment. "I am always on the lookout for
them; if I am lucky, I hide, but the nature of my work makes quick getaways
nearly impossible," he said.
But rather than calling it quits, workers in the informal sector will
always struggle to keep their businesses going. "We borrow from friends and
colleagues, beg and plead with the landlords not to lock up our business
premises, because this is all we have got," explained a group of
businesspeople who rent space in an Indian's shop on William Street. But
often, he locks them out all the same and no amount of begging can soften
him - not before getting the full rent. On such days, they camp outside the
office or appeal for charity and loans from family and friends in order to
get the shop reopened.
Although statistics are not readily available, the informal sector is
said to be the biggest employer in Uganda in both the urban and rural
areas. Organisations that champion the causes of informal sector workers
have sprung up in recent years, including a new project funded by the
International Labour Organisation (ILO) called Small Enterprise Media in
Africa (Sema).
According to the Sema technical specialist, Gavin Anderson,
informal-sector workers have problems with corruption, are unaware of their
rights and are vulnerable to exploitation. The project is now using radio to
sensitise people in the informal sector about their rights and
responsibilities, and to teach them how to run their businesses more
efficiently and profitably.
For the majority of Ugandans, joining the informal sector is a last
resort, when employment opportunities fail to materialise, prompting
suggestions that the country's economy mostly depends on this disorganised
and haphazard sector. Critics, however, blame it for the rampant
rural-to-urban migration, which has left many rural areas without their
youth and contributed to the fall in food production over the years.
Many of the people in the informal sector are able-bodied young men who
spend long hours at their businesses waiting for the occasional
customer. Others are taxi operators, touts, handcart pushers or airtime
peddlers. A few lucky ones are operating successfully in the second-hand
clothes business. As they wait for the elusive customers, the young men
often gossip, heckle women and hurl abuses at passersby, including skimpily
dressed women.
Critics say that informal businesses are doing more harm than good
because young men doing this kind of work pretend to be working in the city
when they could in fact engage in more productive work in the villages.
Working in environmentally polluted surroundings including dusty roads,
broken sewers, garbage dumps and dirty streams, people in the informal
sector rarely worry about cleanliness. At the Kalerwe market near Kampala,
women can often be seen sitting next to stench-filled garbage dumps, from
where they sell foodstuffs such as sweet potatoes, onions and potatoes.
It is not only poor people who purchase their requirements from informal
sector workers. A closer look reveals that a good majority of people in
Kampala actually make most of their daily purchases from these people at
bargain prices, which is why their businesses never collapse.

Original_Search_Title: Paraprop Shows It is On the Road to Recovery
Headline: South Africa: Paraprop Shows It is On the Road to Recovery
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — LISTED property loan stock company Paramount Property Fund (Paraprop) is on the road to recovery with a 4% surge in distributions to unitholders for the year to October. The previous year was bad, and saw a significant decline in its distributions.
Paraprop's turnaround in fortunes was also recently reflected by its winning the office property category of the Financial Mail/Investment Property Databank Listed Property Investment Awards.
The awards recognised property funds' return on capital employed, taken over two years, and the consistent top performance of the underlying property assets of listed property funds in the database.
Paraprop reported a total return of 12,6%, which included capital growth and income return, against an average of 7,2% among listed property funds and 6,3% among all funds, both listed and unlisted.
Of interest to investors in Paraprop is the fund's property portfolio growth over the past year. The portfolio has grown to R1,07bn at the year-end from R906m the same time last year and subsequently to more than R1,2bn at the end of last month.
Paraprop has acquired several high-profile commercial properties to the value of R302m in the past year, including the landmark Claremont Pick 'n Pay Centre and the Golden Acre retail component, both in Cape Town.
The fund has also aggressively let vacant space, reducing its vacancy ratio to 4% far lower than the listed property sector average.
Tight management has been an important factor in boosting the fund's future viability. Paraprop's management team is respected by the market and its efforts are showing returns.
Paraprop also has interesting development plans coming on stream in the future.
For instance, it plans to convert the office block, 34 St Georges, Cape Town, into residential apartments. The fund has sold virtually all of them. It will also redevelop the Longkloof Studios in Cape Town.
Paraprop owns the property, as well as the two adjacent properties. It plans to develop a mix of retail and residential properties in this precinct.
Age of Aquarius
IN KEEPING with its attempts to portray itself as an African exchange, it was sorghum beer and not champagne that was used to welcome Australia's Aquarius Platinum onto the JSE Securities Exchange SA yesterday morning. However, the dawning of the JSE's age of Aquarius was more than a fleeting event.
After battling for three-anda-half years against an exchange-control barrier to its listing in SA, the Australian miner was cheered in February when Finance Minister Trevor Manuel relaxed the rules to allow secondary listings of foreign companies on the local bourse.
The very next day, Aquarius CE Stewart Murray marched in to the JSE to start negotiating his Johannesburg listing.
The real significance of yesterday's development is that Murray has acted as the guinea pig, and with the help of lawyers, bankers and, not least, JSE officers themselves he has secured his listing here.
It should, in theory at least, now be easier for others to follow his example.
It is absurd that so many mineral resources in southern Africa are being exploited by Australian or Canadian or UKbased companies, and one outcome of Manuel's regulatory relaxation is that a thriving junior mining sector could be developed here, giving local investors a much better choice of where to put their money, assuming there is an appetite for greater diversity of choice.
There will also be benefits for empowerment. Murray has empowered his company, with Savannah Resources now owning just less than 30% of the local assets. The listing will allow this stake to be traded for an investment in the listed vehicle, which will give Savannah a far more tradeable asset.
This model may well find appeal elsewhere in the resources sector, and beyond.
Now, if the rest of the laagermentality exchange-control regulations that hark back to the apartheid days could be torched in the same way as those that govern stock exchange listings, who knows what further benefits the changes could hold for our economy ?

Original_Search_Title: Gateway of Crime
Headline: Botswana: Gateway of Crime
Body_Text:
Sebina — The once peaceful Central District village of Sebina has undergone a major revolution. After sunset the once sleepy village bursts into life with heavy human movement. There is seemingly no age restriction as the old and the young mix freely.
Being a gateway to several other villages like Nshakazhogwe, Marobela, Tutume, Masunga, Matenge and Makaleng, the two main drinking spots in Sebina - Botshelo and Welcome bar - provide a strategic stopover. But the elders and other residents are worried by what Sebina has become at night. They say the two strategic bars have become a breeding place for "hooliganism and lawlessness".
"Confrontations develop from the drinking holes and are taken to the bushes or homes and anything can happen at night," says Bontshetse Moremi who hails from neighbouring Nshakazhogwe. He cites a recent case where a jealous lover killed his girlfriend before taking his own life. "Something ought to be done," he asserts and dismisses claims that the drinking spots in Sebina fuel crime. "We are a nation of copycats. Some of these things are imported by the youths from urban centres and major villages and are slowly catching up with us to the extent that it threatens stability in our village". Moremi is worried by what he describes as the decadence of the moral fabric of society.
"Sebina has been known as a peaceful society and these things are slowly eroding that image". Drinks or no drinks, Moremi reiterates that the problem will persist. "It is the attitude of the villagers that needs to be worked on."
The chief, Kgakanyane Sebina concurs that there is a problem in his village. "Petty crime is prevalent and our children literally don't sleep. The worst thing is that they fight, especially after drinking." Cases of house breaking and burglary are common. Offenders are caned, counselled and punished but the crime goes on unabated. "Come at around 9 pm to this once quiet and peaceful village and see for yourselves," he declares. Drunken youth roam the streets at night and they are relatively unfriendly, he says. "They fight each other and generally misbehave. If we don't take control of the situation we fear that the worst will befall our village." He says he has attempted several times to call Kgotla meetings to address the problem without success. "We can't win this problem without reaching out to villages around Sebina. Unfortunately, we don't have an official motor vehicle to fulfil our mission," he lamented.
Octogenarian, Tsenang Sebina, says that the Sebina of today is diametrically different from the Sebina of the past. "We used to plough and live happily without all these hassles and our children grew up listening to their parents," he asserts. He says it is not a surprise HIV/AIDS is ravaging the country. "What else do these young people do in the night besides mischief?". The rate of car accidents and suicides are alarming, he says. He believes that parents are doing everything possible to guide their children, but the children prefer to ignore the guidance. "If children can kill and hang themselves over petty things, then it is a serious cause for concern."
Sebina local police station commander, Sub-Inspector Bajaki Thebe Matsetse agrees that there is a problem in the village. "Weekends in particular never have happy endings. These young people fight and injure each other in the night over petty things". It is common for young women to fight over lovers. Young men also give their lovers a thorough hiding and these cases reach the local police in the end. Matsetse says there was a disturbing trend of young local women fighting over Zimbabwean men who in most cases are illegal aliens. "A loser will come straight to us to report that there was someone harbouring an illegal alien only to discover that the man was previously hers." He was concerned that women register cases of assault only to withdraw them.
"Burglary and theft are the most common offences," explains Matsetse.
At the Beer garden near Botshelo complex, Ronald and Boitshepho relax on metal stools and enjoy the sorghum beer - Chibuku. They do not buy the story that Sebina is no longer safe. "I drink, enjoy myself and then go home," says Boitshepho. He has been mugged only once when he was heading home after a drinking spree. "One swallow does not make a summer. I can't conclude that the village is not peaceful based on a single issue," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Marketing Helps SABMiller Sell 5% More Beer Globally
Headline: South Africa: Marketing Helps SABMiller Sell 5% More Beer Globally
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Global brewer SABMiller plc's investments in marketing and distributing
its brands and in brewery infrastructure helped deliver 5% organic growth in
global beer volumes in the six months to September.
CEO Graham Mackay said yesterday the momentum in all its markets
suggested the trend would continue.
The group reported a 38% increase in adjusted earnings a share to $0,48
in the six months to September compared with a year before, making for an
18% increase in local currency to R3,16 a share.
Turnover grew 14% to $7,2bn on which earnings before interest, tax and
amortisation (ebita) were 28% higher at $1,1bn.
SABMiller's global portfolio of brands includes Castle Lager, Miller
Genuine Draft and Miller Lite, Pilsner Urquell and Snow beer in China. The
group's total beverage volume sold in the past six months was 100-million
hectolitres, of which 82-million was lager.
SABMiller acquired Miller Brewing in the US three years ago. The deal,
combined with the strengthening of the rand, knocked the group's share price
down from over R85 in mid-2002 to a low of R45 in March last year as the
market showed scepticism over the company's ability to make headway with
Miller in the highly competitive US beer market. But as Miller has started
to deliver results, the group's shares hit a new peak of R94,50.
Shareholders were also rewarded with a 60% increase in the latest
interim dividend to $0,12 a share from $0,075 last year.
Mackay said this increase partly reflected growth in earnings but also
the intention to increase the interim dividend as a proportion of the
full-year's dividend because the group's performance has become more evenly
balanced throughout the year.
In the US Miller grew domestic shipments to retailers 2,3% and domestic
shipments to wholesalers 2,2% against a total growth of US domestic
shipments of 1% during this period.
Miller Lite experienced doubledigit growth, driven more by marketing and
distribution initiatives than the trend towards lowcarbohydrate beers,
Mackay said.
Management is now turning its attention to key economy brands such as
Miller High Life and Milwaukee's Best.
In SA ebita grew 16% in local currency terms and 35% in dollars to
249m. South African Breweries (SAB) MD Tony van Kralingen said the group
grew its market share in all segments. SAB's share of the total liquor
market grew to 59,8% from 58,3% a year ago as the growth in beer volumes was
underpinned by a switch from sorghum beer to lager.
In Europe SABMiller grew organic lager volumes 4%, and 10% including
acquisitions. A cool summer dampened sales initially but there was an
acceleration of demand in September.
Total lager volumes in Africa grew 5%, with particularly strong sales in
Mozambique and Tanzania.
In China beer volumes grew 10%, partly off the low base established last
year when the severe acute respiratory syndrome epidemic affected
sales.

Original_Search_Title: South Africans Run Amok in Africa
Headline: South Africans Run Amok in Africa
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — SOME members of the intelligentsia like to call it imperialism, but not Philip Ihenacho. The London-based adviser with specialist Nigerian financiers Afrinvest Securities favours more straightforward words when he speaks. Words like arrogance.
Speaking earlier this year at Chatham House, a well-known policy institute based in London, Ihenacho was acquainting his audience with the character of South Africa's bustling trade in West Africa.
Not only were South African businessmen arrogant, he said, they were not dependable and didn't stick to agreements. Now there's a welcome reversal of opinion. Offering first-hand accounts from Nigerian businessmen, Ihenacho presented a rather curious report of how business was being conducted north of Beit Bridge.
"South Africans view me as a Mr Fixit to sort out political issues," complained one Nigerian businessman. Another remarked: "South Africans assume every African can be bought."
Most revealing was the observation that SA businessmen tended to lapse into Afrikaans when in a pinch. I can hear the words perfectly: "Wat maak ons nou manne?"
Ihenacho is not the only person to have publicly remarked on the peculiar character of South Africa's outward-looking boeremag. Just last month Nigerian-born public relations specialist Moky Makura, of FCB Redline, told a journalist about a cheery group of white businessmen she accompanied to a Nigerian pub. The night ended in Afrikaans song.
Doubtlessly the executives were giddy from a hefty intake of "Africa's best-selling beer", as South African Breweries likes to tout its golden lager.
With 13 beer breweries in 10 African states, and another 35 sorghum breweries in five countries, Breweries is not immune to criticism. Earlier this year, a Zimbabwean economist expressed concern over the monopolistic role Breweries has played in Africa. One cannot but agree with him if it is the golden stuff that is inducing heartfelt refrains of Sarie Marais in Lagos pubs.
It is, of course, easy game to make sport of whitey running amok in Africa. It is also nothing new. Far newer as a phenomenon is the ambitious expansion of black South African capitalists into Africa.
Among the companies heading across the Limpopo is Tokyo Sexwale's Mvelaphanda Holdings, which is aggressively pursuing a vision of becoming a pan-African resources company.
One of the reasons for this headlong thrust into Africa is the attractive rate of return offered to investors. According to the HSRC's Sanusha Naidu and Jessica Lutchman, investors are generally seeing rates of return between 30% and 60%, compared with 16% to 20% locally.
Which makes it fair to say that the arrogance so much a part of our export culture is doubtless tempered by a judicious amount of greed.
Not that Naseem Davids, Vodacom's head of mergers and acquisitions puts it quite that way. "Vodacom sees its approach as Afrocentric."
He made the remark at the end of March, a mere two months before Vodacom's Alan Knott-Craig publicly grumbled: "The recent events involving Vodacom in Nigeria have to do with good corporate governance and trust, and nothing else." I reiterate, nothing else.

Original_Search_Title: Future of Industrialisation in 3rd Term, Says Bukenya
Headline: Uganda: Future of Industrialisation in 3rd Term, Says Bukenya
Body_Text:
Kampala — The future of industrialisation is in the third term, the Vice President Prof. Gilbert Bukenya has said. He said there was no way development could take place if the country is not politically stable. He said the five-year-term for a president is useless because no government could accomplish its development programmes in such a short time.
"The three-finger sign means the future. The girls can also see the future. Industrialisation is President Yoweri Museveni. Uganda needs leadership, which could propel the country into industrialisation. This needs long term planning," Bukenya said while touring the Apparels Tri-Star Factory at Bugoloobi on Friday.
He said when the government appeals to the public for political stability, they think they are bootlicking, yet in actual sense they are telling the future.
He said Ugandans should be sensitised to support industrialisation.
"The challenge is now upon Ugandans to support and attract investors because they provide employment to us. Even if we don't tax them, they employ the people and help to market our own products. Anyone who employs people is dear to me," said Bukenya.
Bukenya, who recently returned from a Smart Partnership Conference in Malaysia, said he inspired Malaysians to come and invest in Uganda and was happy that three companies responded immediately.
They are IRIS Corporation Berhad, which produces electronic passports, Taglink Group, producers of starch from Cassava and a construction company specializing in building highways.

Original_Search_Title: Kisenyi Women Join Forces to Start Small Businesses
Headline: Uganda: Kisenyi Women Gift Away Poverty
Body_Text:
Kampala — Cathy Namarwa has to escape from work at least twice a day to a nearby shack.
In this shack with no ventilation, Namarwa sits among bricks, wooden planks, sand and broken chairs to breast feed her nine-month old daughter before she returns to work.
The path to her shelter is not just a path, but an experience. A stream of dirty water that collects rubbish along the way leads to her home.
Along this route is a man selling mandazi and fried cassava, a tailor sitting behind a sewing machine, a woman peeling potatoes and a young boy roasting meat. Some young men and little boys sit along the path talking and shouting at each other and passers by.
Namarwa, 25, has sunken eyes, and speaks in a calm and dispassionate manner. She works in a dingy eating place she calls a "hotel" in Makerere, Katanga, one of Kampala's many slums, where she earns sh1,000 a day. Her sister stays home with her four-year and nine-month old children while she works.
"The girls' father abandoned them and cannot give them even a shilling when they are sick," she says.
As the sole breadwinner, she barely meets all their needs. A cup of milk which costs sh250, a quarter a kilo of sugar at sh350 and a small tin (katasa) of charcoal at sh500 costs more than she earns per day. On top of this, she has to pay a monthly rent of sh15,000, and buy food for the four of them daily.
Namarwa is one of the urban women who are very vulnerable to poverty. Most people think the poor urban woman lives her lifestyle.
Nuriat Kawuma lives in Kisenyi, another slum in Kampala. She peels and sells matooke for a living. However, she is not a woman in need. Her hair is relaxed and tinted. Despite the fact that she lives in a shack, much like Namarwa's, she has a more positive outlook to life.
Nuriat and some other women in Kisenyi may not earn very much, but neither are they the image of the poor woman in a slum. They do not expect any help through a government plan and are not waiting for one.
Their hope is in the success of a poverty eradication project run by women in the area. This association of women brings them more than any micro finance scheme or government plan could.
These women get more than goods or money. They are actually empowered by it.
This project called "gift giving" is was started by Florence Kapero, the LC1 information secretary. Women form groups of 10, which meet every other Sunday to exchange gifts. One member is selected from each group to be the mugole (bride) of the day.
The mugole will receive gifts from her group members, spouses and well-wishers when the women meet on Sunday. The mugole also chooses a lady of honour to escort her to receive her gifts.
The mugole sits at the front and acts like an actual bride. The women who bring gifts, dance all the way to her and pose for photographs as they hand over gifts.
The gifts include flasks, lanterns, saucepans or an envelope (with money).
Through this project, they have been able to acquire material resources and tools, which they use to make money.
Later, each member of the group is required to bring sh10,000, which is given to one member who leaves the group but still contributes to it.
Most women have used this money to educate their children and start businesses such as selling saucepans, tomatoes and charcoal.
From the proceeds of their businesses, the women also contribute to others who are still in the group so that they are equally able to fight poverty.
Mwasa Mugenyimubi, the area's general secretary, says the women have now "pulled up a bit. If one was jobless, one can now get employment with accumulated capital."
According to an economic analysis paper on poverty in Uganda, urban poverty in the central region, formerly estimated at 7%, is now approaching zero. 40% of the urban poor are emerging from poverty.
Schemes like this gift-giving scheme may actually be one of the reasons for this decline in urban poverty.
Margaret Zziwa, the Kampala women MP has only praises for the Kisenyi women's project and forsees its success in eradicating poverty among the women. In her opinion, this project is bound to be successful, "if its leadership is upright, if they are consistent with their objectives and if they keep to their constitution."

Original_Search_Title: Reading Obasanjo's Mind
Headline: Nigeria: Reading Obasanjo's Mind
Body_Text:
OBJ critics are disappointed because he has failed to meet
yesterday's ideals ...
JUST like other countries of the world, the aim of Nigeria is to become
a proud member of the developed world. This was the vision of our founding
fathers when they risked their lives, bared their chests and took on the
colonialists for the brighter future of all of us.
That envisaged brighter future is still being expected and not because
we lack resources. Properly harnessed, Nigeria probably has enough human and
material resources to sustain the rest of Africa. But so many years after
Independence, we are yet to get our bearings right. We have enough natural
resources but we have suffered repeated ship-wreck because of our lack of
visionary leaders who can be said to be totally committed to the Nigerian
project.
We are lacking, most of all, leaders who have the courage to make
unpopular but necessary decisions, for the fear of public opinion. They want
to satisfy those who make the most noise, even when such people do not
understand the issues involved. Once you take unpopular decisions, you are
likely to be buffeted. Journalists, especially the least informed among
them, would not mind roasting you with burning indignation. If you are not
really committed to the project, you will likely scamper for cover, lest you
are made object of launch/dinner jollity. But Olusegun Obasanjo is one
Nigerian leader who is resolved to take unpopular decisions he knows would
benefit us in the long run and boldly stand by his decisions. This has led
to so much misunderstanding of his motives, good will and mission. He has
been accused of insensitivity, of arrogance, of being inhuman, of being out
of touch with reality, and, laughable though, of not seeking the good of the
country.
Uncomplimentary comparison
As a result of what people perceive as Obasanjo's problems, some have
quietly started comparing him with General Sani Abacha. But the taciturnity,
coldness, mean aloofness and inexplicable love for apples of Abacha are not
exactly Obasanjo's vices. His critics are disappointed because Obasanjo's
government has failed to meet the ideals of yesterday and its
misunderstanding of the purpose of governance. To these people, good
government is the ability to satisfy the whims of every rascal; it is about
satisfying the immediate needs of the populace, irrespective of what they
would fare in the long run if those needs are satisfied the way they want it
today.
Government is a serious affair, which must not be reduced to a magical
ability to provide, at any moment, the fleeting and fanciful pleasures of
vagrants. Government is about structures, solid structures that can make for
sustainable development. It is a misnomer and, indeed, a wrong notion of
development, to take it to mean the immediate and expedited supply of garri
to a person who wants garri. We should worry about what happens if the garri
is suddenly exhausted. We are therefore to be concerned with how to sustain
the cultivation of cassava, so that the source of garri is not
eliminated. This is what we mean when we talk in terms of sustainable
development.
If you appraise Obasanjo's efforts so far, you will notice that he is
one man who understands this country. He knows the psyche of Nigerians, and
has refused to lose focus of his vision for the country, despite the clamour
of superficial observers, who are not able to read the working of the man's
mind.
On coming to power, Obasanjo's first move was to send an Anti-Corruption
Bill to the National Assembly. He knew that only the death of corruption
will guarantee life and development for the Federal Republic. He also
retired all military officers who had served in one government or the other
and had become something less than professional soldiers. The move was to
protect our nascent democracy in which Obasanjo is just one out of many
variables. It will be naÃ¯ve to have pretended that all was well when it was
clear that having tasted political power, those soldiers would have hovered
around the boundaries of democracy seeking small openings to let them
in.
Anti-democratic forces
There have been cases of anti-democratic forces attempting to rock the
boat of State by acting in ways and manners unbecoming of a people who think
good about the country. Take Sharia case for example, when the mullah of
Zamfara went on rampage, "Baba" was ordered to say one or two things, but he
advisedly appeared unmoved. Behind those occasional taciturnity is a
discerning intelligence that has considered the likely manner any statement
at such critical time would be misconstrued. The man is a lesson on the
efficacy of timely silence. In diplomacy, it is said, silence in its self is
a great statement.
Reading Obasanjo's mind, you are bound to observe the subdued
frustrations of a man who has sworn not to be deterred by the tomfoolery
around him. He can relate to the original vision of what Nigeria was meant
to be and he has refused to accept the caricature of it on display. He has
refused to give up on a project that would drive most people out of their
wits.
Some feel he is brash and aggressive sometimes. There are those who
sense something bothering on the uncouth about the man. The only way to
explain his shortcomings is to say that the man is still human. The problems
of Nigeria are complex and the way things are today, gentlemanly disposition
towards them will certainly lead to no meaningful progress. Baba's perceived
faults are therefore forgivable.

Original_Search_Title: Mirror Mirror On the Wall
Headline: Nigeria: Mirror Mirror On the Wall
Body_Text:
Lagos — ...Who's the fairest of them all?
Butler: Lanre Bayewu
The headline should remind you of the Cinderalla story, which ironically is on the list of box offices now. I have asked myself often that on what basis can a fair assesment be given of eateries as a whole (consisting of all their outlets)? Should experts give a verdict or should it be determined by the patrons who buy what is given them and this make the continued survival of these eateries possible?
Remember that the essence of the Cinderalla story is that if you are beautiful and defined for greatness, no evil guardian can stop you from savouring what is yours. So being the biggest or finest in structure eatery does not necessarily make you the best.
Well, I threw the power to the people with the help of Theresa Moses and let them express what they feel about the top four eateries in the industry, just like the prince expresses his interest for the lady of his choice.
Seyi Brown, Entertainer/T.v Presenter of Style Quest on Silverbird T.V: I'm in Tastee Fried Chicken (TFC) because I love their spiced chicken and rice. Apart from TFC, I also like going to Sweet Sensation because of their doughnuts and Mr Bigg's bacause of their ice cream.
Ikebude Chidiebere, Managing Director Ezeebunafo Links: I'm in Tantalizers because they have equal value for money. Their food is always hot. I do go to Mr Bigg's because of their reputation.
Patrick Chuckwuedo, Filmaker: I'm in Sweet Sensation because of their barbique chicken and also the cakes. I also go to Mr Bigg's because of their fried rice, burger and customer care. I visit tanterlizers because of their chicken and chips as well as the nice ice cream they offer.
Phillips Okpkoro, Filmaker: I'm in Sweet Sensation because I love their Africana section most especially their porrage, vegetable soup and pounded yam. I also go to Mr Bigg's because of their fried rice and chicken, fiesta and pies. Tantalizers is also a favourite because of their chickens, ice cream and the fact that their outlets are venues to catch fun.
Vivian Ogroh, student of Banking & Finance at Lagos State University (LASU): I'm in Mr Bigg's because of their ice cream, cakes and chicken. I do go to Tantalizers and TFC because of their spiced chicken and ice cream.
Boniface Ejerenwa, Lawyer Chizbon/Enyioho/Ejerenwa & Co.: I'm in Mr Bigg's because of its clean environment and promt service . The food offered is delicious. Sweet Sensation and Tanterlizers are also my choice.
Voke Oviri, staff of OM limited in Kirikiri Industrial Estate Apapa: I'm in Tantalizers because of its proximity and the fact that their food is nicely prepared. I do go to Bigg's because I like their pastries and salad. TFC is also my choice when it comes to picking other eateries.
Amy Oziri, student at Columbia International College Canada: I'm in Mr Bigg's because of its proximity. I love their meals and the service provided. I do go to Tantalizers because of the clean environment and nicely prepared meals. TFC is another great spot of mine because of their spiced chickens.
Chukwudi Ugoh, an intern at Intercontinental Bank: I'm in TFC because of my love for their chicken while the rice served is also nice. I like Sweet Sensation, Mr Bigg's and Tantalizers because of their clean environment, promt service and nicely prepared meals.
Mayo Johnson, fashion designer: I'm in TFC simply because of my love for their chicken, the taste is so wonderful. Mr Bigg's, Tantalizers and Sweet Sensation are places I go to because I like their services and environment.
Olumide Ayodele, Businessman trading in electronics: I'm in Mr Bigg's because I'm used to eating their food after a very long time because it's well prepared. I don't know about TFC, Sweet Sensation or even Tantalizers because I do not patronise them.
Hitler Ojegba, Businessman trading in provisions at Iyana Ipaja market: I'm in love with Tantalizers because its everywhere. I love the food being served most especially their pies and chicken. I visit Mr Bigg's(though not regularly) because I believe they are the best fast food eatery. I see Sweet Sensation and Tanterlizers as a place for fun and relaxation.
Ngozi Ikenna, Housewife: My husband brings me to Sweet Sensation because of their pastries particularly their cakes and doughnuts. I've not been to Tantalizers and TFC but definitely Mr Bigg's because my kids love its surrounding, rice and ice cream served and the friendlship the workers extend to them. I'm so much in love with Sweet Sensation's pastries that I find it hard to switch to other eateries.
Stephen Alexander, Communications expert: TFC is my choice because I love the meals, environment and promptness in service. I visit Bigg's because of its burgers and fiesta while I go to Tantalizers because its a place to catch some fun.
Diamond Davidson, trades in Glomobile lines: Tantalizers is my choice because of their rice, pies and ice cream. Bigg's has a reputation I cannot ignore with a special way of calling you back whenever you visit. Sweet Sensation and TFC may also be nice places for eating.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Traditional Rulers' Council Chairman Defends Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Traditional Rulers' Council Chairman Defends Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Awka — CHAIRMAN of Anambra State Council of Traditional Rulers, Igwe Alex Nwokedi has defended the economic polices of President Olusegun Obasanjo, saying that he (Obassanjo) is not insensitive to the plight of Nigerians. Nwokedi who spoke on the last day of the 4-day warning strike called by the organised labour to protest the recent increase in the pump prices of petroleum products however, called for a closer tie between labour and the government as a way of resolving their economic differences. Nwokedi also called on the National Assembly to amend the Joint Account system which handed over the funds of local government councils to the state so that funds for local government councils should go direct to them, saying "we can hold them responsible for non-performance".
Nwokedi, who is the traditional ruler of Achalla, in Awka North Local Government Area of Anambra State however took to task Minister of Works, Chief Adeseye Ogunlewe for what he described as "his inconsistent statements on the Onitsha-Owerri road project." Nwokedi said that the contractors handling the road project which he has interest in has not received 25% of the contract sum which is N8 billion after three years and wondered why the minister has been molesting the contractors. Igwe Nwokedi who spoke to journalists on the occassion of his 2004 New Yam Festival (Iwa Ji) expressed worry over the condition of state roads in Anambra State.
The traditional ruler who appreciated the sufferings of Nigerians however insisted that the President was working hard to assuage the effect ofs government's economic reforms. "It is not right to say that the President is not sensitive to the sufferings of the people. Before he came in, we had no economy, everything was in ruins. The only thing I suggest is that there should be a closer understanding between the government and the people. "All the interest groups who said that they were mobilising have their own agenda. (Alhaji) Balarabe Musa, Gani. Fawehinmi and labour have their differnt objectives. Some of them said Obasanjo must go, but that is not the issue. Obasanjo will never cheat this country, he will never take their money," Nwokedi said.
On the controversial N24 billion Onitsha-Owerri road, awarded to a Greek firm, Consolidated Construction Company (CCC) Ltd. he said the company had not failed as the Government has not properly fulfilled its own contractual obligation. "All these compaign against CCC is not good. CCC is an international company. They have done jobs in America and other countries.
The way the minister runs down the company is not good. "CCC has written the Government to ask that it be allowed to use its own money to do the work but it has not got a reply yet. After going with the minister on inspection tour to Owerri, he (Ogunlewe) promised to give the compnay N600 million the following week, but till now nothing has happened. Let him be consistent. "He is always talking of N6 billion.
The mobilisation for the contract should be N8 billion, yet he has not met the target. The company should be allowed to do its work unmolested. The thing is getting on my nerves. What we expect from CCC is that government will do its own part and the company will do its own. "This is a road project that should be built in three years at a rate of N8 billion per year. It is now three years and only N6 billion was given and people are being misled. They have not even fully given the first instalmental payment of N8 billion," he said.
The Anambra State traditional council chairman said the state roads were in terrible conditions, and called for the return of Local Government funds directly to the council coffers as against the present situation where it goes to the state government coffers. "Those who have their roads done should come out and tell us so that we rejoice with them. You passed through Achalla road, is it done. I live in Achalla, the roads have not been done, the Local Government has not done anything. "A labourer is worthy of his wage. As a catholic, to deny a labourer his wages is a sin. Paying workers their salary is not an achievement. The Joint Account System should be amended by the National Assembly to give Local Government Councils their own funds so that we can hold them responsible for non-performance", the Monarch said.

Original_Search_Title: Speakers Seek Return of Ngige's Aides
Headline: Nigeria: Speakers Seek Return of Ngige's Aides
Body_Text:
Owerri — Conference of Speakers of South-east states Houses of Assembly rose from her meeting in Owerri, Imo State last Saturday, with a call on the Federal Government to restore without further delay, the full security details of Governor Chris Ngige of Anambra State.
Ngige's security was withdrawn following an order by the Enugu High Court early this year.
The conference took the decision in an eight-point communiquÂ issued at the end of its four-hour closed door meeting at the Concorde Hotel, Owerri, insisting that absence of the governor's security apparatus had exposed him to a lot of danger and violated his rights to full security as a serving governor.
In the communiquÂ read by Imo State Assembly speaker, Mr. Kelechi Nwagwu, it considered in full details the Igbo presidency project and therefore urged the people of South-east both at home and in diaspora to unite towards full realisation of the laudable project.
They hoped that the emergence of a Nigerian president of Igbo extraction would remove all vestiges of marginalisation against the Igbos, as currently observed in the country's body polity .
They viewed as imperative, the need to revive and uphold the Igbo cultural heritage and consequently called on the governors of South-east zone to as a matter of expediency, revive any aspect of Igbo culture that fostered unity among Igbo states, especially the New Yam festival.

Original_Search_Title: Pay Traditional Rulers, Egwu Orders Chairmen
Headline: Nigeria: Pay Traditional Rulers, Egwu Orders Chairmen
Body_Text:
Abakaliki, Ebonyi — Ebonyi State Governor, Dr Sam Egwu, has ordered local government
chairmen in the state to pay traditional rulers their entitlements.
Egwu, who described the natural rulers as custodians of people's culture
gave the directive when he attended the new yam festival of Ezze Ezekuna,
clan the largest clan in the state, at Onueke, Ebonyi Central Senatorial
District yesterday.
He described culture as a very important aspect of life in the overall
development of the people, advising that all good aspects of the people's
culture be preserved and utilised to the service of the society.
He noted that it was this important aspect of culture that prompted his
administration to create a separate Ministry of Culture.
He said the new yam festival should be preserved and promoted,
describing Ezza as the ancestral father of Abakaliki people, adding that as
the great grand son of Ezza Ezekuna, he would build a befitting house at the
compound of the founding father of the clan so that leaders of Ezza would be
holding meetings there.
Egwu described the people of Ezza Clan as useful allies of his
administration, who have proved themselves very dependable at challenging
moments.
He promised to contribute to the development of Onueke town,
headquarters of Ezza Ezekuna Clan, and said his administration recognises
Onu-eke as urban community.
Earlier, Leader of Ebonyi State House of Assembly, Hon. Tobias Okwuru,
who spoke on behalf of the Clan, said Egwu's administration has brought
development to Ebonyi beyond expectation and expressed appreciation to his
efforts, commending his administration's people-oriented programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Orunmila, Odia and the NLNG Prizes
Headline: Nigeria: Orunmila, Odia and the NLNG Prizes
Body_Text:
Controversy — What about Chike Obi? And younger presences like Shittu Hammed, Funsho Aweda, Dupe Omueti, Elimma Ezeani? Except Odia wants to join voice with Roland Barthes, the French structuralist, in proclaiming the death of creativity in Nigeria with the clause of foreign intervention! Only a fool, a Russian proverb states, would pass through a forest and say there is no firewood. It is 9ija-pessimism and also an outline of intellectual rubbish that somebody in this country would say I cannot be innovative in here except... Odia's essay is a good ambassador of this.
Today, Emegwali's use of assembly coded routines in massive parallel computing has made it possible to generate X-rays of subsurface formations of impervious layers of soil that contains hydrocarbons thereby making it possible to recover more oil. Its fingerprints are also in aerospace technology, weather predictions and it has travelled all over the world when hardware manufacturers like Cray, IBM, Linux Network bought the research after it was patented. Let me tell you that the ability to mass-convert the innovative outputs to large-scale commercial use maybe lacking in Nigeria due to lack of technological wherewithal but that does not mean, the deep thought, the innovative flash, the paper work, the prototype cannot come from a Nigerian in Nigeria. And this is what the idea of the prizes wants to work on. This distinction, I reckon, is what Odia, a black-belter in verbosity, conflates and builds the first part of his disquisition on with the vigour of a just-turned end time propagandist who has just received the vision of hell and the illusory 666. Let us also be remained that the yam pounder was invented in Nigeria but taken to Japan for large-scale production. Zobo, a Nigerian drink has been taken to Germany to be exported in large quantities to countries all over the world including Nigeria.
Furthermore, besides the fact that 'foreign Nigerians' will remote control the prize through the politics of yahoo (tendency of those abroad to exult themselves in a triumphalisitc manner over those at home whom they put under their pyramid of power), that Nigeria (both with the educational institutions and outside of them) is not of technological splendour is enough reason to discourage the prize from casting wide to embrace inputs of 'foreign Nigerians' if it wants to abide by the philosophy of a prize as a stimulant of tremendous ferment of enthusiasm and outburst of creativity. However, it is left for the judges not to submit to easy standards because that is the sole determinant of the prizes' stature. Note: I am not articulating justifications for the status quo. And I am not saying that the extant situation of rottenness is not affecting scholarship, It is really limiting the diverse directions creativity can take, not that it has annulled it's right to life and it's right to flourish in Nigeria. It is this that the idea of the prizes wants to work on.
The concept of producing NLNG laureates is therefore an act of self-defence for Nigerian education that has turned into a valley of tears courtesy of the government and all of us. At the same time, it administers a stern rebuke on that government and 9aija-pessimists: those who believe that all the minds and muscles of our race are offshore hence the rest is all decay. It is finally, the first chapter of the success story in the battle to call our soul our own. Yes those who cannot create their own system, Lord Orunmila admonishes, must be prepared to be enslaved by the system of others.
Technologically no country can boast of absolutely self-sufficiency. Artistically, none epitomises the richness of creativity. Omode gbon agba gbon lafi da le Ife. (Nobody has a monopoly of wisdom) It is the Other that does the boasting, which often commences on the foundation of fallacy of hasty generalisation and unwarranted assumption. I dare ask: at what level of scientific or technological know-how/ignorance must a country reach before such a country can institute a science prize for itself and not be guilty of Odia's mythical fear: lower order of excellence, valorisation of disabilities, competiphobia? The way Japanese technology has overtaken western (an absurd term in this regards, hence) British, German, American technology is breadth taking. I dare ask again: should we then say The Rumford Prize of the American Academy of Sciences is less worth it because it glorifies unsofisitcation and avoids competition because it bars nationals of a superior technology? Does Odia know that Stephen Hawking, a victim of the progressive neurological disease: amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, yet a genius of The Brief History Of Time and Hawking Radiation of the cosmic blackhole who took a magnificent step further in unifying the quantum world of electromagnetism and gravitational field in a single mathematical formulation, is being flown out to Switzerland from Britain for quality medical attention? And that patients from America are flown to Japan for treatment? Yet too many Nigerians see places like Britain, America representing absolute competence. Over to you Orunmila:
Ose Itura says what is truth? I ask what is truth? Orunmila answers:
Truth is the Unseen One guiding the earth.
Truth is the wisdom of Lord of Heaven guiding the earth.
Truth is the character of Olodumare.
Ose Itura says what is truth? I ask what is truth? Orunmila answers:
Ifa is truth
Truth is the word that cannot fall, the word that cannot spoil
Might surpassing all
Speak the truth tell the fact. Tell the fact speak the truth.
Those who speak the truth are those whom the gods will bless
(Ose Otura)
The Nigerian NLNG prizes with its incumbent conditions would give Nigerian creativity a home and a hope; a hope that is home and a home that is hope. However the logic of reason coincided with the logic of Odia and with the logic of the inner workings of my mind when he categorically affirmed: the NLNG prizes must be endowed. And indeed they must be endowed.
Damola Awoyokun is a veteran winner of National and State JETS Prizes in Physics, Chemistry and Mathematics. He is also a graduate of Civil Engineering in OAU, ILE-IFE.

Original_Search_Title: When Arebamen Was Honoured By His People
Headline: Nigeria: When Arebamen Was Honoured By His People
Body_Text:
Lagos — For Mr. Young Arebamen, Commissioner of Police, Lagos State, Saturday,
January 17, 2004, was one he would not forget in hurry.
for the officer, who is at the peak of his career, as he edges closer to
bowing out of the Force later this year after the maximum 35-year of
service, nothing could be more dignifying as the party thrown in his honour
pen ultimate Saturday in Lagos, by the Esan people.
Organised by Esan Social Elites, an umbrella of Esans of prominence, as
the name suggests, Arebamen, a patron of the association, and the day's
chief guest was cheerful and at its best.
Nothing best suggests that the gathering was one of Esan's eminence and
their friends as the presense of such prominent Esans as erstwhile Military
Vice-President, Admiral Augustus Aikhomu, and his wife, Rebecca.
By 12 pm, guests have started trooping into the Police Officer's Mess,
GRA, Ikeja, Lagos, venue of the occasion scheduled for 2pm.
When it eventually began, it showcased the best of Esan cultural
heritage and values as guests were treated to various cultural dances,
including Asonogun Dance and light Esan music.
The Police Band also did not disappoint the large gahtering as they
rendered their music all through in flawless Esan language which the guest
of honour and Chairman of the occasion, Aikhomu, nodded to in approval all
through. And when it was time to dance, the Aikhomus showed that they were
true Ishans. So was Arebamen who had a white lace and Yoruba cap on.
The highlight of the ceremony was the presentation of the popular Esan
acrobatic dance. After a sumptuous meal of pounded yam, fried rice and
assorted drinks, guests climbed down from the two-storey building hall to
watch the delightful accrobatic displays presented by a specialised Esan
group.
The guest speaker, an industrialist and Ex-chairman, NAL Merchant Bank,
Mr. Ben Ehizuelen followed that up with a lecture on "marriage in
Esanland".
"Marriage decision until 1948 was taken by parents for their children",
he said, while giving the evolution of marriage in Esanland and the impact
of western culture on it today.
Marriage decision, he said, is taken by children concerned today. He
identified four types of marriages that were in practice and were still
being practiced in Esanland till date.
They include marriage to a divorcee, marriage without betrothing, and
marriage in the event of death of any partner, and re-marriage.
"We are now in an era of influx of foreign cultures. Anybody does what
suits him/her", he said, concluding that it was necessarythat people
preserve their cultural heritage, and improve on them when there is need for
it.
The Chairman of the occasion, Vice-Admiral Augustus Aikhomu, who
extolled the virtues of Arebamen, called for unity of purpose among Esan
people.
Aikhomu spoke of his first contact with the Esan Social Elite, which he
said began during a social function with a friend. Since then, he said, he
has been reaching out to the club.
"Today, we are going through stress in the history of this nation. Esan
people are going through political, socio-cultural stress too. But a major
problem is lack of unity of purpose among our people", he declared.
He praised the efforts of the Esan Social Elite.saying, "I am happy that
this club is helping in this regard. Don't be weary. Don't look back. Do not
look at the immediate reward", he further advised the group.
He went on to extol Arebamen's qualities.
"His humility is second to none. It is a big fish for him to be your
patron", he told members of the club, charging them to continue in their
service to humanity. He pledged to encourge the club members in areas where
they could see and learn new things to meet with greater challenges of human
endeavour.
On his part, Arebamen, who expressed gratitude and appreciation to the
club corroborated what Aikhomu's position, charging: "Let Esan people
unite. If we have unity, we will fare better. We have to develop Esanland
ourselves through unity of purpose and by pulling resources together,
"I am not comfortable with the multiplicity of Esan clubs", He
advised. that Esan people can vigorously pursue the development of Esan
land.
"The survival of Esan people is in your hands. Pull your resources
together and achieve just that", he further urged them..
The Secretary General of the Club, Dr. Ehichioya Oni spoke on the aims
and objectives of the club to include unity, cooperation and harmony among
the Esan people stating that the club was determined to bring all the Esan
people in Lagos together for the development of their father land. The Club
which was established in 1986, he said, was open to all Esan people in
diaspora.
Since its establishment, he said, the club has been able to reach out to
needing members, assist on special occasions as weddings, burials, naming
ceremonies and inaddition take developmental projects to villages.
Major projects so far carried out in the rural areas by the club, he
said, include a community health centre at Amendokhian, Uromi, Esan
North-East Local Government Area, medical equipment and drugs for community
health centre, Ewatto, Esan South, among others.
He expressed the desire of the group to reach out to all communities in
all the local government areas in Esan land, stressing however that the
group lack adequate finance to execute all proposed projects.
He called on all Esan sons and daughters living in diaspora to come to
their assistance in the bid to make their fatherland a better place to live
in.
"You see in all the five local government areas that made up the Esan
land, there is shortage of water supply, lack of good roads, no quality
healthcare delivery system, dilapidated primary and post primary schools,
among others. We call on our highly placed Esan people to assist us to carry
out all these projects. Esan land will be better for it. We will be proud
when we travel home", he further stressed.

Original_Search_Title: Zanzibar's Sauti Za Busara Festival: Part 1
Headline: Tanzania: Zanzibar's Sauti Za Busara Festival: Part 1
Body_Text:
Zanzibar — Zanzibar, a pair of islands just off the Indian Ocean coast of Tanzania,
is best known for slave-trading sultans, exotic spices, beautiful tropical
beaches and high living. It became part of Tanzania officially only in 1964,
but the marriage has remained uneasy, and political tensions there made it a
risky destination for all but the most adventurous travelers until quite
recently.
These days, Zanzibar is coming into its own, hosting an annual
international film festival (ZIFF), another festival devoted to the cultures
of the Dhow countries--so called because of their affinity with the elegant,
single-sail boats that grace these waters--and now, a brand new music
festival dedicated to presenting the performance arts of Swahili-speaking
Africa. Sauti Za Busara--Swahili for "sounds of wisdom"--launched this year
with three afternoons and nights of diverse music in one of the most
enchanting settings imaginable for a festival, the Forodhani Gardens of the
Zanzibari capital, Stonetown.  Afropop Worldwide has developed a taste for
music festivals held in Africa. They provide atmosphere unmatched by any
European or North American festival, and also access to musicians and groups
that will likely never make it to the international tour circuit. Our
experience at the 2003 Festival in the Desert in Essakane, Mali, delivered
all that and much more. This year, we forwent the unique pleasures of
musical nights in the Sahara for what might be glibly called The Festival at
the Beach. This is the first of our reports on what we saw and heard at the
inaugural Sauti Za Busara festival.
To begin with, you don't have to ride on camels or spend long days in
dusty four-wheel-drive trucks barreling across sand and savannah to reach
Stonetown. A 20-minute hop in a ten-seater airplane from Dar Es Salaam, or a
1 Â½ hour ride on a comfortably air-conditioned power boat, and you're
there. You check into a cozy hotel, such as the elegant, seaside Tembo where
we stayed, and it's an easy walk to the beach, the festival site, or
gorgeous, narrow streets lined with stone buildings and filled with friendly
people, rich history, and fabulous, affordable restaurants.
Cheapest and most atmospheric of all is the Forodhani Gardens site,
right at the water's edge. Every night, food vendors set up their sugar cane
presses, charcoal pits, and grills to prepare an amazing array of
foods--marinated fish kebabs, savory Zanzibar pizzas, and fresh grilled
squid, prawns and octopus, all served with delicious tamarind sauce and
other garnishes, on fried bread, rice or potatoes--as the Rough Guide to
Tanzania puts it, five-star hotel fare at fast food prices. Festival
visitors might have had the impression that this fabulous gastronomic spread
was part of the event, but on the nights before and after Sauti Za Busara,
the gourmet extravaganza went on in force. This is a favorite eating spot
for locals, and for obvious reasons.
One thing you did not find on the festival site was alcohol. There were
bars at close proximity, so no one was deprived. Mali's Festival in the
Desert did have an on-site bar--We hear that this year, there were
three--but of course, the nearest real bar or hotel there was 100 km away!
In fact, the air of sobriety at Sauti Za Busara--just one reflection of
