where they were detained in a military prison.  On April 2nd, 2004, Alsadig
Ahamed Harba was reportedly released. The other seven detainees' whereabouts
remain unknown.
It has been reported that the eight detainees were subjected to torture
during their custody in the military camp in Bouram. Military intelligence
officers allegedly tied them to each other with robes and put them into a
lorry for four days without food or enough water.  They were beaten with
sticks and gun butts, punched, kicked and tied by the legs and arms upside
down against a tree for many hours. Mr.  Haroon Basheer reportedly had metal
objects inserted into his rectum.  They were not provided with any food for
three days after their arrival at the military prison in Nyala.
In a separate event, Abdel Kareem Jabir Narow, 48, a merchant living in
Bouram and Abdel Shafi Badawi Basheer, 35, a merchant living in Nyala, both
of whom are Zaghawa tribe members, were arrested by the security forces in
Bouram province on March 29th, 2004. They were suspected of joining the
opposition rebel group and, as with the eight other persons arrested on the
March 19th, 2004, of participating in an attack on Bouram town on March
10th, 2004.
The two men were arrested at Abdel Kareem's shop, and then detained in
the police station in Bouram town without any official charges being brought
against them. During their detention, it has been alleged that they have
been tortured by the police officers in Bouram police station. They were
reportedly beaten on their backs with fists and metal stick, burnt on their
backs, stomachs and faces with hot metal, whipped using a water hose and
kicked all over their bodies.
They are reportedly badly wounded as a result of this treatment and
require medical attention, which the authorities have thus far refused.
In a further separate event, the security forces in Nyala reportedly
arrested Ahmed Abdel Hameed, 38, on 4th May, 2004. Ahmed Abdel Hameed, who
is a member of the Fur tribe, was suspected of joining the Sudan Liberation
Army/ Movement (SLA/M). Mr. Ahmed works for the Faisal Islamic Bank, Nyala
branch. He was arrested at his house in Nyala in the evening and was taken
to the security offices and detained there. He has been denied any visits
from his family.
The International Secretariat of OMCT is gravely concerned for the
personal integrity of the ten persons who remain in detention, notably those
being detained incommunicado, as they run further risks of being subjected
to torture, and those who require medical attention. OMCT condemns the
Sudanese security forces' alleged use of torture and calls upon the
authorities to immediately locate the detainees' whereabouts, provide them
with adequate medical assistance, and release them in the absence of legal
charges that are consistent with international law and standards. OMCT calls
upon the authorities to immediately launch prompt, thorough and impartial
investigations into these events, in order to identify those responsible,
bring them to trial and award adequate reparation to the victims.
Background information
OMCT is seriously concerned about the situation in Darfur, which has
been characterised as being the worst humanitarian situation in the world
Following international pressure and demands for humanitarian access, a
ceasefire agreement was signed on April 8th 2004 in N'Djamena between the
Government of Sudan and the two main rebel groups, the Sudan Liberation
Army/Movement (SLA/M) and the Justice and Equality Movement (JEM). The
ceasefire has been effective since April 12th 2004. The parties had agreed
to cease hostilities within 72 hours, for a renewable period of 45
days. They had also agreed to guarantee safe passage for humanitarian aid to
the region, to free prisoners of war, and to disarm the 'Janjaweed' militias
that have been blamed for much of the "ethnic cleansing" and "atrocities"
against civilians.
Action requested
Please write to the authorities in Sudan urging them to:
i.  take all measures necessary to guarantee the physical and
psychological integrity of the eleven afore-mentioned persons;
ii.  immediately locate the whereabouts of all the persons detained;
iii.  intervene with the proper authorities to ensure that adequate
medical assistance is provided as a matter of urgency to all the detainees
who require it;
iv.  guarantee that the detainees are granted access to legal
representation and family visits;
v.  order the immediate release of the eleven detainees, in the absence
of legal charges that are consistent with international law and standards,
or, if such charges exist, bring them before an impartial and competent
tribunal and guarantee their procedural rights at all times;
vi.  guarantee the respect of human rights and the fundamental freedoms
throughout the country in accordance with national laws and international
human rights standards.
Addresses
Â· His Excellency Lieutenant General Omar Hassan al-Bashir President of
the Republic of Sudan, President' s Palace, PO Box, 281, Khartoum, Sudan ,
Fax: + 24911 783223
Â· Mr Ali Mohamed Osman Yassin, Minister of Justice and, Attorney
General, Ministry of Justice, Khartoum, Sudan , Fax: + 24911 788941
Â· Mr Mustafa Osman Ismail, Minister of Foreign Affairs, Ministry of
Foreign Affairs, PO Box 873, Khartoum, Sudan , Fax: + 24911 779383
Â· Mr Yasir Sid Ahmed, Advisory Council for Human Rights, PO Box 302,
Khartoum, Sudan , Sudan, Fax: + 24911 770883
Â· Mr Osman Mohammad Yusif Kubor, Governor of North Darfur State, c/o
People's Palace, PO Box 281, Khartoum, Sudan, Fax: + 249 11 771 024
Â· Governor of South Darfur, Lieutenant-General Adam Hamid Musa, c/o
People's Palace, PO Box 281, Khartoum, Sudan, Fax: + 249 11 771024
Â· His Excellency Ambassador Mr. Ibrahim Mirghani Ibrahim, Permanent
Mission of the Republic of Sudan to the United Nations in Geneva, PO Box
335, 1211 Geneva, Switzerland, Fax: +4122 731 26 56, , E-mail:
mission.sudan@ties.itu.int.
Please also write to the embassies of Sudan in your respective
country.

Original_Search_Title: Moving of Sudanese Refugees Extremely Challenging
Headline: Chad: Moving of Sudanese Refugees Extremely Challenging
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
Although our team in eastern Chad is continuing to move tens of
thousands of Sudanese refugees from the dangerous border area with Sudan's
Darfur region to safer camps further inland, we are extremely concerned
about the ongoing challenges we and our partners are facing in what is one
of the most difficult humanitarian operations anywhere. Those include a
vast, insecure and remote area of operations, stretching along some 600 kms
of borderland; severe logistical challenges, including transport
difficulties; water shortages for growing camp populations, which also makes
finding new sites extremely difficult; a looming shortage of fuel and
firewood; and last but certainly not least, slow funding. So far, we have
only received about $14 million of the $21 million needed for this urgent
operation. Given the continuing seriousness of the situation in Darfur, it
is certainly possible that we'll have to increase UNHCR's planning figure
for this operation in eastern Chad to more than the current 110,000.
We have now moved some 55,000 Sudanese refugees away from the dangerous
Chad-Sudan border to safer camps further inland, including to a new site
opened late last week. It is the sixth camp we've opened so far in eastern
Chad.  The new camp at Mille has received 724 refugees, including some who
have been staying in the border area around Birak and Tine and refugees who
had travelled on their own to the camp at Kounoungo, which is already at
full capacity.  In all, we have transferred 12,498 refugees to Touloum camp,
12,222 refugees to Iridimi, 724 to Mille, 8,269 to Kounoungo, 10,860 to
Farchana and 10,478 to Goz Amer. In addition, some 10,000 refugees have made
their own way to the camps.
This week, we are distributing food and household items to some 27,000
Sudanese refugees in northeastern Chad.  Some 12,500 refugees have been
registered at Cariari, and 14,448 at Bahai.  Together with our partner CARE,
we will distribute jerry cans, buckets and plastic sheeting to ease somewhat
their difficult and precarious existence in the desert.  We also plan to
distribute 15-day food rations of corn flour, sorghum, corn-soya blend,
beans and oil from the World Food Programme.  This is the second time we
have distributed WFP food to refugees at Bahai.  The first distribution was
on March 20 and 21 to the 5,000 refugees registered at that time, but the
numbers have swelled since a new influx of refugees began to the area in
early April.
In addition, we began yesterday a three-day distribution of food in
Iridimi transit camp that should last the refugees for a month.

Original_Search_Title: 45,000 Sudanese Refugees Moved Away From Insecure Border
Headline: Chad: 45,000 Sudanese Refugees Moved Away From Insecure Border
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
UNHCRÂs emergency re__cpLocation operation in eastern Chad has so far
transported some 45,000 Sudanese refugees away from the insecure Chad-Sudan
border area to safer inland camps. We hope to have moved at least 60,000 by
the start of the rainy season in late May.
We and our Chadian partner, CNAR (Commission nationale pour lÂaccueil
et la reinsertion des Refugies), yesterday began registering newly arrived
refugees in the Bahai area near the Chad-Sudan border. The refugees told
UNHCR that they left their villages early this month after attacks by
Janjaweed militia. News of the 45-day cease-fire that was signed on April 8
prompted some of them to come out of hiding in Darfur and walk to
Chad. Between 200 and 300 people a week have been arriving in the Bahai and
Kariari areas of Chad, both on the northern edge of the 600-km stretch of
affected border. The registration in Bahai is scheduled to finish
today. CNAR teams will then go to Kariari over the weekend to register the
refugees there. Registration is expected to be completed by the beginning of
next week. A distribution of food for an estimated 11,000 refugees will then
take place in Kariari. It will include sorghum, beans, and corn-soya blend,
as well as jerry cans, plastic sheeting and buckets.
The search for a new camp site in the Bahai region is
continuing. Yesterday, a team from Norwegian Church Aid identified a
potential site by the lake in Kariari, 40 kms north of Bahai. If UNHCR and
the local authorities approve this site for re__cpLocation, NCA plans to install
a pump system to draw water from the lake. The water will need to be treated
before consumption.  We are also reinforcing our presence in the region with
the opening of a field office in Bahai city in the next two weeks.
Meanwhile, two more sites -- Am Nabak and Mille -- are scheduled to open
by the end of next week, boosting our total camp capacity by another 12,000.
Some 3,000 tents from our stocks in Pakistan are en route to eastern Chad,
where weÂve already erected 11,000 in various camps.

Original_Search_Title: Aid Plea for Genocide Rape Survivors
Headline: Rwanda: Aid Plea for Genocide Rape Survivors
Body_Text:
Kigali — African Rights, a human rights organisation, has appealed to the
international community to provide medical aid for women who were raped,
widowed and infected with HIV/AIDS during the 1994 genocide in Rwanda.
"Providing effective and appropriate support systems for these survivors
is an essential gesture of respect to people stripped of all else," African
Rights reported on Thursday.
Urging the international community to recognise the "unbearable
suffering" endured by women who were widowed and raped, it said aid should
especially target the rape victims who were infected with HIV/AIDS.
In the report, entitled "Broken bodies, torn spirits, living with
genocide, rape and HIV/AIDS", African Rights details the appalling
humanitarian situation the women are living under, with the majority lacking
basic needs such as shelter, adequate food and medication, while others have
been traumatised.
"While recognising the scale of human suffering across the region, we
find that genocide rape victims in Rwanda lead a uniquely troubled
existence, and many feel their survival is another form of martyrdom," it
reported.
The report, based on a survey of 201 widows, said that during the
genocide militiamen did not spare girls as young as seven years or women up
to 71 years old. While some were pregnant when raped, others became pregnant
as a result, and many of the women were gang-raped, it said.
"I was raped by a lot of people and at different times," one of the
widows was quoted as saying. "I can't think how many times. Each time the
killers found me in the sorghum field or in the house, they did nothing but
rape me."
A decade after the genocide, African Rights said, numerous women still
live under a constant shadow of pain or discomfort which reduces their
capacity to work and provide for their families.
"They are either not receiving treatment or have not been diagnosed and
treated properly. Medical care for genocide rape victims in Rwanda can be
summarised as too little too late," it said.
African Rights reported that whether HIV positive, negative or unsure of
their HIV status, the majority of the Rwandan genocide widows described
similar symptoms: stomach pains, discharge, repeated infections, urinary and
gynaecological complications and skin eruptions.
"Even those who had tested negative for HIV saw the rape as the cause of
their ill health, and struggled on from day to day with little expectation
of recovery," it said.
Africa Rights said often women were aware that they may have become
infected with HIV/AIDS, but hesitated to get tested because of a sense of
powerlessness they felt and the stigma associated with the disease.
African rights said rape victims included Tutsi and Hutu widows, who
were persecuted along with their Tutsi husbands.
"The comments and experiences of most genocide rape victims suggest that
they will never feel comfortable in groups where there is a possibility of
meeting relatives of the men who raped them, or simply of exposing
themselves to a community they believe betrayed them," African Rights
said.

Original_Search_Title: KBL Aims for Regional Market
Headline: Botswana: KBL Aims for Regional Market
Body_Text:
SECHABA Breweries Limited, the parent company of Kgalagadi Breweries Limited and Botswana Breweries Limited said on Wednesday that it is gearing up to push-up clear beer volumes as part of its move of navigating its way into the regional markets.
KBL, which is known for its African award winning beer, St. Louis said its strategic plans will be driven by the in sourcing of raw material, such as, barley, sorghum and efforts to promote the establishment of a can making company in the country.
Lonwabo Mtongana, the in-coming managing director told reporters at the company's premises that he will exalt localisation and the group's corporate image and also explore growth opportunities.
"In my road show, which is to state facts about the company I have already spoken to Ministers and Members of Parliament and I am meeting the press as an important partner," Mtongana said.
He said among the regional markets his company is eyeing is the lucrative Namibian market, but expressed frustration over its prohibitive trade rules.
"We plan to expand our market to Namibia, but we still have to get around the import requirements," Mtongana said.
KBL, which is 60 percent owned by Botswana Development Corporation while the rest is held by the world's third largest brewer and South African company SABMiller Plc is already exporting some of its brands to Mozambique. A limited number of its flagship light beer St. Louis are being exported to South Africa - where its acceptance has being sluggish.
The strategic regional growth is expected to help the company from last year's jaded results largely due to the outbreak of foot and mouth disease and government's decision not to increase civil service salaries.
However, Mtongana was bullish at the press conference about this year's sales and broke the conservative corporate governance's record by being the first chief executive to set target sales for a company listed on Botswana Stock Exchange.
"The annual volumes (for last year) went down by 5 percent last year because of the tough year as sales also went down, however, the company plans to reverse the tide as the company was expected to have made over P775 million last year and projected P880 million in 2004.
"The major challenge this year is to grow volumes and increase revenue and also look into other business opportunities in the country," he said.
The master-brewer whose career in the business spans over a period of 30 years gets to the helm of company whose internal relations were ripped apart by a deluge of racism allegations which promised to push locals to the top.
"We are working on the localisation process and we are reporting to the minister of Labour and Home Affairs in this regard and on when the process would be completed," Mtongana said.
He added; "We want to focus on training of the workforce to improve skills. Last year alone P4 million was spent on training".
The company's management boasts of 2 citizen directors at KBL and 17 citizen managers at one of its holdings BBL.
The company share price jumped 25 thebe to 1100 thebe on the news on Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: 41 Import Prohibition: How to Make It Work, TUC
Headline: Nigeria: 41 Import Prohibition: How to Make It Work, TUC
Body_Text:
TRADE Union Congress of Nigeria (TUC) has advised that a major step to
realising the objectives for the recent ban on importation of 41 items into
the country is for government to make it mandatory for all its agencies and
parastatals to patronise only made-in-Nigeria goods.
The body also expressed misgivings to alleged multiple taxation most
manufacturing companies in the country were saddled with.
A statement by the Secretary General of the organisation, Chief John
Kolawole called on government to do more to police the nation's numerous
porous borders and ensure that corrupt security agents who collude with
smugglers to undermine the nation's economic development are flushed
out.
The statement read: "The TUC supports the recent government ban of the
importation of 41 items which includes wheat flour, sorghum, mosquito
repellant, motor vehicle above eight years, used refrigerator, used
air-conditioners, printed fabrics, tooth pick, drinking water, fruit juice
in retail packs, textile materials, fresh fruits, juices, furniture, plastic
bags, ball point pens, flowers etc. Government all over the world have
responsibility to plan, manage and protect their economies. Any government
that stays away from his traditional responsibility will end up creating
distortion within the system that will lead to macro-economic
instabilities. A cursory look on this list will show that the country is
virtually living on importation and no self-respect nation will operate on
this basis.
"It is fashionable to talk about the world becoming a global village,
especially against the background of World Trade Organisation's (WTO)
arrangements. It will however amount to an act of irresponsibility for a
government not take positive and courageous steps to promote and pocket its
industrial base. There are numerous benefits derivable from this laudable
policy of the government. They include the creation and generation of
employment opportunities for the teeming Nigerian graduates and school
leavers. The ban will also enlarge the industrial base of the country as it
will encourage the local industry to thrive and increase their level of
production which will trigger the generation of more employment
opportunities.
Another benefit derivable from this policy is a reduction in vices and
crimes as a result of the menace of unemployment."
According to TUC, "It will also promote national pride within the comity
of nations as the productive capacity of the nation will be enhanced. It
boosts the value of the local currency viz-a-viz the foreign currencies as
there will be less dependent on importation. Also, the Gross Domestic
Products (GDP) of the country/National Income will increase. In addition,
the ban will generate favourable balance of trade.
"As much as the TUC supports this policy, we believe that government
must go beyond mere pronouncement to demonstrate that it has the will to
carry out the enforcement to a logical conclusion. In this regard, we want
to advise government to focus its attention on the following areas: Proper
policing of the nation's porous borders to ensure that the banned items do
not find their ways back to the market. Government should mount a
well-articulated public enlightenment programme to raise the consciousness
of Nigerians to patronise made in Nigeria goods.
"The government should also demonstrate the strong will for its policies
by ensuring that its agencies patronise made in Nigeria goods. Government
should discourage the incidence of multiple taxation and high cost of
energy, which add to the cost of production. Additionally, the government
should provide enabling environment for local industries to thrive by
guaranteeing improvement in power supply, regular and proper roads
maintenance, provision of adequate security and generally, an improvement on
the cost of doing business in the country.
"To ensure that the new policy of government is carried out to the
desired conclusion, the government should urgently convey a meeting of all
stakeholders, including the TUC. This is considered paramount as the TUC
will not sit-by and watch unscrupulous people thwart government efforts to
protect our industries. The TUC will not hesitate to take an appropriate
Trade union action to ensure the survival of local industries and the jobs
of our members."

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Chad & Zambia
Headline: Chad: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Chad & Zambia
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
Geneva — Briefer: Kris Janowski - Media Relations Topics: 1) - CHAD 2)
- ZAMBIA
This is a summary of what was said by the UNHCR spokesperson at
todayÂs Palais des Nations press briefing in Geneva.  Further information
can be found on the UNHCR website, www.unhcr.ch, which should also be
checked for regular media updates on non-briefing days.
1) - CHAD UNHCR and its partners distributed aid to 6,000 Sudanese
refugees in the Chadian border town of Bahai over the weekend. The refugees
received buckets, jerry cans and soap from UNICEF; blankets and mats from
UNHCR; and a 15-day supply of sorghum, corn-soya blend and oil from the
World Food Programme. The distribution was coordinated by UNHCR and CARE and
followed a registration carried out earlier in the week by our partner, CNAR
(Commission Nationale dÂAccueil et de Reinsertion des Refugies).  The
refugees in Bahai fled their villages in the Kornoi area in western
SudanÂs Darfur region after aerial bombing and attacks by armed militia
in January and February. They have been living in flimsy, makeshift shelters
scattered around Bahai village, where they are exposed to regular sandstorms
and cold nighttime temperatures.  Initially, they survived on meagre food
supplies they brought with them when they fled. Later, when that ran out,
they did small jobs for the local people in Bahai to earn enough to buy
food. The Chadians also shared some of their food with the refugees. UNHCR
is offering to transfer any refugees who wish to move to a new transit
center at Iridimi, where they can get regular assistance.  Over the weekend,
UNHCR also organized the first convoys to the Iridimi transit center from
the border town of Tine.  Refugees who recently arrived by foot to the camp
at Touloum were also transferred to Iridimi. Touloum camp has reached its
capacity in terms of water for the time being.  As of yesterday, 367 people
had been transferred to Iridimi.  In all, nearly 13,000 refugees have been
transferred to four camps and transit centers currently in
operation. Farchana camp hosts 3,271 refugees, Touloum has 5,804, Kounoungo
has 3,517, and Iridimi has 367. A fifth camp should soon begin receiving
refugees from the border zone.  The camp, at Goz Amer, will be the first
opened in the southern part of the affected region in eastern Chad and will
receive refugees currently staying near the border town of Ade.
2) -- ZAMBIA UNHCR has heard reports from the government of Zambia that
some 1,000 Congolese have crossed into Zambia since March 18, reportedly as
a result of fighting involving MaÃ¯-MaÃ¯ rebel elements in southeastern
Democratic Republic of Congo. According to sketchy information, about 300 of
the Congolese have sought shelter on Kilwa Island in Lake Mweru on the
DRC-Zambia border. The other 700 are reportedly in mainland Zambia. In
accordance with normal practice, government security, administrative and
border authorities are presently involved in screening these groups to
determine whether they are genuine asylum seekers. UNHCR Lusaka and our
field staff in Kawambwa are in contact with government counterparts and
stand ready to move those of concern to UNHCR to Kala camp, a site for
Congolese refugees in northern Zambia.
The following link will take you to the above: www.unhcr.ch/briefing-notes.

Original_Search_Title: TUC Lauds FG's Ban On Foreign Goods
Headline: Nigeria: TUC Lauds FG's Ban On Foreign Goods
Body_Text:
Lagos — Trade Union Congress (TUC) has expressed its support on the ban of imported products by the Federal Government.
The union in a statement signed by its General Secretary, Chief John Kolawole said: "The TUC supports the recent government's ban on the importation of 41 items which include wheat flour, sorghum, mosquito repellant, motor vehicle above eight years, used refrigerator, used air-conditioner, printed fabrics, tooth pick, drinking water, fruit juice in retail packs, textiles materials, fresh fruits, furniture, plastic bags, ball point pen, flowers e.t.c."
Kolawole stated that a look at this list shows that Nigeria is dependent on virtually foreign goods, stressing that no nation would survive through this. "Governments all over the world have responsibility to plan, manage and protect their economies. Any government that stays away from his traditional responsibility will end up creating distortion within the system which will lead to macro-economic instabilities," the statement noted.
TUC urged the government to take a bold step to promote the local industries since turning the world into a global village without World Trade Organisation (W.T.O.) arrangement is baseless.

Original_Search_Title: On Museveni's Trail
Headline: Uganda: On Museveni's Trail
Body_Text:
Kampala — Following the massacre of civilians at Barlanyo displaced people's camp in Lira a week ago, President Yoweri Museveni visited the area to assess the situation. He took a number of journalists along. Our own Linda Wamboka Nabusayi was among them and now tells the story
Akwang, Lira-- It was a bit of waiting, action and drama.
A journey that started from Lira hotel's main lobby took us to Otuke county in Lira district, one of the most dreaded jungles that was once a constituency for renown politician Omara Atubo.
Mr Atubo has taken refugee in Cecilia Ogwal's space. I saw him cruising around town looking in all manner like a man without a home.
And while Atubo is more or less an internally displaced person in Ogwal's territory, President Yoweri Museveni is pitching camp in his area.
It is 11.40am. We have been waiting for three hours for Museveni to appear and join the hundreds of people who have been flocking Lira town, following the Saturday 21 massacre at Barlonyo camp.
Then he arrives.
I get engrossed in pondering when Lira last saw this many men in green and the latest state of the art chopper, mambas, military jeeps etc. The crowd of people are curiously looking on as the military might descends on the municipality.
I was only woken up from my mental slumber by the president's deputy press secretary, Mr Onapito Ekomoloit, who was by now telling the journalists to 'behave' because in his words "I have worked so hard to let you be let on the presidents detail".
"We don't have transport but if you have a car, you can come. Please don't ambush the president in the corridor; today he will talk to you. He is still with the BBC journalists," Onapito tells us.
Finally, Museveni steps out at Lira hospital - his first stop. If this many people accompanied the president to hospital every day, so I thought, many patients would have died of suffocation. It was a hassle just to peep through the window.
I almost dislocated my ankle.
In less than 20 minutes, he was out and the LC 5 chairman, Mr Franco Ojur, dressed in full military attire, was available to receive a kakhi envelop from the president. I later learnt it contained Shs 5 million for mabugo (condolence money).
"This is not for the press," Museveni said, but it was too late for the flashes.
It was time for the road, which our speculative minds had guessed was Barlonyo. But the main man, we were told, had made his own programme and no body knew what the day's destination would be. So we just moved on until we came to a place called Oloro.
The convoy ground to a halt at an abandoned Oloro High School. Some stranded villagers waited for luck.
It was a small break for the president to attend to nature's call. Mr Museveni, with escorts scavenging the whole area, lest any bogus rebel was hiding beneath the grass, rounded the buildings to ease himself.
One of his attendants ran towards him with a bucket of water, soap and tissue. After washing his hands, he went under a mango tree and called the villagers over.
"What is the problem, why is the school not working?" he asked.
A group of about 20 villagers chorused in Langi, their words drowning in the process.
"We need an interpreter here," Museveni said. A man stepped forward and volunteered. But Museveni's was already eyeing another man.
"You, what is wrong with your eyes. Don't touch them, it will get worse. Where is my nurse?" he asked.
"Nurse," he called out.
She responded promptly. As she ran towards her boss, the sick-eyed villager looked on. Amazed, he was. Luck had afforded him treatment by His Excellency's own nurse.
"Uuhh! So what is the problem," it was to the villagers he was now speaking to.
Then another chorus roared before the interpreter took over.
"The rebels are disturbing us. The school has closed and we can't get our potatoes from the gardens," he started.Then he went on rumbling.
The group had come from Apala, 25 kilometres away.
"Where is your food, I can carry you on my vehicles," Museveni, sensing the danger of letting the villages walk on without protection said.
"It is still in the garden. They chased us before we could uproot it. We are behaving as if we are stealing from our own gardens," the interpreter said.
"Is there no other relief food aid?" the President asked.
"They have registered us so many times but they never come back with food," the interpreter responded.
"Instead of risking your lives, I am going to stay in Okwang. I will help you to solve this problem. Let me take you back and your food will be delivered there," the president told the residents again.
A moment of silence swept the atmosphere. The only thing, which seemed to talk was hesitation, which was written heavily on the faces of the many men and women.
They understood through the interpreter that the president wanted to take them in his convoy.
"Our sorghum is still here what shall we eat?" they chorused.
"Okay let us go and then I will give you escorts to bring you back. I'm sorry about this problem but it is dangerous to get food without escorts. I will bring you relief and will kill these bandits so that you can go back home," Museveni fought on. It worked; his persuasive skills worked.
The villagers jumped onto the back of an army truck with soldiers. And we moved on again. Thirty minutes later, we stopped at Apala.
President Museveni opened the roof of his cross-country and tried to address the crowd that quickly gathered around him.
"I'm going to camp in Okwang and like I have told your colleagues here, I'm going to make sure this bandit Kony is finished," he said.
The public address system let him down but there was a cheer here and an ululation there. He got in and it was time to set off again.
There were two more convenience stops and this time, the soldiers stormed the bushes to flush out some urine. It was not possible to know the name of this village. Apart from the snaking convoy, there was no life here.
From here on, the speed was slower. We by passed a minesweeper that had just cleared the road we were about to travel through, but it still had to do double-checking.
Thirty minutes later, it was another stop.
I noticed something strange. The minesweeper moved ahead of us but a soldier kept running ahead of it. To date I am haunted by the realisation that I (did not) inquire what the logic of such movement was.
After another 20 minutes, it was time to move again.
We made it to the camp shortly after 4.00pm. After a sumptuous meal of ripe bananas, rice, groundnut sauce and cabbages, it was time to face the president.
"As long as there is any terrorist here, I will stay here even if it means going right up to Sudan itself," he said.
"There is a proverb in Runyankole, which says: When you are cultivating, you pick one line until you finish, before you start on another. I'm not like the Europeans who are running all over the world trying to solve problems."
Then he went on.
"This is a comprehensive security package against the Karimojong and the LRA. This bit here is complicated because of the swamps. It needs more effort than Karamoja," he said.
He looked more relaxed than before.
"This uniform is irresistible. I'm retired but I'm still the commander in chief. You want me to wear a Kanzu to Okwang," Museveni answered an inquisitive BBC stringer, Will Ross.
"This uniform is convenient against mosquitoes and with my hood, I don't get malaria. When I retire I will make some khaki clothes. The uniform is a convenient bush dress against snake bites, etc."
Then Andrew Harding, a BBC television journalist, stormed in.
"Mr President, you can't conduct this interview in this tent. You will turn out all yellow in my pictures. Can you step out and look as if you are talking to your soldiers, so I can take some pictures. You know this is very important for this interview if it is to appear on our news tonight," the journalist said.
"My friend, I think let us just forget that one. I'm not an actor," Museveni told him. "It is only in the west that actors are respected."
Harding hurried off after his cameraman but immediately turned and came back.
"Okay Mr President. I think I can conduct the interview here after all," he said.
"This man is like a Hollywood character. Initially my people thought he was a spy for Kony. But I told them to let him take as many pictures as he wants so that Kony knows what is awaiting him," Museveni said.
As an after thought Museveni chuckled.
"I didn't know actors are important in the western world. I had a teacher who said I would make a very good actor and I despised him. I can't act. That I go out and show the troops. I think actors are the most unserious people," he said.
The official business ended with an offer for all the journalists to take a presidential flight in the army chopper, usually meant for eight people.
"I will give you my chopper to take you back to Lira. Leave your cars behind. My soldiers will drive them back for you but I doubt they will be in the same mechanical condition when they get there," the jovial Museveni said, before taking a couple of pictures with us.
True to his word, it was the safest 15 minutes flight over a rebel territory.
The president seems to have mastered how to deal with situations in war troubled areas. The convoy that moved on the road did not arrive in Lira without incident though. The rebels blew off one of the cars hired by the BBC journalists - sky-high, snuffing out a life of a young corporal and injuring the driver.
The corporal was actually the escort of one of the top army chiefs.
Normally he would have travelled in the chopper, but because he had to give room for us, he travelled on road but never arrived in Lira.
In Lira, I found The Monitor driver who drove me up north, thoroughly shaken by the landmine incident. But all said and done, the four hours with Museveni in Kony's bushes gave me a better picture of life in the jungles.

Original_Search_Title: Stimulating Industrial Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Stimulating Industrial Growth
Body_Text:
Lagos — In its determination to breath life into local industries nationwide,
the Federal Government in January 2004 announced the ban on the
importation of some items into the country.
The action of the government is an extension of the similar ban
placed on certain items in February 2003.
In the recent ban tagged: Import Prohibition List 2004, fresh fruits,
juices, furniture, vegetable oil, textile materials, soap and
detergents, plastic plates, among others. constitute the 30 items
which importation has been declared illegal.
In February last year, over 25 items were similarly banned by the
Federal Government. The banned items then include: wheat flour,
sorghum, mosquito repellant, used refrigerators, used
airconditioners, toothpick, envelopes, toilet rolls, vehicles above
eight years, etc.
In taking the steps, President Olusegun Obasanjo, P.M.News learnt,
was worried about the unbridled importation of cheap goods into the
country and its far reaching implication on the economy. Apart from
depleting the foreign exchange of the country, local industries were
badly affected as they could no longer compete with the cheap items
imported from overseas.
Besides, due to low purchasing power of an average worker in Nigeria,
only a few items could be bought. The result was stockpiling of
unsold goods in warehouses while thousands of jobs were lost, thereby
leading to social disequilibrium in Nigeria. The rate of armed
robbery increased, while the health of Nigerians degenerated, leading
to high mortality rate.
To arrest the dangerous trend, the government felt there was the need
to regenerate the industries described as the engine of growth.
Obviously though Nigerians are not new to import ban.
During the regimes of General Ibrahim Babangida (retd) and his
successor, late General Sani Abacha, items like rice were equally
banned. But the efforts did not have the desired impact. Soon after
the ban was announced, the items found their ways through the
nation's porous borders into the country. Therefore, it was business
as usual.
Although Nigeria is a signatory to the World Trade Organisation (WTO)
which allows free trade among member-nations, the government might
have reasoned that its domestic economy was being battered.
Indeed, last year's ban did not meet the envisaged target. Items like
used refrigerators, compressors and airconditioners, otherwise called
Tokunbo, are common sight in markets like Iyana Ipaja, Lawanson,
Mushin, while vehicles above eight years are daily seen on Nigerian
roads. Also, toothpicks, toilet rolls imported from foreign countries
including Taiwan, Thailand, China are readily found in Nigeria.
Government agencies, particularly the Nigerian Customs Service (NCS)
and the Nigerian Immigration Service (NIS) have largely been accused
of frustrating government's efforts. Apart from the problems of poor
value of the naira, high interest rate by banks, poor infrastructure
like pothole-filled roads, erratic electricity, the NCS has been
accused of not effectively policing the borders at Idiroko, Seme.
President Obasanjo lampooned the Customs Service recently in Abuja,
when he received the Acting Comptroller-General of the agency, Mr.
David Ogungbemila. The president said the agency is not only very
corrupt, it has performed below expectation, though it has over the
years surpassed its revenue targets which run into billions of naira.
P.M. News gathered that the president charged the agency to brace up
to the new challenge of government's policy to the letter as regards
their operations.
Although there were protests by the Customs licensed agents that the
ban would affect them, especially in the area of job losses as
various importers of such items would lose their jobs, government is
bent-on implementing the policy.
It is the reasoning of the Organised Private Sector (OPS) and the
Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) that the new policy could
well provide the path to glory for the local industries.
According to MAN's president, Charles Ugwuh, the government
initiative is good.
Mr. Daniel Monday, Managing Director of Dan Real, a footwear
manufacturer, believes that government's new policy will once again
provide a soothing balm for the local industries in Lagos, Kaduna,
Aba, Onitsha, Ibadan, Sango-Ota, and other industrialised cities in
the country.
He told P.M.News in a chat that capacity utilisation of the
industries would improve, while jobs would be created for the many
unemployed youths.
P.M.News further gathered that producers of fruit juice like Funman
Nigeria Limited are bracing up to the challenge through factory
expansion.
Government has been charged, however, to effectively monitor the
Customs and tighten the nation's borders and the sea/airports to
ensure the success of the ban.
The nation watches as implementation of the ban begins.

Original_Search_Title: Death in the Camp: Inside Barlonyo
Headline: Uganda: Death in the Camp: Inside Barlonyo
Body_Text:
Barlonyo — It is 4 p.m. on Monday and Barlonyo camp is practically deserted. Only
the walls remain of the grass-thatched huts, which the rebels lit at 5
p.m. on Saturday evening.
Amuka militia fighters who accompany us to the camp tell us that the
rebels came from three directions.
Mr Boniface Kajoka, 21, one of the militia fighters, said they got
information of strange people in the neighbourhood and the attack happened
immediately afterwards.
"It was too fast," he said.
"They had big guns, RPGs, G2s, LMGs; there were bullets everywhere."
There were 30 militia fighters at the camp, armed only with AK-47 rifles
and two magazines for their bullets.
It was a one-sided contest against rebels estimated to number more than
180.
"We need support," another militia, Mr Bosco Okello says. "We only have
small guns with two magazines. When we ran out of ammunition, we also fled."
Some of the militia fighters at the camp said they only finished their
training on December 15 and came to the camp to replace the UPDF. They have
not yet received their Shs 60,000 monthly salary.
Some of the Amuka fighters said their group includes 30 Local Defence
Unit personnel - but when the attack happened, the LDUs, who had just been
paid, had left the camp to take their money to their relatives
elsewhere.
Smoke still rises from the burnt huts and there is the smell of burning
food from the piles of sorghum on the ground. There are scarred bodies of
people who burnt to death and bodies covered by papyrus mats or the
collapsed walls of houses, yet to be buried.
At the far end of the camp, there are the remains of one man, who was
slaughtered by rebels. A mat covers his body; there are flies everywhere and
there is one prowling dog.
Looking for meat.
Inside the hospital
Dr Jane Aceng, the medical superintendent of Lira hospital, said the
first casualties arrived at 4:30 a.m. on Sunday on an army truck. There are
now 60 in the hospital; eight have died.
There are more victims trickling to the hospital - in twos and
threes.
"We are always prepared; that is what we are trained to do," Aceng
says.
But she adds: "We are overwhelmed in terms of personnel, in terms of
supplies."
The Ministry of Health sent extra supplies of drugs as well as five
extra doctors.
In the children's ward, Mr Vincent Ogwang holds the hand of his
5-year-old sister, Dorcus Akello. Her head has been hacked by a
machete. When the rebels arrived in the camp, he ran away.
The next day he returned to find his baby sister barely alive, lying
next to the dead bodies of their mother and father.
Death in the trenches
Displaced people in Barlonyo camp have buried their dead in trenches
meant for the Amuka fighters to hide in during combat. They did this after
the ground became too hard for them to dig graves.
Some victims were hurriedly buried in the rubble but bodies still litter
the camp.
A few days ago, this camp was home and hope for 4,800 people; it was
filled with playing children and the sounds of life.
Now it lies desolate; a wasted expanse of death and destruction, pillage
and plunder. Hope and optimism no longer lives here; it has gone to the
dogs, which now roam the camp, feasting on human flesh.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agencies Relocate Refugees From Chad Border, Airlift Food Aid to Darfur
Headline: Sudan: UN Agencies Relocate Refugees From Chad Border, Airlift Food Aid to Darfur
Body_Text:
Site at Farchana now hosts close to 1,700 refugees
The United Nations refugee agency is mounting an emergency airlift to
eastern Chad this week to bring more than 256 tons of aid supplies to
110,000 Sudanese refugees currently scattered along a 600-kilometre stretch
of remote borderland.
"The airlift coincides with an urgent refugee re__cpLocation operation aimed
at moving tens of thousands of refugees away from the insecure Chad-Sudan
border to safer camps further inland before the start of the rainy season in
May," Ron Redmond, a spokesman for the UN High Commissioner for Refugees
(UNHCR), said at a press briefing in Geneva.
Nearly 4,000 refugees have been transported so far to two new
camps. "Obviously, we've still got a long way to go in this race against
time and the elements," he added.
Meanwhile, as conditions deteriorate in the Darfur region in western
Sudan, the UN World Food Programme (WFP) is airlifting food aid into the
area to help alleviate the suffering of tens of thousands of people
displaced by the conflict there.
The airlift of some 500 tons of sorghum to El Fasher, in North Darfur,
is an interim measure to ensure that food reaches people who have been cut
off since November. Insecurity continues to prevent WFP from transporting
food by road from its main warehouses to key supply points in El Fasher and
Nyala.
"We are not planning a massive airlift to the region, since we hope that
road transportation will be re-established very soon," said Bradley
Guerrant, WFP's Deputy Country Director for Sudan. "This is intended to meet
the urgent needs of people we have been unable to reach for several months."
The airlift of sorghum comes in response to additional urgent needs in
the region, where in recent weeks WFP has managed to distribute nearly 2,000
tons of food assistance to 105,000 displaced people - 96,000 in Kutum town
and another 9,000 in four other __cpLocations. An additional 28,000 displaced
people are still expecting assistance in Kutum, where WFP's stocks have run
out.
An estimated one million people have been displaced in Darfur as a
result of the conflict between rebel movements, militias and the Government
of Sudan. To escape the fighting, which began a year ago, another 110,000
people have crossed the border into Chad where they will receive WFP
food. On the Sudanese side of the border, humanitarian agencies are hopeful
that current access to Kutum will be extended to cover other areas in
Greater Darfur so that assistance can reach those who desperately need
it.

Original_Search_Title: The Irish Tipple That's Got All Nigeria Thirsting for More
Headline: West Africa: The Irish Tipple That's Got All Nigeria Thirsting for More
Body_Text:
Johannesburg —  Guinness is powering ahead in west Africa where its muscular
marketing is making the beer a giant hit
AT THE Lagos headquarters of the Nigerian Stock Exchange, scores of
traders have packed into a room decked with the red, white and black colours
of Guinness Nigeria, the brewer.
Smells of a company-sponsored lunch waft from silver containers, while a
promotional slogan reminds the audience that Guinness "brings out the action
in you".
Keith Richards, the company's British MD, starts to address the audience
but pauses to say: "I am just going to take power by having a mouthful of
Guinness. Do I have your permission?"
Richards drinks, and loud cries of "power" ripple through the room.
Guinness's muscular approach to selling its product and image in Nigeria
means it is profitably embedded in the business and social life of
Nigeria.
Guinness Nigeria has benefited from a combination of investment in
products adapted to the local market, aggressive marketing colonial-era
history and lack of competition.
The company's success reveals a commercial truism observed in Nigeria
and other African countries: for all the logistical problems and extra costs
faced by consumer goods businesses operating in Africa, many of those that
do business make big profits.
The Guinness brand is owned by Diageo, the UK-based brewer and
distiller, which holds a controlling stake in Guinness Nigeria. The rest is
owned by various Nigerian investors.
In September Diageo said its performance in Africa, its second-largest
market by volume and third-largest by operating profits, was among the
financial highlights in a year of otherwise tough trading conditions.
The company attributed part of the 10% rise in Guinness sales in Africa
to the continuing success of Michael Power, a James Bond-like fictional
figure who represents the brand in the region and stars in a
Guinness-financed feature film.
Guinness's Nigerian pretax profits rose 69% to $71m last year, against
increases of 3%, 22% and 19% in the previous three years. Nigeria is the
thirdlargest market for Guinness in the world, close behind Ireland and
Britain, the biggest consumer.
Richards says the company has benefited from lower beer taxes and
evidence of economic improvements.
A more prosaic factor in Guinness's success is the volatility over the
past 20 years of the Nigerian beer market, which has an average annual
consumption of between 700-million and 800-million litres. The market
reached 1,2-billion to 1,3-billion litres in 1983, before crashing amid
economic crisis and more than 15 years of military dictatorship.
When sales dipped to 400-million litres during the mid-1990s, many
smaller brewers went out of business, leaving the market dominated by
Guinness Nigeria and its larger rival, Nigerian Breweries.
In some ways, the country's brewing market has distilled to a proxy
conflict between two multinationals. Nigerian Breweries is 54,2% owned by
Heineken of the Netherlands; 53,8% of Guinness Nigeria's shares were held
directly or indirectly by Diageo on June 30, although Richards points out
that the remainder of the shares are owned by Nigerians.
The question of identity is sensitive for the makers of Guinness, the
thick, dark brew that might seem better suited to a cold winter in its
native Ireland than to the tropics.
Introduced in west and central Africa by European slave traders 200
years ago, Guinness has a durability that makes it suitable for west
Africa's hot and humid climate.
While Guinness Nigeria uses the brand's familiar harp logo, some
products differ significantly from those in western markets. Sorghum, a
common cereal in west Africa, is the base for Nigerian Guinness, and gives
the drink a sweeter flavour.
The Guinness marketing strategy in Nigeria still includes the famous
catchline "Guinness is good for you" that has been abandoned in
industrialised countries. However, Guinness Nigeria says it will be dropped
soon when the packaging changes.
Like other parts of Diageo, Guinness Nigeria must comply with Diageo's
worldwide code on marketing. This will mean cutting back on words with
potentially sexual connotations, such as "power" and "action".
This could conflict with the company's heavy emphasis on promotional
material featuring Michael Power, who has been used by Guinness in Africa
for four years.
Richards argues that Critical Assignment, the Michael Power film, was
more than just a vehicle for product placement in Nigeria, but also admits
the film promotion helped sales only to a small extent, partly because of a
lack of screens to show it.
Back at the stock exchange, the presentation is over, and it is hard to
escape through the scrum of traders and journalists grasping for packages of
free Guinness products.
But for Guinness the question is whether its strategy and the conditions
under which it operates will enable it to expand as spectacularly as it did
last year.

Original_Search_Title: Equity Initiatives Benefit Only a Few 'Elites'
Headline: South Africa: Equity Initiatives Benefit Only a Few 'Elites'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Privatisation of parastatals has had little effect on empowering
black businesses, with deals collapsing due to lack of funds
THE restructuring of state-owned enterprises had done little to advance
the cause of black empowerment, think-tank BusinessMap Foundation said
yesterday.
This adds to a chorus of critics of government's empowerment initiatives
claiming they benefit only a few elites with connections to the ruling
African National Congress.
This means government still has a lot to do towards empowering black
people through the acquisition of equity in public entities.
Government's intention is to redress the exclusion of the majority of
South Africans from the mainstream economy.
Where possible, government has made empowerment a requirement for doing
business in SA, and it also encourages the private sector to help in this
economic transformation process.
Speaking at the launch of the annual review of restructuring stateowned
enterprises yesterday, BusinessMap director Reg Rumney said that the sale of
public entities had had little effect on the empowering of emerging black
businesses since the first privatisation deal was concluded in 1990.
Rumney said most of these transactions, which included the sale of the
national Sorghum Breweries, SunAir and Ucingo's 3% stake in Telkom, had
collapsed primarily because of lack of funding.
He said government's flagship broad-based empowerment transaction in the
sale of Telkom's shares to the public, including stokvels and many
first-time investors, had also not achieved much in terms of black economic
empowerment.
"Telkom's Khulisa scheme was devised to prefer black bidders for Telkom
shares. However, the Khulisa offer accounted for less than 1% of Telkom's
shareholding," Rumney said.
"Moreover, empowerment consortium Ucingo previously held 3%, set aside
for it from the initial privatisation, but this reverted to financiers
because of Ucingo's inability to finance the shares. The net result is that
Telkom has no significant empowerment shareholding."
Rumney suggested that government should sell a portion of its stake in
South African Airways and Airports Company SA to empowerment companies.
He said government should let go of its ownership in certain key sectors
of the economy.
"The key principle remains that state ownership should have a purpose,
such as supplying services the market cannot supply.
"Otherwise the risk is that the public sector will suppress the
development of the private sector by using its relationship with the state
to stifle competition."
However, BusinessMap commended the parastatals for appointing more
blacks and females into senior managerial positions.
The BusinessMap review has also remarked about the government's apparent
"reversal of attitude" towards privatisation especially in the run up to the
general election this year.
"No grand privatisations were announced in 2003" except the continued
sales of Aventura holiday resorts and Telkom shares, the annual review
said.
"With an election due (this year) it is not in government's interest to
be seen to be pursuing neoliberal' economic policies," said Rumney.
Government has raised about R36bn from 30 restructuring transactions
since 1997.

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Briefing Notes on Sudanese Refugees in Chad
Headline: Chad: UNHCR Briefing Notes on Sudanese Refugees in Chad
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
Geneva — UNHCR teams in Eastern Chad are monitoring the border region with Sudan
amid reports that another 18,000 Sudanese refugees have crossed into Chad in
the past week following heavy fighting in the Darfur region. The new refugee
arrivals are reported at several __cpLocations - along the northern segment of
the affected border zone near Koulbous, Birak and Djoran, and along the
southern segment of the border around Tisi, Ade and Goz-Bedah.
Local authorities report that approximately 2,500 people arrived in the
surroundings of Waddi Sandi located north of Koulbous, 23 kilometres from
the border. According to refugees interviewed by UNHCR, Sudanese forces
attacked ten villages in the canton of Djerbira in the early morning hours
of last Friday (January 16). The refugees said the troops burned houses and
dynamited wells, provoking an exodus by villagers across the border. Most of
the refugees fled immediately when the attack started on Friday and did not
have time to take anything with them, they said. Some of the women reported
that their husbands stayed behind in Sudan. While some of the refugees say
they were themselves victims of the attacks, others left as soon as they
heard shooting in nearby villages.  They said they have no hope at this
point of going back anytime soon to Darfur as their homes have been
destroyed and they fear for their lives if they return.
The 2,500 people are scattered along a 20-30 kilometre stretch of a
sandy river bed, where they have built makeshift shelters out of
branches. One of the main problems of this __cpLocation is the lack of access to
water. UNHCR teams report that in the past two days, some groups of refugees
have started to leave the site and head further inside Chad to Ogona. The
local authorities have identified a temporary site near Ogona where the
refugees can have access to wells for water. Refugees, mainly women,
children and old people, are traveling to Ogona on donkeys.
Today, the UNHCR team will go to Andou, a village located between Waddi
Sandi and Tine to monitor the number of new arrivals there and assess the
needs of the refugees.
Local authorities in Guereda reported that another group of
approximately 4,000 Sudanese refugees arrived in the area of Birak at the
beginning of the week. The local authorities have allocated an open field
some five kms from Birak for the newly arrived refugees.
In Djoran, 13 kilometers from Birak, another 1,315 people arrived in the
past week.  They walked or came on donkeys from the villages of Habsourd and
Berseliba, through the mountains of Hadja Mothahim, and crossed the border
at Korok.
Further south, the local authorities in Abeche informed UNHCR on
Wednesday morning of the arrival of an estimated 10,000 Sudanese in the
region of Tisi, Ade and Goz-Bedah this week. A UNHCR team will be dispatched
on the ground next week together with members of the NGO Africare to
evaluate the situation and identify sites for the refugees.
UNHCR teams continue to urgently assess the immediate needs of the
refugees and collect more detailed information on the total number of people
who crossed over in Chad.  The local governmental agency CNAR (Commission
nationale dÂAccueil et de Reinsertion des Refugies), in coordination with
UNHCR, is deploying teams at the border to immediately register new
arrivals.
UNHCR is pre-positioning blankets, jerry cans and food in Guereda. UNHCR
and WFP are working closely together to deliver food to 15,000 refugees
(5,000 in Tine area, 5,000 in Birak area and 5,000 in Ade/Tisi
area). Distribution of sorghum, white beans and corn soya blend is scheduled
to start at the beginning of next week.
Meanwhile, re__cpLocation of refugees from the volatile border zone to
Farchana camp further inland is still underway.  So far, 621 people have
been transferred in the first three re__cpLocation movements last Saturday,
Monday and Wednesday from Wandalou, near Adre. The next convoy is planned
for Sunday. Convoys will continue every other day, moving an average of 250
people each time. Farchana camp will eventually host between 9,000 and
12,000 people.
At the same time, the site of Kouloungo, near Guereda, has been approved
as a re__cpLocation site for refugees. UNHCRÂs partner GTZ will start
installations in the camp next week. The camp will host up to 8,000
people. Two other sites are also being evaluated as possible re__cpLocation camp
sites.  Saranh, East of Iriba, could host between 15,000 to 20,000 people,
and Hodj, west of Guereda, could host up to 15,000 refugees.
These Briefing Notes are issued by Media Relations & Public
Information, UNHCR, Geneva. Briefer: Kris Janowski

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Briefing with Ron Redmond - Media Relations
Headline: Uganda: UNHCR Briefing with Ron Redmond - Media Relations
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
Geneva —  These Briefing Notes are issued by Media Relations & Public
Information, UNHCR, Geneva.
Briefer: Ron Redmond - Media Relations
Topics:
1) UGANDA / RWANDA
2) ETHIOPIA / SUDAN
3) CHAD
4) LIBERIA
This is a summary of what was said by the UNHCR spokesperson at
todayÂs Palais des Nations press briefing in Geneva.  Further information
can be found on the UNHCR website, www.unhcr.ch, which should also be
checked for regular media updates on non-briefing days.
1) - UGANDA / RWANDA A first group of Rwandan returnees is set to leave
Uganda on Monday (19 Jan.) in a return operation organised by UNHCR and the
governments of Uganda and Rwanda to assist the voluntary repatriation of
some 23,000 Rwandan refugees living in settlements in the Mbarara district
of south-western Uganda. This voluntary movement is part of a much wider
effort in several African nations to help the regionÂs remaining Rwandan
refugees go home and rebuild their lives. In mid-2003, there were an
estimated 60,000 Rwandan refugees scattered throughout several African
countries. The start of the return operation from Uganda is the outcome of a
tripartite agreement signed between the governments of Uganda and Rwanda and
UNHCR in July 2003.
Monday's five-truck convoy is expected to repatriate a first group of
200 refugees from Nakivale -- one of three settlements hosting Rwandan
refugees in Uganda. The other two settlements are Kyakka and Oruchinga. The
returnees are part of a group of 1,165 Rwandan refugees who have registered
with UNHCR for repatriation assistance to various parts of Rwanda.
Returnee convoys are expected to travel from the settlements to Mbarara
and on to the Gatuna crossing, some 300 kms away. Gatuna is on the border
between Uganda and Rwanda. Returnees will be transported to a transit
facility which has been established close to the north-eastern town of
Byumba. Here, they will receive a re-integration package consisting of basic
kitchen utensils, plastic sheeting for shelter, blankets, jerry cans and
soap. Some of the returnees will be transported by UNHCR trucks from the
transit centre to their home areas or to the nearest __cpLocation. Other
returnees will receive transport assistance via commercial vehicles.
A profile of the Rwandan refugee population in Uganda shows that the
refugees are from all areas of Rwanda. The largest number is, however, from
the eastern areas of Kibungo and Utarama, which borders the Akagera National
Park in the north-east of the country. Other refugees are from Gisenyi and
Ruhengeri (north-west), Gitarama, (central) Kibuye, (west), Gikongoro and
Butare (south).
The start of the return operation from Uganda and Rwanda next Monday
follows a one-day tripartite meeting held between the governments of Uganda
and Rwanda, and UNHCR on Jan. 7 to finalise plans for the operation. It is
expected to be one of the last major return movements of Rwandan refugees
remaining outside their country. The meeting held in the Ugandan capital,
Kampala, was followed by a visit of Rwandan and Ugandan officials to the
refugee settlements in Mbarara district on 8 Jan. The visiting Rwandan
officials assured the refugees that upon their return home, they will either
get back their own land or will be allocated plots that can support their
families.
Elsewhere in Africa, UNHCR and host governments are making efforts to
encourage Rwandan refugees to return home. In Zambia, a meeting held in the
capital, Lusaka, on 9 Jan. between Zambian authorities and UNHCR resolved to
send a team consisting of Rwandan refugee leaders, senior Zambian government
officials, parliamentarians and UNHCR staff on a go-and-see-visit to Rwanda
in early March 2004.  The planned go-and-see visit is part of an information
campaign aimed at encouraging the return of some 5,700 Rwandan refugees
still in Zambia. An information campaign has also been launched in Tongogara
camp in Zimbabwe where there are more than 3,500 Rwandan refugees.
In 2003, UNHCR and the government of Rwanda signed seven agreements for
the return of Rwandan refugees currently in various countries in Africa,
including Zambia (Jan.), Republic of Congo (June), Uganda (July), Malawi
(Nov.), Namibia (Nov.), Mozambique (Dec.) and Zimbabwe (Dec). The agreements
signed in 2003 are in addition to others established earlier with the
Central African Republic, Tanzania and Burundi.
In 2003, a total of 19,712 Rwandan refugees returned home from various
countries, the majority of them with UNHCR assistance. Of this number, some
14,981 Rwandan refugees were assisted by UNHCR to return home from the
Democratic Republic of Congo -- 8,214 returned home through Goma in eastern
Kivu while 6,767 were assisted to return through Bukavu, on the southern
shores of Lake Kivu.  Other refugees returned from Tanzania, Burundi,
Zambia, Uganda and Republic of Congo.
2) - ETHIOPIA / SUDAN Two UNHCR staff are part of a nine-person team
that has traveled to the town of Pochalla in southern Sudan to investigate
reports that thousands of Ethiopian and Sudanese Anuaks are fleeing across
the border to escape violence in western Ethiopia. The team reported this
morning that they have seen people crossing the border, most of them young
males. Residents say some 100-200 people are arriving daily. They report
walking for a week or more to reach the border and appear to be in
reasonably good health. However, they are arriving with no possessions, not
even to carry water. There are some children, women and elderly, but most
are young men aged between 14-25.  Local residents are providing some help
to the new arrivals, who are also foraging in the countryside for food.
The team, which headed to the region yesterday, intends to spend two
days in Pochalla on the Ethiopian border and will return tomorrow to
Lokichoggio, Kenya. For its part, UNHCR is investigating the safety and
protection needs of the recent arrivals - both refugees and
returnees. Representatives of private aid agencies are looking into the
health, sanitation and water needs of the Anuaks, while WFP will assess
their food needs.
World Relief, a US-based NGO, is the only non-governmental organisation
operating in Pochalla, and has been the main source of information about the
Anuak influx. A World Relief official said earlier this week that between
100 and 300 Sudanese and Ethiopian Anuaks were arriving every day in
Pochalla county in the Upper Nile region of southern Sudan. The total number
of recent arrivals was reported to be 15,000, mostly in Pochalla town, where
many were camped at a local school and church. For the time being, weÂre
cautious about the figure of 15,000, which comes from local authorities. It
is also unclear how many are Sudanese Anuaks who were refugees in Ethiopia
and may now return to live with their relatives, and how many are Ethiopian
Anuaks who have now become refugees in Sudan.
The Anuaks have reportedly been fleeing reprisals that followed killings
in mid-December in the Gambella region of western Ethiopia. On December 13,
eight people - including three officials of UNHCR's partner, the
Administration for Refugee and Returnee Affairs (ARRA) - were brutally
murdered in an ambush of a vehicle headed to Odier-Bol, in the Gambella
area. In Gambella town, rioting broke out after the bodies were brought
back, targeting members of the Anuak community, who were blamed for the
attacks. Some 58 people were confirmed killed, and some 400 huts of the
Anuaks were burned to the ground.
3) - CHAD The first UNHCR re__cpLocation movement of tens of thousands of
Sudanese refugees scattered along the insecure Sudan-Chad border is
scheduled to depart the Adre region of eastern Chad for a safer, inland camp
at dawn tomorrow (Saturday).  Some 200 people will be transported in five
trucks and one bus to the new camp in Farchana, 55 kms from the border.
Members of UNHCRÂs emergency team, which has been in Chad since December
30, traveled yesterday to Adre and are working closely with UNHCR staff
already present in the area to prepare for tomorrowÂs scheduled
movement. A second re__cpLocation convoy of approximately 200 people is
scheduled for Monday. Similar transfers will be organized every other day
for the next two weeks.
UNHCR and its partners started pre-registering refugees for the
re__cpLocation yesterday. Over the past few weeks, UNHCR and its partners in the
field have been carrying out a campaign to inform the refugees about the
possibility of re__cpLocation to Farchana. Most refugees have indicated they
want to move because itÂs safer away from the border. Refugees scattered
in the border area in Chad have been the victims of periodic incursions by
Sudanese militia.
Today, UNHCR and its local partner SECADEV (Secours Catholique) are
distributing wristbands to each refugee scheduled to leave
tomorrow. MSF-Holland will at the same time identify people needing special
attention, such as women in their last months of pregnancy, unaccompanied
children, elderly and sick people.  These people will be transported to the
camp by ambulance.
The convoy will depart at dawn tomorrow, and the trip is expected to
take approximately three hours. UNHCR vehicles will lead the convoy, and the
Chadian gendarmerie will provide an escort. The Chadian Red Cross will be
responsible for assisting the refugees throughout the process. Red Cross
first aid volunteers will help refugees to embark and will be present in the
trucks to assist them during the journey. On arrival at the camp, the
volunteers will guide the refugees through the different steps of the
reception process up to their settling in their tent. The reception process
will include a formal registration conducted by UNHCR and the governmental
authority CNAR (Commission Nationale dÂAccueil et de Reinsertion des
Refugies), a medical screening by MSF-Holland and the distribution of food
(sorghum, corn meal and oil) provided by WFP. In addition, UNHCR will give
families blankets, mattresses, jerry cans, kitchen sets, soap, and hygiene
items.
Livestock belonging to the refugees will be transferred separately.
UNHCR and the refugees agreed that one member of each family will accompany
the cattle from the site where they are currently staying to the new
camp. It is anticipated that the journey by foot should take three
days.
The camp in Farchana will have capacity for 9,000 to 12,000 refugees. In
the near future, children will be able to start attending school in the
camp. GTZ has been responsible for designing and building up the camp and
has dug three wells in the nearby village which will supply water to the
refugees until completion of well drilling inside the camp area itself.
An estimated 95,000 Sudanese have fled across the border to Chad since
last March - 30,000 in December alone. They are scattered along a 600-km
stretch of remote borderland in dozens of makeshift camps where they are
still vulnerable to periodic incursions by militia from Sudan.
4) - LIBERIA UNHCR and other UN agencies and NGO partners yesterday
carried out what we hope will be one of the last re__cpLocations in the Monrovia
area, taking 690 internally displaced persons (IDPs) from three irregular
shelters in the Montserrado area of Monrovia to five formal IDP camps near
the capital. From some 2,000 people in the centers, 879 had registered for
the re__cpLocation yesterday. On the date of departure, however, only 690
actually showed up and were taken to the IDP camps.
We hope this will be one of the last large movements in an IDP
re__cpLocation operation mounted by UN agencies last September to help decongest
the city of Monrovia. However, some displaced people continue to seek
shelter in public buildings in the capital, leading to new requests for
assistance. The re__cpLocation operation has been under way since September to
help IDPs who had fled to safety in Monrovia, temporarily occupying schools
and other essential public facilities. They are being taken to official IDP
centers, where they are able to receive regular assistance.
Many of the occupants at these irregular shelters were not genuine IDPs
but residents of the area who pretended to be displaced to take advantage of
benefits offered by humanitarian agencies.  During the latest re__cpLocation
exercise, it was once again observed that many of them spontaneously left
the centers for their homes in nearby communities, while others from more
distant areas hired commercial vehicles to leave the centers.
UNHCR provided 20 trucks and two ambulances to transport the IDPs to
Ricks, Siegbeh, Perry Town, Blamasee and Unification IDP camps. UNHCR
security staff and UNMIL soldiers were present to ensure security during the
transfer of the IDPs. It is customary that UNHCR distributes non-food items
to the newly relocated IDPs, including mats, blankets and kitchen
sets. Prior to the re__cpLocation exercise, massive preparation work included
shelter construction, transfer of IDPs recently relocated and still
occupying the much-needed transit centers, to regular shelters within the
IDP camps, and upgrade of facilities like water and sanitation, in order to
enhance the camps' absorption capacity.
Meanwhile, a group of Sierra Leonean refugees was repatriated by air to
Freetown, Sierra Leone, yesterday (Thursday). A group of 37 Sierra Leonean
refugees boarded a commercial flight to Freetown, escorted and assisted by
UNHCR and medical staff at both ends. We hope that repatriation by land will
resume as soon UN peacekeepers deploy in Bomi County and begin patrols in
the Monrovia / Bo-Waterside area, through which the road used for return
convoys passes. In 2003, UNHCR Liberia repatriated over 4,083 Sierra Leonean
refugees. The operation will continue until June 2004. UNHCR is continuing
information campaigns to promote returns to Sierra Leone among the estimated
13,000 Sierra Leoneans remaining in camps in Liberia.
The following link will take you to the above: www.unhcr.ch/briefing-notes

Original_Search_Title: UN Set to Begin Relocating Vulnerable Sudanese Refugees in Chad
Headline: Chad: UN Set to Begin Relocating Vulnerable Sudanese Refugees in Chad
Body_Text:
The United Nations refugee agency is set to begin relocating tens of
thousands of Sudanese who have fled the country's war-torn Darfur region and
now live in makeshift camps scattered along the insecure border with Chad,
where they have been victims of periodic incursions by Sudanese
militia.
Led by vehicles from the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), 200
people will be transported tomorrow in five trucks and a bus on the
three-hour journey to a new camp in Farchana, eastern Chad, 55 kilometres
from the border. A second convoy of 200 people is set for Monday and similar
transfers will be organized every other day for the next two weeks.
Some 95,000 Sudanese have fled across the border to Chad since last
March - 30,000 in December alone, fleeing the three Darfur provinces in
western Sudan as civil war between the Sudanese Government and the Sudan
Liberation Army (SLA) continues to rage there. They have been living
scattered along a 600-kilometre stretch of remote borderland in dozens of
makeshift camps where they are still vulnerable to periodic incursions by
militia from Sudan.
The Farchana camp can hold 9,000 to 12,000 refugees and children will
soon be able to start attending school there. On arrival, the refugees will
be given food, such as sorghum, corn meal and oil, provided by the UN World
Food Programme (WFP), while UNHCR will provide blankets, mattresses, jerry
cans, kitchen sets, soap and hygiene items.
The refugees' livestock will be transferred separately, with one member
of each family accompanying the cattle on the journey, which should take
three days by foot.

Original_Search_Title: UN Food Relief Agency Calls for $11 Million to Help Refugees in Chad
Headline: Chad: UN Food Relief Agency Calls for $11 Million to Help Refugees in Chad
Body_Text:
The United Nations food relief agency today issued an urgent appeal for $11 million to help feed the most vulnerable among the almost 100,000 Sudanese refugees who have fled the country's war-torn Darfur region over the last year for neighbouring Chad.
The World Food Programme (WFP) said the funds are needed to assist some 60,000 of about 95,000 people who have either settled in makeshift camps or moved in with Chadian families along the precarious border between the two countries.
They are considered the worst-off of a growing number of Sudanese fleeing the three Darfur provinces in western Sudan as civil war between the Sudanese Government and the Sudan Liberation Army (SLA) continues to rage there.
The WFP appeal comes as the UN's refugee agency announced plans to speed up the re__cpLocation of many of those refugees to safer camps away from the border, where they have been exposed to repeated raids by militia groups.
The UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) said it expects to begin pre-registering the first refugees on Thursday, ahead of the expected transfer to a refugee camp being constructed at Farchana, 55 kilometres from the Chad-Sudan border.
The Farchana camp should now be ready to accept its first refugees this weekend, several days earlier than previously expected, following construction by UNHCR and the German non-governmental organization GTZ.
But UNHCR cautioned that the re__cpLocation would take several weeks because of poor road conditions and logistical problems. Up to 9,000 people are expected to live in the Farchana camp once it is completed.
The refugees will receive a food package, which includes enough sorghum, corn flour and oil for 15 days, from WFP when they arrive. They will also be vaccinated and receive blankets, mattresses, mosquito nets, kitchen sets, soap and jerry cans from UNHCR.
The Chadian Government and UNHCR have identified two other potential sites for refugee camps which will house 28,000 people between them.

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Staff to Begin Pre-Registering First of Thousands of Sudanese Refugees Thursday
Headline: Chad: UNHCR Staff to Begin Pre-Registering First of Thousands of Sudanese Refugees Thursday
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
UNHCR staff in eastern Chad are set to begin pre-registering the first
of tens of thousands of Sudanese refugees Thursday in the Adre region
bordering Sudan in preparation for their re__cpLocation to a safer camp further
inland. We hope to start moving the refugees by truck to the new Farchana
camp over the weekend.
UNHCR and its German partner, GTZ, are finalising preparations at
