Farchana, 55 kms from the border, to receive the refugees.  By the end of
the week, the camp should be ready for the first 2,000 people, and
eventually will be expanded for up to 9,000 refugees. Twenty-five large
tents are being erected at the camp each day.  GTZ has readied three wells
in the nearby village of Farchana, three kms from the camp.  Water will be
transported by truck from the wells to the camp and will be stored in
bladders for use by the refugees. The agencies plan to drill wells directly
at the site in the near future to provide water for all the refugees.
The journey from the Adre region to Farchana, although only some 70 kms,
is expected to take up to three hours because of poor road conditions and
the logistical difficulties of moving large numbers of people by
convoy. Initially, UNHCR and its partners plan convoys for about 300 people
every couple of days. They will then shift to daily convoys as the operation
gets fully underway.  The whole process is expected to run for a period of
weeks.
Upon arrival at the camp, the refugees will be registered and assigned a
tent, before going through medical screening carried out by a team from
MSF-Holland. The screening will include vaccinations. The refugees will also
receive a food package provided by the World Food Programme with enough
sorghum, corn flour and oil for 15 days.  UNHCR will distribute blankets,
mattresses, mosquito nets, kitchen sets, soap and jerry cans to the
refugees.
UNHCR and the Chadian authorities continue to work closely to identify
more new sites. Potential sites must be located at least 50 kms from the
border for security reasons.  Some refugees gathered in spontaneous sites
near the border have been victims of regular incursions by Sudanese militia
who cross over to Chad and steal their livestock. The sites must also have
sufficient water supplies for the refugees and adequate surrounding land to
allow refugees to grow grain and feed their cattle. So far, of the six sites
identified in addition to Farchana, only two meet these criteria.  Both
sites are located to the north of Farchana -- Saranh, in the Iriba area,
will have a capacity of up to 20,000 people, and Kounoungo, near Guereda,
could host 8,000 people. A Chadian hydro-geologist team is expected to
assess new potential sites in the coming days.
In the meantime, UNHCR and all other humanitarian actors present in
eastern Chad continue regular aid distributions to the refugees still
waiting along the remote and insecure border.
An estimated 95,000 Sudanese have fled to Chad since last March Â
30,000 in December alone. They are scattered along a 600-kms stretch of
remote borderland in dozens of makeshift camps where they are still
vulnerable to periodic incursions by militia from Sudan.

Original_Search_Title: UN Refugee Agency Gives Emergency Aid to More Than 26,000 Sudanese in Chad
Headline: Chad: UN Refugee Agency Gives Emergency Aid to More Than 26,000 Sudanese in Chad
Body_Text:
Women, children majority of recent exodus of refugees
As efforts continue to settle the conflict in southern Sudan, the United
Nations refugee agency is working to address the "invisible emergency" that
has sent nearly 100,000 Sudanese fleeing into Chad from the western part of
their country.
The UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) has begun supplying
emergency aid, including blankets and food, to more than 26,000 refugees in
the border area, a spokesman for the agency reported today. Women and
children form the majority of the estimated 95,000 refugees who have sought
safety in eastern Chad since April. Driven by militia raids, widespread
looting and stealing of livestock, they fled the Darfur region of western
Sudan on foot by night in small groups to avoid detection.
Meanwhile in New York, UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan today hailed
"good progress" being made in peace talks between the Government and the
Sudan People's Liberation Movement (SPLM). "I have been speaking to the
leaders of Sudan and [SPLM leader John] Garang - and in fact yesterday I
spoke to Garang again - urging them to go ahead and complete the process,"
he said, voicing hope that an agreement would be reached before the end of
this month. "That would be another big New Year's gift to the people of
Sudan and to the people of Africa," he added.
On the ground in the eastern part of the country, UNHCR and its
non-governmental organization (NGO) partners have supplied blankets, mats,
jerry cans and food, including cooking oil and sorghum, to over 13,000
people encamped on the insecure border areas.
On Wednesday, the refugee agency will start distributing emergency aid -
including blankets from the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) - to other groups of
13,500 vulnerable Sudanese living along the border.
UNHCR spokesman Ron Redmond characterized the situation as an "invisible
emergency" in comments to reporters today in Geneva. He said the plight of
the Sudanese refugees is of "extreme concern" to the agency.
Many of the refugees have been living for months in flimsy, makeshift
shelters in the remote area where desert conditions mean that temperatures
are high in the day but can plunge to less than 10Â° Celsius at
night. According to agency staff involved in aid distribution, some have
been forced to sell blankets and other possessions, including relief items
distributed earlier, in order to feed their families. They need both food
and supplies to cope with the harsh living conditions.
At the same time, UNHCR and its partner agencies are working to relocate
up to 9,000 refugees from the border by mid-January to an inland camp at
Farchana where they will be formally registered, undergo medical screening,
and receive a family site, blankets, mats, soap, jerry cans and food.
Some estimates place the number of internally displaced Sudanese in the
Darfur region at over 600,000. This displacement is only the latest in 20
years of civil conflicts that have uprooted more than 4 million people
inside Sudan, and driven another 570,000 into neighbouring countries.

Original_Search_Title: Sudanese Rebels Fleeing Darfur Region Number 95,000
Headline: Chad: Sudanese Rebels Fleeing Darfur Region Number 95,000
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
The number of refugees fleeing SudanÂs western Darfur region into
neighboring eastern Chad is now estimated at 95,000 - some 30,000 of them in
December alone. You may recall I recently referred to this situation as an
example of an "invisible emergency." ItÂs still going on and itÂs
still of extreme concern to us.
The Sudanese refugees are scattered in dozens of makeshift settlements
that have sprung up along a 600-km stretch of remote, insecure borderland
between the two countries. They are subjected to periodic raids by bandits
and marauding militias from across the border, and many are in dire need of
assistance. This makes it all the more urgent that a UNHCR re__cpLocation
operation aimed at moving them to safer sites further inland begin as soon
as possible.
UNHCR emergency staff in eastern Chad are now preparing for the
logistically difficult re__cpLocation operation, which is tentatively set to
begin on January 15. The UNHCR team reported this morning from our eastern
Chad office in Abeche that the first of the new inland camps, Farchana,
should be ready to begin accepting an initial population of up to 9,000
refugees in about 10 days. The Farchana site is 55 kms from the border and
is being hurriedly prepared by our German partner, GTZ, which is digging
wells and building latrines and showers. MÃ©decins Sans FrontiÃ¨res Holland
will be in charge of water treatment. UNHCR has 2,000 tents in Abeche, the
first of which are already being erected in Farchana. Other sites are also
being identified in the Adre and Guereda areas.
Meanwhile, UNHCR and its NGO partners are carrying out an emergency
distribution of food and non-food items to some of the most vulnerable
refugees still encamped along the insecure border. This is extremely
difficult given the security concerns, the remoteness of the region and the
logistical constraints involved in covering such a wide area. The first
phase of the emergency distribution began on Friday in the Birak, Senette
and Feguerra areas in Birak Canton, along the northern stretches of the
affected border zone. As of yesterday, aid supplies had been provided to
13,250 of the most vulnerable refugees, many of them women and children. The
refugees received blankets, mats, jerry cans and some food, including
cooking oil and sorghum.
UNHCR staff involved in the distribution said the provision of food was
essential because some of the refugees had been forced to sell blankets and
other possessions, including previously distributed aid items, to feed their
families. Although hot during the day, temperatures can plunge to less than
10 degrees C. at night. Most of the refugees are women and children and
usually arrive at the border on foot following continuing raids by militia
groups on villages across the strife-torn Darfur region of western
Sudan.
They report that the militia raids are marked by widespread looting and
stealing of livestock, sending panicked villagers fleeing on foot toward the
border with Chad. Most of the refugees travel by night and in small groups
to avoid attention Many of the refugees are living in flimsy, makeshift
shelters constructed with whatever materials they can find.  Yesterday,
UNHCR staff in our eastern Chad headquarters at Abeche - 850 kms east of
NÂdjamena -- were on hand to welcome a much-needed Unicef air shipment of
22,000 blankets, nearly 3,000 of which were immediately dispatched in UNHCR
trucks for distribution in the Djoran area beginning tomorrow
(Wednesday). This second emergency distribution will focus on 13,500
vulnerable refugees in the Djoran and Guimeze areas.
The re__cpLocation operation scheduled to begin mid-month will involve
transporting the refugees to the new Farchana camp in UNHCR and GTZ
trucks. Re__cpLocation movements will take place daily. Upon arrival, the
refugees will go through a reception process that will include formal
registration, medical screening, al__cpLocation of a family site and the
provision of blankets, mats, soap, jerry cans and food.
The latest UNHCR emergency team, the third to deploy to Chad in the past
year, arrived in Abeche on Saturday and will bolster UNHCR staff already
working in the eastern border area.

Original_Search_Title: UN Team Heads to Eastern Chad to Help Relocate Sudanese Refugees
Headline: Chad: UN Team Heads to Eastern Chad to Help Relocate Sudanese Refugees
Body_Text:
A six-member team from the United Nations refugee agency is headed to
eastern Chad to help relocate tens of thousands of Sudanese refugees from
insecure areas near the border with Sudan.
Experts from the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) are expected
to arrive tomorrow to assist in setting up safer refugee camps further away
from the Chad-Sudan border, where about 95,000 refugees who have arrived
since April are living in makeshift camps.
The refugees, mainly women and children, have been fleeing raids by
militia groups on their villages in the Darfur region of western Sudan. The
tide of refugees has increased recently, with an estimated 30,000 people
arriving in December, including some 5,000 in just the past few days.
UNHCR officials say Sudanese militias frequently raid the camps along
the border, which is in a remote region of Chad, stealing the cattle of the
refugees. UNHCR has been distributing supplies of blankets, jerry cans,
kitchen sets, sorghum and cooking oil to the refugees.
Many refugees are reported to be suffering from respiratory problems,
exacerbated by the area's weather extremes - temperatures of 35 degrees
Celsius by day, but less than 10 degrees at night.
The new camps, to be located at least 50 kilometres from the border,
will begin accepting refugees by the middle of the month. One camp, near the
town of Farchana, will be able to house up to 15,000 people.
The UNHCR team, which has been in the Chadian capital, N'Djamena, since
Tuesday, comprises a team leader, a protection officer, a field community
services officer, an information officer, an administration officer and a
logistics specialist.

Original_Search_Title: Parliament Now Liberated, Says M7
Headline: Uganda: Parliament Now Liberated, Says M7
Body_Text:
Iganga — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has said the Movement government had liberated Parliament from the Parliamentary Advocacy Forum (PAFO).
Addressing a public rally at Iganga High School on Wednesday, Museveni said PAFO had eaten up Parliament and had spread to the Cabinet. Museveni had earlier before addressing the rally commissioned Tendo Steels (U) Limited in Kasole, Iganga.
"When I realised the situation was getting worse, I shook them up and sacked those who were in Cabinet," he said.
Last year, Museveni reshuffle his Cabinet in which he dropped Eriya Kategaya, Bidandi Ssali, Miria Matembe and Sarah Kiyingi. Kategaya and Matembe joined the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC), which embraced many PAFO members.
Museveni said the recent sh5m facilitation to MPs was his on-going plan to liberate legislators from the ill motives of PAFO.
"I am happy to note that Parliament is now cohesive. When we liberate Parliament from them, they say I have bribed them (MPs) these people had infiltrated us," Museveni said.
He said the sh5m was a facilitation to MPs to spread the gospel of the Movement.
Museveni, who spoke close to two hours, accused PAFO diehards of misdirecting their anger upon defeat.
"They are now telling lies about Museveni after realising that Parliament is no longer a vehicle for agitators of PAFO. These people are poisonous. You should avoid them," Museveni said, amid cheers from the crowd.
"When PAFO sees Museveni dealing with Parliament they think I have bribed them. They only like to see a Parliament that fights the President," he said.
He said PAFO was telling lies about the National Social Security Fund (NSSF).
"It is getting late but I will put it clear on radio one of these days. After my statement, those liars will disappear under their beds because the stand on NSSF is very clear," Museveni said.
At the occasion, Democratic Party stalwart and former Iganga district Speaker, Baite Munobwa crossed to the Movement.
"I welcome my friend Munobwa for having seen light in the Movement. Even those remaining should come out and we work together," Museveni said as the crowd chanted pro-third term slogans.
Munobwa said, "I have not been forced but I am tired of supporting a group that can never win." Munobwa was donned in essanja (dry banana leaves, a symbol of the third term support for Museveni).
Museveni blamed unemployment on non-marketable courses Ugandans study. He said many Ugandans study courses without job demands.
"Someone goes and studies psychology and expects to be employed. How many jobs in Uganda need that psychology capacity?" he asked, causing laughter.

Original_Search_Title: Support Movt to Finish Its Plans, Says Bukenya
Headline: Uganda: Support Movt to Finish Its Plans, Says Bukenya
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS have been asked to support the Movement Government programmes until their completion, Vice-President Prof. Gilbert Bukenya has said.
He was addressing residents of Kibujjo village in Wakiso district at the opening of a health centre II on Wednesday.
Bukenya said political excitement should not force people to abandon programmes that are still in the pipeline.
He asked the residents to give the Movement a chance to finish its programmes.
"The Movement is the only strong hope. It is gifted with foresight and headed by a man who can go all the miles," he said.
Bukenya told the people who were clad in essanja (dry banana leaves) and chanting third term slogans that in 1995, there was no school, no health facility and no water in his constituency.
He said he was de-campaigned by his opponents when he started a struggle to put up hospitals in the area, but vowed to serve his people.
Wakiso Chief Administrative Officer George Ntulume pledged to staff the hospital and ensure availability of drugs.
He said the health centre needed expansion, adding that patients move long distances to access the hospital.
The district director of health services, Dr. Betty Namala, thanked Bukenya for his commitment towards improving health care delivery in the district.
She said the centre would improve the community health.

Original_Search_Title: Omara Atubo Signs for Third Term T-Shirt
Headline: Uganda: Omara Atubo Signs for Third Term T-Shirt
Body_Text:
Parliament — Anti-third term MP, Mr Omara Atubo, (Otuke) on Tuesday signed for a
third term T-shirt at Parliament.
Ministers and pro third term MPs, every day line up at the reception of
Parliament to sign for third term T-shirts and strong supporters in their
constituents.
The project is spearheaded by Kabula MP, Mr James Kakooza.
The T-shirts bear three M letters on the front meaning Museveni,
Movement and Me.
When The Monitor asked Atubo why he had signed for the T-shirt, he said
he would use it to campaign against the third term.
"I have not signed for this T-shirt because I support third term but I
shall use esanja (dry banana leaves) to burn," he said.
Atubo said Museveni has no heart for Uganda because the T-shirt has on
indication of Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Nasasira Warns On Burning of Essanja
Headline: Uganda: Nasasira Warns On Burning of Essanja
Body_Text:
Mbarara — The Minister of Works Transport and Communications, Mr John Nasasira, has warned those burning the third term symbol of 'essanja' (banana leaves) to stop it.
He was meeting Kazo residents in a consultative meeting on the White paper in Mbarara town on Sunday. "I understand people in Kashaari and Ntungamo district have burnt the dry banana leaves, but I warn them to desist from this practice," Nasasira said.
However, the LC 2 Chairman central ward Ntungamo town council, Mr Joseph Matsiko, said they burnt the leaves to rid their ward of the dirt left behind by the participants and also to avoid the spread of banana wilt disease that had spread to many parts of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Parents Debunk Film Star's 'Resurrection'
Headline: Nigeria: Parents Debunk Film Star's 'Resurrection'
Body_Text:
Parents of Balaraba Mohammed, a film star who lost her life on her wedding day in a ghastly motor accident on Kaduna-Zaria expressway, have debunked rumours that their daughter had risen from death.
Rumours that late Balaraba had resurrected spread like wild-fire in almost all major cities of the North recently."
The news is all lies started by some mischievous crooks," lamented Mallam Mohammed Kaura, Balaraba's 60-year-old father who said he first heard the news while on business at Panbegua town in Kaduna State.
Responding to questions from Weekly Trust Magazine, Mallam Mohammed who for more than half an hour could only utter "Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihir Raji'un" meaning "From Allah we all came and to Him will we return," later managed to find the right words to describe the rumours."
It was only the devil's clarion call and echoed by the devil's disciples among us," he said.He said he never believed that the news was aired on the Federal Radio of Nigeria Kaduna which he described as a reputable media outlet. The rumours had it that Jakar Magori a Hausa magazine programme had broadcasted that a certain Nupe man had declared that Balaraba had risen from death or that she never died in the first place.
Reportedly, the anonymous Nupe man had confessed to the use of metaphysical means to cause the 'artificial accident' and made it appear real. The rumour also had it that the death was equally faked and made to seem true to mortal sensitivities.
His motive, according to the myth was that he loved Balaraba so much that he could not bear the humiliation of watching her getting married to another man.
The Nupe man, the rumour said, was reported by Jakar Magori as having said that Balaraba is presently safe in his custody and that if the parents agreed to give her in marriage to him, he would produce her.
The unsubstantiated story also had it that the grave in which late Balaraba was buried had been dug open but only banana peels were found in place of her body. "What abomination, what lies, malice. How could anyone believe that rubbish?" Balaraba's father asked, saying that as a muslim he had faith that one only dies once and will never rise until the day of reckoning.
Said he, "Balaraba has died. Her body was brought to us right here. I felt the pulse and confirmed her dead. The body went through all the rituals for funeral. This story cannot be true. All well-meaning persons should disregard it.
"He said he had to cut short his business and return home to his family for fear that they may be in some danger. "I suspected that this was the machinations of some con men and that they might be up to some mischief. I feared for the safety of my family.
"Mallam Mohammed then called on all those who have any affection for his late daughter to join him in prayers for the repose of her soul.
Our reporters found that people have been trooping to Balaraba's family house since the news began to go round.Family sources also said that Shu'aibu Lawal a.k.a. 'Kumurchi,' Balaraba's star-crossed groom was shocked by the rumours and had to travel to Kaduna from his hometown, Kano to cross-check the news.
The source told Weekly Trust Magazine that Kumurchi's first point of call was the cemetery where he confirmed that Balaraba's tomb had not been tampered with. From the graveyard, he went to Baralaba's family house where the mother confirmed his suspicion and consoled him. Malama Binta Mohammed, late Balaraba's mother, told our reporters that she too never believed the story.
However, Malama Binta could not help getting confused as crowds kept trooping to their house and gossip flew around the quarters. "I find it very unreasonable. How could people start this sort of wicked rumour?" she asked Malama Binta who apparently had not yet overcome the shock of the sudden death of her children was not in the mood of talking to newsmen.
In tears, she appealed to everybody to leave her alone to her calamities. "Only I alone know the agony I went through when they died. And just when I was beginning to cheer up, wicked souls are tormenting me again," she said. Asked whether she viewed the rumours as an attempt by Balaraba's admires to immortalise her, Malama Binta said "there is no admiration in this. It is the devil trying to hurt us.
I know they have died. No power on earth can bring them back to life. Let these evil souls allow the poor girl to lie peacefully in her grave.
"Perhaps no other natural phenomenon has so haunted mankind as death. It has figured in the legends and myths of nearly every culture so that it has become second nature to build certain myths round the death of loved ones or those who in their life time were highly admired. This may explain how the rumour of Balaraba's 'resurrection' got started.

Original_Search_Title: Cure Political Trauma By Attending Wedding Bash
Headline: Zimbabwe: Cure Political Trauma By Attending Wedding Bash
Body_Text:
FOR a man who had apparently just survived a political coup of some sort, President Robert Mugabe looked chipper at his relative's wedding in Soweto last week.
Had this been planned in advance? There is nothing guaranteed to banish the political blues than a wedding in Soweto, Johannesburg's south-western township famous for throwing grand shindigs, weddings and political riots included.
The meeting with Thabo Mbeki had traces of an afterthought. Which had been given top priority - the meeting or the wedding in Soweto?
Mugabe has a knack for the unusual, if not the unexpected. But at heart he remains that quintessential African leader. He will not exit the scene a la Nyerere or Mandela.
Before he rides off into the sunset, he will ensure his legacy remains intact. Whoever takes over must not think of doing a Levy Mwanawasa on Gushungo.
Not that Emmerson Mnangagwa had hatched such a plot after his succession to the top. He too has skeletons in his cupboard. He would most likely have to do a deal, either with Mugabe or his supporters.
Which must inevitably brings us to a discussion on ZAM - Zimbabwe After Mugabe. For some, it is well-nigh impossible to imagine a Zimbabwe without Mugabe. But as sure as the sun will set in the west, such an occasion may soon be upon us.
Wise people would prepare for that day, instead of mopping around with long faces, as if this old man is immortal. After all, the innocent commuter passenger who counselled his brother not to be as (expletive deleted) as Mugabe, was only highlighting the mortality of the man.
Apart from being flesh and blood, he can also be pretty bigoted. A few years ago, he made a statement for which he must secretly regret.
"They should not be as hard-hearted as the Jews," was a statement attributed to him. It raised a huge storm, not only among Jews around the world, but also among Mugabe supporters who had always thought such a well-educated, well-travelled and politically savvy man would not make such a "Chinotimba" gaffe.
But he did. Many Zimbabweans, though frightened of his hard-line on freedom of expression and freedom of anything else under sun, had always thought he was fairly level-headed and pragmatic when it came to such issues as religion, race and creed.
But after that statement, they revised downwards their esteem of him as a leader. Events in the next few years after that statement were to prove them tragically accurate.
Needless to say, his "totemless people" statement only helped to fuel the suspicion that the man was no lightweight in the bigotry department.
Today, when a citizen on a commuter omnibus, believing in his freedom to express an opinion about any other citizen in his country - including his president - which is neither libelous nor inflammatory - is locked up, people must wonder.
Which makes you wonder about the identity of the authors of the so-called Tsholotsho declaration: had they possessed credentials attractive to the majority of the hungry, the Green Bombers-bashed, the down-trodden, the disadvantaged, the jobless, the shelterless and the people without friends in high places, would they not have won support in challenging Mugabe at the Zanu PF congress?
But Jonathan Moyo himself was the big slimy bug in the ointment. People could not be possibly attracted to him politically, his politics not being what you would call "regular".
One day, he is this acid-tongued critic of Zanu PF, and the next he says the same party is the best thing that ever happened to Zimbabwe. Nowhere does he illuminate for the doubting Thomases the reasons for his huge volte-face.
He is dismissive of the accusation that his motives are, at the very least, dubious. In fact, his retort could be summarised as a defiant, brusque: "Well, why not? They are winning everything in sight. I like a winner."
Is the man capable of such cynicism? Yes, say those who read his articles describing Zanu PF and its leaders in language which, under his own Access to Information and Protection of Privacy Act, would have landed him in jail for many years.
No, say his admirers, he is a very sensitive, very warm-hearted citizen who dearly loves his country and his president. Even if Moyo, Patrick Chinamasa and the other so-called Young Turks - some have rechristened them The Young Goons - had possessed credentials suggesting they would transform Zanu PF into a genuine democratic party not obsessed with its Marxist-Leninist past, and a governing party earnestly trying to improve the lot of the poor people - there is no guarantee they would ever have pulled it off. Most African dictators who have lost power have done so either by getting a bullet in the head, insanity or fleeing the country in shame. Habib Bourgiba of Tunisia is the one who went spectacularly bananas before they carted him off into enforced retirement. Kenneth Kaunda of Zambia, eventually went off peacefully, although he tried strenuously to convince his people they could not do without him. Frederick Chiluba almost proved him right, until along came Levy Mwanawasa, the most unlikely knight in shining armour. Mugabe wants to time his exit so meticulously that there would be no upheaval when he is gone. But mostly he seems to want there to be no recriminations against him when he leaves office. Two people against whom there were no such recriminations were Julius Nyerere and Nelson Mandela. If Mugabe possesses the same political credentials as they did, then he has yet to expose them to his people, let alone the world.

Original_Search_Title: Cocaine: Police Raid Another Warehouse
Headline: Kenya: Cocaine: Police Raid Another Warehouse
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police officers put a seal on a container at Pepe Ltd container terminal at Athi River yesterday.
Police yesterday searched a warehouse belonging to Nairobi businessman and politician John Harun Mwau as investigations continued into the Sh5.3 billion cocaine haul.
Officers searched Pepe Limited, the Athi River-based warehouse belonging to Mwau, who is also a former director of the Kenya Anti Corruption Authority.
Pepe offers customs-bonded warehouses and the goods stored by them are not necessarily theirs. Police may also expect to hear from Mwau's lawyers for he is one of Kenya's most litigious businessmen.
And the head of the Special Crime Prevention Unit (SCPU), Mr Musa Yego, said police officers had not found any drugs at Pepe but were still searching.
"This is not the only depot we will visit because there are many others that we must check," Yego said. When the team of about 30 detectives from SCPU and the Anti-Narcotics Unit arrived at the Pepe Container Terminal they ordered employees who were busy loading and offloading containers to leave the compound.
They first went to the main offices where they perused documents detailing incoming and outgoing containers before moving out to inspect them.
Yego said they were interested in specific containers, which, he added, they had to break into and verify their contents.
In the vast compound there were about 40 containers that were waiting to be cleared.
About 10 police vehicles drove in and out of the compound several times as journalists waited outside before they were allowed in briefly.
The new development came as police in Malindi arrested two more suspects in connection with the Sh5 billion-drug haul found recently in Malindi and Nairobi.
This brings to 10 the number of people who have been arrested in connection with the haul believed to have originated from Colombia.
Mr Salim Bedi and Mr Ken Mgogo are employees of an Italian national, Mr Angelo Ricci, who is also in custody.
Ricci owns the villa in which high the grade Colombian cocaine was found. Ricci and his wife are among three Italians being held by police.
Three of them - two Italians and a Kenyan - were arrested in Malindi while another Kenyan was nabbed in Nairobi.
Dutch police have also arrested four other suspects, including a Kenyan, in Holland.
They are believed to be members of a drug cartel with tentacles stretching from South America, Europe and Kenya.
Police are also looking for five Dutch and German citizens who had rented a villa known as Rocky House in Malindi where a speedboat packed with 701, packets of cocaine worth about Sh1.5 billion was seized.
The Italian who was arrested in Nairobi is suspected to have been at the villa and escaped to the city.
On Tuesday night, police raided a godown used by Kenomar International (Kenya) Limited in Embakasi and seized 253 packets of cocaine worth Sh3.5 billion.
The drugs had been brought into the country disguised as bananas from Colombia.
General Service Unit commandos carried out the Malindi raid. The drugs were found packed in a Sh12 million speedboat bought by a Nairobi company, Central Valleys Supplies Limited from Franco Cespedos of Costa Nave, Spain, in June.
The deadly cargo was airlifted to Nairobi by helicopter and taken to GSU headquarters where it will be kept until it is destroyed.
Meanwhile, police are investigating whether a quarrel that led to the killing of a Swiss tourist in Malindi by a German man last week is linked to the cocaine haul.
Mr Hugo Meischerhaus died as he attempted to protect two British women who were being attacked by a German man at a hotel.
Mr Olaf Klinkusch, 41, attacked one of the women and stabbed Meischerhaus before fleeing into the hotel's grounds where he stabbed himself and bled to death.
At the hotel grounds, he confronted another British woman whom he also stabbed.
The two women were admitted to a Mombasa hospital with serious injuries.
"The detectives are investigating whether their quarrel had anything to do with the drugs seized in Nairobi and Malindi," said a source.
On Wednesday, former Co-operative Bank chairman Hosea Kiplagat denied police had questioned him over the godown.
He said he had rented the godown but moved out four years ago. " The godown is not mine. I was only a tenant, but stopped renting it because it became too expensive for me, " he told The Standard.
CID director Joseph Kamau said investigations had indicated that the drug were destined for Venezuela, US, Poland and the UK.
He said the consignment had originated from Colombia via the Mombasa port disguised as bananas.
In Nairobi, the drugs were repackaged and labeled as roofing materials.
Kamau said the bust came following the arrest of four men in Poland by Interpol.

Original_Search_Title: Investigations Ongoing On Mzekezeke Impersonation Case
Headline: Botswana: Investigations Ongoing On Mzekezeke Impersonation Case
Body_Text:
Police in Maun are still trying to piece together clues in a case in which a man who masqueraded as the masked South African artist, Mzekezeke swindled revellers of their money at a village nightclub.
Last month patrons at Trekkers nightclub were stunned when they realised that the man on stage whom they had expected to be Mzekezeke, was a fake.
According to some revellers, the man belted about two songs. But when he was about to sing the third song, he panicked and fled the stage.
The owner of the club, Tops Masole had to calm the angry crowd, as they were about to riot. But Masole denied that he had anything to do with the aborted show. He said the promoters of the show had hired his premises.
Masole said earlier he could not speak to "Mzekezeke" because he was told that he could only communicate in Zulu.
Masole claimed that the con artist, turned out to be a showbiz personality, Bull Dogg. He said Bull Dogg later turned up at the nightclub in the morning to apologise for the incident. Masole said it was Bull Dogg's girlfriend who collected the entrance fees for the show.
Even two dancers, who were performing with the artist, revealed that the masked man was Bull Dogg. According to them, they had earlier planned to stage a similar show in Serowe before they came to Maun.
However, Bull Dogg has denied any involvement in the controversial concert. He has since threatened to sue people who associated him with the show.
However, Showbiz has learnt that Bull Dogg was locked up for a night at the Sir Seretse Khama Airport police station in connection with the case.
The Station Commander for Maun Police, Superintendent Moitali Thokweng confirmed that three suspects were detained last month. Thokweng said the investigating officer came to Gaborone to follow up the suspects. "The suspects gave their side of the stories which we wanted to verify."
But he said investigations are continuing in this matter. He said so far, nobody has been formally charged. He added that once when they have finished with the investigations, they would decide on what charges the suspects should face.
Meanwhile, Bull Dogg who turned up at the Showbiz offices to promote his newly released album, said he was not interested in discussing the matter. When he was asked about the Mzekezeke saga, he said did not want to comment on that matter. "I came here to promote my album and not to talk about Mzekezeke," he said. He added that his lawyers were handling the case.
Meanwhile, Bull Dogg has released a new album, entitled "Ditonki Tsele". The eight-track album features songs Ditonki tsele, Diwagawaga, Jika, Banana, Ngwedzi, Chanana, Chiwele and Ditonki (instrumental). He promised that the album was going to take everybody by storm.

Original_Search_Title: Police Probe Mafia Link Over Sh5 Billion Cocaine Haul
Headline: Kenya: Police Probe Mafia Link Over Sh5 Billion Cocaine Haul
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Routes used by international drug traffickers, with Kenya as a transit point.
Eight people were arrested yesterday over the Sh5 billion cocaine haul, as police investigated a possible link with the Mafia.
Three of them - two Italians and a Kenyan - were seized in Malindi while a fourth key suspect, another Kenyan, was held in Nairobi.
Four others including one Kenyan were arrested in Holland, as the worldwide crackdown on the drug smugglers continued.
The eight are believed to belong to a drugs cartel with agents as far as Colombia and Venezuala in South America, and in Holland and the UK in Europe, as well as in Kenya.
Police were also looking for five Dutch and German citizens who are believed to have rented the villa known as Rocky House in Malindi, where a speed boat loaded with 701 packets of cocaine was seized.
The Dutchmen and the Germans are believed to be hiding in Mombasa after abandoning their getaway car at Malindi airport.
Police in Nairobi have seized a book with the registration numbers of all vehicles that visited the godown belonging to Kenomar International Kenya Ltd., in Embakasi, where 253 packets of cocaine were seized.
One of the cartons in which cocaine disguised as bananas had been stashed.
They also want to question the director of Kenomar to find out who had rented the godown.
"We will not go for the small boys; we shall use the small boys to smoke out the bigger boys," said CID director Joseph Kamau. "We will not allow Kenya to be used both as a conduit or as a destination of drugs."
Mr Kamau announced the seizure of the villa, the speed boat and a vehicle as high level investigations into the drug network began.
The CID boss added police through Interpol were investigating possible links between the haul and the Mafia, which has long been known to be heavily involved in drug smuggling.
Mr Kamau, who led the police team in Malindi accompanied by his deputy Peter Kavila and GSU commandant Lawrence Mwadime, went on: "From now the house, the boat and a vehicle left at the Malindi airport are exhibits and will be the property of the government once the case is finalised".
The villa is in the exclusive Casuarina area, normally patronised by wealthy Italian, German, Dutch and British tourists.
The speedboat, called Outrage 21, was parked outside the house yesterday, still on its trailer.
Part of the drugs from Colombia and Venezuela, which police seized in a godown in Nairobi's Embakasi area on Tuesday night.
It took 10 officers from the team more than four hours to remove the drug packets from the speedboat's deck and place them on the villa's verandah where they were counted, numbered and placed in metal boxes before being carted off by drug squad officers.
Police said the speedboat belonged to Nairobi-based Central Valleys Supplies Ltd.
The white boat with a red canvas roof was found in abandoned in a compound with its massive cargo of drugs.
Investigations revealed that the German and a Dutchman paid Euro 19,900 Euros (about Sh2,150,000) inclusive of freight cost for the boat from its manufacturer in Malaga, Spain, on June 4 this year.
The boat's fuel tank had been removed and in its place were packets of drugs wrapped in silver foil, rubber material and cellotape and each weighing 1.3 kgs.
Neighbours said the Dutchmen began to rent the house some six months ago.
A food vendor said she had always assumed they were tourists on holiday, although once in a while she saw them inside a car in deep conversation with some of Malindi's Italian residents.
"I used to see them seat in their car for a long time with Italian women and men with cottages and villas here in Casuarina, but was shocked to see the GSU officers and receive information that they were actually drug traffickers," the hawker said.
Rocky House, the luxury villa in Casuarina Estate, Malindi, where 701 kilogrammes of cocaine were found.
Two women - a gardener and a househelp - were arrested when they reported to work yesterday but found their house sealed. They were taken to Malindi police station to record statements.
A Kenyan who linked the Italian caretaker of the house to the Dutchmen was arrested and is being held by the police. Police named him as Mr Ibrahim Abdalla.
Malindi deputy police chief Josec Nasio said, "the man should explain how he ended renting the house on behalf of the Dutchmen who wanted to operate from the villa for six months".
Police linked the people who rented the house to those who had also rented the godown in Embakasi.
The house occupants, described by neighbours as elderly, were last seen on Sunday, leaving hurriedly in a Toyota Corolla.
The car was later discovered abandoned at Malindi airport and is believed to have been used by the five drug traffickers minutes before police raided the villa.
They paid for the house owned by an Italian, Mr Pompo Rocchi, for 400 Euros (about Sh43,200) a month, paying six months' rent in advance on September 13 this year.
Officers count packets outside a house in Malindi where another consignment was recovered.
Mr Rocchi had bought the house from Mr Angelo Ricci, who is currently the caretaker and has also been arrested and is helping police with their investigations.
We want to establish how he could have rented the house to the three couples, received the six months rent in advance without any documentation," Mr Kamau said.
Rocky House, a pink four-bedroom villa sits within a large compound ringed by walls of coral reef, draped in bougainvillaea inside a wire mesh fence.
Its compound, which has a kidney shaped swimming pool, is dotted with coconut trees and exotic flowers. It is well lit, with lamp posts rising from the paved walkways.
All the four bed rooms are ensuite and there is a large lounge sweeping back to the pool area. The living room was furnished with a dark mahogany dining table and four chairs and showed signs of having been lived in recently. Four German novels and several English magazines were strewn on a wall cabinet.
The villa is elaborately furnished with antique and exotic furniture. A fully stocked two door fridge stood the kitchen.
Police at the scene yesterday said they have always suspected drugs were transited at the port to Europe, America and South Africa.
"The present seizure now confirms Interpol's date seizure report that the consignees from Pakistan are handled by our sea port," said the Coast CID boss Mr Patrick Ombimo Ochieng.
He added, "In the recent past there has been an interception of heroin and hashish at Pakistan's Karachi sea port destined for the East African port. We do now confirm that the second could have passed unnoticed."
Reported by Dominic Wabala, Mburu Mwangi and Robert Nyagah in Malindi

Original_Search_Title: Movement Leaders in Retreat
Headline: Uganda: Movement Leaders in Retreat
Body_Text:
Ntungamo — More than 1,000 Movement leaders in the southwestern region are meeting
in Ntungamo town in a regional Movement conference.
The conference, the first of its kind in the region dubbed 'NRM SW
Forum' has drawn four participants from each council at levels LC 3-5 in the
districts of Mbarara, Bushenyi, Ntungamo, Kanungu, Rukungiri, Kabale and
Kisoro.
Others attending are RDCs, Movement chairpersons, women and people with
disabilities at sub-county and district level in the region.
They have been addressed by the Vice Chairman of the NRMO, Alhaji Moses
Kigongo, RDCs, MPs and district chairmen.
Many tents were raised at the district headquarters to accommodate the
participants.
President Museveni is expected to close the conference today.
Meanwhile Felix Basiime from Mbarara reports that a State House official
has been rushed to Kashari following the burning of the Kisanja (third term)
symbol by the residents.
On December 4 the youth in Rweibare village, burnt dry banana leaves as
a sign of rejection of the third term for President Yoweri Museveni.
The Movement has adopted dry banana leaves (essanja) as unofficial
symbol for a third term for Museveni. Kashari MP, Maj. John Kazoora,
witnessed the event.
He was flanked by MPs Capt. Guma Gumisiriza (Ibanda North) and Johnson
Nkuuhe (Isingiro South).
Kazoora was sensitising his constituents on the cabinet's White Paper
which recommends the lifting of presidential term limits from the
Constitution to allow Museveni stand for another term.

Original_Search_Title: Police Seize Sh5 Billion Worth of Europe-Bound Cocaine in Major Raids
Headline: Kenya: Police Seize Sh5 Billion Cocaine Haul in Raids
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police in Nairobi and Mombasa last night seized narcotic drugs worth a
massive Sh5.3 billion.
"This is the largest haul we have ever had in the history of Africa, we
want to send a message out to drug traffickers that they cannot hold this
country to ransom," said Commissioner of Police Major General Mohammed
Hussein Ali.
A team from the CID, the Anti-Narcotics Unit, the Special Crime
Prevention Unit and the General Service Unit raided a godown in Nairobi's
Embakasi area and seized 53 packets of cocaine sealed in a refrigerated
container.
Another team in Malindi intercepted a boat heading out to the high seas
and seized 701 packets of cocaine.
"We have seized a speed boat which was headed to the high sea and found
a haul of drugs. Combined with what we have here it is worth Sh5.3 billion
in European market," said CID Director Joseph Kamau at the scene of the
Embakasi raid.
Disguised as bananas
The cocaine was shipped into the country disguised as bananas from
Colombia and Venezuela.
Shipping documents seized during the raid indicated the consignment
belonged to cargo company based in Industrial Area.
The raid on the premises was conducted by a contingent of over 200 armed
police officers in two trucks, six Land Rovers and 10 other vehicles.
The drugs were to be shipped to the Netherlands disguised as industrial
roofing tiles.
Maj-Gen Ali said that the Kenya security forces were liaising with the
police in the Netherlands, Venezuela and Colombia through the Interpol to
try and catch the drug smugglers.
The Mombasa consignment was part of the Nairobi one and were to be
shipped together. The drugs were packed in two 40-foot shipping
containers.
It is believed that the drugs are transported to Nairobi for packaging,
before they are later transported back to Mombasa for shipping.
In Nairobi, the drugs are opened, re-packaged into smaller packets,
wrapped in polythene papers before being put into shipping containers for
transportation to Mombasa by road.
Police believe that once in Mombasa, the drugs are initially shipped out
clandestinely in small boats and then transferred to big ships on the high seas.
The police suspect that the business had been going on for a long time
in the Embakasi godown which purportedly deals in roofing tiles.

Original_Search_Title: Libya Wants to Import Uganda's Fruits
Headline: Uganda: Libya Wants to Import Uganda's Fruits
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA'S ambassador to Libya, Williams Hakiza, has asked Ugandans to start exporting tropical fruits to the north African country.
Speaking to a Ugandan delegation in Tripoli last week, Hakiza said tropical fruits like bananas, mangoes and pineapples have a potential market in Libya, which is undergoing economic liberalisation.
"l have told the foreign affairs and trade, industry and tourism ministries to advise Ugandans to exhibit tropical fruits in Libya, but there is no response," the ambassador said.
Hakiza said lack of direct flights from Entebbe to Tripoli to transport perishable goods would hinder the venture.

Original_Search_Title: Kayunga's Oldest School On the Verge of Collapse
Headline: Uganda: Kayunga's Oldest School On the Verge of Collapse
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE oldest school in Kayunga district is on the verge of collapse unless major repairs are carried out very soon.
Despite a history of having moulded scores of well-placed people in the country, 94-year- old Ndeeba Church of Uganda primary school is now a display of neglect and derelict.
The 40-acre UPE school is perched on Ndeeba hill, about half a kilometre from Bukolooto along Kayunga-Jinja road, 49 miles from Kayunga town.
The classroom blocks have neither doors nor window shutters. The iron sheets are rusty and rotten, and termites have eaten up all the roofing timber. The classroom floors are dusty and the cement is all gone. When it rains the classrooms flood and get muddy. Both the pupils and teachers scramble for dry places.
"I predict a nasty situation if the old boys and girls and well-wishers don't do something soon for the school," says Ziwa Zirimala , an OB of the school. "Should there be a heavy rainfall or strong wind, these buildings will collapse on the children."
Richard Kulata, the head teacher says the government does not cater for the renovation of old buildings but puts up new ones where there are none.
Kulata cannot direct the school facility grant for renovation. The UPE capitation grant is not only inadequate but also segmented to meet other requirements like buying scholastic materials. Nothing is for maintenance. Moreover the grant is not given every year. It rotates to other schools in the district.
Zirimala says the school problems been worsened by the community's impoverishment after coffee and bananas which used to be the main source of income in the district, were destroyed by disease.
"The parents have no means of supporting the school. The only solution is for the old boys and old girls to fund raise," says Zirimala.
Unfortunately the first OBs and OGs meeting that was called on November 19 to raise money for renovation was a total flop. Not even the area Member of Parliament and education ministry officials showed up.
"The school has produced many high-profile people but it is a pity that despite the invitations that we sent out they have not shown up to restore the school back to its old glory," said Godfrey Kyeyune, an old boy and director of Rubaga Youth Development Association (RYDA) Buloba.
He donated a computer to the school and pledged to buy 10 bags of cement.
Some of the people who have gone through the school include Kitaka Gawela, chairman District Service Commission, Betty Makumbi, chairperson LC III of Kayunga Town Council, Dr. Kefa Kamya Semakula Bishop of West Uganda, Oliver Makumbi, a lecturer at the Ndejje University.
The school started in 1910 as a sub grade centre under Church of Uganda. In 1926, it was upgraded to a primary school. It was taken over by the central government in 1942.
Today the school has 22 teachers with a population of 750 pupils. In 2002 it had seven first grades and the following year there were 17 in the first grade. Will Ndeeba Primary School continue to groom Kayunga leaders?

Original_Search_Title: Zambia Bottlers Launches Top One Maheu Drink
Headline: Zambia Bottlers Launches Top One Maheu Drink
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA has high investment opportunities in the food drink market, Zambia Bottlers sales manager, Kenneth Sinkamba has said.
Speaking at the launch of the company's new brand of Maheu called Top One, Mr Sinkamba said this had prompted his company to concentrate on selling the product on the local market for the time being.
Top One Maheu is a full cream milk based food drink that contains calcium, proteins and carbohydrates.
The product comes in four flavours of butter cream, strawberry, banana and traditional flavours and will sell at K1,800 per 500 milliliters.
"We will concentrate on the Zambian market before we go outside the country because it offers high opportunities in the food drink segment," Mr Sinkamba said.
Top One Maheu is packed in tetra pack containers which are environmentally friendly.
"We are aware that the Zambian consumer is concerned about the environment that is why we have packed our Maheu in the tetra pack which is paper and easily decomposes," Mr Sinkamba said.

Original_Search_Title: Behold the Banana Split Republic
Headline: Zimbabwe: Behold the Banana Split Republic
Body_Text:
DIVISIONS in the central African basket case's ruling party worsened this week, leading to speculation that the country would soon become a banana split republic. The misinformation minister's ejection from the ruling Zany Party's central committee led to further debate about whether he had been working clandestinely for the More Drink Coming Party all along.
The move left the ruling Zany Party reeling in a certain amount of disarray as its members speculated about moving towards an election next year with its chief spin doctor in no mood to cooperate.
However, it wasn't in such disarray that it wasn't able to pass yet another bit of enabling legislation. If readers of this newspaper believe the new NGO Act isn't enabling, then they are wrong. The new law enables the Zany Party, the Zany police and anyone else connected to Zany structures to close down anyone who might be helping victims of violence. In that sense, the law couldn't be more enabling.
So, the troubled central African banana split republic doesn't mean that the Zany Party is any less vindictive - or that a new mood of conciliatoriness has emerged from within the ranks. Stand by for some formidable action against anyone taking foreign currency from enemy states.
Over The Top understands that Zany structures will tolerate NGOs taking money from friendly states like Cuba, China and North Korea - but sadly none of those states have any money and even if they did, it isn't the sort of currency even troubled central Africans would want.
Enemy states are those western powers with the sort of money troubled central Africans like best. The sort of money that actually buys things you can use. In the interest of helping troubled central Africans understand the new law, it needs to be explained that friendly states offer a currency known locally as zhing zhong. If anyone offers you this money, make sure you count it - and your fingers - afterwards.
Of course, zhing zhong money doesn't actually work. It only buys things like zhing zhong shoes, shirts, electrical appliances and ugly ornamental lights - and none of those work either.
Still, the new law leaves troubled central African NGOs with a conundrum. They can break the law by accepting proper western money which buys much needed food, bandages and pain killers, all of which are useful given Zany law enforcement measures. On the other hand, they can legally (well, almost) accept zhing zhong money which doesn't really buy anything except things that don't work and aren't any use.
The new Zany split party is, of course, making heavy use of laws that were drafted by ministers that are no longer in favour. That means that if there's a cabinet reshuffle, it doesn't really matter. The laws already exist and the ministers have served their purpose so if they're cast into the wilderness, tough luck. The Zany Party's been doing it for the last 30 years, so no one should be surprised by what is happening.
Anyway, the new law coming from the split Zany Party isn't a surprise, but it is unfortunate news for victims of Zany violence and other enemies of the state who may find they're now adding bandages to the long list of things that are in short supply in the troubled central African police state.

Original_Search_Title: MP Kiwanuka Crosses to Movt
Headline: Uganda: MP Kiwanuka Crosses to Movt
Body_Text:
Masaka — The Member of Parliament (MP) for Bukomansimbi, Mr Morris Kiwanuka Kajimu, has crossed from the DP to the movement.
Kajimu, also the son of the first prime minister of Uganda, the late Benedicto Kiwanuka, declared his decision to the vice president, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya, at Butenga sub-county on Tuesday.
Ms Margaret Mbabazi, locally known as Maama Kisanja, put a crown of dry banana leaves on his head.
Several other DP members of the Municipality crossed to the Movement on the same occasion.
They included the speaker of Masaka Municipal Council, Mr Emmanuel Ssemango and Mr Henry Busurwa, a councilor of Nyendo.
Only three members of the Municipal Council did not cross to the Movement.
They are the mayor, Eng. John Matovu, the Deputy Mayor, Mr Mulumba Sseddugge and Mr Godfrey Kayemba, the Chairman of the Masaka Uganda Young Democrats.
The defectors told The Monitor that they had decided to cross over to the Movement system because the Matovu, a staunch DP member, had accused them of corruption.

Original_Search_Title: Catwalk Clothes: for Sale Or Show?
Headline: Uganda: Catwalk Clothes: for Sale Or Show?
Body_Text:
Kampala — FASHION shows world over are a glitzy affair, graced with both flamboyant and hair-raising designs. But are some of the extraordinary outfits exhibited for sale or just for the show? Raphael Okello talked to some local fashion designers.
The Uganda international Fashion Week hosted local and international designers celebrating fashion with an African theme.
There were some outrageous designs and then some outrageous designs. Collections that left people wondering if anyone could wear them and stroll along any street in the world without bringing it to a stand still!
"Every designer needs to have an outfit that is a showstopper, one that acts as a signature creation for their design house. Usually it is the most creative and extravagant design in outfit and fabric," explains Santa Anzo, proprietor of Arapapa Consultancy Ltd and organiser of the Ugandan fashion Week.
In her perspective, it is imperative for every designer to have an outfit that draws the audience to their label - an outfit that acts as a marketing tool.
"During fashion shows, we think of marketing the label, sales and public relations. How else can a designer succeed in achieving all that if they do not stand out," she wonders.
The principal and director, Tiner International School of Beauty, Ruth Kibirige, consents, "The bottom line is, during fashion shows, clothes help to sell and market the design house."
However, fashion shows held by the different beauty and design schools are staged with a different concept from that of established designers. They are not held with the idea of sale.
"During school fashion shows, we examine students in different areas that have been taught. We may ask students to create an outfit from bottle tops, buttons, banana fibres or leaves. Our motive is not for them to create a captivating design for sale but, to try and find out how far a student's imagination can stretch," Rebecca Langa, fashion guru and director of Dolphin Fashions, Ntinda, explains.
Sylvia Owori, proprietor Zipa Models, concurs that some of the collections are showcased not for sale, but to portray the creativity of the designer and to also spice up the show.
"You cannot have a boring show. For the audience to enjoy a show, a designer should have a few clothes, not necessarily for sale, but to depict how a creative mind can work," she says.
Although objectives for holding school fashion shows slightly differ from those of established fashion designers, Langa agrees that established designers also exercise their minds during fashion shows since design is all about imagination and how best one can fit a fabric on somebody.
"There is a difference between creativity and nudity. Some designers think a fashion stopper has to reveal so much flesh. It should enhance the model and not disgrace her," Santa says.
Does that mean some 'fashion stoppers' can be worn?
"Yes, everything can be worn but only for the occasion they have been designed," says Kibirige. "Normally, each design has got a purpose. It could be for theatre, wedding or safari."
To wear some of the 'fashion stoppers' and be able to go out in public, "one must be bold, confident and have an ego that lets them soar high," says Santa.
A case in point: Arapapa's signature creation, the fashion stopper, during last year's fashion week, 100% Arapapa, was a sell-out. It is a novel design originally conceived from the Busuti. The elegant blue outfit has a tussled long sash that flourishes all the way to the ankles from the waist, very long and graceful fitting sleeves that flair out beyond the hands, a necklace and a long hat.
While the concept of showcasing out-of-this-world designs may remain an enigma to an ordinary soul, to a designer, it may decide their market share.

Original_Search_Title: I Prayed for Rain in Shrine, Says VP
Headline: Uganda: I Prayed for Rain in Shrine, Says VP
Body_Text:
Kampala — VICE-PRESIDENT Prof. Gilbert Bukenya has said he prayed for rain and not a third term as alleged in the press.
"In my mind I prayed to God to give me more strength to complete my tour of Masaka since I was very tired. I also prayed for rain since the area is dry and the rice very young. I prayed in my Catholic way," he said.
Bukenya said there was no need to ask for a third term because people were already demanding it.
"Another term for President Yoweri Museveni is fait accompli so I don't need to go to a shrine to pray for that. Just go to the countryside and see the massive support he has," Bukenya told journalists at his Ntinda office yesterday.
Bukenya was reacting to media reports, which said he consulted witchdoctors on the third term for Museveni.
"I prayed in my own way. I prayed in my mind. I don't know how your news people read my mind. You have portrayed me as a Catholic who also goes to shrines so that the Catholics hate me. That is between my God and me. They will never hate me," he said.
Bukenya visited a shrine at Masaka on Monday on the invitation of Hajji Bruhan Katale.
"I was very surprised to see headlines that Bukenya prays in shrine for third term. Everyone knows that I am a Catholic 3-1-1 because I was baptised when I was a month old. But I respect other religious groups. I participate in what they do. I have prayed in the mosques, Anglican, Balokole and Orthodox churches. When I see them praying very hard, I also bow and pray in my Catholic way," he said.
"There is no single MP who can go to the villages in Buganda and talk about anti-third term. I don't need to campaign or pray for it. You see them wearing ssanja (dry banana leaves) everywhere. I need to pray for my rice," Bukenya added.

Original_Search_Title: Film Workshop With Stakeholders On the Cards
Headline: Namibia: Film Workshop With Stakeholders On the Cards
Body_Text:
A JOINT workshop between itself and the Oruuano Artists Union on the local film industry is to be held in early January, it was resolved by the Namibia Film Commission (NFC) at its last meeting of the year last week.
"It is imperative that all stakeholders be brought together if the Namibian film industry is to flourish. Our members are ready and willing to serve and contribute towards the betterment of the industry, if allowed to participate," said Banana Shekupe, president of the Oruuano Artists Union during consultations with the Namibia Film Commission's board of directors during the meeting.
Shekupe and the union's secretary-general, Vincent Mwemba, met with the board as part of their ongoing efforts to get Namibian artists more actively involved in the film industry.
"The NFC has made great strides with regard to the implementation of rules and financial regulations to be put in place in the coming year. We will only then be in a position to financially assist the local industry, once the NFC has a budget in place," Nkrumah Mushelenga, chairman of the NFC assured the union-delegation.
"Our members are very keen to make creative inputs on various creative levels in the film industry," Mwemba told the meeting, which was also addressed by one of the producers of the Namibian feature movie, The Pay Master.
Obed Mvula informed the meeting on progress made with the film that is to become a joint venture between Namibian and South African filmmakers, once finances have been secured.
"I would like to thank the local business fraternity and the NFC for contributions made so far. However, to realize the project we would be needing much more support from local and international sources," Mvula told the meeting.
The Namibian Film Commission has also endorsed a possible co-production treaty between itself and the Fiji Film Commission.
The film stakeholders workshop is scheduled to be held during the second week in January in Windhoek.

Original_Search_Title: Abanda Angry Over Czech Racism
Headline: Cameroon: Abanda Angry Over Czech Racism
Body_Text:
He was recently the victim of racist behaviour from a masseur working for his former club Sparta Prague.
Cameroon's Patrice Abanda has criticised the failure of a Czech football anti-racism campaign after becoming victim of another racist incident. The government-funded campaign aimed at stamping out racism was launched amid wide publicity three months ago by Prime Minister Stanislav Gross. The crusade featured media and educational projects and full-page adverts in newspapers and magazines.
But Abanda, who won a gold medal at the 2000 Cameroon Olympic champion side and now plays with Czech first division side Drnovice, said the campaign had little effect. "The only change which I see is the appearance of plaques in stadiums saying 'Football yes, racism no'. That's all that I have noticed," he said. "Otherwise, it is the same chants, cries of monkey, banana skins, all that," he added. "It's not only with me, but also with other black players who have come to play here."
Last Friday, Abanda was the victim of racist behaviour from a masseur working for his former club Sparta Prague. "At the end of the match, as I was leaving the pitch, he said to me in Czech 'You blackman, why do you make mistakes' and I said nothing and continued walking. After he said ' You are ill', I could not take it any more," he recalled. Drnovice coach Josef Mazura stopped a fight breaking out between the two men.

Original_Search_Title: Gibbons Wins Fishing Gala
Headline: Uganda: Gibbons Wins Fishing Gala
Body_Text:
Kampala — COLETTE Gibbons has won the first Crane fishing competition held last weekend at Entebbe Sailing Club, winning herself a revolving trophy.
Gibbons, returned to the shore with a 42-kilogramme Nile Perch to beat off 20 anglers to the day's biggest catch out of Lake Victoria.
The competition was sponsored by Crane Bank in which boats also competed in sizes.
Andrew Estill was runner-up, returning with a 32- kilogramme Nile Perch while Piet Strauss came third after securing a 25- kilogramme fish of the same species.
According to one of the organisers of the inaugural fishing competition Phil Cooper, the fishing conditions on the lake were favourable and calm, though not very good for the anglers.
Crane Bank's Managing Director Robert Warlow and the bank's new face, former Big Brother housemate Gaetano Kaggwa, handed over the prizes to the winners in the various competition categories.
Prizes included fishing rods and an assortment of other fishing gear.
It now becomes a yearly event on the Entebbe sailing club calendar.
