Original_Search_Title: Sankil Unveils New Products in Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Sankil Unveils New Products in Lagos
Body_Text:
Lagos — No fewer than three brands of snacks including Golden chips, potatoes and nuts have been launched by Sankil Nigeria Limited, a fast food company in Lagos.
A statement from the company said Golden Nuts, which are crispy coated delicious peanuts can be enjoyed by children of all ages. Golden Chips Potatoes and another of its products Golden Chips Banana which are available in internationally popular classic salted flavour taste fabulously crispy and right.
Sankil said "Goldie, the happy go lucky fella is the mascot of the Golden Nuts. His philosophy is 'Don't worry Be Happy'. He believes that while there may be moments of happiness and sadness in our lives, we should never let worry run our lives, because as we accomodate it, happiness goes out the next door. So 'be happy because if you have trouble and you worry, you would only make it double."
The company said it has set up its production processes to minimize not only errors, but also to meet the highest quality standards, adding that it is in this vein that te National Agency for Food, Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC) has certify its products fit for consumption.

Original_Search_Title: Union Forges Ahead With Arts Programmes
Headline: Namibia: Union Forges Ahead With Arts Programmes
Body_Text:
ORUUANO Artists Union is to act as facilitator for a soon-to-be-established UNESCO-spearheaded multi-purpose arts and culture cooperative in Namibia.
This was announced on Monday by the union's leadership in Windhoek.
"We are very much encouraged by the response from artists to our first meeting last Saturday to launch this exciting venture early next year. The groundwork has been laid by those present at the meeting and the mechanics have been worked out on how to proceed further," said Vincent Mwemba, secretary-general of the union.
According to the Banana Shekupe, president of the union, the future cooperative will have a lot of benefits for artists.
"The decision to start country cooperatives was taken earlier this year at an international meeting of the Federation of Artists Unions around the world, based on complaints that artists struggle with promoting their products. Through this new initiative artists will now be empowered and enter the business world by formally promoting and selling their artistic products, skills and abilities in a more orchestrated and tangible manner. This cooperative offers them that unique chance," said Shekupe.
The cooperative will function as an independent and autonomous body under the auspices of the union.
"Our union was mandated to initiate the first cooperative due to the country's infrastructure potential, democracy, stability and peace. All art disciplines will be included in the operations of the cooperative unlike in the past when only music shows and musical products were emphasized and promoted. Union and non-union members are eligible to join the cooperative, " Mwemba, who indicated that a UNESCO delegation will be visiting Namibia for the launch of the cooperative, said.
Shekupe also announced that his union is in the process of putting together an arts and culture troupe for next year's world famous Edinburgh Festival in Scotland.
"The union has been formally approached by the British High Commission in Windhoek to look at such a possibility. Unlike in the past with the Arts Department of the Ministry of Basic Education, Sport and Culture, the union will make the selection of artists to represent Namibia at the Edinburgh festival in a democratic and transparent manner," said Shekupe.
In his view that Department of Arts has in the past selected groups that never could match the professionalism of their counterparts at such festivals.
"Such selected groups with known connections to certain individuals within that department always made the grade without real merit. It was a question of whom you knew and not what you could artistically produce. We will fight this wrong and unacceptable approach tooth and nail because such biased selections will destroy the arts industry," Banana said angrily.
Other news from the union is that solid contacts have been made with the Finnish artists union by way of an existing arts exchange programme.
"We have recently returned from Finland where we successfully performed the collective musical stage production Hope. The Finnish artists union has embarked on a programme to assist Namibian musicians with musical instruments and other much needed equipment, too. In the process we have also made contact with a high school that is very anxious and keen to start up an arts and culture exchange programme with a Namibian secondary school," reported Shekupe.

Original_Search_Title: One Name, Two Bands
Headline: Kenya: One Name, Two Bands
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sungusia. That is the name everyone is dancing to in Kisii, Gucha and Nyamira districts.
The band has become so popular that two groups, one in Nyamira and the other in Kisii, are now fighting each other over the name.
The Nation traced the origins of Sungusia to one Elias Kingoina, 52, who works with the ministry of education in Nyamira District. He may be in the business of providing education, but Kiongona believes that music runs in his blood, in genes he inherited from his grandfathers Keraba Nyasae Julius Omandi and Maranga Angwenyi, who were expert players of the guitar and obokano, a traditional stringed instrument.
While working in Narok District in the 1980s, Kingoina bought a guitar, then joined a group of Zaireans who used to perform at a nightclub there. Then he was transferred to Nyamira in the early 1990s, where he approached some church choir guitarists, one of whom was Joseph Siko, and formed Nyamira Superstars.
They did not have enough equipment, save for a guitar and tins for drums. But Lady
Luck smiled on them when a local politician bought them more instruments.
In 1997, they composed an eight-track album titled Sweet Banana, which catapulted them to fame. Recording under the new name, Bana Sungusia Ebirenge Band, the eight-member group produced another hit, Moraa Nyaata, which sold very many units. But their third album, which was a mixture of lingala and benga, was not as well received as the first two.
Gradually, a misunderstanding among band members forced Siko and Kingoina to leave.
"We left the band without anything despite our vital role in its formation," says Kingoina. The two remained out of active music until a year later, when they moved to Kisii and a businessman offered to buy them equipment worth Sh300,000. Thus well armed, they poached some of the members of the Nyamira-based Sungusia, then formed the Bana Sungusia Superstars. Since the break-up, the Kingoina-led Sungusia has produced four tracks, one of which is an attack on their Nyamira-based namesake.
Rivalry aside, the Kisii-based band has been boosted by the inclusion of respected Kisii benga maestro Christopher Monyoncho of Kegogi Jazz Band and Henry Mesa "Computer" of Obwari Soukous. Mesa is currently the lead vocolist and guitarist. With the entry of Monyoncho, now in his mid-'60, and members of the late John Arisi Osababu's Kirwanda Jazz Band, the group has now changed its name to Sungusia Coalition. Siko, too, does vocals, and is the key composer, while other members of the band include Elias Kingoina (rhythmist), Ongera (bassist) and Kennedy Tedius (technician). Their producer is a retired soldier, Omariba Onsomu, who has bought them additional equipment worth another Sh300,000.
The group's biggest break came when they were invited to perform at the cultural Ribina (Kisii) Night held at Nairobi's Panafric Hotel early this year.
Despite their relative success, says Siko, piracy is their main undoing. "When our fans listen to pirated music whose quality is poor, they assume that the original was poorly done and we end up losing their support." He laments the fact that they end up sharing their sweat with thieves.
So how does Kingoina manage his time between music composition, running the band and his civil service job. "I go to work during the day and return to my music after work and during weekends," he explains. The band has so far released 18 songs and is currently working on others. They also play at public and private events. On Monday this week, for instance, they were at Nyanturago Cultural Event at the invitation of Energy Minister Simeon Nyachae. This weekend, they will perform at Isibania Boarder Point Restaurant on the Kenya-Tanzanian border.

Original_Search_Title: A Variety of Brands to Choose From
Headline: Uganda: A Variety of Brands to Choose From
Body_Text:
Kampala — With the advent of the HIV/Aids scourge, there are no two ways as to
whether a condom should be an integral part for those that opt for the C in
the ABC anti-Aids war strategy.
Halima Namakula and Bebe Cool sing as much in their popular
cross-generational hit Sambagala. The former sings about falling pregnant as
the only fear the pre-Aids sexually active woman had, while the latter's
retort is that with HIV, there is no taking chances.
However, most condom users seem to be stuck with the three leading
low-priced heavily marketed brands and are quite oblivious of a varied array
alternate brands mainly retailing in fuel station shops, pharmacies and
upscale supermarkets.
The Preventor brand comes in a colourful pack and includes classic,
extra strong which is thicker and tutti frutti with choice flavours like
strawberry, coconut/mint and banana flavours.
They boast of being anatomically shaped for extra sensitivity and are
made in Belgium.
Wet n' Wild has a picture of the back view of a swimsuit-clad woman with
a jet of water splashing on her tanned body.
The condoms in this three pack are lubricated and promise to overcome
friction and provide that great natural feeling of "wetness" in a most
provocative way.
Although it carries a quality assurance seal, it has a disclaimer that
reads; this product contains natural rubber latex that may cause allergic
reactions. They are manufactured in the U.S.
The Romantic Fit-Tex brand comes in a floral pack and boasts of a long
shock, anatomic shape promising sensitivity and satisfaction.
The reason you should use it is that it follows the curvature of the
glands simply hugging the penis shape and prevents premature leakage while
in use. You can choose from the ribbed rocket or the smooth version of these
Malaysian-made condoms.
Bareback is the super-sensitive condom for those who want to feel as
though they are using nothing at all.
The woman with a bareback, leaning forward with just a cloth covering
her bust probably says as much.
It is ideal for those whose notion of condom use is akin to eating a
sweet with its wrappings on.
The US-made lubricated condom, however, carries a disclaimer that reads;
use of a condom cannot guarantee 100% protection against pregnancy or STIs
although it is tested over three times to ensure the highest quality and
maximum reliability.
The burly brief-clad man sporting a hairy chest and tugging at a thick
rope on the Powerplay brand has got some to christen it as a gay condom. The
condoms in this three-pack are lubricated and are extra strong but very
sensitive. This US-made condom is thicker to prevent breakage and contains a
heavy application of the spermicidal nonoxynol-9, which has been proven
effective in killing sperm in lab tests, on the inside and outside.
The Erotica brand's tagline is "vibra-ribbed for pleasure" and its
poster girl is clad in a leopard print swimsuit exposing her bare back. The
lubricated condom is covered with row after row of raised ribbing that cover
the full length of the condom for the maximum pleasure for both partners but
is specifically designed to create an ecstatic response from women. It is
U.S. made, of the highest quality latex.
Rough Rider is a household brand and still has its signature Caucasian
lady staring hard clad in an unbuttoned blue denim jacket partly covering
her bust as its poster girl.
The studded condom boasts of having hundreds of raised rubber studs that
make it the condom offering maximum sensation and excitement.
It's manufacturers say it was designed for the free spirits seeking to
shed inhibitions and the individual who wants maximum performance.
*The distinctive feature about the Midnight brand is that it is a
black-coloured condom unlike most brands that are white or creamish. Its
cover girl is a lady in seductive sleep position with black cloth ample
enough to cover her torso. The US-made lubricated condom is erotically ebony
to add spice to romance and is meant to provide the added dimension of
visual excitement that will keep you and your partner coming back for more!
Are you condom user?
Be sure to have a condom before needing one.
Use a new condom for every sexual act and one condom for each partner
when having multiple intercourse.
Handle the condom carefully, gently pinch the bulb to squeeze out the
air inside, and then unroll the condom down around the erect penis.
Use a water-based lubricant only.
Never use vaseline, butter or hand cream since these can damage the
condom. Withdraw after ejaculation, holding the condom firmly at its
base.
If the condom is sticky, breaks easily or if its colour is not even,
take another one.
Be careful never to reuse a condom.
Check the expiry date on the packaging.
If the condom breaks, consult a doctor within 24 hours.
Keep the condom in a cool, dry place out of direct sunlight.
Added info from the Net

Original_Search_Title: Women Activists Say No to Kisanja
Headline: Uganda: Women Activists Say No to Kisanja
Body_Text:
Kampala — Are women falling out with President Yoweri Museveni? Recently, 18 high-profile women activists went to Parliament to oppose the lifting of the presidential term limit, popularly known as kisanja (dry banana leaves.
Led by the Uganda Women's Network (UWONET), an advocacy and lobbying coalition of national women's organisations, institutions and individuals in Uganda, the activists shocked the country when they opposed the kisanja.
They argued that amending the constitution to allow Museveni another term in office would undermine respect for constitutionalism, which in turn would mean the constitutional provisions which protect women might easily be scrapped or altered in future.
Jackie Asiimwe Mwesigwa, a lawyer, says although many women admire Museveni and would give him their votes for another term, they would not want to violate the presidential term limit provision of the constitution.
"Many women like Museveni. But what if a monster like Idi Amin (former Uganda's president) assumes state power after we have shown no respect for the constitution by supporting kisanja?"
Solome Kimbogue, acting co-ordinator of UWONET, says during their consultative meetings recently financed by Frederick Ebert Stiftung (FES) in 26 districts, many women leaders said they fear intimidation and harassment which explains why they have not openly opposed ekisanja.
The women, like men, she says, have the right to declare their fears and what they support about a political system without any fear of being intimidated. "Many rural women lack confidence to give their opinion on ekisanja," says Kimbongue adding that women activists realised there were issues of their emancipation that Museveni, as an individual, could not grant them because they are institutional matters.
Kimbogue says women want leaders to respect the constitution.
Without the culture of respect of the constitution, she says, there will be nothing like good governance and democracy in Uganda. Today it is kisanja, tomorrow it may be affirmative action provisions repealed," she said. Hajat Janat Mukwaya, justice and constitutional affairs minister differs from the views of the activist.
She argues that they don't necessarily represent the views of majority women. "The activist are entitled to their views but there is no common position from women on ekisanja. I have consulted with my constituency and women have varying views about the matter."
It is a bumpy ride to emancipation but Ugandan women have scored some success, which according to the activists, could be lost by supporting kisanja. Their view is a departure from a popular view held by many Ugandans that the women, who constitute more than half of the countries estimated to be 25 million, are inseparable from Museveni.
For many rural women, government policy of Universal Primary Education (UPE) is seen as Museveni's effort to emancipate them. Traditionally, women shoulder the burden of the education of children. With UPE, the enrolment of children is 7.3 million, from 2.7 million, eight years ago. Economically, many women are engaged in income-generating activities, a progress they attribute to Museveni.
Many grassroots women have been mobilised into groups to access loans as start-up funds for their businesses. State minister for gender Sam Bitangaro says many women associate their emancipation with Museveni because its promotion received a strong foundation from his government.
Giving an example of Dr. Specioza Kazibwe, the former vice president, Bitangaro said Museveni has given his personal backing to improve the living conditions of most women in the country. In a paper, "Gender analysis of the White Paper (WP): Assessing its implications on women's political participation in Uganda," submitted to the legal and parliamentary committee of parliament last month, the activists assert that they see sense in maintaining the presidential term limit it in accordance with the current constitutional provisions.
They say the practice of changing presidents regularly is in line with the principle of good governance and democracy and will protect the citizens from dictatorship.
The process of amending a constitution is strenuous and expensive, Betty Amongin, woman MP for Apac says. She observes that that if it can be avoided now by opposing the ekisanja, it would protect women from dictators who may want to usurp women's achievements.
Martin Masiga, national co-ordinator Human Rights Network (HURINET) says the position taken by the activists reflects the growth of the women's movement in Uganda. "The women's statement portrays their ability to fight for democracy. It is a departure from reliance on Museveni as an individual."
Masiga says that anybody who needs women's support has to recognise them as an independent and mature constituency. "If the Movement wants the women's vote, they will have to provide strong support to win the women back," he says.
Sylvia Tamale, Dean Faculty of Law says the position taken by the women is to ensure democratic rule and they cannot be bribed to change their decision. The activists however, support shifting from Movement to Multi-party politics, saying it would open up space to dissenting and minority voices.
But they object to the government's proposal to remove the secret ballot for the election of LC1 and LC2 saying adult suffrage does not allow especially, women, to exercise their right of free choice; but are subjected to pressure by spouses, family members and friends to choose a candidate against their will.
For interest group representation, particularly in Parliament, women want a minimum of 40% from 24.6%. The task for ensuring that a common position from women is reached and passed by parliament lies solely in the hands of the MPs, particularly, the women MPs who represent the majority rural women.

Original_Search_Title: Investors Should Pay And Treat Workers Well
Headline: Uganda: Investors Should Pay And Treat Workers Well
Body_Text:
Kampala — This is bad news that will make your ears tingle: Uganda is Africa's
worst employer.
A survey by the International Confederation of Free Trade Unions-Africa
(ICFTU) has established that Uganda has the worst working environment in
Africa.
The survey also holds President Yoweri Museveni personally responsible
for the mess, by his policy of treating investors with kid gloves.
There is nothing new about the revelations of this survey; this is stuff
we have always known. The only fresh angle is the fact that it now comes
from an independent, dis-interested body of international repute, and is
based on a systematic, scientific study.
We applaud the President for his attempts to stimulate economic growth
by increased external investment. That is a vital engine of growth in every
banana republic. Problem is that in his hurry, the President has forgotten
to put the country first.
The father who chooses to please his rich guests at the expense of his
family is not a paterfamilias worth his salt.
These so-called investors know they are the apple of the President's
eye; trophies that he holds up proudly as an indicator of economic
growth. They presume, and correctly so, that they can get away with many a
fault - including abuse of workers.
Many of these employers forget Uganda is our country, and just because
they pay our wages, will treat us like machines or animals. Trade Unions are
thrown out with threats, our people work long, abnormal and unhealthy hours
for very little pay. Many can be fired at short notice without reasonable
cause and without compensation.
There is deliberate, systematic and sustained abuse of human rights in
many institutions; but Government officials are reluctant to tackle these
head-on, because they fear the inevitable crack of the whip by the
President's Office for 'frustrating investors'.
Many of these are not even investors in the strict sense of the word;
just glorified conmen without a penny to their name. But because they meet
our criteria of 'investor' - a non-black skin and nationality of some Asian
Tiger or oil-rich Gulf state, they simply walk into State House, talk big
and walk away with billions of taxpayers' money.
Government and Parliament should come to the rescue of our people. We
need sound labour laws that pin every employer, regardless of skin colour,
to treat our people humanely and pay them decently.

Original_Search_Title: DEC Blocks K11bn Payment to Dubious Food Suppliers
Headline: Zambia: DEC Blocks K11bn Payment to Dubious Food Suppliers
Body_Text:
THE Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC) has blocked payment of K11 billion to dubious food suppliers although K4 billion had already been paid out in the illegal foodstuff transactions.
DEC Spokesperson, Betty Mumba said yesterday the DEC's anti-money laundering unit discovered the scam and blocked the payment to fake food suppliers.
"Through the efforts being made at the anti-money laundering unit, the commission has blocked payments amounting to K11 billion that would have been paid to dubious suppliers.
"The commission is determined to protect Zambia's economy by ensuring that money laundering activities are completely eradicated," she said.
And the commission has arrested 10 people for money laundering, drug trafficking, attempting to obtain and obtaining money by false pretences amounting to more than K500 million.
Ms Mumba said Francis Mulenshi 36 of plot number 477 Kabwata Site and Service and Dora Mukabe of the same residential address were arrested and jointly charged for attempting to obtain K265 million from which K45 million had been obtained by false pretences.
Ms Mumba said John Koko of house number 7 Matero was also arrested for obtaining K55 million as part payment from the total K143 million he was claiming from Zambia Army for fake food supplies.
She said the commission had also arrested Morgan Kalunga Sikazwe 37 of plot number 9 Helen Kaunda and Bennon Ngosa 28 of Chilanga Estates and jointly charged them with Kalo Mwambazi for fraud and money laundering involving K55 million.
For drug offences, DEC arrested Amos Zimba 23 unemployed of house number 44 Twin Palm road, Kabulonga and Chisulo Phiri 30, who trafficked in 64 balls of cannabis known as Malawian Gold weighing 3.707kilogrammes concealed in banana leaves.
She said the DEC had also arrested Luke Zulu 38 of Kapondela village for trafficking in 184 balls and eight packets of loose cannabis.

Original_Search_Title: Celebrating Fruits of University Cooperation
Headline: Cameroon: Celebrating Fruits of University Cooperation
Body_Text:
The 30th anniversary of the university and scientific cooperation between France/Cameroon was celebrated yesterday.
Rectors of the six State Universities and other research institutes in the country, yesterday at the Conference centre in Yaounde, underscored benefits they have derived from the university and scientific cooperation between France and Cameroon. It was during celebrations marking the 30th anniversary of the cooperation.
Speaking during the ceremony, the Minister of Higher Education, Professor Maurice Tchuente said that the cooperation, signed February 21, 1974, is diversified as it involves almost all sectors in the country notably, education, scientific research, public health, science, sports, culture, communication and economic. In 30 years, Minister Tchuente noted, research activities in the country have improved considerably in vital sectors such as the development of cotton, banana, oil palm, and also in the fight against malaria, sleeping sickness and HIV/AIDS.
The cooperation also made it possible France and Cameroon to exchange cultural and artistic programmes in radio broadcasting, training of teachers, research and sports. Reports also holds that a series of technical and scientific assistance was given to researchers from Cameroon who wanted to carry out research within and out of Cameroon.
The Rector of the University of Dschang, Professor Jean Louis Ndongmo, said that the first sector in the university to benefit from the cooperation was the department of forestry, which received teachers from France. He also noted that through the cooperation, the institution benefited from technical and financial assistance to buy equipment for the centre and run the distance education programme.
Professor Sammy Beban Chumbow, Rector of the University of Yaounde I, said his institution was the first and only university that started to benefit from the cooperation, way back in the 1960s. He mentioned that most of the university practices in Cameroon and in the University of Yaounde I in particular are fruits of cooperation. Most of the Deans and Chancellor of the university who contributed in conceiving the programmes and shaping the nature of the university were from France. Professor Chumbow explained that in 1974, there was a new- found cooperation based on partnership which has yielded assistance and cooperation in various areas. He highlighted the assistance given to young lecturers who went to France for research and project writing. Through the cooperation the university also got equipment and assistances in diversified areas.
Rectors of the other State universities also stressed on how effective and beneficial the France/Cameroon cooperation has been especially in the field of training personnel, providing equipment, scholarships, etc.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandan Companies Should Open Up
Headline: Ugandan Companies Should Open Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — One Sunday, I was perusing through the business section of Sunday Nation, Kenya's and indeed East African Community's most outstanding newspaper; there were some damn engrossing stories, stories that shone brightly into corporate Kenya.
One that caught my eye was on the impromptu sacking of chief executive of KenCell, Kenya's second largest cell phone operator, there was another story on a scandalous loaning scheme by several commercial banks during the graft-ridden Moi regime. These are but some of the stories run in the media else where outside Uganda.
How come we never see such in our own media?
In Uganda you rarely read about operations scale ups or cut backs, exciting first quarter profit postings or loss disclosures, periodical performance reviews, sackings of top officials, boardroom turmoil, planned product alterations or redesign, corporate acquisitions and similar corporate stories, which I believe would make interesting reading but also keep investors abreast with Uganda.
When you develop a fanatical taste for business stories like I do, you invariably encounter profound frustration about business news in Kampala. Elsewhere, a few of us who have the opportunity to access international press enjoy reading their business sections. Stories there do not focus on banana economy mainstays like vanilla, small time fishing, potatoes and peasantry women mobilising themselves for micro finance.
No, it's stories about big business; planned mergers, turmoil in boardrooms, besieged corporate executives, conglomerates splitting, quarterly corporate reports which update the public on planned moves, profits or loss reports, expansions etc. These are the business stories that project the maturation of economies and the might and clout of the corporate world that every one of us longs for. These are stories that get investors coming.
What is this mysticism of silence in corporate Kampala? Where are the Nile Bank, StanBic or StanChart or Roofings Ltd, MTN, etcetera annual or biannual corporate reports, where are the stories about grand mergers and acquisitions in the city. No, I haven't read anything of that kind, apart from mandatory lousy and confusing annual financial statements, which run as commercials and a few stories about corporate social responsibility.
So why all the ambivalence to public scrutiny?
A journalist friend of mine once told me that the problem does not actually lie with the media houses. He said actually the media houses have tried in vain to get theis tightly guarded information. And it did not take me long to see the source of the problem. The secrecy hinges from the fact that about 90 percent of Uganda's top companies, which steer the economy are almost all family businesses in one way or another hence the mafia-like secrecy code.
It is this Mafioso mode of doing business that led to huge losses during the infamous bank closures of the 90s. If there was a policy on opening up, who knows the losses would have been minimized?
Ordinarily I would think that all these companies whom I know to be very hungry for good positive images, would love to show all their good side in public. Therefore this secretive way of doing business makes me smell a rat and a dead one at that. Perhaps these shrewd businessmen are hiding away from the taxman or there is shoddy business being conducted behind the customer's backs.
The Ugandan public needs detailed and accurate stories of decisions in great board meetings, which affect them, long-term plans, and financial positions of companies. Even if we don't have mature stock markets like the West, which affords the public an opportunity to constantly pry into the health of companies, we must do with what we have. And let no one place the blame on the media, the process has got to start at the corporate end, first with the tearing down of iron curtains round their operations.
The recent saga in NSSF also lends a hand to my argument. If for example, there was maximum openness the public would have known before hand before the workers' money was swindled.
The public, which is shut out yet so loved by every concern, likes to partner with a company-either as customer/client or equity holder-which puts itself up for some sort of public examination through the press and aggressive public relations functions.
This is partly the magic wand of the Western business and why they tend to succeed, yes, openness is a crucial part of the game. Even when foul play is rife, the media, will soon sniff out the whiff and with in no minute the dirt is out in the open. The opaqueness in Corporate Kampala also reflects our collective failures, our narrowness in conducting business, a desire for dark deals, our love for untruthfulness and a propensity for cheating at the fairest opportunity.
Such an explanation also appears to massage and comfort us in our economic straits; as long as we desire to transact big business in secrecy, we shall eternally enjoy that bottom comfort because no body will ask us. The secrecy also will mean we shall not seek advice and very soon failure will be on our doorstep.
The aloofness and seclusion robs corporate Kampala of the benefit of informed commentary and good debate that could be generated from affording the public good information about business deals, quarterly performance reports and the like.
But by extension this absurdity in the corporate realm is also inexorably impinging on Uganda's overall economic posturing because for one; corporate business is the aorta of modern economies. An ill-managed corporate environment is the crisis of a nation. Little information flow deprives our economists an opportunity to serve us enriched commentary and economic discourse. And in the absence of informed economic debate, confusion descends and a country is trapped.
This, in my view is our current fate and much of Africa; namely that even in places where the media-a perceived banner of democracy-has taken root, an entrenched culture of secretiveness is hamstringing its thrust and in the end there's not much benefit from it.
Corporate Kampala, the embodiment of the nation's economic vision is currently trapped in that morass. It is yet to be seen whether the passage of time will eventually alter things and we open up to the public that supports us or we remain condemned to this Mafioso way of running our corporate business.
The writer is a Post Graduate Accounting student in the UK

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Invites Saudis
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Invites Saudis
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has invited Saudi Arabian businessmen to
invest in the processing industry, reports Cyprian Musoke.
In a meeting with members of the Saudi-Uganda Investment Agency at State
House Nakasero recently, Museveni said items like coffee, milk, bananas and
fruits would yield quick returns from investments.
He advised them to form partnerships with manufacturers in the textile
industry or start new factories.
The Saudi Arabian delegation was led by its chairman Abdullahmna Algamid
and the coordinator of Uganda and Saudi Arabia business, Rashid
Semuddu.
Algamid said they had the capacity to add value to Uganda's products,
open up more trade links and attract more Saudi investors.

Original_Search_Title: Is Ssekikubo Man of Conscience Or Controversies?
Headline: Uganda: Is Ssekikubo Man of Conscience Or Controversies?
Body_Text:
Kampala — Walking like a man on a mission, he bent to answer a call on his mobile
phone. After the call, he adjusted his necktie, pulled out a white
handkerchief and cleaned his coat, before entering Parliament, head high,
and with a few beads of sweat on his forehead.
Indeed, he had a mission. Theodore Ssekikubo, the member of parliament
for Lwemiyaga in Ssembabule had just been to Mosa Courts, where he had not
been invited, demanding his share of "kisanja" cash.
When he was put to task to walk the kisanja support in public before he
could get a share of the money, he lost his cool, turned around and said the
whole thing was a bribe.
He then moved out and spread the news to the world. "They are turning
MPs commodities. How can they be bought like prostitutes? he said banging
the table. His remarks sent shock waves through the political sphere.
This was just another chapter in his controversial political
history. Early this year, while ministers Sam Kutesa, Syda Bumba, and MP
Hanifa Kawooya danced to the tune of kisanja, he could not have any of
it.
A local councillor tried to tie esanja(dry banana leaf) around
Ssekikubo's waist but he refused to dance.
"I am a man of conscience. Nobody can push me around," he said.
But why would a person from a staunch Movement area refuse to support
Kisanja? Those who know him well say this is typical of him.
"He loves being in the limelight. He knows once he supports kisanja, he
will be out of the news," one MP says.
He was told before he could receive the money, he should call a press
conference and announce his kisanja for support.
MPs Hanifa Kawooya and Hope Mwesigye said he is just a confused
man.
"What was he doing in a place where he had not been invited?" Kawooya
asked.
There are claims that Ssekikubo was used by members of the opposition to
go to Mosa Courts, pick the money and expose the Movement.
He, however, denies this and at a press conference where he was expected
to announce his support for Kisanja, he blasted the Movement.
Ssekikubo is an angry man. His actions are a result of an infighting
within the Movement.
"I am still in the Movement. I am a founding member of NRM-O," he
claims. In the 2001 parliamentary elections, the Movement supported Sam
Rwakojjo against him. He could be trying to hit back.

Original_Search_Title: Pitfalls in TI's Graft Rating
Headline: Nigeria: Pitfalls in TI's Graft Rating
Body_Text:
DISPASSIONATE, assessment of Transparency International's (TI) recent
corruption index indicates that it is flawed on at least five grounds. It is
subjective having been based on "perceptions" rather than reality.
Even though TI generated reports from eighteen experts with fixed
opinions about world order, it applied an average of six reports for African
countries and the so-called Banana republics while an average of eleven
reports were applied for developed countries.
None of the experts is known to have its operational base in the
"corrupt" countries. The report was based on stale surveys, some of which
were conducted as far back as 2002. The statistical analysis did not take
into consideration the law of first impressions neither did it take into
consideration the fact that people seldom change their minds once they are
made up.
TI has damaged and is damaging Nigeria as a brand name with these highly
questionable and defective surveys. Those of us supporting the rating should
think carefully of the long term implications of providing TI the weapon to
permanently destroy our reputation on the altar of what has been exposed as
dangerous publicity stunt.
Instead of destructive criticism, we should take positive steps to fight
corruption and save the reputation of our country from permanent damage. It
is time to move beyond criticisms, and blames. It is time to take
responsibility. Ethics friendly Nigerians need to come together to fast
track transparency, due process and good governance; support government
reform initiatives; and take steps to complement the work of Mr. President
and anti-corruption agencies which include EFCC, ICPC, CCB and the Due
Process office. Project Edge provides the opportunity to do something
positive.
This is achievable through the Ethics, Due Diligence and Good Governance
Empowerment Project, a partnership platform for building capacity of key
stakeholders to act as catalyst for introducing, fast tracking and
sustaining anti-corruption reform initiatives in their various units,
departments, institutions, etc.
This is implemented with the technical support and collaboration of
Independent Corrupt Practices and other Related Offences Commission (ICPC),
the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), the Code of Conduct
Bureau (CCB), and the Due Process Unit of the Presidency.
The project was launched on August 11, 2004 at Nicon Hilton Abuja. Since
then, five streams of capacity building retreats have been organised for 795
carefully selected officials from local, state and federal ministries,
agencies, parastatals as well as military, paramilitary and private sector
organisations.
Yes, the Anti-corruption revolution is coming. But it will not come from
TI's flawed annual corruption perception index. It will come from the
growing number of this project's activists. So do not join in running down
your country. Rather, stand up and do something positive. Clear your
calendar and be part of the movement to fast track reforms. Support ICPC,
EFCC, CCB, due process mechanism and other government anti-corruption reform
initiatives. They represent the most credible and serious effort to tackle
corruption in the history of Nigeria. They represent our best chance.
Onyechere is national coordinator, Project Edge and Chairman, Exam
Ethics Project

Original_Search_Title: TV Glare: Parliament Fails Test Number One
Headline: Uganda: TV Glare: Parliament Fails Test Number One
Body_Text:
Kampala — When the 7th parliament opened its 1st sitting of the second meeting for
the fourth session on Tuesday, it turned out the case of making one stride
forward and two backwards.
The moment was a historic one. For the first time, the proceedings were
to be aired live on WBS TV, something that was hoped to bring significant
mileage to the august House; not only in broadening openness of
parliamentary business but also offering the wider public an opportunity to
assess (firsthand) their MPs' performance.
Thus, it was fair to expect sobriety, more active participation in
debates, brighter ideas, alertness and fair attributes. That test though was
miserably failed, sooner than we had hoped - when the floor erupted into the
melee of a roadside market scene; very much like Kamwokya or Nakawa on
Fridays and Saturdays. Mr Isaac Kagoda, 35, a civil engineer who hails from
Bugabula North, in MP Moses Kizige's constituency, says he had never been
impressed by any of his lawmaker's acts.
He however confessed, Wednesday, that he was humbled and dazzled by
Kizige's motion, which quickly drew support and successfully neutralised
Aruu county MP, Odongo Otto's petition for a select committee to investigate
bribery charges against 223 legislators. Mr Kizige pointed out poignantly
that the petition was premised on streetside rumour and inaccurate newspaper
accounts, a fact, he said, rendered it incompetent.
"He won my admiration that day. I actually never voted for him but after
seeing how he powerfully put across his case, I'm ready to deliver him my
support, should he stand again in 2006," he said.
Still, Kagoda and many others were awed by Otto's fervour and spark in
moving the House to embrace his petition; particularly when he provoked
laughter by illustrating how newspaper cartoonists had had a field day,
caricaturing the accused MPs in banana leaves streaming into Mosa Courts and
then scrambling for the cash - upon which Otto argued that such conduct had
occasioned disgrace to the House and embarrassment to the petitioners.
Ugandans though were largely stunned by the unseemly scene that engulfed
the House: the volley of venomous exchanges, the near occurrence of a
fistfight between Otto and junior Defence minister Ruth Nankabirwa, the
provocative gestures, the tempers, and the entire chaos that forced House
Speaker Edward Sekandi to order a five minute adjournment to restore
calm.
"I was embarrassed, watching how our honourable members could bring
themselves to behave like taxi park touts," said Mr Benson Mugume, a masters
student at Makerere University who watched the entire session. He wished the
proceedings hadn't been broadcast, sparing the public that drama.
A number of MPs who are traditionally star performers in the House,
appear to have earned extra marks, as their constituents finally got
real-time view of what they had always read in the papers and heard on FM
stations.
Mr Ben Wacha, the Oyam north MP, a suave and star debater impressed one
of his constituents, Ms Grace Achen, 32, when he argued that while it was
easy to strike down Otto's petition, it was wiser to green-light it so that
it could offer the accused an opportunity to exonerate themselves.
An outright defeat of the petition, he said, would rob them of an
opportunity and history wouldn't judge them well.
"He tried to cut a middle-ground. Even the silence that prevailed as he
spoke was a clear indication of how serious he is taken," she said. While
Wacha's submission never won over any MP, that afternoon, its brilliance was
not lost on many.
It was not the same however for the reputable legislator from Samia
Bugwe South, Mr Aggrey Awori whose wit and articulation are rarely
matched.
That day, when he was supposed to demonstrate his famed brainpower to
his people out there, somebody jinxed him. Awori instead shocked many when
emotion took the better of him.
He stood up to speak but instantly let out an outburst, charging at the
MPs seated right across him: "You ate the money," he charged. He used
language that every MP knows is unparliamentary.
With that statement, Awori actually accelerated the tension; it
culminating into the uproar - till Sekandi halted the
proceedings. Nevertheless, one Awori supporter still revelled in what he
called the "energy, and passion" with which he delivered his
submission.
There was also playing to the gallery; the dramatic style of MP Michael
Mabbike and Rubaga South MP, Ken Lukyamuzi who have a habit of raising
points of Information or points of Order merely to disrupt the speaker or
rouse the House.
That's actually Ken's debating stamp mark. "It was great watching
him. He was still Ken the man; hilarious and exciting," a supporter, Timothy
Nsubuga, 28, said.
There were disappointments too. Tubwita Tumwesigye, who hails from
Rwampara county, in Mbarara (MP Amon Muzoora's constituency) wasn't amused
by his lawmaker's talk on the floor.
"He tried to make an argument in support of Kizige's motion, but it was
really lousy and badly expressed; it never received applause even as the
House was largely in support of Kizige," Tubwita, an apparels retailer in
City centre, told Sunday Monitor.
He wondered whether Muzoora can ever persuade the House to support a
government programme in his area given that "weak expression."
A member of the public, who simply watched the programme out of
curiosity to gauge the general performance of MPs, said she noticed that
women appeared to play more of spectators than actors.
She says he was shocked to see only two or three women utter a word,
throughout the entire debate, on a subject that was so controversial.
"It gave us a terrible picture about their competence and ability to
defend the interests of their millions of colleagues out there."
"I remember one; I can't recall her name, who stood up only to state the
most obvious of arguments; I was let down," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Change of Approach On Stars' Bench Refreshing
Headline: Kenya: As I See It: Change of Approach On Stars' Bench Refreshing
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There was something refreshing about the way way the Kenya technical bench approached the Harambee Stars World Cup and Africa Cup of Nations qualifying round game against Guinea on Wednesday this week.
The almost daily press briefings by new coach Twahir Muhiddin left no one in doubt about what he was doing or thinking in regard to the way he was going to determine the players to start the game.
He talked about formation, fitness and attitude.
In the games where he was not in charge of the team, he said, he had noticed that the Stars tended to drift in the second half and were always at their most vulnerable at that point. He had also wondered at the actual fitness levels of some of the players. Then of course he needed to know what the players themselves thought their personal roles in the team were.
When he took charge of the team following well documented, if disputed, developments after the disastrous game in Gaborone, Botswana, Muhiddin's task was decided. Communication channels were opened between the players, the Harambee Stars Management Board and the Fifa/KFF normalisation committee. Allowances were paid, additional incentives were promised by Sports Minister Ochillo Ayacko and the players obliged.
This is the spirit that one wishes to see continue even at the KFF elections which come next month.
In terms of actual performance, such was our finishing strength that the fans were disappointed we did not score more goals. For the first time in several international matches, we were not holding out for a result but chasing to score more goals.
There was strength on the bench which was good to see. It was also great seeing fresh squad inclusion Zablon Amanaka playing so well after having sat out a couple of years, and 2004 call-ups Edwin Mkenya and Ali Breik win their first caps.
Now as every coach will tell you, whenever a team squad is identified, there are dynamics within it that have to be considered if one desires to maximise on the potential of each member.
In the old days, nearly all Kenya national team selections showed aspects of patronage in one way or the other depending on who had more clout within the soccer governing authorities.
Generally selections had to reflect a sense of parity between the largest soccer playing communities as the favourite catchwords were national outlook. With that as the yardstick the selectors' options were already decided even before they started the business of choosing players.
In other countries these dynamics have forced national soccer federations to opt for foreign coaches. This is because at times foreigners can be the only people excused for transgressing on otherwise sacrosanct issues.
In yet other cultures these aspects take on more ominous perspectives.
Players with distinct negroid features were regarded as bad luck in Brazil after the 1950 World Cup which Brazil lost to Uruguay at their sparkling purposefully built 100,000 seater Maracanha Stadium. The negroid featured Brazilian goalkeeper was blamed for the defeat.
That stigma of being truly negroid in Brazilian society has never been eradicated despite the arrival of a 17 year-old Edson Arantes dos Nascimento in the run-up to the 1958 Swedish World Cup as Pele.
We may feel that modern football should have banished such twisted thinking a long time ago. The fact is it has not. There still exists those who hold on to old-time values. What is disconcerting is that these elements are finding new voice in the economically-challenged European countries.
The British realised it was a problem about a decade ago and have been doing something about it. They have not perfected it, but they have made progress. A glance at Premiership club lineups confirms this.
When Gordon Taylor managed England at the time Luther Blissett was the hottest striker in the British isles, banana skins hurled from the terraces greeted his striker's every move on the pitch.
Now the baton has crossed the English Channel.
In Madrid, a city bidding to host the 2012 Olympics, racist taunts were aimed at the black players on England's soccer teams this week.
While the Spaniards outplayed England for long periods at the Santiago Bernabeu stadium, their fans taunted Shaun Wright-Phillips and Ashley Cole whenever they had the ball.
"It was very bad to hear," said England coach Sven-Goran Eriksson. "When you hear people doing it at players because of the colour of their skin I am sorry to hear it."
It is good and fitting to see that the British government is set to take up the issue with the Spaniards.
"I was absolutely appalled by the reaction of the crowd and I will be writing to the Spanish sports minister to express my outrage at their behaviour," Britain's Sports Minister Andrew Caborn has said.
In Zurich Fifa have expressed their concern about the latest surge of racism in football and has harshly condemned these incidents.
The incidents involving coloured English players who were the target of racial abuse from spectators during the Under 21s friendly match on Tuesday and again when the nations' senior sides met on Wednesday have been mentioned.
Last Saturday, after a match between Bastia and St Etienne, so-called fans attacked Bastia's coloured players. Last month, some of Arsenal's players were the object of racist abuse during a Champions League match between Panathinaikos and the English club.
Fifa say they will be investigating the circumstances of the two friendly matches between Spain and England as they come under Fifa's ambit. They will demand explanations from the Spanish football association so that they can examine the incidents and clarify whether the Statutes, regulations and Code of Ethics of Fifa have been violated. The Code of Ethics was approved by the Fifa executive committee at their meeting on October 6.
Fifa President Joseph Blatter has expressed his horror at the recent spate of incidents and on Thursday appealed to the global football community and society in general to reject racism of any kind.
"There is no room whatsoever for racism or discrimination in our sport. On the contrary, football is a tool for building bridges and nurturing tolerance. The world is already too full of conflict that has its roots in racism and discrimination. Football has a positive influence that can and must be used to eradicate this scourge."
During the extraordinary Fifa Congress in Buenos Aires on July 7, 2001, Fifa held their first anti-racism conference at which a far-reaching resolution was passed. The essential points of the resolution have since been reaffirmed several times and reiterated at various events all over the world.
Racism and discrimination include any kind of ostracism, whether religious, political or otherwise.

Original_Search_Title: How Sh2,500 Meant Difference Between Life, Death to Mother
Headline: Kenya: How Sh2,500 Meant Difference Between Life, Death to Mother
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sunday November 7 came like any other day for 24-year-old Hellen
Kerubo.
The mother of a three-year-old daughter, was an excited lady because her
bananas were ripe for sale at Daraja Mbili market in Kisii town.
Kerubo, her husband James Nyabiyage Matara their daughter, Kang'ina,
were all hoping that the sales would be good enough to help them buy a meal
the following day.
They anticipated for Sh380 sales that day.
Kerubo offered some of the bananas for their evening meal and another
little bunch for her widowed mother-in-law, Mong'ina Matara to take as
lunch.
In her excitement and hope for a big sale at the eagerly awaited day's
market, the three never knew that fate and poverty would bring them tragedy
instead of a big sale.
In the small hours of the night, Kerubo developed stomach pains that she
first jokingly told her husband could have been caused by the bananas they
ate for supper.
Innitially, things appeared not life threatening. She took a glass of
water and rested on a wooden chair in their grass-thatched hut at Gesero
village of Suneka division, Kisii District.
The pains subsided but came back at about 5am. She asked for more water
but the pain went on.
"Between us was Sh35...not enough for us to seek medication at the Kisii
District Hospital," Matara said.
With his wife writhing in pain and desperately calling for attention,
Matara decided to carry her on his back to hospital, nine kilometers
away.
It took him and two other men two hours to get her to hospital shortly
after 7am, and they had to wait for the doctors up to around 8.30am.
Kerubo, who could not speak, was admitted to the district hospital where
the District Physician, Dr J P Owuor, helped lessen the burning pains. Hope
was sluggishly coming back.
"After two days of treatment the doctors ordered that we take an X-ray
of her stomach," Matara said.
The X-ray report indicated that Kerubo had a complication in her
intestines. So Dr Owuor transferred her to Ward 2 since the case required an
operation.
At least that was the only way out to rescue Kerubo's life, according to
Dr Owuor. ForMatara, a man who had just gotten a casual job earning Sh480
per week.
The worry was how to get money. The operation fee would cost
Sh2,500. And there was no blood group AB negative at the hospital.
But they had to get it from wherever they could.
It was such that Matara and his equally poor relatives decided to look
for any person who would buy some half an acre parcel of their family
land.
They got a buyer who offered them Sh10,000. They decided to sell a
family goat, Kang'ina's best playing mate at Sh2,500 and the unsold bananas
at a wholesale bargain price of Sh220.
Kang'ina's cries as the buyer drove the goat out of the home were
distressing. Sadly, it was the only way her mother's life could be
saved.
The fee was promptly paid the following Thursday the blood bought from
two willing donors in the village.
Reaching the hospital, they were told that her operation would be done
the following day.
In the growing anxiety, the family resolved to hire the services of
another surgeon. The hospital could not allow this.
Come Friday morning, and the situation became more complicated.
A 75-year-old retired teacher from Nyanchwa estate had to be attended to
after thugs shot him.
That meant the same surgeon, Dr T N Nyamache, had to attend to the
patient.
Mzee Francis Onyoni Makone, unfortunately, died later.
Come Saturday when Kerubo's operation should have been carried out and
allegedly there was no oxygen at the hospital.
And shortly after midday, she breathed her last, a death her husband and
relatives contend was caused by delay and red tape.
Kisii Medical Officer of Health, Dr Erick Abunga, confirmed that she was
admitted to the hospital.
"I have called for her file and learnt that she was suffering from
intestinal obstruction for which an operation was preferred," Abunga
stated.
Saying his next move would be to summon the surgeon to explain to him
the circumstances of the incident. Abunga said the complication was
manageable.
"She was not at a point of no return though she could die while
undergoing operation by bad luck," Abunga stated.
The surgeon who was enlisted for the patient, Dr T N Nyamache,on his
part blamed the eventuality on lack of oxygen at the hospital.
"i handled a shooting victim on Friday when Kerubo's should have been
attended and I tried to resuscitate her unsuccessfully on Saturday,"
Nyamache said.
Saying Kerubo's fate could be fifty-fifty under normal circumstances, Dr
Nyamache says Kerubo suffered from addition of intestinal obstruction
resulting from an earlier operation.
It was confirmed that she had been operated at a private hospital before
her marriage to Matara three years ago.
"Surprisingly enough, patients from well-off families are usually
attended in these public hospitals within the required times while many poor
people like us are frustrated due to lack of money with which to push things
fast," a relative claimed.
The protesting relatives want the government to compel the Kisii
District Hospital management to explain why they refused to allow the other
willing surgeon on leave to handle the emergency.
Kerubo's body was taken to Nyangena Hospital Mortuary.

Original_Search_Title: 'Third Term Not Enough for Museveni'
Headline: Uganda: 'Third Term Not Enough for Museveni'
Body_Text:
Bushenyi — If President Yoweri Museveni got more 25 years, Uganda would be more developed, and the economy would flourish with self-sustainability, the Vice President, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya, has said.
"I was pleased that a long the Kabwohe-Kyamuhunga road, there were many people dressed in masanja'(dry banana leaves) and yellow M.M.M T-shirts for the support of the third term for President Museveni. Tshese years are still very few, we want him to rule for more 25 years," Bukenya said.
He was on Sunday speaking at the official opening of St Daniel Comboni Hospital in Kyamuhunga sub-county. The Comboni Catholic father's missionaries completed the hospital in the late 1990s.

Original_Search_Title: The wireless revolution
Headline: Mauritius: The wireless revolution
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Each time technology makes advancement, huge revolution occurs in the
world. When man mastered fire, not only cooked food was available but he
also found a new safety device.
When he manufactured tools, not only artefacts were made, but also with
wheels. In more recent times, advancements in communication, medicine, space
and so on act as a time machine, launching him in the future of progress. He
is no longer that rare conspicuous animal roaming and wondering as nomads
looking for food. Today his behaviour has changed, putting him above the
animal kingdom. He has become human, and predictably with the revolution in
wireless communication, he will become super human.
In the 1990s people went bananas about the wireless. Electronic
communications once thought bound permanently to the world of castles and
hard-wired connection suddenly were sprung free and the possibilities seemed
endless. A century back, the "magic box", a wireless invention, kicked off
an era of profound changes. Today again a wireless transformation will
arguably shatter the whole planet. Certainly a huge part of this evolution
comes from untethering the most powerful communication tools. Between mobile
phones and computers, we are always connected. Soon our cars and appliances
will be too. The wireless innovation sheds the tethers of conventional
wisdom.
Wireless technology will be the means by which previously unwired chunks
of civilization will plug into the cyber action, (due to its low cost and
little upfront infrastructure investment). Pioneers are bringing wireless to
places as distant as Pirai, Brazil and Colorado spring. Wi-fi is only one of
dozens of wireless variants in this revolution. You might know GPS and
satellite radio, blue tooth and RFID, but do you know the Zig-Bee? (It's a
means of networking lots of appliances). The result is about losing the wire
further and further. Now let us have an overview of these
possibilities.
In England, more precisely at Soho, the police have turned this small
area into the first wireless law-enforcement district. Wireless cameras and
sensors have been placed around the neighbourhood. They take real time
videos and monitor unusual noises, creating an impression of total
surveyance. Still concerning security and governance, in New York, traffic
police carry barcode scanners linked via Wi-fi to portable printers tracking
contravened vehicles, thus reducing errors.
The life of young South Koreans revolves around the wireless
Internet. During their morning subway commute to school, graduate students
at Yanse University in Seoul chat with each other or play online games
through their PDA phones. They bring their laptops to class, take notes,
search on the Internet and make presentations using Wi-fi hotpots sprinkled
around the campus. The computer even accompanies them on zoology field trips
in the country side. In Seoul, Wi-fi is available in airports, hotels,
public offices, libraries, banks and fast food outlets. Concerning
intellectual abilities, Tallinn, in Estonia, is a good example. It has built
a reputation as a clandestine meeting spot for writers and
intellectuals.
The most astonishing presence of wireless communication is in an
up-to-now close circle - the clergy. Monks and nuns have always thrown an
image of tradition and conservatism. The cloth has always seemed to be a
powerful resistance to science and technology. Their behaviour and way of
life entangled with their inaccesible monasteries have long seemed a barrier
to materialism. But, if you walk through the doors of St Peter's Basilica
today, you might catch the glow of a laptop or wireless PDA through the
smoky haze of burning incense. The distant hum of Gregorian chants may even
be interrupted by the bleep of a mobile phone or the ping of a text
message. The Vatican City is taking advantage of wireless techonology to
spread the work even further. Prior to that, the Church also began issuing a
daily papal prayer in the form of a cell phone text message. It is not
amazing. We may even agree on incredible.
After such a vivid image of what is happening with the wireless
revolution around the world, we may start wondering where Mauritius is in
this train of change. Can this Island aspiring to be a cyber elite in the
Southern hemisphere be up-to-date with new development in communication? Do
you think taking out a huge imposing building out from nowhere and
repeatedly calling it cybertower can push us to control part of this
targeted market? Some optimist says we are on the right track. We already
have a massive use of cellular phones. But can mobile phones used only for
vocal communication and emailing be sufficient to transform this island into
a cyber one ? The answer is simple: new up-to-date Japanese phones are
taking 2 to 3 years to reach us here in Mauritius.
Are we not using obselete equipment to reach our aim as cyber elite ?
When are we going to see our students with their laptops on educational
tours ? Perhaps over 10 years. When are we going to see traffic inserted in
a wireless device to unveil all the secrets of the driver? When are we going
to see antennas, each the size of cigarette pack in hospitals, schools, city
libraries and open-air kiosks ? This will take us at least 10 years and by
that time our sacred cyber tower would have become a rusty white
elephant.

Original_Search_Title: 'Kazibwe's Shs2.5 Billion a Lie'
Headline: Uganda: 'Kazibwe's Shs2.5 Billion a Lie'
Body_Text:
Kabale — The Parliamentary Affairs Minister, Ms Hope Mwesigye, has said the government has never given Shs2.5 billion to former Vice President, Dr Specioza Kazibwe. She said the government contributes a very small section of Kazibwe's fees.
She said the opposition is just using the Shs2.5 billion to incite the local people and civil servants against the Movement government. Mwesigye was on Saturday addressing Kabale women leaders at the district Rukiiko Hall.
Mwesigye said the politicians on the opposition should stick to issues other than promoting none issues She advised the women leaders to form a strong united movement team if they are to defeat the growing opposition in the country.
Mwesigye also defended the dressing of dry banana leaves demonstrating support for the third term for President Museveni saying that it is not madness but showing the confidence the people have in the President and the Movement leadership.
"Do not be confused by rumors being spread around by the politicians opposed to the Movement leadership but stick to what the system has done to develop your areas. The opposition shall use every effort to discredit is but stay firm," she said.
Also present was the national Vice Chairperson for women councils, Ms Pauline Kirasha, who asked local leaders to work hard in soliciting support for the Movement.

Original_Search_Title: Mbarara Choses to Focus On Milk, Tourism
Headline: Uganda: Mbarara Choses to Focus On Milk, Tourism
Body_Text:
Mbarara — Milk and banana production and the development of tourism have been chosen as areas for a competitive strategy for the region.
This was agreed upon at half-day workshop during the launch of Uganda's competitiveness awareness month at Lake View Regency Hotel recently.
This means that the private sector in Mbarara will concentrate in the processing of milk, production of bananas and development of tourism to give them a competitive edge in business over other places in the country.
The workshop was organised by Strengthening the
Competitiveness of Private Enterprise (SCOPE), a USAID- funded project.
SCOPE works with the private sector to improve on Uganda's share of regional and international markets in high priority sectors including coffee, cotton, fish, dairy, floriculture, grains, oilseeds and tourism.
